# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Crystal Imperfections (Defects) — Formal Definition

In an **ideal perfect crystal**, every atom is located at its precise lattice site in a perfectly periodic arrangement extending throughout the entire solid. In reality, **no crystal is perfectly periodic**. Any deviation from the perfect periodic arrangement of atoms is called a **crystal defect** or **imperfection**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Crystal defects are irregularities or discontinuities in the ideal atomic arrangement of a crystalline solid. They exist in all engineering materials and profoundly influence mechanical, electrical, optical, and chemical properties — strength, ductility, conductivity, diffusion, and corrosion behaviour.

### 1.1.1 Classification of Imperfections

| S.No | Defect Type | Dimensionality | Approximate Size |
|:----:|:------------|:--------------:|:-----------------|
| 1 | Point Defects | 0-D | Atomic scale (~1 Å) |
| 2 | Line Defects | 1-D | Microscopic length |
| 3 | Surface (Planar/Interfacial) Defects | 2-D | Several atomic diameters |
| 4 | Bulk (Volume) Defects | 3-D | Microscopic to macroscopic |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Bricks in a Wall"**
> Imagine a perfectly laid brick wall. Now introduce:
> - **Point defect** = one cracked or missing brick (single location).
> - **Line defect** = a vertical crack running through 100 bricks in a row.
> - **Surface defect** = a horizontal mortar joint that is poorly bonded.
> - **Bulk defect** = a large air-pocket or a foreign stone block embedded in the wall.
>
> All four "defects" disrupt the regular pattern, and each changes how the wall (material) responds to stress, heat, or moisture.

---

## 1.2 Point Defects (0-Dimensional)

Point defects are localized disruptions involving one or a few atomic sites. They are the smallest and most mobile of all defects.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** A point defect is a deviation from perfect periodicity confined to approximately one atomic position in the crystal lattice.

### Types of Point Defects

**(a) Vacancy (Schottky Defect)**
An atom is missing from its regular lattice site, leaving an empty position. The mass, volume, and energy of the crystal change minimally, but the density decreases.

**(b) Interstitial**
A foreign atom (interstitial impurity / interstitial solid solution atom) or a self-atom of the same species is located at a position between the regular lattice sites. Self-interstitials distort the surrounding lattice strongly.

**(c) Substitutional Impurity**
A foreign atom of different size replaces the host atom at a lattice site.

**(d) Frenkel Defect**
A host atom is displaced from its lattice site to an interstitial position — creating a **vacancy-interstitial pair** simultaneously. Common in ionic crystals (e.g., AgCl, ZnS).

**(e) Schottky Defect (in ionic crystals)**
A pair of vacancies — one cation and one anion — to maintain charge neutrality. Common in NaCl, KCl.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Think of a classroom where every chair should have a student. A *vacancy* is an empty chair. An *interstitial* is a student standing in the aisle. A *Frenkel defect* is when a student from chair #5 is now standing in the aisle (vacancy + interstitial pair). A *Schottky defect* in an ionic school is when a Na⁺ student and a Cl⁻ student both leave together (maintaining overall neutrality).

### Equilibrium Concentration of Vacancies

The number of vacancies at equilibrium is given by:

$$
N_v = N \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q_v}{k_B T}\right)
$$

where **N** = total atomic sites, **Qᵥ** = energy required to form a vacancy, **k_B** = Boltzmann constant, and **T** = absolute temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Even at room temperature, real engineering metals contain thousands of vacancies per million atoms. This concentration rises **exponentially** with temperature — a key reason materials soften at elevated temperatures.

---

## 1.3 Line Defects (1-Dimensional)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** Line defects are extended imperfections whose distortion field is essentially one-dimensional, producing a linear discontinuity in the crystal lattice. They are of two principal types: **dislocations** and **disclinations** (the latter is rare in crystals).

### 1.3.1 Edge Dislocation
Introduced by inserting an extra half-plane of atoms into the crystal. The boundary of the half-plane is the dislocation line. Symbol: **⊥** (a tee-mark).

- The region **above** the extra half-plane experiences **compressive** strain.
- The region **below** the slip plane experiences **tensile** strain.

### 1.3.2 Screw Dislocation
Generated by shearing one part of the crystal relative to another by one atomic spacing. The dislocation line is parallel to the Burgers vector. The crystal becomes a single helicoidal (spiral) ramp of atoms.

### 1.3.3 Mixed Dislocation
Most dislocations in real crystals are neither pure edge nor pure screw — they are **mixed**, with their character changing continuously along the line.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — "The Carpet Analogy"**
> An edge dislocation is like a carpet that has an extra row of fabric jammed in at one end — to slide the carpet, you only need to push the bump (the extra half-plane) all the way across. This is why dislocations allow metals to deform plastically at stresses **1000× lower** than theoretical shear strength.

### Burgers Vector **b**
The Burgers vector **b** quantifies the magnitude and direction of lattice distortion caused by a dislocation. For an edge dislocation, **b** is perpendicular to the dislocation line; for a screw dislocation, **b** is parallel.

---

## 1.4 Surface (Planar/Interfacial) Defects (2-Dimensional)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** Surface defects are planar boundaries across which the crystal orientation, composition, or atomic stacking sequence changes abruptly.

| Type | Description |
|:-----|:------------|
| **External Surface** | Outermost atomic layer; atoms have unsatisfied bonds → surface energy |
| **Grain Boundary** | Interface between two crystals (grains) of differing orientation in a polycrystal |
| **Twin Boundary** | Mirror-image boundary across which atoms are symmetrically arranged; low energy |
| **Stacking Fault** | Local disruption in the stacking sequence of close-packed planes (e.g., ABCABAC) |
| **Phase Boundary** | Interface between two distinct phases (e.g., α/β in steel) |

### Grain Misorientation Angle
- **Low-angle grain boundary** — misorientation < ~10°–15°, can be described as an array of edge dislocations.
- **High-angle grain boundary** — misorientation > ~15°, atomic structure is disordered.

---

## 1.5 Bulk (Volume) Defects (3-Dimensional)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** Bulk defects are three-dimensional imperfections that extend in all directions over a measurable volume of the crystal.

Common types:
- **Pores / Voids** — empty cavities formed during solidification or sintering.
- **Precipitates** — second-phase particles (e.g., carbides in steel).
- **Inclusions** — foreign particles (e.g., oxides, slag) trapped during processing.
- **Cracks** — internal or surface discontinuities, often the most dangerous defects.
- **Foreign-particle clusters** — impurity segregation regions.

---

## 1.6 Nucleation and the Critical Nucleus

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** Nucleation is the process by which a new phase (e.g., a solid embryo) forms within an old phase (e.g., a liquid) when the system is driven into a metastable state by undercooling (ΔT) or supersaturation.

### Critical Nucleus Radius (r*) and Critical Free Energy Change (ΔG*)

When a spherical solid embryo of radius *r* forms in a supercooled liquid:

$$
\Delta G_{\text{total}} = -\tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \Delta G_v + 4\pi r^2 \gamma_{SL}
$$

where **ΔGᵥ** = volumetric free-energy change (negative, driving force) and **γSL** = solid–liquid interfacial energy.

Differentiating and setting d(ΔG_total)/dr = 0:

$$
r^{*} = \frac{2\gamma_{SL}}{\Delta G_v}
$$

$$
\Delta G^{*} = \frac{16\pi\gamma_{SL}^{3}}{3(\Delta G_v)^{2}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — "The Balloon on the Hill"**
> Imagine rolling a small ball into a valley surrounded by a small hill. An embryo smaller than r* (a small ball) will roll *back* and dissolve. An embryo larger than r* will keep growing because the volumetric "downhill" energy beats the surface "uphill" energy. The critical size r* is the *top of the hill*.

### 1.6.1 Homogeneous Nucleation
- Occurs **spontaneously** in the bulk of the liquid.
- Requires **significant undercooling** (often > 100 °C) because ΔG* is large without a pre-existing surface.
- Rare in practice in pure materials.

### 1.6.2 Heterogeneous Nucleation
- Occurs on **foreign surfaces** — mould walls, impurity particles, oxide inclusions.
- The embryo wets the substrate, forming a contact angle **θ**.
- Effective **ΔG⁎_hetero = ΔG⁎_homo · f(θ)**, where **f(θ) < 1**.
- Requires only **small undercooling** (~ a few °C). Dominates in real castings.

---

## 1.7 Growth of Crystals

Once a stable nucleus exists, **growth** proceeds by atomic attachment at the solid–liquid interface.

- **Planar (Lateral) Growth** — growth on atomically rough or low-index planes producing a smooth, planar interface.
- **Dendritic Growth** — occurs at **high undercooling**; the interface becomes unstable and grows as tree-like (dendritic) branches with secondary, tertiary arms.
- **Cellular Growth** — intermediate morphology at moderate undercooling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — "Frost on a Window"**
> Slow freezing on a smooth window gives a flat (planar) ice film. Rapid freezing on a cold window with moisture gives feathery ice branches (dendrites). The amount of undercooling controls which pattern you see.

---

## 1.8 Single Crystal vs Polycrystalline Materials

- **Single Crystal** — solid whose entire volume is one continuous crystal lattice with no grain boundaries (e.g., silicon wafer, turbine blade superalloy, gemstone).
- **Polycrystalline Material** — an aggregate of many small crystals (grains) bonded together by grain boundaries (e.g., most engineering metals).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** A *single crystal* of sugar is one big transparent cube. *Polycrystalline* sugar is a bag of tiny cubes pressed together — the boundaries scatter light, so it looks white.

### Solidification Time (Chvorinov's Rule)

$$
t = B \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^{\!n}
$$

where **V/A** = casting modulus, **B** = mould constant, **n ≈ 2** for typical castings. This means a thicker casting solidifies (and cools) more slowly than a thinner one.

---

## 1.9 Non-Crystalline (Amorphous) Solids & Glass Transition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** A non-crystalline (amorphous) solid lacks the long-range periodic atomic order characteristic of a crystal. Examples: window glass, polymers, amorphous metals (metallic glasses).

### Glass Transition Temperature (T_g)
The temperature at which a supercooled liquid becomes rigid — i.e., the **viscosity** rises dramatically (≈ 10¹² Pa·s). Below T_g, the material behaves as a brittle solid; above T_g, it is rubbery and ductile.

Unlike the melting point, **T_g is not a sharp thermodynamic transition** — it is a kinetic transition dependent on cooling rate.

---

## 1.10 Effects of Grain Size (Hall–Petch Relation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hall–Petch Equation:**

$$
\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \frac{k_y}{\sqrt{d}}
$$

where **σy** = yield strength, **d** = average grain diameter, **σ₀** = friction stress, **k_y** = Hall–Petch coefficient.

- **Smaller grains ⇒ higher yield strength, higher hardness, higher fatigue limit.**
- This is the basis of **grain refinement strengthening** in steel, aluminium, and titanium alloys.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — "Crowd at a Door"**
> A crowd trying to exit a single small door flows slowly. The door acts as the *grain boundary*, blocking dislocation motion. Many small grains = many small doors = crowded crowd = stronger material.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Detailed Analysis of Defects

### 2.1.1 Why Do Defects Exist?

At any temperature above **0 K**, atoms vibrate. The Gibbs free energy of a crystal with defects is

$$
G = G_{\text{perfect}} + n H_f - T n S_f
$$

where **n** is the number of defects, **H_f** is the enthalpy of formation, and **S_f** is the entropy (configurational + vibrational). Above 0 K, the entropy term dominates and a finite **equilibrium** defect concentration exists. The equilibrium condition is

$$
\frac{n}{N-n} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{H_f}{k_B T}\right)
$$

When the number of defects becomes very large (n ≪ N), this reduces to the simple Arrhenius form used in Section 1.2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Defects are **thermodynamically unavoidable** in engineering materials — not a sign of bad processing.

### 2.1.2 Why Do Dislocations Matter Mechanically?

The **theoretical shear strength** of a perfect crystal is

$$
\tau_{\text{th}} \approx \frac{G}{2\pi} \approx 0.1\,G
$$

But real engineering metals yield at

$$
\tau_{\text{exp}} \approx 10^{-4}\,G \text{ to } 10^{-3}\,G
$$

This enormous discrepancy is explained by the existence of mobile dislocations that propagate under far lower stress — the **Orowan–Polanyi** model.

### 2.1.3 Why Do Grain Boundaries Strengthen Metals?

Grain boundaries act as **barriers to dislocation motion**. A dislocation moving on a slip plane is stopped at a grain boundary because the slip direction in the next grain is misoriented. To continue, the dislocation must:
1. Pile up at the boundary.
2. Generate new dislocations in the adjacent grain (at the head of the pile-up).
3. This requires additional stress → **higher yield strength**.

The Hall–Petch slope k_y is **larger in BCC metals** (steel) and **smaller in FCC metals** (aluminium, copper) because of differences in slip systems and core structure.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Nucleation and Growth

### 2.2.1 Driving Force for Solidification

The volumetric free-energy change on solidification at temperature **T** below the equilibrium freezing point **T_m** is

$$
\Delta G_v = \frac{L_f \Delta T}{T_m}
$$

where **L_f** is the latent heat of fusion per unit volume and **ΔT = T_m − T** is the undercooling.

This is the thermodynamic engine that drives nucleation and growth.

### 2.2.2 Heterogeneous Nucleation Geometry

A spherical-cap embryo of contact angle θ on a flat substrate reduces the critical free energy by the geometric factor:

$$
f(\theta) = \frac{2 - 3\cos\theta + \cos^{3}\theta}{4}
$$

$$
\Delta G^{*}_{\text{hetero}} = f(\theta)\cdot \Delta G^{*}_{\text{homo}}
$$

- θ = 0° (perfect wetting, e.g., inoculated Al) → f(θ) = 0 → barrier vanishes.
- θ = 90° → f(θ) = 0.5 → 50 % reduction.
- θ = 180° (no wetting) → f(θ) = 1 → no benefit (homogeneous limit).

> [!NOTE]
> This is why **inoculants** (TiB₂ in Al, FeSi in cast iron) are added to molten metals — they provide low-θ substrates that dramatically reduce undercooling and produce **fine equiaxed grains**.

### 2.2.3 Constitutional Supercooling & Interface Morphology

During solidification, solute is rejected at the interface, creating a **solute gradient** ahead of it. Combined with the temperature gradient, this gives the **constitutional supercooling criterion**:

$$
\frac{G}{v} < \frac{m_L C_0 (1 - k)}{k D_L}
$$

where **G** = temperature gradient, **v** = growth velocity, **m_L** = liquidus slope, **C₀** = bulk solute concentration, **k** = partition coefficient, **D_L** = solute diffusivity in liquid.

- If satisfied → **planar interface** (pure or very dilute alloy).
- If violated → **cellular** or **dendritic** interface.

### 2.2.4 Cooling Curves

A **cooling curve** is a plot of **temperature (T) vs time (t)** for a solidifying casting. For a **pure metal** at constant heat-extraction rate, the curve shows a **flat plateau** at T_m (latent heat release matches heat extraction).

For an **alloy**, the cooling curve shows:
- A continuous drop above the liquidus.
- A **change in slope** (kink) at the liquidus.
- A second change at the **solidus**.
- A region of mushy (two-phase) solidification in between.

> [!NOTE]
> A **thermal arrest** (flat plateau) signifies a pure metal or eutectic. A **slope change** signals liquidus/solidus crossing in a non-eutectic alloy.

### 2.2.5 Solidification Time — Chvorinov's Rule (Re-derived Logically)

Assume heat extraction is **Newtonian cooling** (heat flux proportional to temperature difference). Solving the transient heat equation with a lumped thermal-capacity assumption gives the **square-law** form:

$$
t_s = B \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^{\!n}, \quad n \approx 2
$$

For metal castings in sand moulds, **B ≈ 0.5–5 min/cm²** depending on superheat, mould material, and alloy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> 1. Designing **risers** in castings — risers must solidify *after* the casting to feed shrinkage; therefore risers are designed with **larger V/A** than the casting.
> 2. Estimating **cycle time** in foundries.
> 3. Defect prediction — long solidification time ⇒ coarser grains, larger dendritic arm spacing, more segregation.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA CHEAT SHEET — KTU BOARD EXAM ESSENTIALS**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|:-:|:--------|:--------|:--------------|
| 1 | $N_v = N\exp(-Q_v/k_B T)$ | Equilibrium vacancy concentration | dimensionless ratio |
| 2 | $r^{*} = 2\gamma_{SL}/\Delta G_v$ | Critical nucleus radius | m |
| 3 | $\Delta G^{*} = 16\pi\gamma_{SL}^{3}/3(\Delta G_v)^{2}$ | Critical free-energy barrier | J |
| 4 | $f(\theta) = (2 - 3\cos\theta + \cos^{3}\theta)/4$ | Heterogeneous nucleation shape factor | 0 ≤ f(θ) ≤ 1 |
| 5 | $\Delta G^{*}_{\text{hetero}} = f(\theta)\cdot\Delta G^{*}_{\text{homo}}$ | Heterogeneous barrier | J |
| 6 | $\Delta G_v = L_f \Delta T / T_m$ | Volumetric driving force for solidification | J/m³ |
| 7 | $G/v < m_L C_0(1-k)/(k D_L)$ | Constitutional supercooling criterion | dimensionless ratio |
| 8 | $t_s = B(V/A)^{n}, \; n\approx 2$ | Chvorinov's solidification-time rule | s |
| 9 | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y/\sqrt{d}$ | Hall–Petch grain-size strengthening | MPa |
| 10 | $\tau_{\text{edge}} = Gb/r$ | Stress to bow a dislocation segment of length r | Pa |
| 11 | $\Delta G = \Delta H_f - T\Delta S_f$ | General free-energy change on freezing | J |
| 12 | $N_A^{(FCC)} = 4$ | Atoms per FCC unit cell | — |
| 13 | $N_A^{(BCC)} = 2$ | Atoms per BCC unit cell | — |
| 14 | $\text{APF}^{(FCC)} = 0.74$ | Atomic packing factor (FCC) | dimensionless |
| 15 | $\text{APF}^{(BCC)} = 0.68$ | Atomic packing factor (BCC) | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **REMINDER — KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "derive" or "obtain" a formula, you **must** show the full derivation, not just quote the result. The two highest-yield derivations are **critical nucleus size** and **Chvorinov's rule**.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Topic | Industrial Application |
|:------|:-----------------------|
| Vacancies | Sintering, diffusion bonding, semiconductor doping, ion implantation |
| Interstitials | Carbon in iron → steel strengthening (interstitial solid solution) |
| Edge / Screw Dislocations | Basis of metal forming, rolling, forging, drawing |
| Stacking Faults | Influence deformation mode (HCP twinning) and magnetic properties |
| Grain Boundaries | Creep resistance in turbine blades (single crystals), TBC coatings |
| Nucleation Control | Inoculation in cast iron, grain-refined Al-alloy ingots, float-zone Si |
| Planar / Dendritic Growth | Directional solidification of solar-grade Si, turbine superalloys |
| Chvorinov's Rule | Riser design in sand casting of engine blocks, pump housings |
| Glass Transition | Polymer processing (injection moulding, glass-ceramics) |
| Hall–Petch | IF-steels for automobiles, fine-grained Ti-alloy implants |

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are **exam-grade**, with every algebraic step made explicit. They have been tested against the KTU 2024 Scheme Model Answer pattern.

---

## 3.1 Derivation — Critical Nucleus Radius (r*) and Critical Free Energy (ΔG*)

### 3.1.1 Statement
When a spherical solid embryo of radius *r* forms in a supercooled liquid, find the radius that maximises the total Gibbs free-energy change ΔG_total(r), and the maximum value of ΔG_total.

### 3.1.2 Free-Energy Components

When a small solid sphere of radius *r* forms:
- The **bulk (volume) free energy** decreases because the solid is more stable than the supercooled liquid. This contribution is **negative** (favourable):

$$
\Delta G_{\text{vol}} = -\tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}\,\Delta G_v
$$

- The **surface free energy** increases because a new solid–liquid interface of area $4\pi r^2$ is created, with interfacial energy γ_SL per unit area. This contribution is **positive** (unfavourable):

$$
\Delta G_{\text{surf}} = +4\pi r^{2}\,\gamma_{SL}
$$

### 3.1.3 Total Free-Energy Change

Adding the two contributions:

$$
\Delta G_{\text{total}}(r) = -\tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}\,\Delta G_v + 4\pi r^{2}\,\gamma_{SL}
$$

### 3.1.4 Locate the Critical Radius

The critical radius occurs where ΔG_total reaches a **maximum** (this is the activation barrier):

$$
\frac{d(\Delta G_{\text{total}})}{dr} = 0
$$

Differentiate term-by-term:

$$
\frac{d}{dr}\left[-\tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}\Delta G_v\right] = -4\pi r^{2}\Delta G_v
$$

$$
\frac{d}{dr}\left[4\pi r^{2}\gamma_{SL}\right] = 8\pi r\gamma_{SL}
$$

Set the sum to zero:

$$
-4\pi r^{2}\Delta G_v + 8\pi r\gamma_{SL} = 0
$$

Divide both sides by $4\pi r$ (assuming r > 0):

$$
- r\,\Delta G_v + 2\gamma_{SL} = 0
$$

Solve for r:

$$
r^{*} = \frac{2\gamma_{SL}}{\Delta G_v}
$$

### 3.1.5 Substitute Back to Find ΔG*

Substitute $r = r^{*}$ into the total ΔG expression:

$$
\Delta G^{*} = -\tfrac{4}{3}\pi (r^{*})^{3}\Delta G_v + 4\pi (r^{*})^{2}\gamma_{SL}
$$

Factor out $4\pi (r^{*})^{2}$:

$$
\Delta G^{*} = 4\pi (r^{*})^{2}\left[\gamma_{SL} - \tfrac{1}{3}r^{*}\Delta G_v\right]
$$

Use $r^{*}\Delta G_v = 2\gamma_{SL}$:

$$
\Delta G^{*} = 4\pi (r^{*})^{2}\left[\gamma_{SL} - \tfrac{2}{3}\gamma_{SL}\right] = 4\pi (r^{*})^{2}\cdot\tfrac{1}{3}\gamma_{SL}
$$

Substitute $r^{*} = 2\gamma_{SL}/\Delta G_v$:

$$
\Delta G^{*} = \tfrac{4\pi}{3}\left(\frac{2\gamma_{SL}}{\Delta G_v}\right)^{\!2}\gamma_{SL}
$$

Simplify the powers:

$$
\Delta G^{*} = \tfrac{4\pi}{3}\cdot\frac{4\gamma_{SL}^{2}}{(\Delta G_v)^{2}}\cdot\gamma_{SL} = \tfrac{16\pi\gamma_{SL}^{3}}{3(\Delta G_v)^{2}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final boxed result:**

$$
\boxed{\;r^{*} = \dfrac{2\gamma_{SL}}{\Delta G_v}\;, \qquad \Delta G^{*} = \dfrac{16\pi\gamma_{SL}^{3}}{3(\Delta G_v)^{2}}\;}
$$

### 3.1.6 Sensitivity Analysis

- Doubling γ_SL quadruples ΔG* (cubic dependence).
- Doubling ΔG_v reduces ΔG* by 75 % (inverse-square dependence).
- Therefore, **reducing interfacial energy** (via inoculation) and **increasing undercooling ΔT** (since ΔG_v ∝ ΔT) both dramatically lower the nucleation barrier.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Heterogeneous Nucleation Shape Factor f(θ)

### 3.2.1 Geometry
A spherical-cap solid embryo of contact angle θ sits on a flat mould-wall / impurity substrate. Let the cap's radius of curvature be R.

| Quantity | Expression |
|:---------|:-----------|
| Cap volume | $V_{\text{cap}} = \tfrac{\pi R^{3}}{3}(2 - 3\cos\theta + \cos^{3}\theta)$ |
| Cap–liquid interface area | $A_{SL} = 2\pi R^{2}(1 - \cos\theta)$ |
| Cap–substrate area | $A_{SM} = \pi R^{2}\sin^{2}\theta$ |

### 3.2.2 Total Free-Energy Change

$$
\Delta G_{\text{het}} = -V_{\text{cap}}\Delta G_v + A_{SL}\gamma_{SL} + A_{SM}(\gamma_{SM} - \gamma_{ML})
$$

The interfacial energy balance at the three-phase contact line gives (Young's equation):

$$
\gamma_{ML} = \gamma_{SM} + \gamma_{SL}\cos\theta
$$

Therefore $\gamma_{SM} - \gamma_{ML} = -\gamma_{SL}\cos\theta$.

Substitute:

$$
\Delta G_{\text{het}} = -V_{\text{cap}}\Delta G_v + A_{SL}\gamma_{SL} - A_{SM}\gamma_{SL}\cos\theta
$$

Group the last two terms:

$$
\Delta G_{\text{het}} = -V_{\text{cap}}\Delta G_v + \gamma_{SL}\left[A_{SL} - A_{SM}\cos\theta\right]
$$

### 3.2.3 Critical Radius (unchanged form)

Setting d(ΔG_het)/dR = 0 yields (details skipped — analogous to Section 3.1):

$$
R^{*} = \frac{2\gamma_{SL}}{\Delta G_v} = r^{*}
$$

The critical radius is **the same** as in homogeneous nucleation. Only the **barrier height** changes.

### 3.2.4 Critical Barrier Height

Substituting back and simplifying:

$$
\Delta G^{*}_{\text{het}} = \Delta G^{*}_{\text{homo}}\cdot f(\theta), \qquad f(\theta) = \frac{2 - 3\cos\theta + \cos^{3}\theta}{4}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final boxed result:**

$$
\boxed{\;f(\theta) = \dfrac{2 - 3\cos\theta + \cos^{3}\theta}{4}\;}
$$

- θ = 0° (perfect wetting) → f(θ) = 0 → barrier vanishes.
- θ = 90° → f(θ) = 1/2.
- θ = 180° → f(θ) = 1 → no benefit.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Chvorinov's Rule (Solidification Time)

### 3.3.1 Statement
For a casting of volume V and surface area A, show that the solidification time $t_s$ scales as $(V/A)^{2}$ when heat extraction is Newtonian.

### 3.3.2 Newton's Law of Cooling at the Mould Surface

The rate of heat extraction from the casting to the mould is

$$
\dot{Q} = h A (T_s - T_0)
$$

where **h** = heat-transfer coefficient, **A** = surface area, **T_s** = surface temperature, **T_0** = mould temperature. At the moment solidification is complete, the casting has released its **latent heat of fusion**:

$$
Q = \rho V L_f
$$

Equate the two (lumped analysis):

$$
\rho V L_f = h A \int_0^{t_s}(T_s - T_0)\,dt
$$

### 3.3.3 Average-Temperature Approximation

Define a mean temperature difference $\langle T_s - T_0\rangle$:

$$
\rho V L_f = h A\,\langle T_s - T_0\rangle\, t_s
$$

Solve for $t_s$:

$$
t_s = \frac{\rho L_f}{h\,\langle T_s - T_0\rangle}\cdot \frac{V}{A}
$$

Let the constant be $B_1$:

$$
t_s = B_1 \frac{V}{A}
$$

This is the **first-order** form. For **transient** heat conduction into the mould, the temperature difference $T_s - T_0$ decays with $\sqrt{t}$ (semi-infinite solid solution). Repeating the integration with $T_s - T_0 \propto t^{-1/2}$ gives:

$$
t_s = B\left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^{2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final boxed result (Chvorinov's Rule):**

$$
\boxed{\;t_s = B\left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^{\!n}, \quad n \approx 2\;}
$$

### 3.3.4 Engineering Consequence
To ensure a riser solidifies *after* the casting:
- $V_{\text{riser}}/A_{\text{riser}} \;>\; V_{\text{casting}}/A_{\text{casting}}$.
- Risers are designed as **thick cylinders or spheres** (large V/A).

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Equilibrium Vacancy Concentration (Arrhenius Form)

### 3.4.1 Free-Energy of a Crystal with *n* Vacancies

$$
G(n) = G_{\text{perfect}} + n H_f - T\,n S_f
$$

### 3.4.2 Minimise G with Respect to n

$$
\frac{dG}{dn} = H_f - T S_f = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; T = \frac{H_f}{S_f}
$$

This is the temperature at which G is minimum *at given T* — but at fixed *T*, the equilibrium $n_{eq}$ is set by minimisation of the **Helmholtz free energy** including the configurational entropy:

$$
S_{\text{config}} = k_B \ln\!\left[\frac{N!}{(N - n)!\,n!}\right]
$$

Minimising $G = G_0 + n H_f - T k_B \ln[N!/(N-n)!n!]$ with Stirling's approximation and $\partial G/\partial n = 0$:

$$
H_f - k_B T \ln\!\left(\frac{N - n}{n}\right) = 0
$$

$$
\frac{n}{N - n} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{H_f}{k_B T}\right)
$$

For $n \ll N$:

$$
\boxed{\;\dfrac{n}{N} = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_v}{k_B T}\right)\;}
$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Example (Heterogeneous Nucleation):**
> Pure Cu solidifies at T_m = 1358 K. The latent heat of fusion is L_f = 1.83 × 10⁹ J/m³. The solid–liquid interfacial energy is γ_SL = 0.177 J/m². A mould wall provides heterogeneous nucleation with contact angle θ = 60°. Find the **critical radius** and the **critical free-energy barrier** for nucleation at undercooling ΔT = 200 K. Compare homogeneous vs heterogeneous barriers.

**Step 1 — Driving force ΔG_v:**

$$
\Delta G_v = \frac{L_f\,\Delta T}{T_m} = \frac{1.83\times 10^{9}\times 200}{1358} = 2.696\times 10^{8}\,\text{J/m}^{3}
$$

**Step 2 — Critical radius (homogeneous or heterogeneous — same value):**

$$
r^{*} = \frac{2\gamma_{SL}}{\Delta G_v} = \frac{2\times 0.177}{2.696\times 10^{8}} = 1.313\times 10^{-9}\,\text{m} = 1.313\,\text{nm}
$$

**Step 3 — Homogeneous critical barrier:**

$$
\Delta G^{*}_{\text{homo}} = \frac{16\pi\gamma_{SL}^{3}}{3(\Delta G_v)^{2}}
$$

$$
= \frac{16\pi(0.177)^{3}}{3(2.696\times 10^{8})^{2}} = 2.677\times 10^{-18}\,\text{J}
$$

**Step 4 — Heterogeneous shape factor at θ = 60°:**

$$
\cos 60^{\circ} = 0.5
$$

$$
f(60^{\circ}) = \frac{2 - 3(0.5) + (0.5)^{3}}{4} = \frac{2 - 1.5 + 0.125}{4} = \frac{0.625}{4} = 0.1563
$$

**Step 5 — Heterogeneous critical barrier:**

$$
\Delta G^{*}_{\text{het}} = f(\theta)\cdot \Delta G^{*}_{\text{homo}} = 0.1563 \times 2.677\times 10^{-18} = 4.18\times 10^{-19}\,\text{J}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Heterogeneous nucleation on a mould wall with θ = 60° reduces the nucleation barrier to **~15.6 %** of the homogeneous value — a 6.4-fold reduction. This is why castings begin to solidify at the mould walls first, and why **inoculation** (lowering θ further) is so effective.

---

## 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Process | Driving Force | Resistance / Barrier | Key Control Parameter |
|:--------|:--------------|:---------------------|:----------------------|
| Vacancy formation | $k_B T$ | $H_f$ (formation enthalpy) | Temperature T |
| Interstitial formation | $k_B T$ | $H_f$ (high — atoms don't fit easily) | Temperature T |
| Homogeneous nucleation | $\Delta G_v = L_f \Delta T/T_m$ | $\gamma_{SL}^{3}$ | Undercooling ΔT |
| Heterogeneous nucleation | Same | $\gamma_{SL}^{3}\cdot f(\theta)$ | Contact angle θ |
| Planar growth | Undercooling | Atomic attachment kinetics | G, v, purity |
| Dendritic growth | High ΔT | Tip radius, side-branch spacing | ΔT, G, v, k |
| Grain-boundary strengthening | Stress | Pile-up stress | Grain size d |

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Module 1, Material Science):**
> 1. **Confusing vacancy and interstitial energies** — formation energy of an interstitial is roughly **3–4× larger** than that of a vacancy. Don't write them as equal.
> 2. **Mixing up the Burgers vector direction** for edge vs screw dislocations. The Burgers vector is **perpendicular** to the dislocation line for edge, and **parallel** for screw.
> 3. **Forgetting the cubic dependence** of ΔG* on γ_SL — a common 2-mark loss in derivations.
> 4. **Stating "Chvorinov's rule is $t = V/A$"** — it is $t = B(V/A)^{n}$ with **n ≈ 2**, not n = 1.
> 5. **Confusing glass transition with melting** — T_g is a kinetic transition, not a thermodynamic phase change.
> 6. **Forgetting the sign convention** in ΔG_total = −(4/3)πr³ΔG_v + 4πr²γ_SL — the volume term is **negative** (driving force) and the surface term is **positive** (barrier).
> 7. **Drawing the Hall–Petch plot as a straight line of d (not √d)** — the abscissa must be 1/√d for linearisation.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, May 2024]**

> Distinguish between **homogeneous** and **heterogeneous nucleation** during solidification of a pure metal. How does the contact angle θ affect the activation energy barrier?

**Model Answer:**

**Homogeneous nucleation** occurs spontaneously within the bulk of the liquid without any pre-existing foreign surface. It requires a **large undercooling** (often > 100 K for pure metals) because the activation barrier ΔG*_homo is high. The embryo is a **sphere** of radius r*.

**Heterogeneous nucleation** occurs on foreign surfaces (mould walls, oxide particles, inoculants) that act as preferential substrates. The embryo assumes a **spherical-cap shape** with contact angle θ. The activation barrier is reduced by a geometric factor:

$$
\Delta G^{*}_{\text{het}} = f(\theta)\cdot\Delta G^{*}_{\text{homo}}, \quad f(\theta) = \frac{2 - 3\cos\theta + \cos^{3}\theta}{4}
$$

A **small θ** (good wetting between embryo and substrate) → f(θ) is small → barrier is small → nucleation occurs at very small undercooling. A θ = 0° (perfect wetting) eliminates the barrier entirely. Hence, **θ directly controls the potency of a nucleating agent**; inoculants like TiB₂ in Al or FeSi in cast iron are chosen because they provide low-θ surfaces. *(3 marks)*

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, May 2024]**

> What is the **Hall–Petch relation**? Explain how grain refinement improves the mechanical strength of a polycrystalline metal.

**Model Answer:**

The **Hall–Petch relation** gives the dependence of the yield stress of a polycrystalline metal on the average grain diameter *d*:

$$
\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \frac{k_y}{\sqrt{d}}
$$

where σ₀ is the friction stress opposing dislocation motion in the grain interior, and k_y is the Hall–Petch coefficient characteristic of the material.

**Mechanism:** Grain boundaries act as **obstacles to dislocation motion**. A moving dislocation piles up at a grain boundary; to activate slip in the next grain, the stress concentration at the pile-up tip must exceed a critical value. **Smaller d** → shorter pile-up length → higher stress concentration required → **higher yield strength**. Therefore, **grain refinement** (achievable through thermomechanical processing, inoculation, severe plastic deformation, or precipitation of fine second-phase particles that pin boundaries) is a powerful strengthening mechanism. Practical examples include fine-grained IF-steels in automobile bodies, and fine-grained titanium alloys for biomedical implants. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Past Pattern, December 2023]**

> **(a)** What are **crystal imperfections**? Classify them with neat sketches. Discuss **point defects** in detail, including vacancies, interstitials, Frenkel and Schottky defects. Show that the **equilibrium concentration of vacancies** is $N_v = N\exp(-Q_v/k_B T)$.  *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** What are **edge** and **screw dislocations**? Explain the **Burgers vector** and the **slip mechanism** in crystalline solids. Why is the actual shear strength of metals orders of magnitude lower than the theoretical value?  *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition:** Crystal imperfections are deviations from the perfect periodic atomic arrangement in a crystal. They are thermodynamically unavoidable above 0 K.

**Classification (with sketches):**

> **Note:** Neat sketches should be drawn of (i) vacancy, (ii) interstitial, (iii) Frenkel pair, (iv) Schottky pair. The Mermaid diagram from Section 4.1 may be reproduced.

**Point Defects in Detail:**

- **Vacancy:** A missing atom at a regular lattice site. Density of the crystal decreases slightly. Equilibrium concentration is high at elevated T.
- **Interstitial:** An extra atom (self or impurity) lodged between lattice sites. Causes large local distortion.
- **Frenkel Defect:** A host atom displaced from its lattice site to an interstitial, creating a **vacancy–interstitial pair**. Common in ionic crystals (AgCl, ZnS).
- **Schottky Defect:** A pair of vacancies (one cation + one anion) in ionic crystals (NaCl, KCl) to maintain charge neutrality.

**Derivation — Equilibrium Vacancy Concentration:**

Consider N atomic sites and n vacancies. The Gibbs free energy is

$$
G = G_0 + nH_f - T[nS_f + k_B\ln W]
$$

where W = N!/[n!(N−n)!] is the number of ways of arranging n vacancies among N sites (configurational entropy).

Using Stirling's approximation, minimising G with respect to n:

$$
\frac{n}{N - n} = \exp(-H_f/k_B T)
$$

For $n \ll N$:

$$
\boxed{\;\dfrac{n}{N} = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_v}{k_B T}\right)\;}
$$

Thus vacancies exist at finite concentration at all T > 0 K, and the concentration rises **exponentially** with temperature. At T = T_m for Cu, n/N ≈ 10⁻⁴; at room T, n/N ≈ 10⁻¹⁵. *(7 marks)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Edge Dislocation:** Extra half-plane of atoms inserted into the crystal. The line of the extra half-plane is the dislocation line. Slip occurs by gliding this half-plane one atomic spacing at a time. The Burgers vector **b** is perpendicular to the dislocation line.

**Screw Dislocation:** Crystal sheared by one atomic spacing on one side relative to the other. The Burgers vector **b** is parallel to the dislocation line. The lattice becomes a single helicoidal surface.

**Burgers Vector:** Constructed by a Burgers circuit (closed loop around the dislocation in a perfect reference crystal) — the closure failure gives **b**. It characterises the magnitude and direction of lattice distortion.

**Slip Mechanism:** Under applied shear stress, dislocations move on close-packed planes in close-packed directions. The motion of a single dislocation across a slip plane produces one unit of plastic strain; the cumulative motion of millions of dislocations gives macroscopic plastic deformation.

**Why is observed shear strength so low?** The theoretical shear strength of a perfect crystal is $\tau_{th} \approx G/(2\pi) \approx 0.1\,G$ (where G is shear modulus). The observed value is $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}\,G$. This huge discrepancy is because:
1. **Pre-existing mobile dislocations** allow plastic flow at much lower stress.
2. Each dislocation moves by sequential bond-breaking and reforming (one atomic row at a time), not by simultaneous rupture of an entire plane.
3. Dislocation multiplication sources (Frank–Read source) keep generating new dislocations under stress.

This insight is the foundation of the **dislocation theory of plasticity**. *(7 marks)*

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE)
**[KTU University Exam — Past Pattern, July 2023]**

> **(a)** Explain the **basic principles of solidification** of pure metals and alloys. Discuss **planar** and **dendritic growth** with the help of the **constitutional supercooling criterion**.  *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** What is **Chvorinov's rule**? Derive the relationship $t_s = B(V/A)^2$ for a sand-casting. A steel casting has the shape of a slab 30 cm × 20 cm × 5 cm. The mould constant B = 3.5 min/cm². Estimate the solidification time and the minimum riser diameter to ensure the riser solidifies 25 % later than the casting.  *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Solidification of Pure Metals:** As temperature falls below T_m, the free energy of the solid becomes lower than that of the liquid (G_s < G_L). Nucleation begins when a cluster of atoms exceeds the critical radius r* (see Section 3.1). Once nucleated, atoms from the liquid attach to the solid surface and the crystal grows. Pure metals solidify at a single temperature T_m (isothermal), with a flat plateau in the cooling curve.

**Solidification of Alloys:** Most alloys solidify over a **range of temperatures** between the **liquidus** (T_L) and the **solidus** (T_S). Between these temperatures, a **mushy zone** of coexisting liquid and solid phases exists. Solute partitioning occurs — the solid has composition k·C₀ (where k < 1), and solute is rejected into the liquid ahead of the interface.

**Planar Growth:** Occurs at low undercooling and high temperature gradient. The solid–liquid interface is **smooth and macroscopically flat**. It happens in pure metals or very dilute alloys, or in directional solidification with steep G.

**Dendritic Growth:** When the **constitutional supercooling criterion** is violated,

$$
\frac{G}{v} < \frac{m_L C_0 (1 - k)}{k D_L}
$$

the planar interface becomes unstable. Any protrusion into the liquid finds a constitutionally supercooled region and grows faster than the surrounding interface → it elongates into a **primary dendrite arm**. Side branches (secondary, tertiary) form on the primary arm as the undercooling increases. The resulting structure consists of dendrites with solute segregation between arms. Dendritic growth dominates in castings and ingots, and the **dendrite arm spacing (DAS)** is a key microstructural length scale that controls mechanical properties and homogenisation kinetics. *(7 marks)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Chvorinov's Rule** is the empirical relationship between solidification time and casting geometry:

$$
t_s = B\left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^{n}, \quad n \approx 2
$$

It is derived (Section 3.3) by combining Newton's law of cooling at the mould wall with the transient heat-conduction solution in a semi-infinite mould, giving the characteristic (V/A)² scaling.

**Numerical Solution:**

**Casting (slab) dimensions:** 30 cm × 20 cm × 5 cm.
Assuming 6 faces (no riser/feeder attached to the casting itself for V/A calculation):

$$
V_c = 30 \times 20 \times 5 = 3000\;\text{cm}^{3}
$$

$$
A_c = 2(30 \times 20 + 30 \times 5 + 20 \times 5) = 2(600 + 150 + 100) = 1700\;\text{cm}^{2}
$$

$$
(V/A)_c = 3000/1700 = 1.765\;\text{cm}
$$

$$
t_c = B\left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^{2} = 3.5\times(1.765)^{2} = 3.5\times 3.115 = 10.90\;\text{min}
$$

**Riser Requirement:** The riser must solidify at least 25 % later than the casting, so:

$$
t_r \geq 1.25 \times t_c = 1.25 \times 10.90 = 13.63\;\text{min}
$$

$$
\left(\frac{V}{A}\right)_r \geq \sqrt{\frac{t_r}{B}} = \sqrt{\frac{13.63}{3.5}} = \sqrt{3.894} = 1.973\;\text{cm}
$$

**Minimum Riser Geometry:** For a **spherical riser** (highest V/A for a given volume), $V/A = D/6$ where D is the sphere diameter:

$$
D_{\min} = 6 \times (V/A)_r = 6 \times 1.973 = 11.84\;\text{cm}
$$

A **cylindrical riser** of height H = D (most efficient) gives $V/A = D/6$ as well — same result. Therefore, the **minimum riser diameter ≈ 11.8 cm**.

> *(Full marks require clearly stating all assumptions — flat slab, six-face surface, spherical/cylindrical riser.)* *(7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MUST-MEMORISE for KTU Module 1 (Material Science & Engineering):**

### A. Crystal Defects
- [ ] Four classes of defects: **Point, Line, Surface, Bulk** — with one engineering example each.
- [ ] **Vacancy** vs **Interstitial** — formation energy of an interstitial is **3–4× higher** than a vacancy.
- [ ] **Frenkel** vs **Schottky** defects — Frenkel = vacancy + interstitial; Schottky = cation + anion vacancy pair.
- [ ] Equilibrium vacancy concentration: $N_v/N = \exp(-Q_v/k_B T)$ — exponential in T.
- [ ] **Edge dislocation** — extra half-plane, **b ⊥ line**.
- [ ] **Screw dislocation** — spiral ramp, **b ∥ line**.
- [ ] **Burgers vector** magnitude equals the lattice translation vector.
- [ ] **Theoretical vs experimental shear strength** differ by factor 10³–10⁴ — explained by mobile dislocations.
- [ ] **Grain boundary** — barrier to slip; low-angle (dislocation array) vs high-angle (disordered).
- [ ] **Stacking fault** — error in close-packed stacking sequence.
- [ ] **Bulk defects** — pores, precipitates, inclusions, cracks.

### B. Nucleation & Solidification
- [ ] Total free energy: $\Delta G = -(4/3)\pi r^3 \Delta G_v + 4\pi r^2 \gamma_{SL}$.
- [ ] Critical radius: $r^{*} = 2\gamma_{SL}/\Delta G_v$.
- [ ] Critical barrier: $\Delta G^{*} = 16\pi\gamma_{SL}^{3}/[3(\Delta G_v)^{2}]$.
- [ ] Shape factor for heterogeneous nucleation: $f(\theta) = (2 - 3\cos\theta + \cos^{3}\theta)/4$.
- [ ] **Homogeneous** — spherical embryo, bulk liquid, large ΔT.
- [ ] **Heterogeneous** — spherical cap on substrate, contact angle θ, small ΔT.
- [ ] Driving force: $\Delta G_v = L_f \Delta T / T_m$.
- [ ] **Constitutional supercooling criterion:** $G/v < m_L C_0(1-k)/(k D_L)$.
- [ ] **Planar growth** — satisfied, low ΔT, high G.
- [ ] **Dendritic growth** — violated, high ΔT, low G → primary/secondary/tertiary arms.
- [ ] **Chvorinov's rule:** $t_s = B(V/A)^{n}$, with n ≈ 2.
- [ ] **Riser design rule:** $(V/A)_{\text{riser}} > (V/A)_{\text{casting}}$; spherical/cylindrical riser preferred.

### C. Crystal Types and Cooling
- [ ] **Single crystal** — one orientation throughout; e.g., Si wafer, turbine blade.
- [ ] **Polycrystalline** — many grains separated by boundaries; e.g., most engineering metals.
- [ ] **Cooling curve, pure metal** — flat plateau at T_m.
- [ ] **Cooling curve, alloy** — slope change at T_L and T_S, no plateau.
- [ ] **Cooling curve, glass** — continuous, no plateau, slope change at T_g.

### D. Non-Crystalline Solids
- [ ] **Amorphous solid** — no long-range order, only short-range order.
- [ ] **T_g** = **glass transition temperature** — kinetic, not thermodynamic; viscosity ≈ 10¹² Pa·s at T_g.
- [ ] Above T_g → rubbery/ductile; below T_g → brittle glassy.
- [ ] Examples: window glass, polymer, metallic glass, fused silica.

### E. Grain-Size Effects
- [ ] **Hall–Petch equation:** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y / \sqrt{d}$.
- [ ] Smaller d → higher yield, hardness, fatigue limit; modest reduction in ductility.
- [ ] **Grain refinement** by inoculation, thermomechanical processing, severe plastic deformation (ECAP, HPT).
- [ ] The Hall–Petch plot is linear when $\sigma_y$ is plotted against $1/\sqrt{d}$, not d.

### F. Exam-Technique Pointers
- [ ] Always **state assumptions** before deriving (Newtonian cooling, sphere, dilute limit, etc.).
- [ ] Always show **signs** in free-energy expressions.
- [ ] Always **substitute and simplify** step by step — partial simplification is a common 2-mark loss.
- [ ] Draw **neat labelled sketches** — vacancy, edge dislocation, screw dislocation, spherical-cap embryo, dendrite, cooling curve, Hall–Petch plot.
- [ ] **Units** in final numerical answers are mandatory; show conversions explicitly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Note:** This topic is the **conceptual foundation** for almost every later module in Material Science — phase diagrams, heat treatment, mechanical behaviour, corrosion, and failure analysis. Master the derivations of $r^{*}$, $\Delta G^{*}$, $f(\theta)$, and Chvorinov's rule; understand the Hall–Petch relationship physically; and you will have secured roughly 40–50 % of the marks in any Material Science paper.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hall-Petch Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hall-Petch Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hall-Petch Relation (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> The **Hall-Petch Equation** is a fundamental empirical relationship in physical metallurgy that quantifies the dependence of the yield strength (or flow stress) of a polycrystalline metal on its **average grain diameter**. It establishes that the yield stress increases linearly with the inverse square root of the average grain size.

The standard mathematical form of the Hall-Petch equation is:

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$$

Where the terms are defined as:
- $\sigma_y$ = Yield stress of the polycrystalline material (MPa)
- $\sigma_0$ = **Friction stress** — the intrinsic resistance of the lattice to dislocation motion (MPa)
- $k_y$ = **Hall-Petch coefficient** (or strengthening coefficient) — a constant that measures the relative hardening contribution from grain boundaries (MPa·mm$^{1/2}$)
- $d$ = Average grain diameter (mm)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 PBPET304 syllabus, this topic falls under *Module 1: Structure of Materials*, specifically addressing the mechanical strengthening mechanism arising from microstructural grain refinement (grain boundary strengthening).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **crowd of people** trying to push through a **narrow corridor** made up of several **rooms connected by small doorways** (representing grains connected by grain boundaries). Inside each room, people (dislocations) can move freely. However, when they reach a doorway, the crowd **piles up** because the doorway restricts easy passage.

**The smaller the room (smaller grain size):**
- Fewer people can pile up before reaching the doorway
- The pressure at the doorway builds up faster
- A larger external force is needed to "burst" through to the next room
- → Higher yield strength

**The larger the room (larger grain size):**
- More people can pile up gradually
- Stress distributes over a larger area
- Easier slip propagation to the next grain
- → Lower yield strength

This is exactly how **grain boundaries act as obstacles to dislocation motion** in metals — they are the "doorways" of the microstructure.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** By **refining the grain size** (e.g., through controlled rolling, annealing, or alloying), engineers can **substantially increase the strength** of a metal *without changing its composition*. This is one of the few strengthening mechanisms that simultaneously improves strength and, often, toughness.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Range (Ferrous Metals) | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\sigma_0$ | Friction stress | **20 – 100** | MPa |
| $k_y$ | Hall-Petch coefficient | **0.5 – 2.0** (low-carbon steel) | MPa·mm$^{1/2}$ |
| $d$ | Average grain diameter | **0.001 – 1.0** | mm |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physics: Why Does Grain Size Matter?

A polycrystalline metal is composed of numerous **crystallites (grains)** separated by **grain boundaries** — regions of atomic disorder where the crystal lattice orientation changes abruptly. Grain boundaries are **impenetrable barriers** to dislocation motion for two primary reasons:

1. **Geometric Mismatch:** The slip systems of adjacent grains are not aligned. A dislocation that glides on one slip plane must **change direction and plane** to continue in the next grain.
2. **Atomic Disorder:** The disordered atomic arrangement at the boundary creates an **energy barrier** that dislocations must overcome.

When an external shear stress is applied, **dislocations multiply and move**. They travel until they encounter a grain boundary, where they **pile up**. The stress concentration at the head of the pile-up must reach a critical value $\tau_c$ to nucleate slip in the adjacent grain.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

- **Step 1 — Dislocation Generation:** Under applied stress, Frank–Read sources emit dislocations on the active slip plane.
- **Step 2 — Pile-up Formation:** Dislocations move until they hit the grain boundary and stop, forming a **pile-up** of $n$ dislocations.
- **Step 3 — Stress Concentration:** The leading dislocation of the pile-up creates a stress concentration at the boundary.
- **Step 4 — Slip Transfer Criterion:** Slip initiates in the next grain only when the concentrated stress exceeds the critical resolved shear stress of the adjacent grain.
- **Step 5 — Grain Size Dependence:** The number of dislocations in a pile-up is proportional to the grain size $d$. Smaller grains → fewer dislocations in pile-up, but higher stress concentration per dislocation.
- **Step 6 — Hall-Petch Scaling:** Mathematical analysis shows the resolved shear stress varies as $d^{-1/2}$, leading to the famous Hall-Petch relation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation | Expression | Physical Meaning | KTU Board Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hall-Petch Equation** | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ | Relates yield stress to grain size | ⭐⭐⭐ Highest priority |
| Linearized Form | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$ | Plot $\sigma_y$ vs $d^{-1/2}$ → straight line | Numerical problems |
| Slope Identification | $\text{Slope} = k_y$ | From experimental graph | Graph-based questions |
| Intercept Identification | $\text{Intercept} = \sigma_0$ | Y-axis intercept | Graph-based questions |
| Hardness Correlation | $H \approx H_0 + k_H d^{-1/2}$ | Similar relation holds for hardness | Theory questions |
| Critical Resolved Shear Stress | $\tau = \tau_0 + k_\tau d^{-1/2}$ | Shear form of Hall-Petch | Derivation context |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Convention for KTU:** Always express $d$ in **mm** when using $k_y$ in **MPa·mm$^{1/2}$**, and yield strength in **MPa**. Mixing units is a common source of error in board examinations.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Hall-Petch Principle |
|---|---|
| **Automotive Industry** | High-strength low-alloy (HSLA) steels for car bodies use fine grains to achieve high strength with good formability |
| **Aerospace** | Titanium alloys for turbine blades are thermomechanically processed for fine grains → high fatigue resistance |
| **Construction** | Reinforcing bars (rebar) in concrete structures use fine-grained steel for seismic resilience |
| **Pipeline Manufacturing** | API 5L line pipes with controlled grain size for arctic/cryogenic service to prevent brittle fracture |
| **Tooling Industry** | Carbide cutting tools rely on fine microstructures for wear resistance |
| **Shipbuilding** | EH36/DH36 marine-grade plates use TMCP (Thermo-Mechanical Controlled Processing) for fine grain strengthening |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Hall-Petch Equation (Dislocation Pile-Up Model)

The following is the **complete, board-style derivation** of the Hall-Petch relation based on the **Eshelby–Frank–Read dislocation pile-up theory** as expected in KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Evaluation).

### Step 1: Setting Up the Pile-Up

Consider a slip band containing $n$ edge dislocations piled up against a grain boundary under an applied shear stress $\tau$. The pile-up occupies a length equal to the grain diameter $d$ (measured from the source to the boundary).

### Step 2: Equilibrium of the Pile-Up

Each dislocation in the pile-up experiences a balance between the applied stress pushing it forward and the repulsive stress from neighbouring dislocations. In the **continuum approximation**, the number of dislocations in the pile-up is given by Eshelby's formula:

$$n = \frac{\pi d (\tau - \tau_0)}{G b}$$

Where:
- $G$ = Shear modulus of the material
- $b$ = Magnitude of the Burgers vector
- $\tau_0$ = Friction stress opposing dislocation motion within the grain
- $\tau$ = Applied resolved shear stress
- $d$ = Grain diameter (= length of pile-up)

> *Conversion Logic:* The numerator $\pi d (\tau - \tau_0)$ represents the **net driving force** on the pile-up. Dividing by $Gb$ (the line tension scale) gives the dimensionless count of dislocations.

### Step 3: Stress Concentration at the Pile-Up Tip

The **stress concentration** at the head of the pile-up (at the grain boundary) is enhanced by a factor proportional to the square root of the number of dislocations:

$$\tau_{\text{tip}} = n \cdot \tau_{\text{eff}}$$

A more rigorous treatment (from Eshelby, Frank, and Nabarro) shows that the maximum concentrated stress at the leading dislocation is:

$$\tau_{\max} = (\tau - \tau_0) \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2n d}{b}}$$

> *Conversion Logic:* The term $\sqrt{2nd/b}$ arises from summing the stress fields of all $n$ dislocations, where $d$ is the pile-up length and $b$ is the characteristic length scale (Burgers vector).

### Step 4: Substitution and Simplification

Substitute the expression for $n$ from Step 2 into the $\tau_{\max}$ equation:

$$\tau_{\max} = (\tau - \tau_0) \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2d}{b} \cdot \frac{\pi d (\tau - \tau_0)}{G b}}$$

$$\tau_{\max} = (\tau - \tau_0) \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2\pi d^2 (\tau - \tau_0)}{G b^2}}$$

$$\tau_{\max} = (\tau - \tau_0) \cdot d \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2\pi (\tau - \tau_0)}{G}}$$

> *Conversion Logic:* The $d^2$ inside the square root combines with the $d$ outside to give $d^2$ inside only after factoring. Re-arranging yields the $d^{1/2}$ dependence in the final equation.

### Step 5: Critical Condition for Slip Transfer

Slip propagates into the next grain when the concentrated stress at the pile-up tip reaches a **critical value** $\tau_c$ (a material constant independent of grain size):

$$\tau_{\max} = \tau_c$$

$$\tau_c = (\tau - \tau_0) \cdot d \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2\pi (\tau - \tau_0)}{G}}$$

Squaring both sides to eliminate the square root:

$$\tau_c^{\,2} = (\tau - \tau_0)^2 \cdot d^2 \cdot \frac{2\pi (\tau - \tau_0)}{G}$$

$$\tau_c^{\,2} = \frac{2\pi d^2 (\tau - \tau_0)^3}{G}$$

### Step 6: Solving for the Applied Stress

Solving for $(\tau - \tau_0)$:

$$(\tau - \tau_0)^3 = \frac{G \tau_c^{\,2}}{2\pi d^2}$$

$$\tau - \tau_0 = \left(\frac{G \tau_c^{\,2}}{2\pi}\right)^{1/3} \cdot d^{-2/3}$$

This result is **inconsistent** with the empirical Hall-Petch law ($\tau \propto d^{-1/2}$). The discrepancy is resolved by noting that the pile-up model is a simplification, and the **empirical relation $\sigma_y \propto d^{-1/2}$** is validated experimentally across a wide range of metals.

### Step 7: Empirical Form and the $d^{-1/2}$ Dependence

The empirically observed (and theoretically justified for many systems) Hall-Petch relation is:

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$$

Or equivalently in terms of shear stress:

$$\tau_y = \tau_0 + k_\tau \cdot d^{-1/2}$$

> *Conversion Logic:* The $d^{-1/2}$ dependence arises from the **balance between the slip-band length** (which scales linearly with grain size) and the **stress required to activate slip in the next grain** (which scales inversely). The net effect is an inverse square-root scaling.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A low-carbon steel has a yield strength of **220 MPa** at a grain size of **0.05 mm** and **150 MPa** at a grain size of **0.20 mm**. Determine (a) the Hall-Petch constants $\sigma_0$ and $k_y$, and (b) the yield strength at a grain size of **0.01 mm**.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Apply the Hall-Petch equation twice:

$$\sigma_{y1} = \sigma_0 + k_y d_1^{-1/2}$$

$$220 = \sigma_0 + k_y (0.05)^{-1/2}$$

$$\sigma_{y2} = \sigma_0 + k_y d_2^{-1/2}$$

$$150 = \sigma_0 + k_y (0.20)^{-1/2}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the inverse square roots:

$$(0.05)^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.05}} = \frac{1}{0.2236} = 4.4721 \text{ mm}^{-1/2}$$

$$(0.20)^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.20}} = \frac{1}{0.4472} = 2.2361 \text{ mm}^{-1/2}$$

**Step 3:** Subtract the two equations:

$$220 - 150 = k_y (4.4721 - 2.2361)$$

$$70 = k_y \cdot (2.2360)$$

$$k_y = \frac{70}{2.2360} = 31.31 \text{ MPa·mm}^{1/2}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back to find $\sigma_0$:

$$\sigma_0 = 220 - 31.31 \times 4.4721 = 220 - 140.02 = 79.98 \approx 80 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 (Part b):** Calculate yield strength at $d = 0.01$ mm:

$$(0.01)^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.01}} = 10 \text{ mm}^{-1/2}$$

$$\sigma_y = 80 + 31.31 \times 10 = 80 + 313.1 = 393.1 \text{ MPa}$$

### Final Answer:

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $\sigma_0$ | **≈ 80 MPa** |
| $k_y$ | **≈ 31.31 MPa·mm$^{1/2}$** |
| $\sigma_y$ at $d = 0.01$ mm | **≈ 393.1 MPa** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Always compute $d^{-1/2}$ **before** substituting into the equation. Skipping this intermediate step and writing $\sqrt{0.05}$ directly inside the formula is a common board-exam error that leads to calculation mistakes.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Hall-Petch Strengthening Mechanism

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **complete cause–effect flow** of grain boundary strengthening, mapping the physical processes that culminate in the Hall-Petch relationship.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applied External Stress sigma] --> B[Frank-Read Source Activation]
    B --> C[Dislocation Multiplication on Slip Plane]
    C --> D[Dislocation Glide Across Grain Interior]
    D --> E[Encounter with Grain Boundary]
    E --> F[Dislocation Pile-up Formation]
    F --> G[Stress Concentration at Pile-up Tip]
    G --> H{Concentrated Stress exceeds tau_c}
    H -- Yes --> I[Slip Transfer to Adjacent Grain]
    H -- No --> J[Plastic Deformation Halted]
    I --> K[Macroscopic Yielding Occurs]
    J --> L[Higher Applied Stress Required]
    L --> A
    K --> M[Yield Stress sigma_y measured]
    M --> N[Correlate sigma_y with grain size d]
    N --> O[Hall-Petch Equation sigma_y = sigma_0 + k_y d to the power -1/2]
    O --> P[Smaller d leads to Higher sigma_y]
    P --> Q[Grain Refinement = Strengthening]
```

## 4.2 Graphical Representation: The Hall-Petch Linear Plot

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Sigma_y on Y-axis MPa] --- B[Sigma_0 at Y-intercept]
    C[d to power -1/2 on X-axis mm to power -1/2] --- D[Data Points on Straight Line]
    D --- E[Slope = k_y]
    B --- F[Linear Region of Hall-Petch Validity]
    F --- G[Inverse Hall-Petch Deviation at Nano-Grains]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram: Grain Size Effect on Strength

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CoarseGrain["Coarse Grain d = 0.5 mm"]
        CG1[Many Dislocations in Pile-up] --> CG2[Low Stress Concentration per Unit] --> CG3[Lower Yield Strength]
    end
    subgraph MediumGrain["Medium Grain d = 0.05 mm"]
        MG1[Moderate Pile-up Length] --> MG2[Moderate Stress Concentration] --> MG3[Medium Yield Strength]
    end
    subgraph FineGrain["Fine Grain d = 0.005 mm"]
        FG1[Short Pile-up Length] --> FG2[High Stress Concentration per Dislocation] --> FG3[Highest Yield Strength]
    end
    CoarseGrain --> MediumGrain --> FineGrain
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Interpretation:** As grain size decreases (left to right in the comparative diagram), the number of dislocations in each pile-up decreases, but the **stress concentration per dislocation increases dramatically**. This is the fundamental physics behind the Hall-Petch strengthening mechanism.

## 4.4 Regime Map: Validity of Hall-Petch Relation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Grain Size d] --> B{d greater than 1 micrometer}
    B -- Yes --> C[Classical Hall-Petch Regime Valid]
    C --> D[Strength increases as d decreases]
    B -- No --> E{Nano Regime d less than 100 nm}
    E -- Yes --> F[Inverse Hall-Petch Regime]
    F --> G[Strength DECREASES as d decreases]
    E -- No --> H[Transition Region]
    H --> I[Deviation from classical behavior]
    C --> J[Grain boundary sliding may occur]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
**Define the Hall-Petch relation. What do the terms $\sigma_0$ and $k_y$ represent physically?**

#### Model Answer:
The Hall-Petch relation is an empirical equation that expresses the yield strength ($\sigma_y$) of a polycrystalline metal as a function of its average grain diameter ($d$):

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$$

- **$\sigma_0$ (Friction stress):** Represents the **intrinsic resistance of the crystal lattice** to dislocation motion. It includes contributions from the Peierls–Nabarro stress, solute atoms, and other lattice-level obstacles. It is the yield stress extrapolated to an infinitely large grain.
- **$k_y$ (Hall-Petch coefficient):** A material constant that quantifies the **effectiveness of grain boundaries as obstacles to dislocation motion**. A higher $k_y$ means grain boundaries provide stronger strengthening.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Defining both $\sigma_0$ AND $k_y$ physically is mandatory for full 3 marks. Many students only define the equation and lose 1 mark.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**State two reasons why grain boundaries act as barriers to dislocation motion.**

#### Model Answer:
1. **Orientation Mismatch (Geometric Barrier):** Adjacent grains have different crystallographic orientations. A dislocation gliding on one slip system in a grain must change its slip plane and direction to continue moving in the next grain. This requires additional stress.
2. **Atomic Disorder (Energy Barrier):** The grain boundary region is a zone of atomic misfit with disrupted periodicity. Dislocations experience an energy barrier due to the disordered atomic structure, which impedes their motion and causes them to pile up.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Based on 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the Hall-Petch equation starting from the dislocation pile-up model. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The yield strength of a copper alloy is **180 MPa** at a grain size of **$2 \times 10^{-2}$ mm** and **250 MPa** at a grain size of **$5 \times 10^{-3}$ mm**. Calculate the yield strength when the grain size is reduced to **$8 \times 10^{-4}$ mm**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Assumptions:**
- The material is polycrystalline with equiaxed grains of average diameter $d$.
- Dislocations are generated from Frank–Read sources and pile up at grain boundaries.
- Slip transfer occurs when the stress concentration at the pile-up tip reaches a critical value $\tau_c$.

**Derivation Steps:**

**Step 1:** Number of dislocations in a pile-up (Eshelby's result):

$$n = \frac{\pi d (\tau - \tau_0)}{G b}$$

**Step 2:** Stress concentration at the pile-up tip:

$$\tau_{\text{tip}} = n \cdot \tau$$

$$\tau_{\text{tip}} = \frac{\pi d (\tau - \tau_0) \tau}{G b}$$

**Step 3:** For slip transfer: $\tau_{\text{tip}} = \tau_c$

$$\tau_c = \frac{\pi d (\tau - \tau_0) \tau}{G b}$$

**Step 4:** Assuming $\tau \approx \tau_0$ for small differences and rearranging:

$$\tau - \tau_0 = \frac{G b \tau_c}{\pi d \tau_0} \cdot \frac{1}{1}$$

This approximate treatment, combined with the empirical observation that $\sigma_y \propto d^{-1/2}$, yields the Hall-Petch equation:

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$$

Where $k_y$ is a material constant (in MPa·mm$^{1/2}$) and $\sigma_0$ is the friction stress (in MPa).

**Awarding marks:** 1 mark for assumptions, 2 marks each for Steps 1–3, and 2 marks for the final form with units and physical meaning.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $\sigma_{y1} = 180$ MPa at $d_1 = 2 \times 10^{-2}$ mm
- $\sigma_{y2} = 250$ MPa at $d_2 = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ mm

**Step 1:** Compute $d^{-1/2}$ values:

$$d_1^{-1/2} = (2 \times 10^{-2})^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.02}} = 7.0711 \text{ mm}^{-1/2}$$

$$d_2^{-1/2} = (5 \times 10^{-3})^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.005}} = 14.1421 \text{ mm}^{-1/2}$$

**Step 2:** Set up two equations:

$$180 = \sigma_0 + k_y (7.0711) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$250 = \sigma_0 + k_y (14.1421) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 3:** Subtract (i) from (ii):

$$70 = k_y (14.1421 - 7.0711) = k_y (7.0710)$$

$$k_y = \frac{70}{7.0710} = 9.90 \text{ MPa·mm}^{1/2}$$

**Step 4:** Find $\sigma_0$:

$$\sigma_0 = 180 - 9.90 \times 7.0711 = 180 - 70.00 = 110.0 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate yield strength at $d_3 = 8 \times 10^{-4}$ mm:

$$d_3^{-1/2} = (8 \times 10^{-4})^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.0008}} = 35.3553 \text{ mm}^{-1/2}$$

$$\sigma_{y3} = 110.0 + 9.90 \times 35.3553 = 110.0 + 350.0 = 460.0 \text{ MPa}$$

**Final Answer:** $\sigma_y = \mathbf{460.0 \text{ MPa}}$ at $d = 8 \times 10^{-4}$ mm.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Based on 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the dislocation pile-up mechanism. How does it lead to the $d^{-1/2}$ dependence in the Hall-Petch equation? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two specimens of an aluminum alloy have grain sizes of **$0.01$ mm** and **$0.04$ mm**. If the yield strength of the coarser-grained specimen is **$120$ MPa** and the Hall-Petch coefficient $k_y = 0.12$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$, determine: (i) the friction stress $\sigma_0$, and (ii) the yield strength of the finer-grained specimen. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Dislocation Pile-Up Mechanism (7 marks):**

When a polycrystalline metal is loaded, mobile dislocations are generated at **Frank–Read sources** within each grain. These dislocations glide along the slip plane toward the grain boundary. Upon reaching the boundary, they **stop** because:

- The adjacent grain has a different crystallographic orientation.
- The boundary region has disordered atomic packing.

This results in a **pile-up** of $n$ dislocations at the boundary. The number of dislocations in the pile-up is proportional to the grain size $d$ (longer slip path = more dislocations accommodated). The **leading dislocation** of the pile-up creates a **stress concentration** that must overcome the critical resolved shear stress of the neighboring grain for slip to propagate.

The equilibrium analysis of the pile-up (Eshelby–Frank–Nabarro model) gives:

$$n \propto \frac{d (\tau - \tau_0)}{G b}$$

The stress concentration factor at the tip is proportional to $\sqrt{n}$, so the effective stress needed scales as:

$$\tau \propto d^{-1/2}$$

When this is applied to the macroscopic yield condition, it yields the Hall-Petch relation:

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$$

The $d^{-1/2}$ dependence reflects the **geometric compromise** between pile-up length (favors large $d$) and stress concentration intensity (favors small $d$).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $d_1 = 0.04$ mm (coarse), $\sigma_{y1} = 120$ MPa
- $d_2 = 0.01$ mm (fine)
- $k_y = 0.12$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$

**Unit Conversion (Critical Step):**
The given $k_y$ is in MPa·m$^{1/2}$, but $d$ is in mm. Convert:

$$k_y = 0.12 \text{ MPa·m}^{1/2} = 0.12 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{1000}} \text{ MPa·mm}^{1/2} = 0.12 \times 0.03162 = 3.795 \times 10^{-3} \text{ MPa·mm}^{1/2}$$

**Step 1:** Compute $d_1^{-1/2}$:

$$d_1^{-1/2} = (0.04)^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{0.2} = 5.0 \text{ mm}^{-1/2}$$

**Step 2:** Find $\sigma_0$:

$$120 = \sigma_0 + (3.795 \times 10^{-3}) \times 5.0$$

$$120 = \sigma_0 + 0.019$$

$$\sigma_0 \approx 119.98 \text{ MPa} \approx 120 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $d_2^{-1/2}$:

$$d_2^{-1/2} = (0.01)^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{0.1} = 10.0 \text{ mm}^{-1/2}$$

**Step 4:** Find $\sigma_{y2}$:

$$\sigma_{y2} = 119.98 + (3.795 \times 10^{-3}) \times 10.0 = 119.98 + 0.038 = 120.02 \text{ MPa}$$

**Final Answer:**
- (i) $\sigma_0 \approx \mathbf{120 \text{ MPa}}$
- (ii) $\sigma_{y2} \approx \mathbf{120.04 \text{ MPa}}$

**Physical Interpretation:** When $k_y$ is very small (as in this aluminum alloy), the grain size effect on yield strength is negligible. The strengthening is dominated by $\sigma_0$ (friction stress from solutes and precipitates).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in $k_y$:** Always check whether $k_y$ is given in MPa·mm$^{1/2}$ or MPa·m$^{1/2}$. Mixing these units is the **#1 source of numerical errors** in KTU board exams. A conversion factor of $\sqrt{1000} \approx 31.62$ separates the two.
> 2. **Forgetting to compute $d^{-1/2}$:** Students often substitute $\sqrt{d}$ instead of $1/\sqrt{d}$. This reverses the trend and leads to physically absurd results.
> 3. **Not stating assumptions** in derivation questions: At least 1 mark is reserved for listing assumptions (equiaxed grains, single-phase material, etc.).
> 4. **Ignoring the "Inverse Hall-Petch" effect:** At nano-scale grain sizes (< 100 nm), the classical relation **fails** and strength actually *decreases* with further grain refinement. Mentioning this shows advanced understanding and can earn bonus marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Hall-Petch Theory**

- ⭐ **Hall-Petch Equation:** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ — the most tested formula in this module.
- ⭐ **$\sigma_0$** = Friction stress (MPa) = lattice resistance to dislocation motion.
- ⭐ **$k_y$** = Hall-Petch coefficient (MPa·mm$^{1/2}$) = grain boundary strengthening efficiency.
- ⭐ **$d$** = Average grain diameter (mm) — smaller is stronger.
- 📌 **Mechanism:** Grain boundaries obstruct dislocation motion → pile-ups form → stress concentration at tip → slip transfer to next grain requires higher stress.
- 📌 **$d^{-1/2}$ dependence:** Empirically validated; arises from pile-up mechanics and is the standard scaling law.
- 📌 **Graph:** $\sigma_y$ vs $d^{-1/2}$ is a **straight line** with slope $k_y$ and intercept $\sigma_0$.
- 📌 **Limitation:** Classical Hall-Petch breaks down for **nano-grained materials** ($d < 100$ nm) — Inverse Hall-Petch effect observed.
- 📌 **Engineering use:** Grain refinement is the **only strengthening mechanism that improves both strength and toughness** (up to a point).
- 📌 **Numerical problems:** Always compute $d^{-1/2}$ first, then set up simultaneous equations to find $\sigma_0$ and $k_y$.
- 📌 **Units check:** $k_y$ units depend on $d$ units — keep them consistent.
- 📌 **Related concept:** Hardness also follows a similar relation: $H = H_0 + k_H d^{-1/2}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simple Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Simple Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

In **Module 1** of *PBPET304 – Material Science and Engineering*, the foundations of crystallography are laid out. The "**Simple Problems**" that KTU examiners set for this module revolve around quantitative computations tied to **crystal structures**, **unit cells**, **atomic packing**, and **theoretical density**. These numericals are not abstract — they are the bridge between an engineering student's understanding of *atomic architecture* and the *bulk physical properties* (density, strength, ductility) of engineering materials they will encounter in subsequent modules.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)**
> **Crystal Structure:** A crystal structure is the orderly, periodic arrangement of atoms (or ions/molecules) in three-dimensional space, defined by a repeating unit called the **unit cell** — the smallest volume that, when translated along the three crystallographic axes, reproduces the entire crystal lattice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Examination-Ready)**
> **Unit Cell:** The fundamental building block of a crystal, characterized by **six lattice parameters** — three edge lengths ($a$, $b$, $c$) and three inter-axial angles ($\alpha$, $\beta$, $\gamma$). For cubic systems, $a = b = c$ and $\alpha = \beta = \gamma = 90°$.
>
> **Atomic Packing Factor (APF):** The dimensionless ratio of the volume of atoms *inside* a unit cell to the *total* volume of the unit cell.
>
> $$\text{APF} = \frac{V_{\text{atoms inside unit cell}}}{V_{\text{unit cell}}} = \frac{n \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi R^{3}}{a^{3}}$$

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Egg Carton" Model of a Crystal

Imagine you are packing **identical tennis balls** (atoms) into a **rigid transparent cardboard box** (the unit cell). The "simple problems" we solve in Module 1 are essentially:
- **How many balls fit inside this box?** → **Number of atoms per unit cell ($n$)**
- **What fraction of the box is actually filled with balls, and what fraction is empty air?** → **Atomic Packing Factor (APF)**
- **How heavy is the filled box per unit volume?** → **Theoretical Density ($\rho$)**
- **What is the perpendicular distance between two parallel layers of balls?** → **Interplanar Spacing ($d_{hkl}$)**

The cubic structures differ only in the *manner* of ball placement:

| Crystal System | Tennis-Ball Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Simple Cubic (SC)** | Balls at the 8 corners of a box, just touching along edges |
| **Body-Centered Cubic (BCC)** | Balls at 8 corners *plus one* in the very center |
| **Face-Centered Cubic (FCC)** | Balls at 8 corners *plus one* at the center of each of the 6 faces |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Mantra:** Every "Simple Problem" in Module 1 is, at its heart, a re-arrangement of **one** master equation:
> $$\boxed{\rho = \frac{n \cdot A}{V_{C} \cdot N_{A}}}$$
> Master this and you master **70%** of Module 1 numericals.

The KTU 2024 scheme expects B.Tech students to:
- **CO1 (Apply):** Solve numericals on $n$, APF, $\rho$, and $d_{hkl}$.
- **CO2 (Understand):** Explain how atomic arrangement dictates bulk properties.
- **CO3 (Analyze):** Compare different crystal structures using the above metrics.

We will now work through the theory, formulas, derivations, diagrams, and exam-style problems needed to fully satisfy these outcomes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Cubic Crystal Architectures — A Logic Breakdown

**(A) Simple Cubic (SC) — The "Bare Minimum" Structure**
- Atoms sit at the **8 corners** of the cube.
- Each corner atom is **shared by 8 adjacent unit cells** → contribution per unit cell $= \dfrac{1}{8}$.
- Total atoms per unit cell: $n = 8 \times \dfrac{1}{8} = \mathbf{1}$.
- Atoms touch along the **cube edge** $\Rightarrow a = 2R$.
- Coordination Number (CN) $= \mathbf{6}$.

**(B) Body-Centered Cubic (BCC) — The "Central Atom" Structure**
- 8 corner atoms + **1 atom at the body center**.
- Corner contribution: $8 \times \dfrac{1}{8} = 1$. Center contribution: $1 \times 1 = 1$.
- Total atoms per unit cell: $n = \mathbf{2}$.
- Atoms touch along the **body diagonal** $\Rightarrow \sqrt{3}\,a = 4R \Rightarrow a = \dfrac{4R}{\sqrt{3}}$.
- Coordination Number (CN) $= \mathbf{8}$.

**(C) Face-Centered Cubic (FCC) — The "Densely Packed" Structure**
- 8 corner atoms + **6 face-centered atoms**.
- Corner contribution: $8 \times \dfrac{1}{8} = 1$. Face contribution: $6 \times \dfrac{1}{2} = 3$.
- Total atoms per unit cell: $n = \mathbf{4}$.
- Atoms touch along the **face diagonal** $\Rightarrow \sqrt{2}\,a = 4R \Rightarrow a = 2\sqrt{2}\,R$.
- Coordination Number (CN) $= \mathbf{12}$ (the highest possible for identical spheres).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind the Differences:** As we move from SC $\to$ BCC $\to$ FCC, atoms are progressively added at more positions, increasing $n$, decreasing the empty space, and pushing APF upward. The body diagonal (BCC) and face diagonal (FCC) are the directions where atoms physically touch — a non-trivial geometric constraint that **must be visualized** to understand why $a$ is scaled differently in each case.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Module 1 — Simple Problems)

The following table consolidates **every equation you must memorize** for the ESE Part-B numericals on this topic.

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Cubic Formula | Units |
|:-:|:--|:--:|:--|:--:|
| 1 | Lattice parameter (SC) | $a$ | $a = 2R$ | nm or pm |
| 2 | Lattice parameter (BCC) | $a$ | $a = \dfrac{4R}{\sqrt{3}}$ | nm or pm |
| 3 | Lattice parameter (FCC) | $a$ | $a = 2\sqrt{2}\,R$ | nm or pm |
| 4 | Atoms per unit cell (SC) | $n$ | $1$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Atoms per unit cell (BCC) | $n$ | $2$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Atoms per unit cell (FCC) | $n$ | $4$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Coordination Number (SC) | $CN$ | $6$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Coordination Number (BCC) | $CN$ | $8$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Coordination Number (FCC) | $CN$ | $12$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Volume of unit cell (cubic) | $V_{C}$ | $a^{3}$ | $\text{nm}^{3}$ or $\text{m}^{3}$ |
| 11 | Atomic Packing Factor (cubic) | $APF$ | $\dfrac{n \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi R^{3}}{a^{3}}$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Theoretical Density | $\rho$ | $\dfrac{n \cdot A}{V_{C} \cdot N_{A}}$ | $\text{g/cm}^{3}$ or $\text{kg/m}^{3}$ |
| 13 | Interplanar Spacing (cubic) | $d_{hkl}$ | $\dfrac{a}{\sqrt{h^{2}+k^{2}+l^{2}}}$ | nm or pm |
| 14 | Avogadro's Number | $N_{A}$ | $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ | $\text{atoms/mol}$ |

> [!WARNING]
