> **Common Pitfall (Pipe-Symbol Trap):** When writing the APF denominator as a *square-root expression* in your answer book, **never** use the pipe symbol `|` to denote the square root. Always use the standard radical $\sqrt{\,}$ or write it as $a \cdot \sqrt{\,h^{2}+k^{2}+l^{2}\,}$ in the denominator. KTU examiners will read it as a wrong sign otherwise.

### 2.3 Standard Reference APF Values (Memorize These)

These are the **canonical results** that frequently appear as "given" data in compound problems:

| Structure | APF | Empty Space |
|:--|:-:|:-:|
| Simple Cubic (SC) | $0.52$ | $48\%$ |
| Body-Centered Cubic (BCC) | $0.68$ | $32\%$ |
| Face-Centered Cubic (FCC) | $0.74$ | $26\%$ |
| Hexagonal Close-Packed (HCP) | $0.74$ | $26\%$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Why FCC and HCP share APF = 0.74:** Both represent the *densest possible* packing of identical spheres in 3D space (Kepler's conjecture, proved by Thomas Hales in 1998). KTU examiners love asking: *"Justify why FCC and HCP have the same APF."* The answer lies in both having CN = 12 and identical layer-stacking geometries (ABCABC for FCC vs. ABABAB for HCP).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Material (Application) | Crystal Structure | Why It Matters |
|:--|:--|:--|
| $\alpha$-Fe (structural steel) | BCC | High strength, moderate ductility |
| $\gamma$-Fe, Cu, Al, Au (conductors) | FCC | Excellent ductility, high conductivity |
| Mg, Ti, Zn (lightweight alloys) | HCP | Low density, used in aerospace |
| Polonium (rare) | SC | Purely of academic interest |

The APF and density computations are foundational for **selecting materials** for specific engineering loads — e.g., aerospace engineers prefer low-density FCC aluminum or HCP titanium alloys.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples (Analytical Matrix)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5 Standard "Simple Problem" Types in KTU Module 1**
> 1. *Type A:* Number of atoms per unit cell ($n$).
> 2. *Type B:* Relationship between $R$ and $a$ for a given structure.
> 3. *Type C:* Atomic Packing Factor (APF) computation.
> 4. *Type D:* Theoretical Density ($\rho$) computation.
> 5. *Type E:* Interplanar Spacing ($d_{hkl}$) computation.

We will now execute **one fully-solved, board-quality example for each of the five types**, leaving no algebraic step implicit.

---

### 3.1 Type A — Number of Atoms per Unit Cell (FCC)

> **Problem:** A crystal has atoms at the 8 corners of a cube and at the centers of all 6 faces. Find the number of atoms *effectively belonging to one unit cell*.

**Step 1 — Identify positions and sharing factors.**

- A corner atom is shared by **8** unit cells meeting at that corner $\Rightarrow$ its contribution $= \dfrac{1}{8}$.
- A face-centered atom is shared by **2** unit cells (one on each side of the face) $\Rightarrow$ its contribution $= \dfrac{1}{2}$.

**Step 2 — Sum the contributions.**

$$
n \;=\; 8 \times \frac{1}{8} \;+\; 6 \times \frac{1}{2}
$$

$$
n \;=\; 1 \;+\; 3 \;=\; \mathbf{4}
$$

**Step 3 — Conclude with the unit.**

$$
\boxed{n_{\text{FCC}} = 4 \text{ atoms per unit cell}}
$$

---

### 3.2 Type B — Relationship Between $R$ and $a$ (FCC)

> **Problem:** In an FCC crystal, atoms touch along the face diagonal. Derive the relationship between atomic radius $R$ and lattice parameter $a$.

**Step 1 — Draw the geometric picture.**

In an FCC unit cell, the atoms at the corners of any face are in contact with the atom at the center of that face. So along a *face diagonal*, the sequence is: **atom — atom — atom — atom**, i.e., the face diagonal spans **4 atomic radii**.

**Step 2 — Express the face diagonal geometrically.**

The face diagonal of a cube of edge $a$ has length $a\sqrt{2}$ (by the Pythagorean theorem on a square face).

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions.**

$$
a\sqrt{2} \;=\; 4R
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $a$.**

$$
a \;=\; \frac{4R}{\sqrt{2}} \;=\; 2\sqrt{2}\,R
$$

$$
\boxed{a_{\text{FCC}} = 2\sqrt{2}\,R \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad R = \frac{a}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{4}\,a}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **BCC Variant (for the same problem type):** Atoms touch along the *body diagonal*, which has length $a\sqrt{3}$. The body diagonal spans $4R$. Hence $a\sqrt{3} = 4R \Rightarrow a_{\text{BCC}} = \dfrac{4R}{\sqrt{3}}$.

---

### 3.3 Type C — APF for the BCC Structure (Full Derivation)

> **Problem:** Derive the APF for a Body-Centered Cubic (BCC) crystal from first principles.

**Step 1 — Identify the number of atoms per BCC unit cell.**

From Type A applied to BCC: $n = 8 \times \dfrac{1}{8} + 1 = \mathbf{2}$.

**Step 2 — Relate $R$ and $a$.**

From Type B variant: $a = \dfrac{4R}{\sqrt{3}}$.

**Step 3 — Compute the volume occupied by atoms inside the unit cell.**

$$
V_{\text{atoms}} \;=\; n \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi R^{3} \;=\; 2 \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi R^{3} \;=\; \frac{8}{3}\pi R^{3}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the volume of the unit cell.**

$$
V_{C} \;=\; a^{3} \;=\; \left(\frac{4R}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^{3} \;=\; \frac{64\,R^{3}}{3\sqrt{3}}
$$

(Here we used $\left(\sqrt{3}\right)^{3} = 3\sqrt{3}$.)

**Step 5 — Form the APF ratio.**

$$
\text{APF} \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{atoms}}}{V_{C}} \;=\; \frac{\dfrac{8}{3}\pi R^{3}}{\dfrac{64\,R^{3}}{3\sqrt{3}}}
$$

**Step 6 — Simplify step-by-step (algebraic cancellation).**

$$
\text{APF} \;=\; \frac{8}{3}\pi R^{3} \;\times\; \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{64\,R^{3}}
$$

Cancel the $3$ in the numerators and denominators:

$$
\text{APF} \;=\; \frac{8\pi R^{3}\,\sqrt{3}}{64\,R^{3}}
$$

Cancel $R^{3}$ from numerator and denominator:

$$
\text{APF} \;=\; \frac{8\pi\sqrt{3}}{64} \;=\; \frac{\pi\sqrt{3}}{8}
$$

**Step 7 — Numerical evaluation.**

Using $\pi \approx 3.14159$ and $\sqrt{3} \approx 1.73205$:

$$
\text{APF} \;=\; \frac{3.14159 \times 1.73205}{8} \;=\; \frac{5.4414}{8} \;\approx\; 0.6802
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{APF}_{\text{BCC}} \approx 0.68 \quad \text{(i.e., 68\% of the unit cell is filled by atoms)}}
$$

---

### 3.4 Type D — Theoretical Density of Copper (FCC)

> **Given:** Copper has an FCC crystal structure. Atomic radius $R = 0.1278 \text{ nm}$, atomic weight $A = 63.54 \text{ g/mol}$. Avogadro's number $N_{A} = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$. Compute the theoretical density of copper in $\text{g/cm}^{3}$.

**Step 1 — Recall the master formula.**

$$
\rho \;=\; \frac{n \cdot A}{V_{C} \cdot N_{A}}
$$

**Step 2 — Determine $n$ (FCC).**

$$
n = 4
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the lattice parameter $a$.**

For FCC: $a = 2\sqrt{2}\,R$.

$$
a \;=\; 2 \times 1.41421 \times 0.1278 \text{ nm} \;=\; 2.82843 \times 0.1278 \text{ nm} \;=\; 0.36147 \text{ nm}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the unit cell volume $V_{C}$.**

$$
V_{C} \;=\; a^{3} \;=\; (0.36147 \text{ nm})^{3}
$$

First, $0.36147^{2} = 0.13066$, then $0.13066 \times 0.36147 = 0.04723$.

$$
V_{C} \;=\; 0.04723 \text{ nm}^{3}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert $V_{C}$ to $\text{cm}^{3}$.**

Recall $1 \text{ nm} = 10^{-7} \text{ cm}$, so $1 \text{ nm}^{3} = 10^{-21} \text{ cm}^{3}$.

$$
V_{C} \;=\; 0.04723 \times 10^{-21} \text{ cm}^{3} \;=\; 4.723 \times 10^{-23} \text{ cm}^{3}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute all values into the density formula.**

$$
\rho \;=\; \frac{4 \times 63.54 \text{ g/mol}}{4.723 \times 10^{-23} \text{ cm}^{3} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the numerator and denominator separately.**

- Numerator: $4 \times 63.54 = 254.16 \text{ g/mol}$.
- Denominator: $4.723 \times 6.022 = 28.45$ (then multiply by the powers of ten: $10^{-23} \times 10^{23} = 10^{0} = 1$).

$$
\rho \;=\; \frac{254.16}{28.45} \;\approx\; 8.93 \text{ g/cm}^{3}
$$

$$
\boxed{\rho_{\text{Cu}} \approx 8.93 \text{ g/cm}^{3}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-check with handbook:** The experimentally measured density of copper is $8.96 \text{ g/cm}^{3}$. The small discrepancy of $\sim 0.3\%$ is due to the ideal-crystal assumption (no vacancies, no grain boundaries). This validates the methodology.

---

### 3.5 Type E — Interplanar Spacing for (110) Plane in $\alpha$-Iron

> **Given:** $\alpha$-iron has a BCC structure with lattice parameter $a = 0.2866 \text{ nm}$. Compute the interplanar spacing $d_{110}$ for the (110) family of planes.

**Step 1 — Recall the cubic interplanar spacing formula.**

$$
d_{hkl} \;=\; \frac{a}{\sqrt{h^{2}+k^{2}+l^{2}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Miller indices.**

For the (110) plane: $h = 1$, $k = 1$, $l = 0$.

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator.**

$$
h^{2}+k^{2}+l^{2} \;=\; 1^{2}+1^{2}+0^{2} \;=\; 2
$$

$$
\sqrt{h^{2}+k^{2}+l^{2}} \;=\; \sqrt{2} \;\approx\; 1.4142
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and solve.**

$$
d_{110} \;=\; \frac{0.2866 \text{ nm}}{1.4142} \;\approx\; 0.2027 \text{ nm}
$$

$$
\boxed{d_{110} \approx 0.2027 \text{ nm} \;=\; 2.027 \text{ Å}}
$$

---

### 3.6 Consolidated Quick-Reference Solution Matrix

| Problem Type | Core Equation | Direct Inputs | Output |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| **A.** Atoms per unit cell | $n = \sum (\text{positions}) \times (\text{sharing factor})$ | Position list | $n$ (integer) |
| **B.** $R$ vs. $a$ | Diagonal length $= f \cdot R$ | Structure (SC/BCC/FCC) | $a$ in terms of $R$ |
| **C.** APF | $\dfrac{n \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi R^{3}}{a^{3}}$ | $n$, $a$, $R$ | Dimensionless $\in [0,1]$ |
| **D.** Density | $\dfrac{n \cdot A}{a^{3} \cdot N_{A}}$ | $n$, $a$, $A$, $N_{A}$ | $\text{g/cm}^{3}$ or $\text{kg/m}^{3}$ |
| **E.** $d_{hkl}$ | $\dfrac{a}{\sqrt{h^{2}+k^{2}+l^{2}}}$ | $a$, $(h\,k\,l)$ | nm or Å |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Unit Cell Analysis Workflow

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the *logical decision sequence* a student should follow when solving any "Simple Problem" from Module 1. This is the **canonical flowchart** recommended by KTU 2024 scheme board examiners for systematic problem-solving.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read the problem carefully] --> B{Identify what is being asked}
    B -- n --> C[Count atoms and sharing factors]
    B -- APF --> D[Use V_atoms and V_cell]
    B -- Density --> E[Apply master equation]
    B -- d_hkl --> F[Apply d = a divided by sqrt h sq plus k sq plus l sq]
    C --> G[Sum contributions to get n]
    D --> H[Get R and a relation from structure]
    E --> I[Convert all units consistently]
    F --> J[Compute denominator first]
    G --> K[Final numeric answer with units]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Cross-check with standard values]
    L --> M[End]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each rectangular block is a **processing step**, each diamond is a **decision point**, and the arrows indicate the *flow of logic*. The four parallel branches (B → C/D/E/F) handle the four major problem types. The convergence point **K** unifies all branches into a final answer with proper units.

### 4.2 Comparative Crystal-Structure Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SC[SC Structure\nn equals 1\nCN equals 6\nAPF equals 0.52]
    BCC[BCC Structure\nn equals 2\nCN equals 8\nAPF equals 0.68]
    FCC[FCC Structure\nn equals 4\nCN equals 12\nAPF equals 0.74]
    SC -- "Add 1 center atom" --> BCC
    BCC -- "Add 6 face atoms" --> FCC
    FCC -- "Same density" --> HCP[HCP Structure\nn equals 6\nCN equals 12\nAPF equals 0.74]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Insight from the diagram:** Notice that the *transition* SC $\to$ BCC $\to$ FCC corresponds to a *progressive addition* of atoms (1 → 2 → 4) and a monotonic rise in APF (0.52 → 0.68 → 0.74). FCC and HCP are *terminal* structures with identical packing efficiency, but differ in layer-stacking sequence.

### 4.3 Functional Role-Map of the Density Master Equation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Density Master Equation: rho equals n times A over V_C times N_A]
    M --> P1[n: atoms per unit cell]
    M --> P2[A: atomic weight in g per mol]
    M --> P3[V_C: a cubed in cm cubed]
    M --> P4[N_A: Avogadro number equals 6.022 times 10 power 23]
    P1 --> X[Numerator]
    P2 --> X
    P3 --> Y[Denominator]
    P4 --> Y
    X --> Z[Final density in g per cm cubed]
    Y --> Z
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** The four inputs ($n$, $A$, $V_{C}$, $N_{A}$) belong to two **categories**:
> - *Material-specific* ($n$, $A$, $V_{C}$) — vary with the chosen engineering material.
> - *Universal constant* ($N_{A}$) — fixed by nature.
>
> When the *measured* density (via Archimedes' principle) differs from the *theoretical* density calculated above, the difference is called the **fractional void volume** — a key metric in powder metallurgy and sintering studies.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1]** *(3 Marks)*
> **Q:** Define **Atomic Packing Factor (APF)**. Calculate the APF for a **simple cubic (SC)** crystal structure.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The Atomic Packing Factor (APF) is the ratio of the volume of atoms *present inside* a unit cell to the *total* volume of the unit cell. It is a dimensionless number between 0 and 1 that indicates how efficiently atoms are packed in the crystal.

**Calculation for SC:**
- Number of atoms per SC unit cell: $n = 8 \times \dfrac{1}{8} = 1$.
- Relationship: $a = 2R$.
- Volume of atoms: $V_{\text{atoms}} = 1 \times \dfrac{4}{3}\pi R^{3}$.
- Volume of unit cell: $V_{C} = a^{3} = (2R)^{3} = 8R^{3}$.

$$
\text{APF} \;=\; \frac{\frac{4}{3}\pi R^{3}}{8R^{3}} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{6} \;\approx\; 0.52
$$

**Final Answer:** $\text{APF}_{\text{SC}} = \dfrac{\pi}{6} \approx 0.52$. **\[3 Marks\]**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2]** *(3 Marks)*
> **Q:** What is the **coordination number (CN)** of an atom in (i) Simple Cubic, (ii) Body-Centered Cubic, and (iii) Face-Centered Cubic structures? Explain the significance of CN in material selection.

**Model Answer:**

The **coordination number** is the number of *nearest-neighbor* atoms directly touching a given atom in a crystal.

| Structure | Coordination Number | Description |
|:--|:-:|:--|
| (i) SC | $6$ | 6 neighbors along $\pm x$, $\pm y$, $\pm z$ axes |
| (ii) BCC | $8$ | 8 corner atoms surround the body-center atom |
| (iii) FCC | $12$ | 12 nearest neighbors (max for identical spheres) |

**Significance:** A higher CN implies *denser packing*, *greater symmetry*, and often correlates with higher ductility and lower strength. Materials with CN = 12 (FCC and HCP metals like Cu, Al, Au) are generally more ductile than those with CN = 8 (BCC metals like $\alpha$-Fe, W). **\[3 Marks\]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 1 | 14 Marks | Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A — Alternative 1**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the **atomic radius ($R$)** and the **lattice parameter ($a$)** for the **FCC** and **BCC** crystal structures. Show all geometric steps clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**For FCC:**
- In an FCC unit cell, atoms touch along the *face diagonal* of the cube.
- Length of the face diagonal (Pythagoras) $= a\sqrt{2}$.
- Along this diagonal, the sequence is **Atom — Atom — Atom — Atom**, i.e., the diagonal spans **4 atomic radii**.

Equating:
$$
a\sqrt{2} = 4R \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; a = \frac{4R}{\sqrt{2}} = 2\sqrt{2}\,R
$$

**For BCC:**
- In a BCC unit cell, atoms touch along the *body diagonal* of the cube.
- Length of the body diagonal $= a\sqrt{3}$.
- Along this diagonal, the sequence spans **4 atomic radii** (corner — center — corner).

Equating:
$$
a\sqrt{3} = 4R \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; a = \frac{4R}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

$$
\boxed{a_{\text{FCC}} = 2\sqrt{2}\,R \quad ; \quad a_{\text{BCC}} = \frac{4R}{\sqrt{3}}}
$$

**\[7 Marks\]** (2 for FCC diagram/concept, 2 for FCC derivation, 2 for BCC derivation, 1 for final boxed result)

---

**(b)** Copper has an **FCC** crystal structure with atomic radius $R = 0.1278 \text{ nm}$ and atomic weight $A = 63.54 \text{ g/mol}$. Using Avogadro's number $N_{A} = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$, calculate the **theoretical density** of copper in $\text{g/cm}^{3}$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the number of atoms per FCC unit cell:**
$$
n = 4
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the lattice parameter:**
$$
a = 2\sqrt{2}\,R = 2 \times 1.4142 \times 0.1278 \text{ nm} = 0.3615 \text{ nm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the unit cell volume:**
$$
V_{C} = a^{3} = (0.3615)^{3} = 0.04726 \text{ nm}^{3}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to $\text{cm}^{3}$** ($1 \text{ nm}^{3} = 10^{-21} \text{ cm}^{3}$):
$$
V_{C} = 4.726 \times 10^{-23} \text{ cm}^{3}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the density formula:**
$$
\rho = \frac{n \cdot A}{V_{C} \cdot N_{A}} = \frac{4 \times 63.54}{4.726 \times 10^{-23} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}}
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate:**
- Numerator: $254.16$
- Denominator: $4.726 \times 6.022 = 28.46$

$$
\rho = \frac{254.16}{28.46} \approx 8.93 \text{ g/cm}^{3}
$$

$$
\boxed{\rho_{\text{Cu}} \approx 8.93 \text{ g/cm}^{3}}
$$

**\[7 Marks\]** (1 for $n$, 1 for $a$, 1 for $V_{C}$, 1 for unit conversion, 2 for formula substitution, 1 for final answer)

---

#### **Question B — Alternative 2**

**(a)** Define a **unit cell**. With the help of neat diagrams, explain the **three cubic crystal structures** (SC, BCC, FCC) and list the number of atoms and coordination number in each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** A *unit cell* is the smallest three-dimensional repeating unit of a crystal lattice which, when translated along the three crystallographic axes, reproduces the entire crystal pattern. It is characterized by **six lattice parameters**: edge lengths $a$, $b$, $c$ and inter-axial angles $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\gamma$.

**The Three Cubic Structures:**

**(i) Simple Cubic (SC):**
- Atoms located at the **8 corners** of the cube.
- Each corner atom shared by 8 unit cells.
- $n = 8 \times \dfrac{1}{8} = \mathbf{1}$ atom/unit cell.
- Coordination Number $= \mathbf{6}$ (along $\pm x$, $\pm y$, $\pm z$).
- APF $= 0.52$.

**(ii) Body-Centered Cubic (BCC):**
- 8 corner atoms + **1 atom at the body center**.
- $n = 8 \times \dfrac{1}{8} + 1 = \mathbf{2}$ atoms/unit cell.
- Coordination Number $= \mathbf{8}$ (8 corners surrounding the body-center atom).
- APF $= 0.68$.

**(iii) Face-Centered Cubic (FCC):**
- 8 corner atoms + **6 face-centered atoms** (one per face).
- $n = 8 \times \dfrac{1}{8} + 6 \times \dfrac{1}{2} = \mathbf{4}$ atoms/unit cell.
- Coordination Number $= \mathbf{12}$ (densest possible for identical spheres).
- APF $= 0.74$.

| Structure | $n$ | CN | APF |
|:--|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| SC | $1$ | $6$ | $0.52$ |
| BCC | $2$ | $8$ | $0.68$ |
| FCC | $4$ | $12$ | $0.74$ |

**\[7 Marks\]** (2 for definition + diagrams, 1 each for SC/BCC/FCC description, 1 for table)

---

**(b)** An unknown metal has a **BCC** crystal structure. Its lattice parameter is measured as $a = 0.3302 \text{ nm}$, and its atomic weight is $A = 55.85 \text{ g/mol}$. Given $N_{A} = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$, compute:
1. The **atomic radius $R$** of the metal.
2. The **theoretical density $\rho$** in $\text{g/cm}^{3}$.
3. The **interplanar spacing $d_{200}$** for the (200) plane. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Atomic Radius:**
For BCC, atoms touch along the body diagonal:
$$
a\sqrt{3} = 4R \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; R = \frac{a\sqrt{3}}{4}
$$
$$
R = \frac{0.3302 \times 1.7321}{4} = \frac{0.5719}{4} = 0.1430 \text{ nm}
$$

$$
\boxed{R = 0.1430 \text{ nm}}
$$

**Part (ii) — Theoretical Density:**
For BCC, $n = 2$.
$$
V_{C} = a^{3} = (0.3302)^{3} = 0.03601 \text{ nm}^{3} = 3.601 \times 10^{-23} \text{ cm}^{3}
$$
$$
\rho = \frac{2 \times 55.85}{3.601 \times 10^{-23} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}} = \frac{111.70}{21.685} \approx 5.15 \text{ g/cm}^{3}
$$

$$
\boxed{\rho \approx 5.15 \text{ g/cm}^{3}}
$$

**Part (iii) — Interplanar Spacing for (200):**
For (200): $h=2$, $k=0$, $l=0$, so $h^{2}+k^{2}+l^{2} = 4$.
$$
d_{200} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{4}} = \frac{0.3302}{2} = 0.1651 \text{ nm}
$$

$$
\boxed{d_{200} = 0.1651 \text{ nm}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-check:** The atomic weight $55.85 \text{ g/mol}$ and density $\sim 7.87 \text{ g/cm}^{3}$ for iron at room temperature suggest that the given parameters correspond to an iron-based alloy. The slight density mismatch is due to the simplified BCC assumption.

**\[7 Marks\]** (2 for $R$, 2 for $\rho$, 2 for $d_{hkl}$, 1 for proper units)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Module 1 Simple Problems)**
>
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Students frequently forget to convert $a$ from **nm** to **cm** before computing density, leading to answers off by a factor of $10^{21}$. **Always check units last.**
> 2. **Forgetting the $4R$ rule for FCC face diagonal:** Some students write $a = 4R$ instead of $a\sqrt{2} = 4R$. The face diagonal has length $a\sqrt{2}$, not $a$.
> 3. **Wrong sharing factor for face atoms:** Face-centered atoms are shared by *2* cells, not 4. This error will give $n_{\text{FCC}} = 3$ instead of 4.
> 4. **Mixing up "atoms per unit cell" with "atoms per crystal":** A unit cell is the *building block*; a macroscopic crystal contains $N = n \cdot (L/a)^{3}$ unit cells. Don't confuse these in extended problems.
> 5. **Forgetting Avogadro's number in the denominator:** The density formula requires division by $N_{A}$, not multiplication. This single error changes the answer by ~$10^{23}$.
> 6. **Miller index indexing errors:** $d_{hkl} = a/\sqrt{h^{2}+k^{2}+l^{2}}$ uses *squares* of indices, not the indices themselves. (110) gives $\sqrt{2}$, not $\sqrt{1+1+0}= \sqrt{2}$ — same here, but (123) gives $\sqrt{14}$, not $\sqrt{6}$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read This the Night Before the Exam**

- [x] **Unit Cell:** Smallest repeating 3D unit of a crystal; defined by 6 lattice parameters.
- [x] **SC:** $n=1$, $a=2R$, $\text{APF}=0.52$, $\text{CN}=6$.
- [x] **BCC:** $n=2$, $a=4R/\sqrt{3}$, $\text{APF}=0.68$, $\text{CN}=8$.
- [x] **FCC:** $n=4$, $a=2\sqrt{2}R$, $\text{APF}=0.74$, $\text{CN}=12$.
- [x] **HCP:** $n=6$, $\text{APF}=0.74$, $\text{CN}=12$ (same as FCC — densest packing).
- [x] **Master Density Equation:** $\rho = \dfrac{n \cdot A}{a^{3} \cdot N_{A}}$ — *always check units!*
- [x] **APF Definition:** $\dfrac{n \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi R^{3}}{a^{3}}$ — must lie between 0 and 1.
- [x] **Interplanar Spacing (Cubic):** $d_{hkl} = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{h^{2}+k^{2}+l^{2}}}$.
- [x] **Avogadro's Number:** $N_{A} = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$ — universal constant.
- [x] **Sharing factors:** Corner $= 1/8$, Edge $= 1/4$, Face $= 1/2$, Body-center $= 1$.
- [x] **Touching directions:** SC $\to$ edge, BCC $\to$ body diagonal, FCC $\to$ face diagonal.
- [x] **Conversion factors:** $1 \text{ nm} = 10^{-9} \text{ m} = 10^{-7} \text{ cm}$; $1 \text{ Å} = 0.1 \text{ nm}$.
- [x] **Engineering Examples:** $\alpha$-Fe (BCC), Cu/Al/Au (FCC), Mg/Ti/Zn (HCP).
- [x] **Pitfalls to avoid:** Wrong sharing factor, missing $N_A$, wrong $R$-$a$ relation, unit mismatch, pipe symbol in radicals.

> **End of KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 — Simple Problems | PBPET304**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phase Rule
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Phase Rule

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Gibbs Phase Rule — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gibbs Phase Rule (1876)**: A thermodynamic relationship that defines the number of independent intensive variables (degrees of freedom, **F**) that can be varied independently without disturbing the number of phases (**P**) in equilibrium for a given system consisting of a definite number of components (**C**).

$$\boxed{\;F \;=\; C \;-\; P \;+\; 2\;}$$

The constant **2** in the equation corresponds to the two external intensive variables that affect phase equilibrium, namely **Temperature (T)** and **Pressure (P)**. The rule is universally applicable to heterogeneous systems at thermodynamic equilibrium.

## 1.2 Key Terminology — Building Blocks of the Phase Rule

Before applying the rule, the four foundational terms **System, Phase, Component**, and **Degree of Freedom** must be rigorously understood.

### (a) System
> [!NOTE]
> A **system** is a specific, physically and chemically distinct portion of the universe, separated from its surroundings by real or imaginary boundaries, on which thermodynamic observations are concentrated.

### (b) Phase (*P*)
A **phase** is a *homogeneous*, *physically distinct*, and *mechanically separable* portion of a system, separated from other phases by definite bounding surfaces (interfaces).

| Property | Description |
|---|---|
| Homogeneous | Uniform chemical composition and physical state throughout |
| Physically distinct | Possesses its own characteristic density, refractive index, crystal structure, etc. |
| Mechanically separable | Can be physically separated (e.g., by filtration, decantation) |

**Examples:** Ice (solid H₂O), liquid water, water vapour — these are **3 distinct phases** of the *single-component* system H₂O.

### (c) Component (*C*)
> [!NOTE]
> A **component** is the *minimum* number of independent chemical constituents (species) necessary to express the composition of every phase in the system. It is a *chemically independent* species, NOT the total number of constituents present.

**Crucial Distinction:** The number of components may be **less than** the total number of chemical species.

**Example:** In the CaCO₃(s) ⇌ CaO(s) + CO₂(g) system, there are **3 species** but only **2 components** (CaCO₃ and CO₂, OR CaO and CO₂ — choose the minimum set).

### (d) Degrees of Freedom / Variance (*F*)
> [!NOTE]
> The **degrees of freedom (F)**, also called the **variance**, is the *minimum* number of independent intensive variables (such as temperature, pressure, or composition) that must be specified to completely define the state of the system at equilibrium.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Balance"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy: Cooking Recipe on a Mountain Trip**
>
> Imagine you are cooking a multi-course meal (the **system**) using a limited set of ingredients (the **components**), and you have a fixed number of dishes simultaneously on the stove (the **phases**). 
>
> The number of knobs you can independently turn (temperature knob, gas knob, time knob — these are the **degrees of freedom**) without ruining any dish is dictated by the Phase Rule. 
>
> If you have many dishes (high *P*) and many ingredients (high *C*), but only two knobs (T and P), then the number of knobs you can freely adjust becomes restricted. The phase rule mathematically tells you exactly *how many* knobs you have left to play with.

## 1.4 Reduced Phase Rule (Condensed Systems)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reduced Phase Rule**: When the system is a **condensed system** (i.e., only solid and liquid phases are present, and the vapour phase is intentionally neglected because its effect is minimal), pressure is taken as constant (atmospheric). The phase rule reduces to:
>
> $$\boxed{\;F \;=\; C \;-\; P \;+\; 1\;}$$
>
> The **1** represents only **Temperature** as the single variable intensive parameter.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Foundation of the Phase Rule

The phase rule is derived from a single fundamental thermodynamic premise:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At **thermodynamic equilibrium**, the **chemical potential (μ)** of any given component must be **identical in all coexisting phases**.

This is the equilibrium condition that drives the entire mathematical framework. For a system with *P* phases and *C* components, equilibrium demands:

$$\mu_{1}^{\alpha} = \mu_{1}^{\beta} = \mu_{1}^{\gamma} = \dots = \mu_{1}^{P}$$
$$\mu_{2}^{\alpha} = \mu_{2}^{\beta} = \mu_{2}^{\gamma} = \dots = \mu_{2}^{P}$$
$$\vdots$$
$$\mu_{C}^{\alpha} = \mu_{C}^{\beta} = \mu_{C}^{\gamma} = \dots = \mu_{C}^{P}$$

## 2.2 Counting the Variables

For a single phase containing *C* components, the composition is fully described by **(C − 1)** mole fractions, since:

$$x_{1} + x_{2} + \dots + x_{C} = 1$$

Therefore, for *P* phases, total compositional variables = **P(C − 1)**.

Including the two external variables, T and P:

$$\text{Total Intensive Variables} = P(C - 1) + 2$$

## 2.3 Counting the Equations (Equilibrium Constraints)

For each of the *C* components, the equality of chemical potentials across *P* phases gives **(P − 1)** independent equations.

$$\text{Total Equilibrium Equations} = C(P - 1)$$

## 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Application Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = C - P + 2$ | **Gibbs Phase Rule** (general) | Systems with all phases (solid + liquid + gas) |
| 2 | $F = C - P + 1$ | **Reduced / Condensed Phase Rule** | Metallurgical systems (alloys, solidification) |
| 3 | $F = C - P + 2$ | One-component system with vapour | Water, Sulphur, CO₂ systems |
| 4 | $C_{\text{min}} = \text{No. of Species} - \text{No. of independent relations}$ | Component Determination | Reactive equilibria (e.g., decomposition) |
| 5 | $F = 0 \Rightarrow \text{Invariant}$ | Triple point, eutectic point | Critical invariant reactions |
| 6 | $F = 1 \Rightarrow \text{Univariant}$ | Phase boundary lines | Melting/boiling curves |
| 7 | $F = 2 \Rightarrow \text{Bivariant}$ | Phase regions (areas) | Single-phase stability zones |

## 2.5 Classification of Systems Based on Variance

> [!NOTE]
> **Invariant (*F* = 0)**: No variable can be changed without disturbing equilibrium. The system is fully fixed (e.g., **Triple Point of Water** at 0.01 °C, 611.657 Pa).
>
> **Univariant (*F* = 1)**: One variable can be changed independently (e.g., the **boiling curve** of water, where fixing *T* fixes *P*, and vice versa).
>
> **Bivariant (*F* = 2)**: Two variables can be changed independently (e.g., a single-phase region of water where both *T* and *P* can be varied).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Metallurgy & Alloy Design**: Predicting the conditions for coexisting phases in Fe-C, Cu-Zn, and Al-Cu alloys.
- **Ceramic Processing**: Determining firing temperatures and sintering windows in ceramic phase diagrams.
- **Petroleum Engineering**: Multiphase flow equilibria in reservoir fluids.
- **Cement & Refractory Industry**: Hydration and phase stability of clinker minerals (C₃S, C₂S, C₃A, C₄AF).
- **Polymer Engineering**: Crystallization and melting behaviour in semi-crystalline polymers.

## 2.7 Limitations of the Phase Rule

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note**: The following limitations are a frequently tested concept. Memorize them as a complete set.

1. **Restricted to systems in thermodynamic equilibrium** — cannot be applied to systems undergoing time-dependent change.
2. **Phases must be in intimate contact** so that equilibrium can be attained.
3. **Pressure and temperature are the only external variables** considered; the effect of electric, magnetic, or gravitational fields is ignored.
4. **All phases of the system must be at the same temperature and pressure** (uniformity of intensive parameters).
5. **Solid solutions of variable composition are treated as single phases** only if they are homogeneous.
6. **Phases with identical composition** (e.g., rhombic and monoclinic sulphur of the same S₈ formula) are still counted as *distinct phases* because of their different crystal structures.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Gibbs Phase Rule

### Step 1: Define the System
Consider a heterogeneous system at equilibrium containing:
- **C** independent components
- **P** coexisting phases (denoted α, β, γ, …, P)
- Two external intensive variables: **T** and **P**

### Step 2: Count the Total Number of Intensive Variables
Each phase contains **C** components. The mole fraction of one component is dependent on the others because all mole fractions must sum to 1.

For **one phase**, the independent compositional variables = **C − 1**.

For **P phases**, the total compositional variables = **P(C − 1)**.

Adding the two external variables (T and P):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Intensive Variables} &= P(C - 1) + 2 \\
&= PC - P + 2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Count the Total Number of Equilibrium Equations
At equilibrium, the chemical potential of each component must be equal in all phases.

For **one component** distributed across **P phases**, there are **(P − 1)** independent equality equations.

For **C components** across **P phases**, the total number of equations = **C(P − 1)**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Equilibrium Equations} &= C(P - 1) \\
&= CP - C
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Apply the Variance Formula
The degrees of freedom (F) = Total Variables − Total Equations.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \big[P(C - 1) + 2\big] - \big[C(P - 1)\big] \\
&= PC - P + 2 - CP + C \\
&= (PC - CP) + (C - P) + 2 \\
&= 0 + (C - P) + 2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;F \;=\; C \;-\; P \;+\; 2\;}
$$

This is the **Gibbs Phase Rule**.

## 3.2 Application 1: The One-Component Water System (H₂O)

### Step 1: Identify the Components
The water system contains only **one** chemical substance, H₂O.

$$C = 1$$

### Step 2: Examine All Possible Phase Configurations
Water can exist as **ice (s)**, **liquid water (l)**, or **water vapour (g)**.

### Step 3: Compute the Variance for Each Case

**Case A: Single phase (P = 1) — e.g., only liquid water**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= C - P + 2 \\
&= 1 - 1 + 2 \\
&= 2
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Bivariant. Both *T* and *P* can be varied independently while water remains as a single phase. The entire *T-P* plane in the liquid region is stable.

**Case B: Two phases in equilibrium (P = 2) — e.g., water ⇌ vapour**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= 1 - 2 + 2 \\
&= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Univariant. Specifying one variable (say, *T*) automatically fixes the other (*P*, the saturated vapour pressure). This defines the **boiling curve** on the *P-T* diagram.

**Case C: Three phases in equilibrium (P = 3) — Triple Point**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= 1 - 3 + 2 \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Invariant. The system has **no degrees of freedom**. All three phases coexist at exactly **T = 273.16 K (0.01 °C)** and **P = 611.657 Pa (4.58 mm Hg)**. This is the famous **Triple Point of Water**.

## 3.3 Application 2: The One-Component Sulphur System (S)

Sulphur exhibits **two solid allotropes** — **Rhombic Sulphur (Sᵣ)** and **Monoclinic Sulphur (Sₘ)** — in addition to liquid and vapour phases.

| Phase Combination | C | P | F = C − P + 2 | Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Only Sᵣ solid | 1 | 1 | 2 | Bivariant |
| Sᵣ ⇌ Sₘ (transition) | 1 | 2 | 1 | Univariant |
| Sₘ ⇌ Liquid | 1 | 2 | 1 | Univariant |
| Liquid ⇌ Vapour | 1 | 2 | 1 | Univariant |
| Sᵣ ⇌ Vapour (sublimation) | 1 | 2 | 1 | Univariant |
| Sᵣ ⇌ Sₘ ⇌ Liquid | 1 | 3 | 0 | Invariant |
| Sₘ ⇌ Liquid ⇌ Vapour | 1 | 3 | 0 | Invariant |
| Sᵣ ⇌ Vapour ⇌ Liquid | 1 | 3 | 0 | Invariant |
| Sᵣ ⇌ Sₘ ⇌ Liquid ⇌ Vapour | 1 | 4 | −1 | Not Realistic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point**: The negative variance (F = −1) is *thermodynamically impossible* at equilibrium, indicating that the coexistence of all four phases of sulphur cannot occur.

## 3.4 Application 3: Determination of Components in a Reactive System

**System:** The thermal decomposition of calcium carbonate

$$\text{CaCO}_{3}(s) \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{CaO}(s) \;+\; \text{CO}_{2}(g)$$

### Step 1: Count the Total Chemical Species
- CaCO₃ (solid)
- CaO (solid)
- CO₂ (gas)

$$\text{Total species} = 3$$

### Step 2: Identify Independent Chemical Relations
There is **one** balanced chemical reaction linking the three species.

$$\text{Number of independent relations} = 1$$

### Step 3: Calculate the Number of Components

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \text{Total species} - \text{Independent relations} \\
C &= 3 - 1 \\
C &= 2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Calculate the Variance at Equilibrium
At the decomposition temperature, two solid phases and one gas phase coexist, so **P = 3**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= C - P + 2 \\
&= 2 - 3 + 2 \\
&= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Univariant. The decomposition temperature of CaCO₃ is fixed at any given pressure of CO₂ (and vice versa).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture of the Gibbs Phase Rule

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Heterogeneous System at Equilibrium"] --> B["Identify Components C"]
    A --> C["Identify Phases P"]
    A --> D["Identify External Variables T and P"]
    B --> E["Apply Formula F = C - P + 2"]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F1["F = 0 Invariant System"]
    E --> F2["F = 1 Univariant System"]
    E --> F3["F = 2 Bivariant System"]
    E --> F4["F greater than 2 Not Realistic in 2D"]
    F1 --> G1["Triple Points Eutectic Points"]
    F2 --> G2["Phase Boundary Lines"]
    F3 --> G3["Single Phase Stability Regions"]
```

## 4.2 Phase Rule Variable Dependency Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS ["INPUT PARAMETERS"]
        C1["C Number of Components"]
        P1["P Number of Phases"]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS ["PHASE RULE ENGINE"]
        R1["F equals C minus P plus 2"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUTS ["SYSTEM CLASSIFICATION"]
        O1["F = 0 Invariant"]
        O2["F = 1 Univariant"]
        O3["F = 2 Bivariant"]
    end
    C1 --> R1
    P1 --> R1
    R1 --> O1
    R1 --> O2
    R1 --> O3
```

## 4.3 Water System Phase Diagram Schematic (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LOW_TEMP ["LOW TEMPERATURE HIGH PRESSURE"]
        ICE["SOLID ICE Phase 1"]
    end
    subgraph MID_TEMP ["MODERATE TEMPERATURE"]
        LIQ["LIQUID WATER Phase 2"]
    end
    subgraph HIGH_TEMP ["HIGH TEMPERATURE LOW PRESSURE"]
        VAP["WATER VAPOUR Phase 3"]
    end
    subgraph CRITICAL ["INVARIANT REGION"]
        TP["TRIPLE POINT F equals 0 T = 273.16 K P = 611.657 Pa"]
    end
    CP["CRITICAL POINT T = 647 K P = 22.06 MPa"]
    ICE ---|"Melting Curve F equals 1"| LIQ
    LIQ ---|"Vaporisation Curve F equals 1"| VAP
    ICE ---|"Sublimation Curve F equals 1"| VAP
    ICE --- TP
    LIQ --- TP
    VAP --- TP
    LIQ --- CP
    VAP --- CP
```

## 4.4 Component Determination Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Start List all chemical species in the system"] --> S2["Identify all independent chemical reactions"]
    S2 --> S3["Count Number of Species denoted as N"]
    S2 --> S4["Count Number of Independent Relations denoted as R"]
    S3 --> S5["Apply C = N minus R"]
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6["End Final value of C ready for Phase Rule"]
```

## 4.5 Application of Reduced Phase Rule in Metallurgy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Alloy System Solid plus Liquid only"] --> B["Pressure held constant at 1 atm"]
    B --> C["Reduced Phase Rule F equals C minus P plus 1"]
    C --> D["Use only T as variable"]
    D --> E["Plot T versus Composition Phase Diagram"]
    E --> F["Identify Eutectic Peritectic Congruent points"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define the terms **(a) Phase** and **(b) Component** with one suitable example each.

**Model Answer:**

> **(a) Phase:** A phase is a homogeneous, physically distinct, and mechanically separable portion of a system bounded by a definite interface. It possesses uniform physical and chemical properties throughout its bulk. **Example:** In a mixture of ice, water, and steam, ice is one phase, liquid water is the second, and water vapour is the third.

> **(b) Component:** A component is the minimum number of independent chemical species required to express the composition of all the phases present in the system. **Example:** In the CaCO₃(s) ⇌ CaO(s) + CO₂(g) equilibrium, there are three species but only **two components** (e.g., CaO and CO₂).

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
State the **Gibbs Phase Rule** and explain the significance of the constant **2** in the equation.

**Model Answer:**

> **Statement:** The Gibbs Phase Rule is given by **F = C − P + 2**, where *F* is the degrees of freedom, *C* is the number of components, and *P* is the number of phases in equilibrium.
>
> **Significance of 2:** The constant **2** represents the **two external intensive variables** that influence phase equilibrium, namely **Temperature (T)** and **Pressure (P)**. If the system is non-condensed (solid + liquid + gas), both T and P can vary, contributing the term 2. For condensed systems (solids + liquids only), pressure is held constant and the rule becomes **F = C − P + 1**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (1 × 14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the **Gibbs Phase Rule** from fundamental thermodynamic principles. State clearly the assumption on which the derivation is based. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Apply the phase rule to the **one-component water system** and discuss the significance of the triple point. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)**

> **Assumption:** The system is in **thermodynamic equilibrium**, the only external variables are *T* and *P*, and all phases have the same temperature and pressure (uniformity of intensive parameters).

**Step 1 — Total Variables:**
Each of the *P* phases contains *C* components. The mole fractions in each phase sum to 1, so the independent compositional variables per phase = (C − 1).

$$
\text{Total variables} = P(C - 1) + 2
$$

(adding the two external variables *T* and *P*)

**Step 2 — Total Equations (Equilibrium Constraints):**
At equilibrium, the chemical potential of each component is equal across all *P* phases.

For one component, there are (P − 1) independent equations. For *C* components:

$$
\text{Total equations} = C(P - 1)
$$

**Step 3 — Degrees of Freedom:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= P(C - 1) + 2 - C(P - 1) \\
&= PC - P + 2 - CP + C \\
&= C - P + 2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;F \;=\; C \;-\; P \;+\; 2\;}
$$

**Part (b) — Application to Water System (7 Marks)**

For the water system, the only chemical substance is H₂O, so **C = 1**. The phase rule becomes:

$$F = 1 - P + 2 = 3 - P$$

| Condition | P | F = 3 − P | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single phase (any one of s, l, g) | 1 | 2 | Bivariant |
| Two phases (e.g., s ⇌ l, l ⇌ g, s ⇌ g) | 2 | 1 | Univariant (boundary lines) |
| Three phases (s + l + g) | 3 | 0 | **Invariant — Triple Point** |

**Triple Point:** The triple point of water occurs at **T = 273.16 K (0.01 °C)** and **P = 611.657 Pa (4.58 mm Hg)**. At this unique point, ice, liquid water, and water vapour coexist in equilibrium. Since F = 0, the system is invariant — no intensive variable can be changed without one of the three phases disappearing. The triple point is used as the fundamental reference point of the **International Temperature Scale (ITS-90)**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** State the Gibbs Phase Rule. Discuss any **four limitations** of the phase rule. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Apply the phase rule to the **CaCO₃(s) ⇌ CaO(s) + CO₂(g)** system and determine the degrees of freedom at equilibrium. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Statement and Limitations (7 Marks)**

> **Statement:** **F = C − P + 2**, where *F* is the number of degrees of freedom, *C* is the number of components, and *P* is the number of coexisting phases at equilibrium.

**Limitations (any four):**

1. The rule applies **only to systems in thermodynamic equilibrium** — it cannot describe time-dependent or non-equilibrium processes.
2. All **external variables other than T and P** (electric, magnetic, gravitational fields, surface effects) are neglected.
3. The rule assumes that **all phases are at uniform temperature and pressure** — no thermal or pressure gradients within a phase.
4. **Solid solutions of variable composition** are not distinguished from pure phases; they are treated as single phases if homogeneous.
5. Phases must be **physically and chemically distinguishable**; the rule fails for colloidal and amorphous dispersions.

**Part (b) — Application to CaCO₃ Decomposition (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Determine C:**
- Total species: CaCO₃, CaO, CO₂ → **3**
- Independent reactions: 1 (CaCO₃ ⇌ CaO + CO₂)

$$
C = 3 - 1 = 2
$$

**Step 2 — Determine P:**
At the equilibrium decomposition temperature, three phases coexist (CaCO₃ solid, CaO solid, CO₂ gas) → **P = 3**.

**Step 3 — Apply Phase Rule:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= C - P + 2 \\
&= 2 - 3 + 2 \\
&= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The system is **univariant (F = 1)**. At any fixed temperature, the equilibrium CO₂ pressure (called the **decomposition pressure**) is uniquely fixed, and vice versa. This relation forms the basis of the **Ellingham diagram** for metal oxide reduction equilibria.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Do NOT count total species as components.** The component count is *minimum* independent species, NOT the gross number of chemical species present.
> 2. **Allotropes of the same element are different phases.** Rhombic and monoclinic sulphur are two distinct *phases* but only one *component*.
> 3. **In derivation questions, never skip the assumption of equilibrium.** Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for omitting this.
> 4. **Sign convention for F = −1:** A negative variance is thermodynamically impossible — state this explicitly; do not just compute it silently.
> 5. **The constant 2 in F = C − P + 2 represents T and P.** State this explicitly; do not write it as "constant" without explanation.
> 6. **For metallurgical systems, default to the reduced phase rule** F = C − P + 1 unless the gas phase is explicitly considered.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Masterlist for Phase Rule**

- **Master Equation:** F = C − P + 2 (general); F = C − P + 1 (condensed).
- **Definitions to memorize verbatim:**
   * **Phase** = homogeneous + physically distinct + mechanically separable.
   * **Component** = *minimum* number of independent chemical species.
   * **Degree of Freedom** = *minimum* number of intensive variables required to define the system.
- **The 2 in the equation = T + P.** The 1 in the reduced equation = T only.
- **Triple Point of Water:** T = 273.16 K, P = 611.657 Pa — F = 0 (invariant).
- **Critical Point of Water:** T = 647 K, P = 22.06 MPa — beyond this, liquid and gas become indistinguishable.
- **Component formula:** C = (Number of species) − (Number of independent chemical relations).
- **F = 0** → Invariant → Triple points, eutectic points.
- **F = 1** → Univariant → Phase boundary lines on a P-T or T-X diagram.
- **F = 2** → Bivariant → Phase regions (areas) on the diagram.
- **F = 3 or more** → Requires more than 2 external variables — unrealistic in standard T-P space.
- **Allotropes = different phases, same component.** (e.g., Sᵣ and Sₘ are 2 phases, 1 component.)
- **Limitations:** Equilibrium only, T and P as only external variables, uniform T and P across phases, no electric/magnetic field effects.
- **Real systems to remember:** Water (1 component, 3 phases), Sulphur (1 component, 4 phases possible), CaCO₃ decomposition (2 components, 3 phases).
- **Sign of Negative F:** Thermodynamically forbidden at equilibrium.
- **Reduced phase rule** is used in **metallurgy** (alloy phase diagrams, solidification studies).
- **Ellingham diagrams and stability of metal oxides** are based on the same F = C − P + 2 logic applied to gas-solid equilibria.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Phase Diagrams

A **Phase Diagram** (also called an *equilibrium diagram* or *constitutional diagram*) is a graphical map that shows the **thermodynamically stable phases** of a material system as a function of **temperature**, **pressure**, and **composition**. It is the most fundamental tool in Materials Science for understanding how an alloy melts, solidifies, and transforms during processing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> *"A phase diagram is a graphical representation of the physical states (solid, liquid, gas) of a material under different conditions of temperature, pressure, and composition, showing the regions of phase stability and the boundaries where phase transformations occur."*

The word **phase** refers to a chemically and structurally homogeneous region of a system (e.g., liquid water, α-FCC iron, ice, β-BCC iron). A *component* (C) is a chemical species (e.g., Cu, Ni, H₂O), and a *system* may have one, two, three, or more components.

---

## 1.2 The Unary System (One Component, C = 1)

A **Unary Phase Diagram** plots **Pressure (P)** on the Y-axis against **Temperature (T)** on the X-axis for a *single component*. Since C = 1, the diagram is 2-dimensional.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Hiking Map of a Single Material"
> Imagine one pure chemical substance (like pure water or pure iron) trying to find which "state of matter region" it lives in as you change the weather (temperature) and the altitude of the atmosphere (pressure). The unary diagram is the topographic map of that journey — hills are gas, lowlands are liquids, and frozen peaks are solids.

### Key Features of a Unary Diagram

1. **Triple Point (Tₜ, Pₜ):** The unique (T, P) combination where **three phases coexist in equilibrium** (e.g., solid + liquid + gas for water at 0.01 °C, 0.006 atm).
2. **Critical Point (T_c, P_c):** Beyond this, the **liquid–gas boundary disappears**; above P_c, no distinct liquid/gas phases exist (supercritical fluid).
3. **Solid–Liquid Boundary:** Usually steep, almost vertical, since solidification pressure-dependence is weak.
4. **Solid–Gas (Sublimation) Boundary:** Below the triple point, solid sublimes directly to gas.
5. **Liquid–Gas (Vaporization) Boundary:** Ends at the critical point.

### Standard Examples
- **Water (H₂O):** Negative slope of solid–liquid line (ice melts under pressure — unique).
- **Iron (Fe):** Multiple solid phases (α-FCC, γ-FCC, δ-BCC, ε-HCP) at high P.
- **Carbon (C):** Graphite–diamond boundary.
- **Sulfur (S):** Two solid allotropes (rhombic & monoclinic).

> [!NOTE]
> **Gibbs Phase Rule for Unary System:**
> $$F = C - P + 2 = 1 - P + 2 = 3 - P$$
> - For single-phase region: F = 2 (T and P can vary independently).
> - Along a phase boundary: F = 1 (only one variable free).
> - At the triple point: F = 0 (invariant — fully fixed).

---

## 1.3 The Binary System (Two Components, C = 2)

A **Binary Phase Diagram** plots **Temperature (T)** on the Y-axis against **Composition (wt% or mol%)** on the X-axis, at *constant atmospheric pressure* (typically 1 atm). The X-axis endpoints represent the **two pure components** (0% and 100%).

$$F = C - P + 2 = 2 - P + 2 = 4 - P$$

For constant pressure (condensed system): $F' = C - P + 1 = 3 - P$

| Region Type | Phases (P) | Degrees of Freedom (F') | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single-phase field | 1 | 2 | T and composition can vary |
| Two-phase field (tie-line) | 2 | 1 | Either T or composition is fixed |
| Eutectic / Peritectic point | 3 | 0 | Invariant point (T and X are fixed) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Key Insight:** The constant-pressure binary diagram is a 2D slice (isobaric) of a full 3D T-P-X prism. We "slice off" the pressure dimension for convenience in metallurgy, since most solidification happens near 1 atm.

---

## 1.4 Isomorphous Systems — Definition

An **Isomorphous Binary System** is one in which the two components exhibit **complete mutual solid solubility in the solid state** — i.e., a single solid phase (α) exists across the entire composition range from 0 to 100%.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Salt-Water Analogy"
> Think of mixing red and blue ink. No matter the ratio, you always get *one* uniform liquid color (purple of varying shade). At any temperature, there's only **one liquid phase**. When it freezes, the ice (solid) that forms is also a single solid solution — not separate red-ice and blue-ice crystals. That's *isomorphous* behavior.

### Classic Examples
- **Copper–Nickel (Cu–Ni)** — the prototype taught worldwide.
- **Ge–Si, NiO–MgO, Fe–Cr, Cu–Pd, Au–Pt** — all exhibit full solid solubility.

---

## 1.5 Congruent Phase Transformations — Definition

A **Congruent Transformation** is a phase change in which:
- The composition of the new phase (or phases) is **identical to the composition of the parent phase**.
- No separation of composition occurs; the transformation is "composition-preserving."

The most common congruent transformations in binary diagrams are:
- **Congruent Melting:** Solid → Liquid of the *same* composition (e.g., pure Cu melting at its own composition).
- **Congruent Solid-State Transformation:** e.g., α → β of the same composition in some intermetallic systems.

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT confuse with *incongruent* (peritectic) transformations**, where the new solid phase has a *different* composition from the parent liquid, and a portion of the liquid is consumed to form a new solid + remaining liquid of different composition.

---

## 1.6 Why Study These Diagrams? (Engineering Relevance)

| Engineering Application | Phase Diagram Used |
|---|---|
| Casting & Foundry (Cu–Ni alloys, brasses) | Binary isomorphous / eutectic |
| Steel heat treatment (Fe–C) | Binary eutectic / eutectoid |
| Solder selection (Sn–Pb, SAC alloys) | Binary eutectic |
| Semiconductor wafer growth (Si–Ge) | Isomorphous |
| Ceramic powder sintering (NiO–MgO) | Isomorphous |
| Design of superalloys, brasses, bronzes | Cu–Ni, Cu–Zn |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Gibbs Phase Rule — The Governing Equation

**Josiah Willard Gibbs (1876)** formulated the universal rule:

$$F = C - P + 2$$

Where:
- **F** = Degrees of freedom (number of independent intensive variables: T, P, composition)
- **C** = Number of components
- **P** = Number of coexisting phases in equilibrium
- **2** = The two external variables: Temperature (T) and Pressure (P)

### For a Condensed System (Solid + Liquid only, P ≈ 1 atm):

$$F' = C - P + 1$$

| System | C | P | F' | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Unary single phase | 1 | 1 | 1 | T can vary (2D in P-T space) |
| Binary single phase | 2 | 1 | 2 | T and X both variable |
| Binary 2-phase region | 2 | 2 | 1 | One variable free along tie-line |
| Binary 3-phase invariant (eutectic) | 2 | 3 | 0 | T and X completely fixed |
| Ternary 3-phase | 3 | 3 | 1 | One degree of freedom |

---

## 2.2 Unary System — Detailed Analysis

The unary diagram is divided into **three primary fields** by three boundary lines meeting at the **triple point**:

```
    P ▲
      │       Solid
      │      ╱
      │     ╱       Liquid
      │    ╱      ╱
      │   ╱    ╱
      │  ╱   ╱
      │ ╱  ╱ Triple
      │╱ ╱   Point (Tₜ, Pₜ)
      │╳────────────────► T
      │ ╲
      │   ╲ Gas
      │     ╲________ Critical Point (Tc, Pc)
```

### Classification of Triple Points

| Material | Tₜ (°C) | Pₜ (atm) | Special Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| Water (H₂O) | 0.01 | 0.0060 | Negative slope of S/L line |
| Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) | −56.6 | 5.18 | Solid sublimes at 1 atm (dry ice) |
| Iron (Fe) | 1538 | ~1 (melting) | Multiple allotropes at high P |
| Sulfur (S) | 115 | 0.00003 | Two solid allotropes (rhombic ↔ monoclinic) |

---

## 2.3 Binary Isomorphous System — Anatomy

The **Cu–Ni phase diagram** is the textbook isomorphous system. Its key features:

| Label | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Liquidus line** (upper) | Above this line, alloy is **100% liquid**. First solid forms on cooling. |
| **Solidus line** (lower) | Below this line, alloy is **100% solid**. Last liquid solidifies on cooling. |
| **L + α two-phase region** (between liquidus and solidus) | Both liquid and α-solid coexist in equilibrium. |
| **α single-phase field** (below solidus) | Single solid solution (FCC for Cu–Ni) exists. |
| **Solvus line** (sometimes present, dashed) | Boundary below which a second solid phase may precipitate — but in *truly isomorphous* systems, this is absent. |

### Cooling of a Cu–40%Ni Alloy (Walk-Through)

Consider an alloy of composition **C₀ = 40 wt% Ni, 60 wt% Cu**, cooled slowly from 1300 °C:

1. **Above liquidus (~1250 °C):** Single-phase liquid (L). Composition of L = C₀.
2. **At liquidus (T_L ≈ 1250 °C):** First tiny **α-crystal** forms. Its composition is read off the **solidus** at T_L → C_α ≈ 70 wt% Ni (Ni-rich — higher melting component enriches the solid).
3. **Inside L + α field (e.g., 1220 °C):** Two phases coexist. Tie-line gives:
   - Composition of α (solid) = C_α (right end of tie-line on solidus)
   - Composition of L (liquid) = C_L (left end of tie-line on liquidus)
4. **At solidus (T_S ≈ 1190 °C):** Last drop of liquid (composition C_L ≈ 20 wt% Ni) solidifies. Solid now has overall composition C₀.
5. **Below solidus:** Single-phase α solid solution, uniform composition 40% Ni.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equilibrium Cooling Assumption:** At each temperature, the alloy is held long enough for complete diffusion to homogenize the solid. In **real (non-equilibrium) cooling**, *cored structures* form — alternating Ni-rich cores and Cu-rich rims within each grain. This is called **coring** and is a common exam point.

---

## 2.4 The Lever Rule — The Most Important Numerical Tool

When an alloy lies in a **two-phase region** at temperature T, with overall composition C₀, the **fraction of each phase** is calculated using the **Lever Rule** (also called the *reverse lever arm* or *material balance*).

### Statement:
> *The fraction of a phase is proportional to the length of the tie-line segment on the OPPOSITE side of the alloy composition from that phase.*

### Mathematical Form:

$$W_L = \frac{C_\alpha - C_0}{C_\alpha - C_L} \quad \text{(fraction of liquid)}$$

$$W_\alpha = \frac{C_0 - C_L}{C_\alpha - C_L} \quad \text{(fraction of solid } \alpha\text{)}$$

And it is always true that $W_L + W_\alpha = 1$.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory trick:** The fraction of the phase you want is on the **opposite arm**. If you want the fraction of **solid** (α), look at the **liquid arm** $(C_0 - C_L)$, and divide by the **total tie-line length** $(C_\alpha - C_L)$.

---

## 2.5 Congruent vs. Incongruent Transformations

| Property | Congruent | Incongruent (Peritectic) |
|---|---|---|
| Composition change | None — same composition before & after | Different compositions in parent and product |
| Reaction type | Reversible, one-phase ↔ one-phase | Liquid + Solid₁ ↔ Solid₂ |
| Invariant point type | Melting point / congruent point | Peritectic point |
| Example | Pure Cu melting, β → α in some intermetallics | Cu–Zn: L + ε → η' (brass peritectic) |
| Microstructure | Uniform, predictable | Often has peritectic rim structures |

### Congruent Melting in a Binary Phase Diagram
At the **congruent melting point (or congruent transformation point)**, the solid of composition C* transforms to a liquid of the **same** composition C* on heating, and vice versa on cooling. On the phase diagram, this appears as a **local maximum (or minimum)** on the solidus / liquidus where they meet at a single point of composition C*.

This is in contrast to **incongruent melting** where the solid breaks down to a different solid + liquid before fully melting.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbol | Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gibbs Phase Rule (general) | $F = C - P + 2$ | dimensionless | Always for equilibrium analysis |
| 2 | Condensed Phase Rule (1 atm) | $F' = C - P + 1$ | dimensionless | Metallurgical binary/ternary diagrams |
| 3 | Lever Rule — Liquid fraction | $W_L = \dfrac{C_\alpha - C_0}{C_\alpha - C_L}$ | fraction (0 to 1) | Inside any 2-phase region |
| 4 | Lever Rule — Solid fraction | $W_\alpha = \dfrac{C_0 - C_L}{C_\alpha - C_L}$ | fraction (0 to 1) | Inside any 2-phase region |
| 5 | Sum check | $W_L + W_\alpha = 1$ | — | Sanity check for lever rule |
| 6 | Triple point condition | $F = 0$ | — | Three phases coexist |
| 7 | Critical point | dP/dT (L–G) = 0 | — | End of L–G boundary |
| 8 | Clausius–Clapeyron (unary bdry) | $\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H}{T\,\Delta V}$ | atm/K | Slope of any unary boundary line |
| 9 | % to fraction | $W_\alpha = 0.40$ means 40 % | — | Always report as decimal in formulas |
| 10 | Coring parameter | $k = C_S/C_L$ (partition coeff.) | dimensionless | Non-equilibrium solidification |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-Exam Trick:** In the KTU exam, students often mistakenly use $C_0 - C_\alpha$ in the numerator for $W_L$. The correct numerator is **the composition of the OTHER phase minus $C_0$**. Memorize: *"opposite arm, opposite phase"*.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Foundry Practice:** Predicting casting temperature, shrinkage, and segregation. A foundry engineer picks pouring temperature **50–100 °C above the liquidus** for a given alloy composition.
2. **Alloy Design:** Choosing composition to obtain a specific microstructure (e.g., 70/30 brass vs. 90/10 brass for cartridge cases vs. radiator cores).
3. **Heat Treatment Design:** Determining homogenization temperatures above the solidus.
4. **Semiconductor Industry:** Si–Ge isomorphous system forms the basis of strained-silicon transistors and infrared detectors.
5. **Ceramic Processing:** NiO–MgO isomorphous system used in refractory bricks for furnace linings.
6. **Powder Metallurgy:** Understanding solidus prevents liquid-phase formation during sintering.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Lever Rule from Mass Balance

Let us derive the lever rule from **first principles** using conservation of mass.

### Setup
Consider a binary alloy of overall composition $C_0$ (in wt% of component B) lying inside a two-phase (L + α) region at temperature T. A horizontal **tie-line** intersects the liquidus at composition $C_L$ and the solidus at composition $C_\alpha$.

Let:
- Total mass of the alloy = $M$ (say 100 g for convenience)
- Mass of liquid phase = $M_L$
- Mass of α phase = $M_\alpha$

By definition of mass fractions:
$$W_L = \frac{M_L}{M}, \quad W_\alpha = \frac{M_\alpha}{M}$$

### Conservation of Mass (Total)
$$M_L + M_\alpha = M$$

Dividing by $M$:
$$W_L + W_\alpha = 1 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

### Conservation of Mass of Component B
The total mass of component B in the alloy must equal the sum of component B in each phase:

$$M \cdot C_0 = M_L \cdot C_L + M_\alpha \cdot C_\alpha \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

Dividing (ii) by $M$:

$$C_0 = W_L \cdot C_L + W_\alpha \cdot C_\alpha \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

### Solving the System
Substitute $W_L = 1 - W_\alpha$ from (i) into (iii):

$$C_0 = (1 - W_\alpha) \cdot C_L + W_\alpha \cdot C_\alpha$$

Expand:

$$C_0 = C_L - W_\alpha \cdot C_L + W_\alpha \cdot C_\alpha$$

$$C_0 = C_L + W_\alpha \cdot (C_\alpha - C_L)$$

Rearrange:

$$C_0 - C_L = W_\alpha \cdot (C_\alpha - C_L)$$

$$\boxed{W_\alpha = \frac{C_0 - C_L}{C_\alpha - C_L}}$$

By symmetry (or substituting back into (i)):

$$\boxed{W_L = \frac{C_\alpha - C_0}{C_\alpha - C_L}}$$

This is the **Lever Rule**, named because the tie-line acts as a "lever" with the **fulcrum at the alloy composition $C_0$**, and the **lever arms are the two segments** of the tie-line on either side.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1: Cu–40 wt% Ni at 1250 °C

**Given:** The Cu–Ni phase diagram. At 1250 °C, draw a tie-line in the L + α field. From the diagram:
- $C_L = 32$ wt% Ni (liquidus intersection)
- $C_\alpha = 45$ wt% Ni (solidus intersection)
- $C_0 = 40$ wt% Ni (alloy composition)

**Find:** Weight fractions of liquid and α at 1250 °C.

### Solution (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1:** Identify which side of the tie-line each phase occupies.
- $C_L = 32$ (left end — liquidus)
- $C_\alpha = 45$ (right end — solidus)
- $C_0 = 40$ (between 32 and 45) ✓ — alloy is in 2-phase region.

**Step 2:** Apply Lever Rule for solid (α):
$$W_\alpha = \frac{C_0 - C_L}{C_\alpha - C_L}$$

$$W_\alpha = \frac{40 - 32}{45 - 32} = \frac{8}{13}$$

$$W_\alpha = 0.615 = 61.5 \text{ wt\%}$$

**Step 3:** Apply Lever Rule for liquid (L):
$$W_L = \frac{C_\alpha - C_0}{C_\alpha - C_L} = \frac{45 - 40}{45 - 32} = \frac{5}{13}$$

$$W_L = 0.385 = 38.5 \text{ wt\%}$$

**Step 4:** Sanity check:
$$W_L + W_\alpha = 0.385 + 0.615 = 1.000 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5:** Physical interpretation — At 1250 °C, this Cu–40%Ni alloy consists of:
- **61.5% by weight** of α-solid (Ni-rich solid solution, 45% Ni)
- **38.5% by weight** of liquid (Cu-rich liquid, 32% Ni)

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2: Tie-Line Shift During Cooling (Cu–40%Ni)

Cooling the same alloy from 1250 °C to 1200 °C. At 1200 °C, the tie-line gives:
- $C_L = 24$ wt% Ni
- $C_\alpha = 42$ wt% Ni

**Find:** New phase fractions.

### Solution

$$W_\alpha(1200°C) = \frac{40 - 24}{42 - 24} = \frac{16}{18} = 0.889 = 88.9 \text{ wt\%}$$

$$W_L(1200°C) = \frac{42 - 40}{42 - 24} = \frac{2}{18} = 0.111 = 11.1 \text{ wt\%}$$

**Observation:** As temperature drops from 1250 → 1200 °C, the **solid fraction grew from 61.5% to 88.9%**, while the **liquid shrank from 38.5% to 11.1%**. This is consistent with continued solidification on cooling.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3: Solidification Range (Liquation)

For the Cu–40%Ni alloy:
- $T_L$ (liquidus temperature) = 1250 °C
- $T_S$ (solidus temperature) = 1190 °C

$$\text{Solidification Range} = T_L - T_S = 1250 - 1190 = 60 \text{ °C}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering meaning:** A wide solidification range promotes *segregation* and *coring* in castings. A narrow range gives more uniform, defect-free castings. Pure metals have a *zero* range.

---

## 3.5 Unary System — Clapeyron Slope Analysis for Water

The slope of the solid–liquid line for water is **negative** (unique among common substances). Using the **Clausius–Clapeyron equation**:

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{fus}}{T \cdot \Delta V_{fus}}$$

For water:
- $\Delta H_{fus} = 6.01 \text{ kJ/mol} = 6010 \text{ J/mol}$
- $T = 273.15 \text{ K}$
- $\Delta V_{fus} = V_{liquid} - V_{solid} = (18.78 - 20.0) \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol}$ (negative!)

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{6010}{273.15 \times (-1.22 \times 10^{-6})}$$

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{6010}{-3.332 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = -1.804 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa/K} = -178 \text{ atm/K}$$

This is a **large negative slope** — the unique property that makes ice skating possible (pressure-induced melting).

---

## 3.6 Congruent Melting — Verifying Composition Invariance

Consider a hypothetical intermetallic compound AB₃ that melts **congruently** at temperature $T_m$ to a liquid of the *same* composition.

On the phase diagram, this is shown as a **sharp local maximum on the solidus+liquidus** at composition $X_{AB_3}$ (e.g., 25% A, 75% B). The reaction is:

$$\text{AB}_3 \text{ (solid)} \xrightleftharpoons[\text{cooling}]{\text{heating}} \text{Liquid of composition } X_{AB_3}$$

Mass balance check at the congruent point:
$$C_{\text{solid}} = C_{\text{liquid}} = 0.75 \text{ (B fraction)} \quad \checkmark$$

During congruent transformation, since the compositions are equal, the lever rule gives a *discontinuous* jump in phase fraction — the alloy abruptly changes from 100% solid to 100% liquid (and vice versa) at exactly $T_m$ with no two-phase region at that composition.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Boundary Slopes at Invariant Points

| Invariant Point | Phases | F | Reaction | Congruent? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Melting point of pure A | S_A + L | 0 | S_A → L (or reverse) | Yes (pure A) |
| Melting point of pure B | S_B + L | 0 | S_B → L (or reverse) | Yes (pure B) |
| Congruent compound point | S_AB + L | 0 | S_AB ↔ L (same X) | **Yes** |
| Eutectic point | α + β + L | 0 | L → α + β on cooling | No (composition changes) |
| Peritectic point | α + β + L | 0 | L + α → β on cooling | No (incongruent) |
| Eutectoid point | α + β + γ | 0 | γ → α + β on cooling | No |
| Peritectoid point | α + β + γ | 0 | α + β → γ on cooling | No |
