# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the Gibbs Phase Rule. State the degrees of freedom for a unary system in (i) a single-phase region and (ii) at the triple point.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The Gibbs Phase Rule, formulated by J.W. Gibbs (1876), gives the number of degrees of freedom (F) — i.e., the number of independent intensive variables (T, P, composition) that can be changed without disturbing the number of phases in equilibrium:
> $$F = C - P + 2$$
> where C = number of components, P = number of coexisting phases.
>
> For a **unary system** (C = 1):
> - (i) **Single-phase region** (P = 1): $F = 1 - 1 + 2 = \mathbf{2}$ — both T and P can vary independently.
> - (ii) **Triple point** (P = 3): $F = 1 - 3 + 2 = \mathbf{0}$ — invariant point; T and P are completely fixed (e.g., water triple point at 0.01 °C, 0.006 atm).

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is an isomorphous phase diagram? Give two examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An **isomorphous phase diagram** is a binary phase diagram in which the two components exhibit **complete mutual solid solubility** in the solid state over the entire composition range, forming a **single solid solution phase (α)** across 0–100 % composition.
>
> **Two examples:**
> 1. **Copper–Nickel (Cu–Ni)** — the prototype textbook system; both have FCC crystal structure and similar atomic radii, allowing complete solubility.
> 2. **Germanium–Silicon (Ge–Si)** — both diamond-cubic semiconductors, used in infrared detectors and heterojunction transistors.
>
> *(Other acceptable examples: Au–Pt, NiO–MgO, Cu–Pd, Fe–Cr.)*

---

## PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **Cu–Ni isomorphous phase diagram**. Label all regions, the liquidus, solidus, and explain the phase changes occurring during the **equilibrium cooling of a Cu–40 wt% Ni alloy** from 1300 °C to room temperature. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Lever Rule** from first principles (mass balance) and apply it to determine the weight fractions of liquid and solid α at **1220 °C** for a **Cu–40 wt% Ni** alloy, given the tie-line at 1220 °C intersects the liquidus at $C_L = 28$ wt% Ni and the solidus at $C_\alpha = 43$ wt% Ni. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a):

**Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the Cu–Ni phase diagram:
- Y-axis: Temperature (°C), X-axis: wt% Ni (0% to 100%).
- Pure Cu melting point: 1085 °C; pure Ni melting point: 1455 °C.
- **Liquidus** (upper curve) connects 1085 °C to 1455 °C, bowing upward in the middle.
- **Solidus** (lower curve) connects 1085 °C to 1455 °C, bowing downward in the middle.
- Above liquidus: single-phase **Liquid (L)**.
- Between liquidus and solidus: two-phase **L + α** region.
- Below solidus: single-phase **α (FCC solid solution)**.

**Cooling Description of Cu–40%Ni alloy (4 marks):**
- **1300 °C → ~1250 °C:** All liquid.
- **At 1250 °C (liquidus crossing):** First crystals of α (Ni-rich, ~70% Ni) form.
- **1250 → 1190 °C (inside L + α):** Continued growth of α; composition of L and α shift along liquidus and solidus (peritectic-like tie-line movement). At any T, $C_L$ is on the left end of tie-line, $C_\alpha$ on the right.
- **At 1190 °C (solidus crossing):** Last drop of liquid (~20% Ni) solidifies.
- **1190 °C → RT:** Uniform single-phase α with 40% Ni throughout.

> Equilibrium cooling assumes **complete diffusion** in both liquid and solid states. In practice, coring occurs.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b):

**Derivation (4 marks):** Let $M$ = total mass, $M_L$ and $M_\alpha$ = masses of liquid and solid.

Total mass: $M_L + M_\alpha = M$ → $W_L + W_\alpha = 1$ ...(i)

Mass balance on Ni: $M \cdot C_0 = M_L \cdot C_L + M_\alpha \cdot C_\alpha$ → $C_0 = W_L \cdot C_L + W_\alpha \cdot C_\alpha$ ...(ii)

Substituting (i) into (ii):
$$C_0 = (1 - W_\alpha) C_L + W_\alpha C_\alpha = C_L + W_\alpha(C_\alpha - C_L)$$

$$\boxed{W_\alpha = \frac{C_0 - C_L}{C_\alpha - C_L}}$$

$$\boxed{W_L = \frac{C_\alpha - C_0}{C_\alpha - C_L}}$$

**Numerical Application (3 marks):**
- $C_0 = 40$, $C_L = 28$, $C_\alpha = 43$

$$W_\alpha = \frac{40 - 28}{43 - 28} = \frac{12}{15} = 0.800 = 80.0 \text{ wt\%}$$

$$W_L = \frac{43 - 40}{43 - 28} = \frac{3}{15} = 0.200 = 20.0 \text{ wt\%}$$

**Sanity check:** $0.800 + 0.200 = 1.000$ ✓

**Physical meaning:** At 1220 °C, the Cu–40%Ni alloy is 80% solid α and 20% liquid by weight.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper, Module 2 - Alternative]**

**(a)** Define a **Unary Phase Diagram**. Sketch and explain the **P–T diagram of water (H₂O)**, clearly marking the triple point, critical point, and all phase boundaries. Why is the solid–liquid boundary slope negative for water? Use the Clausius–Clapeyron equation to justify. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a **congruent phase transformation**? Compare and contrast congruent and incongruent transformations with suitable examples and sketches. Explain the engineering significance of congruent transformations in alloy design. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a):

**Definition (2 marks):** A unary phase diagram is a 2-dimensional graphical representation of the stable phases of a *single component* as a function of **Pressure (Y-axis)** and **Temperature (X-axis)**.

**Sketch of H₂O P–T diagram (3 marks):**
- Three primary fields: **Solid (ice)**, **Liquid (water)**, **Gas (steam)**.
- **Triple point** at T = 0.01 °C, P = 0.006 atm.
- **Critical point** at T = 374 °C, P = 218 atm.
- **Sublimation curve** (S–G) below triple point.
- **Vaporization curve** (L–G) between triple and critical point.
- **Fusion curve** (S–L) — slopes **backward (negative slope)** — unique to water.

**Negative slope justification (2 marks):** Using Clausius–Clapeyron:
$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{fus}}{T \cdot \Delta V_{fus}}$$

For water, $\Delta V_{fus} = V_{liquid} - V_{solid} < 0$ (water is denser than ice), making dP/dT **negative** — the fusion curve tilts slightly to the left with increasing P. This is why ice melts under pressure (skating / Regelation).

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b):

**Congruent transformation definition (2 marks):** A phase transformation is **congruent** if the composition of the new phase(s) is **identical** to the composition of the parent phase (composition-preserving). For example, melting of pure Cu at 1085 °C: S(Cu) → L(Cu), both at 100% Cu.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Property | Congruent | Incongruent |
|---|---|---|
| Composition change | None | Yes |
| Reaction type | S ↔ L (same X) | L + S₁ → S₂ |
| Invariant point | Melting point | Peritectic |
| Diagram feature | Local max/min of liquidus-solidus | Horizontal peritectic line |
| Examples | Pure metal melting; intermetallic AB₃ melting | Cu-Zn peritectic; Fe-C peritectic |

**Engineering significance (2 marks):**
- Congruent melting compounds (e.g., Ni₃Al, TiAl) are used as **single-crystal superalloys** for turbine blades — they melt uniformly without composition segregation.
- Easier to **cast and remelt** without peritectic reaction complications.
- Predictable **solidification behavior** in foundry and investment casting.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **Lever Rule sign error (loses ~2 marks):** Students often write $W_L = (C_0 - C_L)/(C_\alpha - C_L)$ — WRONG. The numerator must be the *opposite* arm: $(C_\alpha - C_0)$.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert wt% to fractions (loses ~1 mark):** Final answer must be reported as a decimal (0.385) OR a percentage (38.5%) consistently — not both.
> 3. **Confusing liquidus with solidus (loses ~2 marks):** The *liquidus* is the **upper** curve (where the first solid forms on cooling); the *solidus* is the **lower** curve (where the last liquid solidifies). Reverse these and the lever rule gives negative fractions.
> 4. **Gibbs Phase Rule formula error (loses ~1 mark):** Use $F = C - P + 2$ (general) or $F = C - P + 1$ (condensed, 1 atm) — don't mix. If the question says "atmospheric pressure," use the condensed form.
> 5. **Skipping the sanity check (loses ~1 mark):** Always verify $W_L + W_\alpha = 1$. Examiners reward this.
> 6. **Confusing congruent with eutectic (loses ~2 marks):** Congruent = same composition before/after. Eutectic = one liquid → two solids of *different* compositions. They are opposite concepts.
> 7. **Drawing P–T diagram with positive S/L slope for water (loses ~1 mark):** Water's solid–liquid line is famously NEGATIVE — drawing it positive is a mark-loser.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Gibbs Phase Rule** $F = C - P + 2$ — your master key for any phase equilibrium problem.
- [ ] **Unary systems** are 2D (P–T); only ONE component; triple point has $F = 0$.
- [ ] **Water's negative S–L slope** is unusual — caused by $\Delta V_{fus} < 0$ (ice less dense than water).
- [ ] **Binary diagrams** are usually plotted at constant 1 atm, so use condensed rule $F' = C - P + 1$.
- [ ] **Isomorphous = complete solid solubility** → ONE solid phase across all compositions (e.g., Cu–Ni).
- [ ] **Liquidus (upper) + Solidus (lower)** — liquidus is reached first on cooling; solidus is reached last.
- [ ] **Lever Rule** = mass balance; fraction of a phase = opposite arm / total tie-line length.
- [ ] **Congruent transformation** = same composition before and after; **incongruent** (peritectic) = different.
- [ ] **Coring** (non-equilibrium solidification) = alternating Ni-rich cores & Cu-rich rims; not seen under *true* equilibrium.
- [ ] **Solidification range** = $T_L - T_S$; wide range → segregation risk in castings.
- [ ] **Sanity check** $W_L + W_\alpha = 1$ after every lever-rule problem.
- [ ] **Invariant points** (eutectic, peritectic, congruent melting): always $F = 0$ — fixed T and X.
- [ ] **Hume-Rothery rules** (implicit) — isomorphous behavior requires similar atomic size, structure, valence, electronegativity.
- [ ] **Engineering use:** Cu–Ni for coinage, marine fittings; Si–Ge for semiconductors; NiO–MgO for refractories.

### Mnemonics

- **"S**olidus **F**irst **on H**eating" — No, the **liquidus** is reached first on *heating* (last solid to melt). On *cooling*, **liquidus is reached first** (first solid to form).
- **"L**iquidus = **L**ine where **L**iquid dies" (on cooling) — when you cross liquidus going down, liquid starts to disappear.
- **"L**ever is **L**ong arm = **L**iquid" — the longer the arm from fulcrum, the bigger the fraction of the phase on the *opposite* side.

### Final KTU Mantra for Module 2
> *"Master the phase rule, master the lever rule, master the cooling curve — and you master Module 2."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tie Line - Lever Rule - Eutectic, Peritectic, Eutectoid and Peritectoid Reactions - Typical Phase Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBPET304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Tie Line - Lever Rule - Eutectic, Peritectic, Eutectoid and Peritectoid Reactions - Typical Phase Diagrams # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Tie Line (Isotherm / Conode) A **Tie Line** (also called an **isotherm** or **conode**) is an **isothermal horizontal line** drawn within a **two-phase region** of a binary phase diagram. Its two ends touch the **phase boundary curves** (solvus lines) on either side, thereby identifying the **equilibrium compositions of the two coexisting phases** at that specific temperature. Formally, at constant temperature $T_0$ and overall alloy composition $C_0$, the tie line connects: - Point A on the $\alpha$-phase boundary → composition $C_\alpha$ - Point B on the $\beta$-phase boundary → composition $C_\beta$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A tie line exists **only inside a two-phase field**. You CANNOT draw a tie line in a single-phase region (the composition is identical at every point) or at an invariant point. ## 1.2 The Lever Rule (Material Balance Equation) The **Lever Rule** is a geometric construction used to determine the **relative mass (or mole) fractions** of the two phases present in equilibrium at a given temperature, for an alloy of known overall composition. $$\boxed{F_\alpha = \frac{C_\beta - C_0}{C_\beta - C_\alpha} \quad ; \quad F_\beta = \frac{C_0 - C_\alpha}{C_\beta - C_\alpha}}$$ where $F_\alpha + F_\beta = 1$. > [!IMPORTANT] > The fractions are proportional to the lengths of the **opposite** segments of the tie line — hence the analogy with a **mechanical lever in balance**. ## 1.3 The Four Invariant Three-Phase Reactions These are reactions involving **three phases coexisting at a single invariant point** (fixed $T$ and fixed composition). They are central to KTU Module 2. | Reaction Type | Generic Form | Meaning | |---|---|---| | **Eutectic** | $L \rightarrow \alpha + \beta$ | Liquid freezes into two distinct solids on cooling | | **Peritectic** | $L + \beta \rightarrow \alpha$ | Liquid reacts with a solid to form a new solid on cooling | | **Eutectoid** | $\gamma \rightarrow \alpha + \beta$ | One solid transforms into two new solids on cooling | | **Peritectoid** | $\alpha + \beta \rightarrow \gamma$ | Two solids react to form a new solid on cooling | ## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Juice Bottle" Intuition Imagine a sealed bottle of mixed fruit juice where mango and orange layers have just separated. The **horizontal line** at the boundary is the tie line. The mango phase is rich in mango ($C_\alpha$), the orange phase is rich in orange ($C_\beta$). The **length of the lever arm on the opposite side** tells you how much of the *other* layer is present — just like a seesaw in a children's playground. The **eutectic** is the exact temperature below which the bottle splits into two pure layers (no mixture), the **peritectic** is when one of the layers *consumes* the other to form a new one, and the **eutectoid/peritectoid** are the same reactions but happening entirely inside the **solid state** (both reactants and products are solids — no liquid). > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric Used:** Phase diagram temperatures are reported in **°C** or **K**; compositions in **wt %** (engineering) or **at %** (scientific). Conversion: $X$ wt% of A = $\dfrac{X/M_A}{X/M_A + (100-X)/M_B} \times 100$ at%. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Underlying Thermodynamic Foundation A phase diagram is essentially a **map of minimum Gibbs free energy regions** at equilibrium. The boundaries (phase boundary curves / solvus lines) represent loci where the **chemical potentials** $\mu_i$ of each component are equal in the two adjoining phases: $$\mu_i^\alpha(T, C) = \mu_i^\beta(T, C) \quad \text{at the } \alpha/(\alpha+\beta) \text{ boundary}$$ This is why two phases of *different* compositions can coexist at the same temperature — and why we need a tie line to "connect" them. ## 2.2 The Lever Rule — Logical Derivation Steps Consider an alloy of mass $M$ with overall composition $C_0$ at temperature $T_0$ inside the $(\alpha + \beta)$ two-phase region. **Step 1 — Mass conservation of the solute (B atoms):** The total mass of B in the alloy must equal the sum of B in the $\alpha$ phase and B in the $\beta$ phase. **Step 2 — Express total B in terms of fractions:** Using $F_\alpha$ and $F_\beta$ as the mass fractions of the $\alpha$ and $\beta$ phases. **Step 3 — Substitute and solve simultaneously** with the constraint $F_\alpha + F_\beta = 1$. **Step 4 — Identify the geometric meaning** — the length $L_\alpha = C_0 - C_\alpha$ is the lever arm opposite to phase $\alpha$, and $L_\beta = C_\beta - C_0$ is the lever arm opposite to phase $\beta$. The fraction is proportional to the **opposite arm length**. ## 2.3 The Eutectic Reaction — In-Depth $$L \;\xrightarrow{\text{cooling}}\; \alpha + \beta \quad \text{at } T = T_E, \; C = C_E$$ - **Eutectic composition $C_E$** and **Eutectic temperature $T_E$** are unique values for the system. - The reaction proceeds **isothermally** (latent heat is evolved at constant $T$). - The resulting microstructure is a characteristic **alternating lamellar (plate-like) structure** — classic example: **Pb-Sn solder** at 61.9 wt% Sn, 183 °C. ## 2.4 The Peritectic Reaction — In-Depth $$L + \beta \;\xrightarrow{\text{cooling}}\; \alpha \quad \text{at } T = T_P, \; C = C_P$$ - On cooling, the **liquid** and the **preexisting solid $\beta$** react at the peritectic temperature to form a **new solid $\alpha$**. - Classic example: **Cu-Zn system** at 590 °C, ~36.5 wt% Zn ($L + \epsilon \rightarrow \eta$). - Microstructurally, the $\alpha$ phase tends to **encase the $\beta$ phase** because $\alpha$ nucleates at the L/$\beta$ interface — a phenomenon called **peritectic encasement**. ## 2.5 The Eutectoid Reaction — In-Depth $$\gamma \;\xrightarrow{\text{cooling}}\; \alpha + \beta \quad \text{(all solid-state)}$$ - Both parent and product phases are solids — this is a **solid-state invariant reaction**. - Most commercially important example: **Fe-C system** — Austenite ($\gamma$-Fe) → Ferrite ($\alpha$-Fe) + Cementite (Fe$_3$C) at 727 °C, 0.76 wt% C. - Produces the famous **lamellar Pearlite microstructure** (alternating $\alpha$ + Fe$_3$C plates). ## 2.6 The Peritectoid Reaction — In-Depth $$\alpha + \beta \;\xrightarrow{\text{cooling}}\; \gamma \quad \text{(all solid-state)}$$ - Two solid parent phases combine to form a new solid phase. - Less common than the previous three; appears in **Cu-Sn, Cu-Zn**, and the **Fe-C** system (δ-ferrite + liquid → γ-austenite is peritectic; α + Fe$_3$C → γ does not occur, but Cu-Sn shows true peritectoid). ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Concept | Equation / Rule | Units | Validity | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Mass fraction of $\alpha$ | $F_\alpha = \dfrac{C_\beta - C_0}{C_\beta - C_\alpha}$ | dimensionless | Two-phase region only | | 2 | Mass fraction of $\beta$ | $F_\beta = \dfrac{C_0 - C_\alpha}{C_\beta - C_\alpha}$ | dimensionless | Two-phase region only | | 3 | Sum check | $F_\alpha + F_\beta = 1$ | dimensionless | Always | | 4 | Eutectic | $L \rightarrow \alpha + \beta$ | — | $T = T_E$, $C = C_E$ | | 5 | Peritectic | $L + \beta \rightarrow \alpha$ | — | $T = T_P$, $C = C_P$ | | 6 | Eutectoid | $\gamma \rightarrow \alpha + \beta$ | — | $T = T_{Ed}$, $C = C_{Ed}$ | | 7 | Peritectoid | $\alpha + \beta \rightarrow \gamma$ | — | $T = T_{Pd}$, $C = C_{Pd}$ | | 8 | wt% → at% | $C_{at,A} = \dfrac{C_{wt,A}/M_A}{C_{wt,A}/M_A + (100-C_{wt,A})/M_B}\cdot 100$ | at% | Conversion | | 9 | Phase fraction at $C_0$ using lengths | $F_\alpha = \dfrac{L_{\beta}}{L_{\alpha}+L_{\beta}}$ | dimensionless | Geometry | | 10 | Gibbs phase rule (binary) | $F = C - P + 1 = 3 - P$ | dimensionless | At constant pressure | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL KTU Trick:** In a binary diagram at atmospheric pressure, $F = 3 - P$. At an invariant point (eutectic/peritectic/eutectoid/peritectoid), $P = 3$, hence $F = 0$ — i.e., **temperature and composition are BOTH fixed**. This is why the reaction is isothermal. ## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Pb-Sn eutectic solder** is used in electronics because the 183 °C eutectic is the **lowest melting point** of the system — minimizing thermal damage to components. - **Fe-C eutectoid (Pearlite)** dictates the mechanical properties of plain-carbon steels through heat treatment. - **Lever rule** allows metallurgists to predict phase fractions quantitatively from a diagram, critical for alloy design and property estimation. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Lever Rule Consider a binary A–B alloy of total mass $M$ at temperature $T_0$ inside the $(\alpha + \beta)$ two-phase field. Let the overall composition be $C_0$ wt% B. **Step 1 — Define phase fractions.** Let $F_\alpha$ and $F_\beta$ be the mass fractions of the $\alpha$ and $\beta$ phases respectively. $$F_\alpha + F_\beta = 1 \quad \text{...(i)}$$ **Step 2 — Conservation of mass of solute B.** The total mass of B in the alloy equals the sum of B in each phase. $$M \cdot C_0 = (M \cdot F_\alpha) \cdot C_\alpha + (M \cdot F_\beta) \cdot C_\beta$$ **Step 3 — Cancel $M$ from all terms.** $$C_0 = F_\alpha \cdot C_\alpha + F_\beta \cdot C_\beta \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute $F_\beta = 1 - F_\alpha$ from (i) into (ii).** $$C_0 = F_\alpha C_\alpha + (1 - F_\alpha) C_\beta$$ $$C_0 = F_\alpha C_\alpha + C_\beta - F_\alpha C_\beta$$ **Step 5 — Collect $F_\alpha$ terms on one side.** $$C_0 - C_\beta = F_\alpha (C_\alpha - C_\beta)$$ **Step 6 — Solve for $F_\alpha$.** $$F_\alpha = \frac{C_0 - C_\beta}{C_\alpha - C_\beta} = \frac{C_\beta - C_0}{C_\beta - C_\alpha} \quad \text{...(iii)}$$ **Step 7 — By symmetry (or by $1 - F_\alpha$).** $$F_\beta = \frac{C_0 - C_\alpha}{C_\beta - C_\alpha} \quad \text{...(iv)}$$ **Step 8 — Geometric interpretation.** Define lever arm lengths on the tie line: $$L_\alpha = C_0 - C_\alpha \quad \text{(arm opposite to } \beta\text{)}$$ $$L_\beta = C_\beta - C_0 \quad \text{(arm opposite to } \alpha\text{)}$$ Therefore: $$F_\alpha = \frac{L_\beta}{L_\alpha + L_\beta} \quad ; \quad F_\beta = \frac{L_\alpha}{L_\alpha + L_\beta}$$ The phase fraction is proportional to the **opposite** lever arm — exactly like a balanced seesaw. ## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Lead-Tin (Pb-Sn) Eutectic System **Problem:** A Pb-30 wt% Sn alloy is slowly cooled from the liquid state. Determine: (a) The phases present at 200 °C. (b) The mass fraction of each phase. (c) The mass fraction of Sn-rich phase (β) at 150 °C. **Given Eutectic data:** $T_E$ = 183 °C, $C_E$ = 61.9 wt% Sn. **Solid solubilities (approx):** At 200 °C, $\alpha$ (Pb-rich) holds ~18 wt% Sn, $\beta$ (Sn-rich) holds ~97 wt% Sn. At 150 °C, $\alpha$ holds ~11 wt% Sn, $\beta$ holds ~99 wt% Sn. ### Part (a) and (b): At 200 °C The alloy is in the $(\alpha + \beta)$ two-phase region. Draw a horizontal tie line at 200 °C between $C_\alpha = 18$ and $C_\beta = 97$ wt% Sn. The overall composition is $C_0 = 30$ wt% Sn. $$F_\alpha = \frac{C_\beta - C_0}{C_\beta - C_\alpha} = \frac{97 - 30}{97 - 18} = \frac{67}{79}$$ $$F_\alpha = 0.8481 \approx 84.81 \% \quad (\text{Pb-rich phase})$$ $$F_\beta = 1 - 0.8481 = 0.1519 \approx 15.19 \% \quad (\text{Sn-rich phase})$$ ### Part (c): At 150 °C $$F_\beta = \frac{C_0 - C_\alpha}{C_\beta - C_\alpha} = \frac{30 - 11}{99 - 11} = \frac{19}{88}$$ $$F_\beta = 0.2159 \approx 21.59 \%$$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Quick-Reference Reaction Identifier | Diagnostic Feature on Diagram | Reaction | |---|---| | Horizontal line with a **valley** (V-shape opening downward) in liquidus | **Eutectic** | | Horizontal line with an **inverted V** (peak of liquidus meeting solidus) | **Peritectic** | | Horizontal line **below the solidus**, with V-shape between two solid solvi | **Eutectoid** | | Horizontal line **below the solidus**, with inverted V between two solid solvi | **Peritectoid** | | Slope of liquidus at invariant point | **Negative (eutectic) vs. Positive on at least one side (peritectic)** | ## 3.4 Worked Example — Eutectoid Reaction in Fe-C System **Problem:** A plain-carbon steel containing 0.4 wt% C is slowly cooled from 1000 °C to room temperature. Determine the phases and their mass fractions just above and just below the eutectoid temperature (727 °C). **At 750 °C (just above eutectoid):** Alloy is in $(\alpha + \gamma)$ region. Approximate solubilities: $C_\alpha \approx 0.02$ wt% C, $C_\gamma \approx 0.80$ wt% C. $$F_\alpha = \frac{C_\gamma - C_0}{C_\gamma - C_\alpha} = \frac{0.80 - 0.40}{0.80 - 0.02} = \frac{0.40}{0.78} = 0.5128 \approx 51.28 \%$$ $$F_\gamma = 1 - 0.5128 = 0.4872 \approx 48.72 \%$$ **At 700 °C (just below eutectoid):** Alloy is in $(\alpha + \text{Fe}_3\text{C})$ region. Approximate solubilities: $C_\alpha \approx 0.02$ wt% C, $C_{Fe_3C} = 6.67$ wt% C (stoichiometric, fixed). $$F_\alpha = \frac{6.67 - 0.40}{6.67 - 0.02} = \frac{6.27}{6.65} = 0.9429 \approx 94.29 \%$$ $$F_{Fe_3C} = 1 - 0.9429 = 0.0571 \approx 5.71 \%$$ > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** The proeutectoid ferrite ($\alpha$) fraction increases sharply across the eutectoid because the $\gamma$ phase has transformed into pearlite, which is itself a mixture of fine $\alpha$ + Fe$_3$C lamellae. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Tie Line Construction Logic (Generic Binary Phase Diagram) ```mermaid graph LR A1["Pure A
100% A"] -->|"Liquidus L1"| B1["Point M
on liquidus
C = C_L"] B1 -->|"Tie line at T0
horizontal isotherm"| C1["Point N
on solidus
C = C_alpha"] C1 -->|"Solidus L2"| D1["Pure B
100% B"] E1["Overall alloy
composition C0
at temperature T0"] -.->|"located between
M and N"| B1 E1 -.->|"Lever arm L_beta"| B1 E1 -.->|"Lever arm L_alpha"| C1 ``` ## 4.2 Eutectic Phase Diagram — Schematic (Pb-Sn type) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "EUTECTIC REACTION: L = alpha + beta at TE = 183 C, CE = 61.9 wt% Sn" A2["100% Pb
Melting 327 C"] -->|"Liquidus
decreases"| B2["Eutectic Point E
T = 183 C
C = 61.9 wt% Sn"] B2 -->|"Liquidus
decreases"| C2["100% Sn
Melting 232 C"] B2 -->|"alpha solvus
boundary"| D2["alpha phase boundary"] B2 -->|"beta solvus
boundary"| E2["beta phase boundary"] F2["Two-phase
region: L + alpha"] -.-> B2 G2["Two-phase
region: L + beta"] -.-> B2 H2["Two-phase
region: alpha + beta"] -.-> B2 end ``` ## 4.3 Peritectic Phase Diagram — Schematic (Cu-Zn type) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "PERITECTIC REACTION: L + beta = alpha at TP, CP" A3["100% Cu
Melting 1085 C"] -->|"Liquidus L"| B3["Peritectic Point P
T = TP
C = CP"] C3["beta phase
region"] -->|"at TP, C_beta"| B3 B3 -->|"peritectic
horizontal"| D3["alpha phase
region"] E3["100% Zn"] -.-> C3 end ``` ## 4.4 Eutectoid Phase Diagram — Schematic (Fe-C type, Iron-rich corner) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "EUTECTOID REACTION: gamma = alpha + Fe3C at 727 C, 0.76 wt% C" A4["delta Ferrite
delta region"] -->|"A4 to gamma"| B4["Austenite gamma
gamma region"] B4 -->|"cool to 727 C"| C4["Eutectoid Point S
T = 727 C
C = 0.76 wt% C"] C4 -->|"alpha solvus"| D4["alpha Ferrite
C_alpha approx 0.02 wt% C"] C4 -->|"cementite line
Fe3C at 6.67 wt% C"| E4["Cementite Fe3C
C = 6.67 wt% C"] C4 -->|"transformation"| F4["Pearlite
lamellar alpha + Fe3C"] end ``` ## 4.5 Peritectoid Phase Diagram — Schematic ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "PERITECTOID REACTION: alpha + beta = gamma at TPd, CPd" A5["alpha phase
region"] -->|"at TPd, C_alpha"| P5["Peritectoid Point Pd
T = TPd
C = CPd"] B5["beta phase
region"] -->|"at TPd, C_beta"| P5 P5 -->|"peritectoid
horizontal"| G5["gamma phase
newly formed"] end ``` ## 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Lever Rule Application Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["STEP 1
Identify alloy composition C0
and temperature T0"] --> S2{"STEP 2
Locate C0, T0 on phase diagram.
Single or two-phase region?"} S2 -->|"Single phase"| S3["STEP 3A
100 percent of that phase
No lever rule needed"] S2 -->|"Two phase"| S4["STEP 3B
Draw horizontal tie line
at T0 across two-phase field"] S4 --> S5["STEP 4
Read boundary compositions
C_alpha and C_beta at tie line ends"] S5 --> S6["STEP 5
Compute lever arm lengths
L_alpha = C0 - C_alpha
L_beta = C_beta - C0"] S6 --> S7["STEP 6
Apply lever rule
F_alpha = L_beta / L_total
F_beta = L_alpha / L_total"] S7 --> S8["STEP 7
Verify F_alpha + F_beta = 1
Report phase fractions"] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2] **Define a Tie Line. Explain its significance in interpreting a binary phase diagram.** **Model Answer:** A **tie line** (or isotherm/conode) is a horizontal line drawn at constant temperature within a **two-phase region** of a binary phase diagram, connecting the compositions of the two equilibrium phases in contact with each other. Its **significance** is threefold: 1. It identifies the **equilibrium compositions** of the two phases coexisting at that temperature (i.e., the **solvus boundary compositions** $C_\alpha$ and $C_\beta$). 2. It enables application of the **Lever Rule** to calculate the relative amounts of the two phases. 3. It is constructed **only inside a two-phase field** — no tie line exists in a single-phase region or strictly at an invariant three-phase point. **(3 marks)** ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2] **Distinguish between a eutectic and a peritectic reaction with one example each.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Eutectic | Peritectic | |---|---|---| | Generic form | $L \rightarrow \alpha + \beta$ | $L + \beta \rightarrow \alpha$ | | Slope of liquidus | Both arms decrease toward invariant point | At least one arm increases | | Products on cooling | Two new solids from one liquid | One new solid from liquid + old solid | | Example | **Pb-Sn** at 183 °C, 61.9 wt% Sn | **Cu-Zn** at 590 °C, 36.5 wt% Zn | | Microstructure | Lamellar / fine alternating plates | New phase encases the old solid phase | **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2] **(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **eutectic phase diagram** of the **Pb-Sn system**. Label the key points, fields, and the eutectic composition. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A **Pb-50 wt% Sn** alloy is slowly cooled from 350 °C to 50 °C. Determine: - (i) The **liquidus temperature**. - (ii) The phases present and their **mass fractions at 200 °C**. - (iii) The phases present and their **mass fractions at 150 °C** (just above eutectoid analogue, but Pb-Sn has no eutectoid — confirm two-phase equilibrium). **Given data (approximate solvus values):** | Temperature | $C_\alpha$ (wt% Sn in Pb) | $C_\beta$ (wt% Sn in Sn-rich) | |---|---|---| | 200 °C | 18 | 97 | | 150 °C | 11 | 99 | | Liquidus at 50 wt% Sn | ~245 °C (approx) | — | #### Model Solution for (a) — Eutectic Pb-Sn Diagram Description The Pb-Sn system exhibits a classic **eutectic reaction**: $$L \;(61.9 \text{ wt\% Sn}) \;\xrightarrow{183\,°C}\; \alpha (\text{Pb-rich}) + \beta (\text{Sn-rich})$$ **Key features to label:** - **Liquidus lines**: Two curves descending from the melting points of pure Pb (327 °C) and pure Sn (232 °C) meeting at the eutectic point E. - **Solidus lines**: The solvus curves defining the solid solubility limits of Sn in Pb ($\alpha$) and Pb in Sn ($\beta$). - **Eutectic point E**: $T_E = 183$ °C, $C_E = 61.9$ wt% Sn, $C_E = 38.1$ wt% Pb. - **Three two-phase fields**: $(L + \alpha)$, $(L + \beta)$, $(\alpha + \beta)$. - **Three single-phase fields**: $L$, $\alpha$, $\beta$. - **Eutectic microstructure**: Fine alternating lamellae of $\alpha$ and $\beta$. #### Model Solution for (b) — Numerical Phase Fractions **(i) Liquidus temperature at 50 wt% Sn:** From the standard Pb-Sn phase diagram, the liquidus temperature for a 50 wt% Sn alloy lies between 183 °C (eutectic) and 232 °C (pure Sn melting). Reading from the diagram: $T_{liquidus} \approx 245$ °C (interpolated from tabulated Pb-Sn data: at 50 wt% Sn, $T_{liq} \approx 244$ °C). **(ii) At 200 °C — in the $(\alpha + \beta)$ field:** The alloy has already crossed the eutectic isotherm at 183 °C. The microstructure at 200 °C (above eutectic) actually lies in the $(\alpha + L)$ region for compositions between $\sim$18 wt% Sn and the eutectic composition. However, since 50 wt% Sn > 19.5 wt% Sn (the solubility limit of $\alpha$ at 183 °C), the alloy at 200 °C is in the **$(\alpha + L)$ two-phase region** for temperatures between $T_{liquidus}$ and $T_E$. If we interpret the problem as asking for the state **at 200 °C above the eutectic**, then the alloy is in the $(\alpha + L)$ region with: - $C_\alpha \approx 18$ wt% Sn - $C_L \approx ?$ — read from liquidus at 200 °C (≈ 54 wt% Sn from Pb-Sn data). $$F_\alpha = \frac{C_L - C_0}{C_L - C_\alpha} = \frac{54 - 50}{54 - 18} = \frac{4}{36} = 0.1111 \approx 11.11\%$$ $$F_L = 1 - 0.1111 = 0.8889 \approx 88.89\%$$ **Alternatively**, at 150 °C (below the eutectic), the alloy lies in the $(\alpha + \beta)$ field. The eutectic constituent (transformed liquid) is part of the microstructure; for a hypereutectic 50 wt% Sn alloy, we must account for the **proeutectic $\beta$** (Sn-rich dendrites) and the **eutectic mixture**. $$F_\beta = \frac{C_0 - C_E}{C_\beta - C_E} = \frac{50 - 18}{99 - 18} = \frac{32}{81} = 0.3951 \approx 39.51\%$$ $$F_{eutectic} = 1 - 0.3951 = 60.49\%$$ (Here we used 18 wt% Sn as the maximum solubility in $\alpha$ and assumed $C_\beta = 99$ wt% Sn.) ### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2] **(a)** Define the **lever rule**. With a suitable diagram, derive the lever rule for a binary A–B alloy lying in the $(\alpha + \beta)$ two-phase region. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What are the **four invariant three-phase reactions** in binary phase diagrams? Write the generic form, name an example system for each, and state the temperature and composition of occurrence. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) — Lever Rule Derivation *(See the exhaustive derivation in Section 3.1 above. The student must draw a phase diagram, label the tie line, mark $C_\alpha$, $C_\beta$, $C_0$, and derive:)* $$F_\alpha = \frac{C_\beta - C_0}{C_\beta - C_\alpha} = \frac{L_\beta}{L_\alpha + L_\beta}$$ $$F_\beta = \frac{C_0 - C_\alpha}{C_\beta - C_\alpha} = \frac{L_\alpha}{L_\alpha + L_\beta}$$ The phase fraction is proportional to the **opposite** lever arm length. (7 marks distributed as: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for diagram, 4 marks for derivation) #### Model Solution for (b) — The Four Invariant Reactions | # | Reaction | Generic Form | Example System | Temperature | Composition | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Eutectic** | $L \rightarrow \alpha + \beta$ | **Pb-Sn** | 183 °C | 61.9 wt% Sn | | 2 | **Peritectic** | $L + \beta \rightarrow \alpha$ | **Cu-Zn** | 590 °C | ~36.5 wt% Zn | | 3 | **Eutectoid** | $\gamma \rightarrow \alpha + \beta$ | **Fe-C** | 727 °C | 0.76 wt% C | | 4 | **Peritectoid** | $\alpha + \beta \rightarrow \gamma$ | **Cu-Sn** | ~587 °C | ~25 wt% Sn | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing eutectoid with eutectic** — students often write "L → α + β" for both. Remember: **eutectoid = solid → two solids**; **eutectic = liquid → two solids**. > 2. **Forgetting to verify the phase rule** at invariant points: $F = 0$ at three-phase equilibrium, which is why the reaction is isothermal. > 3. **Sign errors in lever rule** — the numerator is always the **far-end** composition minus $C_0$. A frequent error is $(C_0 - C_\alpha)$ placed in the wrong position. > 4. **Drawing tie lines in single-phase regions** — this is geometrically meaningless and earns **zero** marks. > 5. **Mixing up peritectic and peritectoid** — both are "+" reactions; the difference is whether one reactant is **liquid** (peritectic) or **both are solid** (peritectoid). > 6. **Not specifying units** in numerical answers — always state wt% or at% explicitly. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Tie Line** = horizontal isothermal line inside a **two-phase field only**; endpoints are solvus boundary compositions $C_\alpha$ and $C_\beta$. - **Lever Rule** gives mass (or mole) fraction of each phase; the fraction is proportional to the **opposite** lever arm length. - **Eutectic** $L \rightarrow \alpha + \beta$ at fixed $T_E$, $C_E$ — example: **Pb-Sn at 183 °C, 61.9 wt% Sn**. - **Peritectic** $L + \beta \rightarrow \alpha$ at fixed $T_P$, $C_P$ — example: **Cu-Zn at 590 °C, ~36.5 wt% Zn**. - **Eutectoid** $\gamma \rightarrow \alpha + \beta$ (solid-state) — example: **Fe-C at 727 °C, 0.76 wt% C → Pearlite**. - **Peritectoid** $\alpha + \beta \rightarrow \gamma$ (solid-state) — example: **Cu-Sn at ~587 °C, ~25 wt% Sn**. - All four are **invariant three-phase reactions** in a binary system at constant pressure → $F = 0$ per the **Gibbs phase rule** ($F = 3 - P$, with $P = 3$). - **Pb-Sn eutectic** produces a characteristic **lamellar microstructure** used in solder. - **Fe-C eutectoid** (Pearlite) governs **plain-carbon steel heat treatment** — austempering, normalizing, annealing all rely on this reaction. - **Peritectic encasement** is a typical microstructural feature where the new $\alpha$ phase surrounds the residual $\beta$ phase. - **Lever rule** cannot be applied at the exact invariant point itself (three phases coexist — the lever rule is strictly for **two-phase** equilibrium). - For **hypoeutectic** alloys (e.g., < 61.9 wt% Sn in Pb-Sn): proeutectic $\alpha$ + eutectic mixture. - For **hypereutectic** alloys (e.g., > 61.9 wt% Sn in Pb-Sn): proeutectic $\beta$ + eutectic mixture. - **Composition scale** is conventionally **wt%** in engineering phase diagrams and **at%** in scientific/compound-energy diagrams — always check the axis label. - The **eutectic reaction** is the only one of the four that yields a fine, regular lamellar structure under equilibrium cooling — recognizing this microstructure in metallography is a frequent KTU lab-viva question.

topics.title: Fe-Fe3C System - Cu-Zn System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBPET304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Fe-Fe3C System - Cu-Zn System # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Fe-Fe₃C Phase Diagram (Iron-Iron Carbide System) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** > The **Fe-Fe₃C phase diagram** is a *binary equilibrium phase diagram* that maps the stable phases, phase boundaries, and invariant reactions occurring in alloys of iron and carbon (or iron and iron carbide, Fe₃C, also called *cementite*) over a temperature range from ambient to the liquidus. It is the single most important metallurgical diagram in ferrous metallurgy, governing the design, heat treatment, and microstructural engineering of every commercial **steel** and **cast iron**. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of it like a "Recipe Map" for Steel:** > Imagine you are baking a cake. The Fe-Fe₃C diagram is the *master recipe chart* that tells you: > 1. **What structure (phase)** will form when you mix a specific amount of carbon (sugar) with iron (flour). > 2. **At what temperature** the structure will change (e.g., from solid batter to molten mix). > 3. **At what exact temperatures** the mixture will undergo magical transformations (like eutectoid reaction — where one solid simultaneously splits into two different solids, similar to how a single solid mixture crystallizes into two distinct crystals at a precise temperature). > > Low carbon (≤ **0.022 wt% C**) = "Bland cake" → very soft *ferrite*. > High carbon (≈ **0.77 wt% C**) = "Special cake" → forms alternating layers of *ferrite* and *cementite* called *pearlite*. > Very high carbon (> **2.14 wt% C**) = "Cement cake" → *cast iron*, brittle but hard. ### Key Components of the Fe-Fe₃C System | Component | Composition | Description | |---|---|---| | **Fe (α-Ferrite parent)** | Pure Iron | Body-Centered Cubic (BCC) base metal, melting point **1538 °C** | | **Fe₃C (Cementite)** | 6.67 wt% C | Intermetallic compound, orthorhombic, extremely hard & brittle | ### Key Phases Present - **α-Ferrite (α-Fe):** BCC solid solution of C in iron. Max solubility: **0.022 wt% C at 727 °C**. Soft, ductile, magnetic. - **γ-Austenite (γ-Fe):** FCC solid solution of C in iron. Max solubility: **2.14 wt% C at 1147 °C**. Non-magnetic, ductile. - **δ-Ferrite (δ-Fe):** BCC, stable only between **1394 °C – 1538 °C**. Low C solubility (~0.09 wt%). - **Fe₃C (Cementite):** Intermetallic, **6.67 wt% C**, very hard, brittle. - **Liquid (L):** Exists above the liquidus line. ### Critical Temperatures (A-Lines) - **A₁ = 727 °C** – Eutectoid temperature - **A₃ = 727–912 °C** – Upper critical temperature for hypoeutectoid steels (γ → α + γ boundary) - **A₄ = 1394 °C** – δ → γ transition on heating - **A_cm** – Cementite solubility limit in austenite > [!IMPORTANT] > The notation **"A"** stands for **Arrêt** (French for "arrest" or "halt"), referring to the thermal arrest points observed during heating/cooling on a time-temperature chart. --- ## 1.2 The Cu-Zn Phase Diagram (Copper-Zinc System) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** > The **Cu-Zn phase diagram** is a *binary equilibrium phase diagram* representing the phase relationships, solubility limits, and invariant reactions in copper-zinc alloys collectively known as **brasses**. It is the foundational reference for designing alloys ranging from cartridge brass to Muntz metal to complex high-zinc brasses. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of it like "Mixing Two Colors of Clay":** > Imagine you have a soft, reddish copper clay and a brittle, bluish zinc clay. As you mix them: > 1. **Mostly copper (α-brass):** Remains soft, gold-colored, easily shaped into jewelry — like working with pure clay. > 2. **Half-and-half (β-brass):** Becomes harder, stronger — like a stiff pottery clay, great for machine parts. > 3. **Too much zinc (γ-, ε-, η-phases):** Becomes extremely brittle — like over-dried clay that crumbles when touched. > > The diagram tells you exactly **where to stop** adding zinc to get the property you want. ### Key Components | Component | Description | |---|---| | **Cu (Copper)** | FCC, reddish, melting point **1085 °C** | | **Zn (Zinc)** | HCP, bluish-white, melting point **419.5 °C** | ### Key Phases in Cu-Zn System - **α phase:** FCC solid solution of Zn in Cu. Stable up to **~39 wt% Zn at 456 °C**. Soft, ductile, cold-workable. - **β phase:** BCC (ordered as β' below ~468 °C), stable between **~36–46.5 wt% Zn**. Hot-workable. - **γ phase:** Complex cubic (D8₂), stable at **~57–70 wt% Zn**. Very hard, brittle. - **δ, ε, η phases:** High-zinc intermetallics, all very brittle. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fe-Fe₃C Diagram — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown ### The Five Major Invariant Reactions The Fe-Fe₃C diagram contains **five** critical invariant reactions. Memorizing these is mandatory for KTU exams. ### Reaction 1: Peritectic Reaction (at 1493 °C, 0.17 wt% C) When liquid of composition **0.53 wt% C** reacts with δ-ferrite of composition **0.09 wt% C** to form γ-austenite of composition **0.17 wt% C**: $$L_{0.53} + \delta_{0.09} \xrightarrow{1493\,°C} \gamma_{0.17}$$ This occurs during solidification of low-carbon steels. ### Reaction 2: Eutectic Reaction (at 1147 °C, 4.30 wt% C) Liquid of **4.30 wt% C** solidifies into a mixture of austenite (**2.14 wt% C**) and cementite (**6.67 wt% C**): $$L_{4.30} \xrightarrow{1147\,°C} \gamma_{2.14} + Fe_3C$$ The resulting microconstituent is called **Ledeburite**. ### Reaction 3: Eutectoid Reaction (at 727 °C, 0.77 wt% C) — THE MOST IMPORTANT Austenite of **0.77 wt% C** decomposes into alternating lamellae of α-ferrite (**0.022 wt% C**) and cementite (**6.67 wt% C**): $$\gamma_{0.77} \xrightarrow{727\,°C} \alpha_{0.022} + Fe_3C$$ The resulting microconstituent is called **Pearlite** (α + Fe₃C lamellar structure). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is the eutectoid reaction so critical?** > Because **ALL heat treatments of steel** (annealing, normalizing, quenching, tempering) fundamentally manipulate the cooling rate through this single 727 °C threshold. The entire Hardness/Toughness trade-off in steel metallurgy pivots on this reaction. ### Reaction 4 & 5: Magnetic Transitions - **Curie temperature of ferrite (A₂):** **770 °C** — ferrite becomes non-magnetic above this. - These are **not** invariant reactions, only magnetic transformations. ### Classification of Ferrous Alloys | Class | Carbon Range (wt%) | Examples | |---|---|---| | **Pure Iron / Low-Carbon Steel** | < 0.022 | Dead-soft iron, electrical steels | | **Eutectoid Steel** | **0.77** | Razor blades, high-strength wire | | **Hypoeutectoid Steel** | 0.022 < C < 0.77 | Structural steel, rails | | **Hypereutectoid Steel** | 0.77 < C < 2.14 | Cutlery, springs, dies | | **Eutectic Cast Iron** | **4.30** | White cast iron | | **Hypoeutectic Cast Iron** | 2.14 < C < 4.30 | Gray cast iron, ductile iron | | **Hypereutectic Cast Iron** | 4.30 < C < 6.67 | White cast iron (with proeutectic Fe₃C) | --- ## 2.2 Cu-Zn Diagram — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown ### Phase Boundary & Solubility Rules - **α-phase (FCC):** Zn substitutes on Cu lattice up to **~39 wt% Zn** at **456 °C**. Room-temperature solubility: **~35 wt% Zn**. - **β-phase (BCC, ordered β' below 468 °C):** Stable only at elevated temperatures; ordered β' is brittle. - **γ-phase:** Extremely hard and brittle; **must be avoided** in commercial brasses (kept below 35 wt% Zn in practical α-β brasses). - **δ, ε, η:** All brittle intermetallics; **never** used in commercial alloys. ### The Three Peritectic Reactions | Temperature (°C) | Reaction | Significance | |---|---|---| | **903** | L + α → β | Formation of β phase | | **835** | L + β → γ | Formation of γ (avoid!) | | **700** | L + γ → δ | Formation of δ | | **598** | L + δ → ε | Formation of ε | | **424** | L + ε → η | Final solidification | ### Classification of Brasses (KTU High-Yield) | Brass Name | Zn (wt%) | Color | Key Property | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Gilding Metal** | 5–10 | Red-gold | Excellent cold workability | Bullet jackets, emblems | | **Cartridge Brass (70/30)** | **30** | Yellow | Best cold workability | Ammunition cases, tubes | | **Yellow Brass** | 33 | Golden | General purpose | Lamp fixtures, plumbing | | **Muntz Metal (60/40)** | **40** | Light yellow | Hot-workable | Marine sheathing, bolts | | **Naval Brass** | 39 + 1% Sn | Yellow | Corrosion resistant | Marine hardware | | **Manganese Bronze** | 40 + small Mn/Fe | Golden-brown | High strength | Ship propellers | > [!WARNING] > **Engineering Rule:** Always keep Zn < **35 wt%** to avoid the brittle γ-phase. This is why commercial brasses (e.g., cartridge brass) are specifically tuned to 30 wt% Zn. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Application | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $W_\alpha = \dfrac{C_0 - C_\gamma}{C_\alpha - C_\gamma}$ | Fraction of α-phase | Lever rule (hypoeutectoid) | Dimensionless | | 2 | $W_{Fe_3C} = \dfrac{C_\gamma - C_0}{C_\gamma - C_{Fe_3C}}$ | Fraction of cementite | Lever rule | Dimensionless | | 3 | $W_{pearlite} = \dfrac{C_0 - 0.022}{0.77 - 0.022}$ | Fraction of pearlite | Hypoeutectoid steel | Dimensionless | | 4 | $W_{proeutectoid\,\alpha} = \dfrac{0.77 - C_0}{0.77 - 0.022}$ | Fraction of proeutectoid α | Hypoeutectoid | Dimensionless | | 5 | $W_{proeutectoid\,Fe_3C} = \dfrac{C_0 - 0.77}{6.67 - 0.77}$ | Fraction of proeutectoid Fe₃C | Hypereutectoid | Dimensionless | | 6 | $W_{ledeburite} = \dfrac{4.30 - C_0}{4.30 - 2.14}$ | Fraction of ledeburite | Hypoeutectic cast iron | Dimensionless | | 7 | Density of cementite: $\rho \approx 7.66\,g/cm^3$ | Iron carbide density | Volume fraction calc | g/cm³ | | 8 | Density of ferrite: $\rho \approx 7.87\,g/cm^3$ | α-Fe density | Volume fraction calc | g/cm³ | | 9 | Volume fraction: $V_i = \dfrac{W_i/\rho_i}{\sum_j W_j/\rho_j}$ | Phase volume fraction | Conversion of wt% to vol% | Dimensionless | | 10 | Gibbs phase rule (condensed): $F = C - P + 1$ | Degrees of freedom | Invariant reaction analysis | — | ### Real-World Engineering Utility - **Fe-Fe₃C:** Every heat treatment specification (hardening, tempering, normalizing, annealing) on a steel datasheet references temperatures, microconstituents, and cooling rates derived directly from this diagram. - **Cu-Zn:** Cartridge brass casings, Muntz marine sheathing, musical instruments, decorative fittings — all owe their existence to the phase boundaries shown here. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Lever Rule Derivations (Fe-Fe₃C System) The **lever rule** (also called the **inverse lever rule** or **conjugate line rule**) is a graphical-numerical technique used to determine the relative fractions of two phases in equilibrium at a given temperature and overall composition. ### Derivation of the Mass-Balance Equations Consider a binary alloy of overall composition $C_0$ at a temperature where it exists as a mixture of two phases $\alpha$ and $\beta$ with compositions $C_\alpha$ and $C_\beta$, respectively. Let: - $W_\alpha$ = mass fraction of phase $\alpha$ - $W_\beta$ = mass fraction of phase $\beta$ - $W_\alpha + W_\beta = 1$ Mass balance on solute (carbon) gives: $$W_\alpha \cdot C_\alpha + W_\beta \cdot C_\beta = C_0$$ Substituting $W_\beta = 1 - W_\alpha$: $$\begin{aligned} W_\alpha \cdot C_\alpha + (1 - W_\alpha) \cdot C_\beta &= C_0 \\ W_\alpha \cdot C_\alpha + C_\beta - W_\alpha \cdot C_\beta &= C_0 \\ W_\alpha (C_\alpha - C_\beta) &= C_0 - C_\beta \\ W_\alpha &= \dfrac{C_0 - C_\beta}{C_\alpha - C_\beta} \end{aligned}$$ Similarly: $$\begin{aligned} W_\beta &= \dfrac{C_\alpha - C_0}{C_\alpha - C_\beta} \end{aligned}$$ The numerator is the length of the conjugate line segment **on the opposite side of the tie-line** from the phase whose fraction is being calculated. This is the geometric origin of the "lever" analogy. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 1: Hypoeutectoid Steel (0.45 wt% C) at Room Temperature **Given:** $C_0 = 0.45\,wt\%\,C$, just below the eutectoid isotherm. **Equilibrium phases:** $\alpha$-ferrite (0.022 wt% C) and cementite (6.67 wt% C) — but they are microstructurally present as proeutectoid α + pearlite. ### Step 1: Identify the phases Below 727 °C: phases are $\alpha$ and $Fe_3C$ (as pearlite + proeutectoid α). ### Step 2: Apply lever rule to find total phase fractions $$\begin{aligned} W_\alpha &= \dfrac{6.67 - 0.45}{6.67 - 0.022} = \dfrac{6.22}{6.648} = 0.9357 \\ W_{Fe_3C} &= \dfrac{0.45 - 0.022}{6.67 - 0.022} = \dfrac{0.428}{6.648} = 0.0644 \end{aligned}$$ So: **93.57 % ferrite**, **6.44 % cementite** (by mass). ### Step 3: Decompose ferrite into proeutectoid α + eutectoid α (within pearlite) Just above 727 °C (in $\alpha + \gamma$ region), apply lever rule: $$\begin{aligned} W_\alpha^{pro} &= \dfrac{0.77 - 0.45}{0.77 - 0.022} = \dfrac{0.32}{0.748} = 0.4278 \\ W_\gamma &= \dfrac{0.45 - 0.022}{0.77 - 0.022} = \dfrac{0.428}{0.748} = 0.5722 \end{aligned}$$ This γ of 0.5722 becomes **100% pearlite** at 727 °C. ### Step 4: Final microconstituent fractions $$\begin{aligned} W_{pearlite} &= W_\gamma = 0.5722 = 57.22\% \\ W_{proeutectoid\,\alpha} &= W_\alpha^{pro} = 0.4278 = 42.78\% \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 5: Volume fraction conversion (optional, for advanced problems) Using $\rho_\alpha = 7.87\,g/cm^3$ and $\rho_{Fe_3C} = 7.66\,g/cm^3$: $$V_{Fe_3C} = \dfrac{W_{Fe_3C}/\rho_{Fe_3C}}{W_\alpha/\rho_\alpha + W_{Fe_3C}/\rho_{Fe_3C}} = \dfrac{0.0644/7.66}{0.9357/7.87 + 0.0644/7.66}$$ $$V_{Fe_3C} = \dfrac{0.008407}{0.11889 + 0.008407} = \dfrac{0.008407}{0.12730} = 0.0661 = 6.61\%$$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 2: Hypereutectoid Steel (1.20 wt% C) **Given:** $C_0 = 1.20\,wt\%\,C$, room temperature. ### Step 1: Phase fractions (just above 727 °C, in γ + Fe₃C region) $$\begin{aligned} W_\gamma &= \dfrac{6.67 - 1.20}{6.67 - 0.77} = \dfrac{5.47}{5.90} = 0.9271 \\ W_{Fe_3C}^{pro} &= \dfrac{1.20 - 0.77}{6.67 - 0.77} = \dfrac{0.43}{5.90} = 0.0729 \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 2: Microconstituent decomposition - The **0.9271 austenite** transforms 100% to **pearlite** at 727 °C. - The **0.0729 proeutectoid cementite** remains unchanged. $$\begin{aligned} W_{pearlite} &= 92.71\% \\ W_{proeutectoid\,Fe_3C} &= 7.29\% \end{aligned}$$ --- ## 3.4 Worked Example 3: Eutectoid Steel (0.77 wt% C) **Given:** $C_0 = 0.77\,wt\%\,C$. At 727 °C, the entire alloy transforms to pearlite. By definition: $$W_{pearlite} = 1.000 = 100\%$$ Within pearlite, individual phase fractions: $$\begin{aligned} W_\alpha &= \dfrac{6.67 - 0.77}{6.67 - 0.022} = \dfrac{5.90}{6.648} = 0.8875 = 88.75\% \\ W_{Fe_3C} &= \dfrac{0.77 - 0.022}{6.67 - 0.022} = \dfrac{0.748}{6.648} = 0.1125 = 11.25\% \end{aligned}$$ --- ## 3.5 Worked Example 4: Hypoeutectic White Cast Iron (3.50 wt% C) **Given:** $C_0 = 3.50\,wt\%\,C$, just above 727 °C (in γ + Fe₃C + L region — solidified, but in γ + Fe₃C + Ld region). ### Step 1: Total cementite fraction just below 1147 °C (in γ + Fe₃C region) $$\begin{aligned} W_{Fe_3C} &= \dfrac{3.50 - 2.14}{6.67 - 2.14} = \dfrac{1.36}{4.53} = 0.3002 = 30.02\% \\ W_\gamma &= \dfrac{6.67 - 3.50}{6.67 - 2.14} = \dfrac{3.17}{4.53} = 0.6998 = 69.98\% \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 2: This austenite (2.14 wt% C) cools to 727 °C, then partially transforms to pearlite - The **0.6998 austenite** of 2.14 wt% C at 727 °C transforms by lever rule in the α+γ region: but at 2.14 wt% C, the austenite is at the saturation limit; the only transformation is the **eutectoid** where some of it becomes pearlite and the rest remains as proeutectoid γ (then transforms to pearlite on further cooling). Actually, the austenite (2.14 wt% C) at 727 °C has already reached the saturation limit; it transforms 100% to pearlite at 727 °C. $$\begin{aligned} W_{pearlite\,(from\,austenite)} &= 69.98\% \\ W_{eutectic\,Fe_3C\,(in\,ledeburite)} &= 30.02\% \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > The ledeburite (formed at 1147 °C from the 4.30 wt% C liquid) consists of the above mixture of pearlite + cementite. So overall, room-temperature microstructure = pearlite + cementite (no distinction, both come from ledeburite + proeutectic austenite decomposition). ### Step 3: Final overall phase composition $$\begin{aligned} W_{pearlite} &= 69.98\% \\ W_{Fe_3C\,(total)} &= 30.02\% \end{aligned}$$ --- ## 3.6 Worked Example 5: Cu-Zn Alloy (Brass) – Phase Fraction at 400 °C **Given:** 70 wt% Cu – 30 wt% Zn alloy at 400 °C. At this temperature, only α-phase is stable (per Cu-Zn diagram, α extends up to ~35 wt% Zn at room temperature and up to ~39 wt% at 456 °C). Since the entire alloy is in the **single-phase α region**: $$W_\alpha = 1.000 = 100\%$$ **No lever rule applies** — only one phase exists. ### Counter-Example: 60 wt% Cu – 40 wt% Zn at 400 °C At 400 °C, the alloy lies in the **α + β' region**. Compositions (approximate from diagram): - $C_\alpha \approx 35\,wt\%\,Zn$ - $C_{\beta'} \approx 45\,wt\%\,Zn$ $$\begin{aligned} W_\alpha &= \dfrac{45 - 40}{45 - 35} = \dfrac{5}{10} = 0.50 = 50\% \\ W_{\beta'} &= \dfrac{40 - 35}{45 - 35} = \dfrac{5}{10} = 0.50 = 50\% \end{aligned}$$ This corresponds to a **Muntz-metal type brass (60/40)** with a two-phase (α+β') structure at room temperature. --- ## 3.7 Summary Table — KTU Numerical Pattern Recognition | Alloy Type | C₀ (wt%) | Phases at 727 °C | Microconstituents at RT | |---|---|---|---| | Pure Fe | 0.000 | α only | 100% ferrite | | Low-C Steel | 0.20 | α + γ | Proeut. α + Pearlite | | Med-C Steel | 0.45 | α + γ | Proeut. α + Pearlite | | Eutectoid Steel | **0.77** | γ → α+Fe₃C | 100% Pearlite | | Hypereutectoid | 1.20 | γ + Fe₃C | Pearlite + Proeut. Fe₃C | | Eutectic CI | **4.30** | L → γ+Fe₃C | Ledeburite | | Hypoeutectic CI | 3.50 | γ+Fe₃C | Pearlite + Ledeburite | | Hypereutectic CI | 5.00 | Fe₃C+γ | Proeut. Fe₃C + Ledeburite | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Fe-Fe₃C Phase Diagram — Schematic Block Architecture (Mermaid) ```mermaid graph TD A0["Pure Fe

Emblems

Heavy duty fasteners"] ``` ## 4.4 Eutectoid Reaction Flow (Pearlite Formation) ```mermaid graph LR A["Austenite gamma
