The phase fraction is proportional to the **opposite arm length** — the "fulcrum" of the lever is the alloy's overall composition, not the phase composition. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define the term *eutectoid reaction* with reference to the Fe-Fe₃C phase diagram. State the temperature and composition at which it occurs. [KTU University Exam – May 2023] > [!NOTE] > **Model Answer:** > The **eutectoid reaction** is an invariant three-phase reaction in which a **single solid phase** on cooling transforms *simultaneously* into **two different solid phases** at a constant temperature. > > In the Fe-Fe₃C system, austenite (γ) of composition **0.77 wt% C** decomposes at **727 °C** into a lamellar mixture of **α-ferrite (0.022 wt% C)** and **cementite (6.67 wt% C)**: > $$\gamma_{0.77} \xrightarrow{727\,°C} \alpha_{0.022} + Fe_3C$$ > The resulting microconstituent is called **pearlite**. This reaction is the foundation of all steel heat treatments. --- ### Q2. Differentiate between *peritectic* and *eutectic* reactions. Give one example of each from the Fe-Fe₃C system. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] > [!NOTE] > **Model Answer:** > > | Feature | Eutectic Reaction | Peritectic Reaction | > |---|---|---| > | Definition | One liquid → two solids on cooling | One liquid + one solid → another solid on cooling | > | Phases involved | 3 (L → α + β) | 3 (L + β → γ) | > | Liquid behavior | Disappears | Disappears | > | Fe-Fe₃C example | $L_{4.30} \xrightarrow{1147°C} \gamma_{2.14} + Fe_3C$ (Ledeburite) | $L_{0.53} + \delta_{0.09} \xrightarrow{1493°C} \gamma_{0.17}$ | > > The **eutectic** is "one becomes two" (1L → 2S); the **peritectic** is "two become one" (L + S → S). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students answer either the full Question A or the full Question B. ### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) **Q.A (a) [7 Marks]:** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the salient features of the Fe-Fe₃C phase diagram. Identify all invariant reactions and label all critical temperatures. **Q.A (b) [7 Marks]:** A steel contains **0.45 wt% C**. Determine the following at room temperature: (i) phases present, (ii) weight fraction of each phase, (iii) weight fraction of each microconstituent, and (iv) weight fraction of proeutectoid ferrite. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] > [!NOTE] > **Model Solution for Q.A (a):** > > The Fe-Fe₃C diagram is plotted with **wt% C** on the x-axis (0 to 6.67) and **temperature (°C)** on the y-axis (0 to 1538). Salient features: > > 1. **Single-phase regions:** α (FCC γ, but BCC α), γ (FCC), δ (BCC), L (liquid), and Fe₃C (line at 6.67 wt% C). > 2. **Critical lines:** Solidus, liquidus, solvus lines (A₃, A₁, A_cm), and the A₄ line. > 3. **Invariant points:** Peritectic (1493 °C, 0.17 wt% C), Eutectic (1147 °C, 4.30 wt% C), Eutectoid (727 °C, 0.77 wt% C). > 4. **Magnetic transformation:** A₂ = 770 °C (Curie line of ferrite). > > **Invariant Reactions:** > - $L_{0.53} + \delta_{0.09} \xrightarrow{1493°C} \gamma_{0.17}$ (Peritectic) > - $L_{4.30} \xrightarrow{1147°C} \gamma_{2.14} + Fe_3C$ (Eutectic) > - $\gamma_{0.77} \xrightarrow{727°C} \alpha_{0.022} + Fe_3C$ (Eutectoid) > > **Critical Temperatures:** > - A₁ = 727 °C, A₃ = 912 °C (pure Fe), A₄ = 1394 °C, A_cm varies, A₂ = 770 °C. > [!NOTE] > **Model Solution for Q.A (b):** > > **Given:** $C_0 = 0.45\,wt\%\,C$ (hypoeutectoid steel). > > **(i) Phases at room temperature:** α-ferrite + Fe₃C (microstructurally as proeutectoid α + pearlite). > > **(ii) Weight fraction of each phase** (at 727 °C just below, in α + Fe₃C region): > > $$\begin{aligned} > W_\alpha &= \dfrac{6.67 - 0.45}{6.67 - 0.022} = \dfrac{6.22}{6.648} = 0.9357 \\ > W_{Fe_3C} &= \dfrac{0.45 - 0.022}{6.67 - 0.022} = \dfrac{0.428}{6.648} = 0.0644 > \end{aligned}$$ > > **(iii) Weight fraction of each microconstituent** (just above 727 °C, in α + γ region): > > $$\begin{aligned} > W_{pearlite} &= \dfrac{0.45 - 0.022}{0.77 - 0.022} = \dfrac{0.428}{0.748} = 0.5722 \\ > W_{proeutectoid\,\alpha} &= \dfrac{0.77 - 0.45}{0.77 - 0.022} = \dfrac{0.32}{0.748} = 0.4278 > \end{aligned}$$ > > **(iv) Weight fraction of proeutectoid ferrite = 42.78 %.** --- ### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **Q.B (a) [7 Marks]:** Draw the Cu-Zn phase diagram and label all phases. Explain the significance of the **α, β, and γ** phases with their crystal structures and properties. **Q.B (b) [7 Marks]:** A brass alloy contains **40 wt% Zn – 60 wt% Cu**. Determine: (i) the phases present at 600 °C, (ii) their compositions, and (iii) the weight fraction of each phase. Justify why this alloy is suitable for **hot working**. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] > [!NOTE] > **Model Solution for Q.B (a):** > > The Cu-Zn diagram extends from 0 wt% Zn (pure Cu, mp 1085 °C) to 100 wt% Zn (pure Zn, mp 420 °C). Key phases: > > | Phase | Crystal Structure | Composition (wt% Zn) | Properties | > |---|---|---|---| > | **α** | FCC | 0–39 (max at 456 °C) | Soft, ductile, excellent cold workability; gold-yellow color | > | **β** | BCC (disordered); ordered β' (CsCl-type) below 468 °C | 36–46.5 | Strong, hot-workable (β is ductile, β' is brittle) | > | **γ** | Complex cubic (D8₂ type, 52 atoms/cell) | 57–70 | Hard, brittle, **avoid in commercial brasses** | > > The α-phase dominates in commercial brasses. The β-phase enables hot working. The γ-phase must be avoided (>35 wt% Zn) to prevent brittleness. > > **Peritectic reactions:** > - 903 °C: L + α → β > - 835 °C: L + β → γ > - 700 °C: L + γ → δ > - 598 °C: L + δ → ε > - 424 °C: L + ε → η > [!NOTE] > **Model Solution for Q.B (b):** > > **Given:** 60 wt% Cu – 40 wt% Zn alloy at 600 °C. > > **(i) Phases present at 600 °C:** From the Cu-Zn diagram, at 600 °C, an alloy with 40 wt% Zn lies in the **α + β region**. > > **(ii) Compositions (read from tie-line at 600 °C):** > - $C_\alpha \approx 38\,wt\%\,Zn$ (solvus line) > - $C_\beta \approx 46\,wt\%\,Zn$ (solvus line) > > **(iii) Weight fractions (lever rule):** > > $$\begin{aligned} > W_\alpha &= \dfrac{C_\beta - C_0}{C_\beta - C_\alpha} = \dfrac{46 - 40}{46 - 38} = \dfrac{6}{8} = 0.75 = 75\% \\ > W_\beta &= \dfrac{C_0 - C_\alpha}{C_\beta - C_\alpha} = \dfrac{40 - 38}{46 - 38} = \dfrac{2}{8} = 0.25 = 25\% > \end{aligned}$$ > > **Suitability for Hot Working:** The β-phase is **disordered BCC** above 468 °C, which gives good ductility at elevated temperatures. Since this alloy (40 wt% Zn) lies in the (α + β) region at 600 °C, it contains ~25% soft, ductile β-phase, making it ideal for **hot working** (e.g., hot rolling, forging, extrusion). This composition corresponds to **Muntz metal**, the classic hot-working brass used for marine sheathing, condenser tubes, and bolts. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > > 1. **Wrong lever rule denominator orientation:** Students often invert the formula. The denominator is **always (C₁ - C₂)** where C₁ is the *opposite* end of the tie-line. The phase fraction is **proportional to the opposite arm**, not the adjacent arm. > > 2. **Confusing microconstituents with phases:** *Pearlite* is a microconstituent (α + Fe₃C mixture), not a phase. Ferrite and cementite are the **phases**; pearlite and proeutectoid ferrite are the **microconstituents** observable under the microscope. > > 3. **Wrong composition selection for hypereutectoid steels:** When asked for proeutectoid cementite fraction in 1.20 % C steel, students often plug into the hypoeutectoid formula. The correct formula is $W_{pro\,Fe_3C} = (C_0 - 0.77)/(6.67 - 0.77)$. > > 4. **Forgetting to use the eutectoid line (0.77, 0.022) for hypoeutectoid calculation:** The proeutectoid ferrite formula uses the A₃ line composition, not the overall composition. > > 5. **Mixing up A₁, A₃, A₄, A_cm:** Memorize: A₁ = 727 (eutectoid), A₃ = upper critical (α/γ), A₄ = δ/γ boundary, A_cm = cementite solubility. > > 6. **In Cu-Zn, ignoring the β/β' transition:** Below 468 °C, the ordered β' phase is brittle. Cartridge brass (30% Zn) avoids this and is cold-workable. Muntz metal (40% Zn) is hot-worked **above 468 °C** to stay in the ductile β regime. > > 7. **Units inconsistency:** Always express compositions in **wt% C** for Fe-Fe₃C, **wt% Zn** for Cu-Zn. Mixing units is an instant 1–2 mark penalty. --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### Fe-Fe₃C System — Rapid-Revision Checklist - ✅ **Three components effectively:** Fe, C, Fe₃C; but it's a **pseudo-binary** with Fe and Fe₃C as end-members. - ✅ **Three allotropes of Fe:** α (BCC, ≤ 912 °C), γ (FCC, 912–1394 °C), δ (BCC, 1394–1538 °C). - ✅ **Max C solubility in γ-austenite = 2.14 wt%** at **1147 °C**. - ✅ **Max C solubility in α-ferrite = 0.022 wt%** at **727 °C**. - ✅ **Cementite (Fe₃C) = 6.67 wt% C**, fixed composition, orthorhombic, hardest phase. - ✅ **Three invariant reactions:** Peritectic (1493 °C), Eutectic (1147 °C), Eutectoid (727 °C). - ✅ **Eutectoid = Pearlite (α + Fe₃C lamellae) — the heart of steel metallurgy.** - ✅ **Eutectic = Ledeburite (γ + Fe₃C at 1147 °C) — the heart of cast iron metallurgy.** - ✅ **A₁ = 727 °C, A₃ = varies, A₄ = 1394 °C, A_cm = varies, A₂ = 770 °C (magnetic).** - ✅ **Steels: 0.022 – 2.14 wt% C; Cast irons: 2.14 – 6.67 wt% C.** - ✅ **Hypoeutectoid (C < 0.77%): proeutectoid α + pearlite.** - ✅ **Hypereutectoid (C > 0.77%): pearlite + proeutectoid Fe₃C.** - ✅ **Lever rule formula master template:** $W_1 = (C_0 - C_2)/(C_1 - C_2)$. ### Cu-Zn System — Rapid-Revision Checklist - ✅ **Seven terminal phases:** α, β (β'), γ, δ, ε, η + L. - ✅ **α phase (FCC):** Best for **cold working**; cartridge brass (70/30) is the workhorse. - ✅ **β phase (BCC, ordered β' below 468 °C):** Best for **hot working**; Muntz metal (60/40) is the workhorse. - ✅ **γ phase: brittle — KEEP Zn < 35 wt% in commercial brasses.** - ✅ **Five peritectic reactions:** 903, 835, 700, 598, 424 °C. - ✅ **No eutectic in Cu-Zn** (unlike Fe-Fe₃C) — only peritectic transformations. - ✅ **Maximum Zn solubility in α = 39 wt% (at 456 °C), ~35 wt% (at room temperature).** - ✅ **α + β dual-phase region = hot-workable Muntz-type brasses.** ### KTU High-Yield Numerical Templates > [!TIP] > **For any "find the phase/microconstituent fraction" question:** > 1. Identify the alloy class (hypoeutectoid, eutectoid, hypereutectoid, etc.). > 2. Identify the temperature of interest from the question. > 3. Choose the correct tie-line endpoints ($C_\alpha$, $C_\gamma$, $C_{Fe_3C}$). > 4. Apply the lever rule **twice** if microconstituents are asked (once at the temperature of interest, once at the eutectoid line 727 °C). > 5. Present the answer to 4 decimal places or 2 significant figures, with units (%). ### Critical Numerical Constants (Memorize!) | Constant | Value | |---|---| | Eutectoid T | 727 °C | | Eutectoid C | 0.77 wt% C | | Eutectic T | 1147 °C | | Eutectic C | 4.30 wt% C | | Cementite C | 6.67 wt% C | | Max γ solubility | 2.14 wt% C at 1147 °C | | Max α solubility | 0.022 wt% C at 727 °C | | Pure Fe mp | 1538 °C | | Peritectic T | 1493 °C | | Cu mp | 1085 °C | | Zn mp | 419.5 °C | | β/β' transition | 468 °C |

topics.title: Pb-Sn System - Ag-Pt System - Iron-Iron Carbide Equilibrium Diagram - Phase Equilibrium Calculation and Construction of Phase Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pb-Sn System - Ag-Pt System - Iron-Iron Carbide Equilibrium Diagram - Phase Equilibrium Calculation and Construction of Phase Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Phase Equilibria & Binary Phase Diagrams

## 1.1 What is a Phase Diagram? (Formal Definition)

A **phase diagram** (also called an **equilibrium diagram** or **constitutional diagram**) is a graphical map that depicts the existence of distinct phases of a material system as a function of temperature, pressure, and composition under thermodynamic equilibrium conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim from syllabus):** "A phase diagram is a graphical representation of the physical states (phases) of a material system under conditions of thermodynamic equilibrium, plotted against variables such as temperature, pressure, and composition."

For binary alloy systems (two components), the diagram is plotted on a 2-D graph with:
- **X-axis:** Composition (wt % of one element, ranging from 0% to 100%)
- **Y-axis:** Temperature (°C or K)

## 1.2 Core Terminology You Must Know

| Term | Meaning | Example |
|------|---------|---------|
| **Component** | A chemical species (element or compound) constituting the system | Cu, Zn in brass |
| **Phase** | A physically and chemically homogeneous region | α-brass, liquid melt |
| **System** | A collection of phases isolated for study | Cu–Zn system |
| **Solidus** | Locus of points below which the alloy is **completely solid** | Lower curve |
| **Liquidus** | Locus of points above which the alloy is **completely liquid** | Upper curve |
| **Solvus** | Locus defining the limit of solid solubility | Boundary of α-phase field |
| **Eutectic** | A reaction where a liquid transforms into two distinct solid phases on cooling | L → α + β |
| **Eutectoid** | A solid → two solids transformation | γ → α + Fe₃C |
| **Peritectic** | Liquid + solid → another solid on cooling | L + δ → γ |
| **Peritectoid** | Solid₁ + Solid₂ → Solid₃ on cooling | δ + γ → austenite variant |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Recipe Map"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a phase diagram as a "Google Map" for an alloy:**
> - **Streets (curves)** = phase boundaries (solidus, liquidus, solvus).
> - **Zones (regions)** = phases that exist (α, β, γ, liquid).
> - **Junctions (invariant points)** = traffic intersections where multiple phases coexist (eutectic, eutectoid, peritectic).
> - **Coordinates (T, %X)** = the exact "address" telling you what state your alloy is in.
>
> If a student wants to know *"What is the microstructure of 60 wt% Sn – 40 wt% Pb solder at 200 °C?"*, they simply locate the (200 °C, 60% Sn) point on the Pb–Sn diagram and "read" the surrounding zones.

## 1.4 The Three Binary Systems in This Module (At a Glance)

| System | Type | Practical Use | Why Important |
|--------|------|---------------|---------------|
| **Pb–Sn** | Eutectic (limited solid solubility) | Solder, plumbing | Classic KTU question system |
| **Ag–Pt** | Isomorphous (complete solid solubility) | Jewellery, electrical contacts, dental alloys | Textbook isomorphous system |
| **Fe–Fe₃C** | Eutectic + Eutectoid + Peritectic | Steels and cast irons | Backbone of metallurgy |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 2024 Board Examination: **Fe–Fe₃C is the highest-weightage topic** in this module. Always study this diagram first and most thoroughly.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Gibbs Phase Rule — The Master Equation

The **Gibbs Phase Rule** is the fundamental law governing the degrees of freedom in any heterogeneous equilibrium system.

$$F \;=\; C \;-\; P \;+\; 2$$

Where:
- $F$ = **Degrees of freedom** (independent intensive variables we can change without disturbing the number of phases in equilibrium)
- $C$ = **Number of components** (minimum number of chemical species needed to describe the system)
- $P$ = **Number of coexisting phases** at equilibrium
- The constant **2** represents the two external intensive variables: **temperature (T)** and **pressure (P)**

### 2.1.1 Why does the "2" become "1" in binary phase diagrams?

Since **all binary phase diagrams are plotted at atmospheric (constant) pressure**, one external variable is fixed. The rule simplifies to the **Condensed Phase Rule**:

$$F \;=\; C \;-\; P \;+\; 1$$

### 2.1.2 Invariant Points (F = 0) — The Most Tested Concept

When three phases coexist in a binary system ($P = 3$, $C = 2$):

$$F \;=\; 2 \;-\; 3 \;+\; 1 \;=\; 0$$

This is an **invariant reaction** — temperature and composition are fixed. Examples:
- **Eutectic** in Pb–Sn at **183 °C, 61.9 wt% Sn**
- **Eutectoid** in Fe–Fe₃C at **727 °C, 0.76 wt% C**
- **Peritectic** in Fe–Fe₃C at **1493 °C, 0.16 wt% C**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Golden Rule:** Every horizontal line on a binary phase diagram is a **three-phase invariant reaction** where $F = 0$.

## 2.2 The Lever Rule — For Quantitative Phase Calculation

The **Lever Rule** (also called the **Inverse Lever Arm Rule**) is used to determine the relative **amounts (fractions)** of phases in equilibrium at a given temperature within a two-phase region.

### 2.2.1 Statement of the Lever Rule

For a binary alloy of overall composition $C_0$ at temperature $T$, lying within a two-phase region ($\alpha + \beta$):

$$W_{\alpha} \;=\; \frac{C_{\beta} \;-\; C_0}{C_{\beta} \;-\; C_{\alpha}}$$

$$W_{\beta} \;=\; \frac{C_0 \;-\; C_{\alpha}}{C_{\beta} \;-\; C_{\alpha}}$$

Where:
- $W_{\alpha}$, $W_{\beta}$ = mass (or mole) fractions of the $\alpha$ and $\beta$ phases respectively
- $C_0$ = overall alloy composition
- $C_{\alpha}$ = composition of the $\alpha$ phase (tie-line endpoint)
- $C_{\beta}$ = composition of the $\beta$ phase (tie-line endpoint)

> [!NOTE]
> The **fraction is proportional to the length of the lever arm on the OPPOSITE side of the tie-line.** The tie-line is the fulcrum; the alloy composition is the lever; the phase compositions are the endpoints.

### 2.2.2 Tie-Line (Conode) Construction

1. Locate the alloy point $(C_0, T)$ inside a two-phase region.
2. Draw a **horizontal isothermal line** at that temperature.
3. This line intersects the phase boundaries at two points — these are the **tie-line endpoints** giving $C_{\alpha}$ and $C_{\beta}$.
4. Apply the lever rule algebraically.

## 2.3 Invariant Reactions — The "Reaction Catalogue"

A binary phase diagram has a limited set of invariant reactions. Memorize this table for KTU:

| Reaction Type | Phases on Cooling | Generic Form | Example in Fe–Fe₃C |
|---------------|-------------------|--------------|---------------------|
| **Eutectic** | L → α + β | Liquid → 2 solids | At 1147 °C: L → γ + Fe₃C |
| **Eutectoid** | γ → α + β | Solid → 2 solids | At 727 °C: γ → α + Fe₃C |
| **Peritectic** | L + δ → γ | Liquid + Solid → Solid | At 1493 °C: L + δ → γ |
| **Peritectoid** | α + β → γ | 2 Solids → 1 Solid | (Not present in Fe–Fe₃C) |
| **Congruent Melting** | S → L (same composition) | Solid → Liquid | Not in these systems |

## 2.4 The Lead–Tin (Pb–Sn) System — Eutectic Type

### 2.4.1 Why is Pb–Sn a Eutectic System?

- **Pb** and **Sn** have **limited solid solubility** in each other.
- Maximum solid solubility of Sn in Pb (Pb-rich α phase) ≈ **19.2 wt%** at the eutectic temperature (183 °C).
- Maximum solid solubility of Pb in Sn (Sn-rich β phase) ≈ **2.5 wt%** at 183 °C.
- This limited solubility gives the characteristic **"humped liquidus"** with a low-melting eutectic point.

### 2.4.2 Key Features of the Pb–Sn Diagram

| Feature | Temperature | Composition | Significance |
|---------|-------------|-------------|--------------|
| Eutectic point | **183 °C** | **61.9 wt% Sn** (38.1 wt% Pb) | Lowest melting point of any Pb–Sn mixture |
| Eutectic reaction | At 183 °C | — | L (61.9% Sn) → α (19.2% Sn) + β (97.5% Sn) |
| α phase (Pb-rich) | < 327 °C | 0–19.2% Sn | Soft, ductile, FCC |
| β phase (Sn-rich) | < 232 °C | 97.5–100% Sn | Hard, BCT (tin structure) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **60/40 Pb–Sn solder** (60 wt% Sn, 40 wt% Pb) is **almost exactly the eutectic composition**, hence it has the lowest melting point (≈ 183 °C) and a **sharp single-temperature solidification** — making it the most widely used solder in electronics assembly.

## 2.5 The Silver–Platinum (Ag–Pt) System — Isomorphous Type

### 2.5.1 Why is Ag–Pt a Complete Solid Solubility (Isomorphous) System?

- Both Ag and Pt are **face-centered cubic (FCC)** metals.
- Their atomic radii are very similar (Ag ≈ 0.144 nm, Pt ≈ 0.139 nm).
- Same crystal structure + similar size → **unlimited substitutional solid solubility** over the entire composition range (Hume-Rothery rules satisfied).

### 2.5.2 Key Features of the Ag–Pt Diagram

- **No eutectic, no peritectic** — just a single lens-shaped two-phase (L + α) region sandwiched between a single-phase liquid region (top) and a single-phase solid α region (bottom).
- Liquidus and solidus curves meet at the **pure-metal melting points**: pure Ag melts at **961 °C**, pure Pt melts at **1769 °C**.
- Solidus is **always below** the liquidus (separation decreases as composition approaches pure ends).
- Solid-state cooling shows **no phase transformation** (no allotropic change).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When asked *"Why is Ag–Pt isomorphous?"*, the model answer should mention **Hume-Rothery's 4 conditions**: (1) Same crystal structure (FCC), (2) Atomic radius difference < 15%, (3) Same valence, (4) Similar electronegativity. All four are satisfied for Ag–Pt.

## 2.6 The Iron–Iron Carbide (Fe–Fe₃C) System — The Backbone

This is the **single most important phase diagram in all of metallurgy**, as it governs the behavior of all **carbon steels** and **cast irons**.

### 2.6.1 Solid Phases in the Fe–Fe₃C Diagram

| Phase | Crystal Structure | Maximum C Solubility | Description |
|-------|-------------------|----------------------|-------------|
| **α-ferrite** | BCC | 0.022 wt% C at 727 °C | Soft, ductile, magnetic below 770 °C (Curie T) |
| **γ-austenite** | FCC | 2.14 wt% C at 1147 °C | Ductile, non-magnetic, dense |
| **δ-ferrite** | BCC | 0.09 wt% C at 1493 °C | High-T BCC phase |
| **Fe₃C (cementite)** | Orthorhombic | 6.70 wt% C (stoichiometric) | Hard, brittle intermetallic |

### 2.6.2 The Five Most Critical Invariant Points

| Point | Temperature | Composition | Reaction |
|-------|-------------|-------------|----------|
| **A** (peritectic) | 1493 °C | 0.16 wt% C | L + δ → γ |
| **B** | 1493 °C | 0.53 wt% C | — (pure δ boundary) |
| **C** (eutectic) | 1147 °C | 4.30 wt% C | L → γ + Fe₃C |
| **D** | 1147 °C | 6.70 wt% C | — (pure Fe₃C boundary) |
| **E** | 1147 °C | 2.14 wt% C | Max C solubility in γ |
| **G** (eutectoid) | 727 °C | 0.76 wt% C | γ → α + Fe₃C |
| **P** | 727 °C | 0.022 wt% C | Max C solubility in α |
| **Q** | 727 °C (room T line) | 0.008 wt% C | C solubility in α at RT |

### 2.6.3 The Eutectoid Reaction — The Heart of Heat Treatment

$$\gamma \;(0.76\% \, C) \;\;\xrightarrow{\text{cooling at } 727 \, °C}\;\; \alpha \;(0.022\% \, C) \;+\; \text{Fe}_3\text{C} \;(6.70\% \, C)$$

- The product **α + Fe₃C** is called **PEARLITE** — a lamellar (alternating plate) microstructure.
- **Pearlite is NOT a phase** — it is a **two-phase aggregate** (mechanical mixture) of soft ferrite and hard cementite.
- The **eutectoid composition (0.76 wt% C)** defines the dividing line between:
  - **Hypoeutectoid steels:** C < 0.76%
  - **Eutectoid steel:** C = 0.76% (100% pearlite)
  - **Hypereutectoid steels:** 0.76% < C < 2.14%

### 2.6.4 Classification of Steels and Cast Irons

| Range | Classification | Microstructure (Slow Cool) |
|-------|---------------|----------------------------|
| 0 – 0.022 wt% C | **Pure iron / low-C iron** | 100% ferrite (α) |
| 0.022 – 0.76 wt% C | **Hypoeutectoid steel** | Proeutectoid ferrite + pearlite |
| 0.76 wt% C | **Eutectoid steel** | 100% pearlite |
| 0.76 – 2.14 wt% C | **Hypereutectoid steel** | Proeutectoid cementite + pearlite |
| 2.14 – 4.30 wt% C | **Eutectic cast iron** | Austenite transforms on further cooling + ledeburite |
| 4.30 – 6.70 wt% C | **Hypereutectic cast iron** | Proeutectic cementite + ledeburite |

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Application |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $F = C - P + 2$ | Gibbs Phase Rule (general) | General systems |
| 2 | $F = C - P + 1$ | Condensed Phase Rule | Binary at constant P |
| 3 | $W_{\alpha} = \dfrac{C_{\beta} - C_0}{C_{\beta} - C_{\alpha}}$ | Mass fraction of $\alpha$ phase | Two-phase regions |
| 4 | $W_{\beta} = \dfrac{C_0 - C_{\alpha}}{C_{\beta} - C_{\alpha}}$ | Mass fraction of $\beta$ phase | Two-phase regions |
| 5 | $W_{\alpha} + W_{\beta} = 1$ | Conservation of mass | Sanity check |
| 6 | $\text{Hypoeutectoid} \rightarrow C < 0.76\%$ | Below eutectoid C | Steel classification |
| 7 | $\text{Hypereutectoid} \rightarrow C > 0.76\%$ | Above eutectoid C | Steel classification |
| 8 | $\gamma \rightarrow \alpha + \text{Fe}_3\text{C}$ | Eutectoid reaction | At 727 °C, 0.76% C |
| 9 | $L \rightarrow \gamma + \text{Fe}_3\text{C}$ | Eutectic reaction | At 1147 °C, 4.30% C |
| 10 | $L + \delta \rightarrow \gamma$ | Peritectic reaction | At 1493 °C, 0.16% C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value separators in the formulas above are written using **fraction notation** (e.g., $\frac{C_\beta - C_0}{C_\beta - C_\alpha}$) instead of the vertical pipe `|`. This is to preserve the markdown table cell integrity. The mathematical meaning is identical.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| System | Industrial Application |
|--------|----------------------|
| **Pb–Sn** | Electronic solders (60/40, 63/37 eutectic), plumbing joints, pewter |
| **Ag–Pt** | Electrical contacts, thermocouple wires (Pt–Pt/Rh), jewellery, dental alloys, laboratory crucibles |
| **Fe–Fe₃C** | All carbon steels (structural, automotive, rails, gears), cast irons (engine blocks, pipes), tool steels, surgical instruments |

> [!NOTE]
> Modern solders are increasingly **Pb-free** (e.g., Sn–Ag–Cu, SAC alloys) due to RoHS environmental regulations. The Ag–Pt and Fe–Fe₃C systems, however, remain metallurgically central and have NO modern substitutes.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Lever Rule

The lever rule is derived directly from the **principle of mass conservation**. Consider an alloy of overall mass $M$ and composition $C_0$ inside an $(\alpha + \beta)$ two-phase region at temperature $T_1$.

The mass balance equations are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\alpha} \;+\; M_{\beta} \;&=\; M \\[4pt]
M_{\alpha} \, C_{\alpha} \;+\; M_{\beta} \, C_{\beta} \;&=\; M \, C_0
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $M_\alpha$ and $M_\beta$ are the masses of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ phases, and $C_\alpha$, $C_\beta$ are the compositions of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ read off the tie-line endpoints.

Dividing both equations by $M$ and defining mass fractions $W_\alpha = M_\alpha / M$ and $W_\beta = M_\beta / M$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\alpha} \;+\; W_{\beta} \;&=\; 1 \\[4pt]
W_{\alpha} \, C_{\alpha} \;+\; W_{\beta} \, C_{\beta} \;&=\; C_0
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $W_\beta = 1 - W_\alpha$ from the first equation into the second:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\alpha} \, C_{\alpha} \;+\; (1 - W_{\alpha}) \, C_{\beta} \;&=\; C_0 \\[4pt]
W_{\alpha} \, C_{\alpha} \;+\; C_{\beta} \;-\; W_{\alpha} \, C_{\beta} \;&=\; C_0 \\[4pt]
W_{\alpha} \, (C_{\alpha} - C_{\beta}) \;&=\; C_0 - C_{\beta} \\[4pt]
W_{\alpha} \;&=\; \frac{C_0 - C_{\beta}}{C_{\alpha} - C_{\beta}} \;\;\equiv\;\; \frac{C_{\beta} - C_0}{C_{\beta} - C_{\alpha}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the lever arm from $C_0$ to $C_\beta$ corresponds to the **opposite end** ($\alpha$ phase), and the sign flip converts $(C_0 - C_\beta)$ to $(C_\beta - C_0)$, the conventional form is:

$$\boxed{\,W_{\alpha} \;=\; \frac{C_{\beta} - C_0}{C_{\beta} - C_{\alpha}} \quad\quad W_{\beta} \;=\; \frac{C_0 - C_{\alpha}}{C_{\beta} - C_{\alpha}}\,}$$

This confirms: **the fraction of a phase is proportional to the lever arm on the *opposite* side of the tie-line.**

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Pb–Sn System (Lever Rule)

> **Problem:** A **30 wt% Sn – 70 wt% Pb** alloy is slowly cooled from the liquid state. Determine (a) the temperature at which the first solid forms, (b) the composition of the first solid, (c) the amounts and compositions of phases at 150 °C, and (d) the temperature at which the last liquid solidifies.

**Given:** $C_0 = 30 \text{ wt\% Sn}$ (so 70 wt% Pb)

### Part (a) & (b): First solid forms

Move vertically down the 30% Sn composition line from the liquid region. The first intersection with the **liquidus** curve occurs at approximately:

$$T_{\text{liquidus}} \;\approx\; 260 \;°C$$

At this temperature, the tie-line intersects the **solidus** on the α-phase boundary at:

$$C_{\alpha} \;\approx\; 12 \text{ wt\% Sn}$$

This is the composition of the **first solid** (Pb-rich α) to form.

### Part (c): Phases at 150 °C

At 150 °C, the 30% Sn isotherm cuts through the **(α + β) two-phase region**.

Reading tie-line endpoints at 150 °C:
- $C_{\alpha} \approx 5 \text{ wt\% Sn}$ (left endpoint — α phase)
- $C_{\beta} \approx 98 \text{ wt\% Sn}$ (right endpoint — β phase)

Applying the lever rule:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\alpha} \;&=\; \frac{C_{\beta} - C_0}{C_{\beta} - C_{\alpha}} \;=\; \frac{98 - 30}{98 - 5} \;=\; \frac{68}{93} \;\approx\; 0.731 \\[6pt]
W_{\beta} \;&=\; \frac{C_0 - C_{\alpha}}{C_{\beta} - C_{\alpha}} \;=\; \frac{30 - 5}{98 - 5} \;=\; \frac{25}{93} \;\approx\; 0.269
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sanity check:** $0.731 + 0.269 = 1.000$ ✓

> **Result at 150 °C:** The alloy is **73.1% α-phase (5% Sn)** and **26.9% β-phase (98% Sn)** by mass.

### Part (d): Last liquid to solidify

Continue cooling 30% Sn composition line. The last intersection with the **liquidus** (going down) before entering the all-α region occurs at the **solidus**, approximately at:

$$T_{\text{solidus}} \;\approx\; 183 \;°C \quad \text{(coincidentally near the eutectic line for off-eutectic alloys)}$$

> **Answer Summary:**
> - First solid forms at ≈ 260 °C with composition ≈ 12% Sn (α)
> - At 150 °C: 73.1% α (5% Sn) + 26.9% β (98% Sn)
> - Last liquid disappears at ≈ 200 °C (precise value depends on which side of eutectic)

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Fe–Fe₃C System (Hypoeutectoid Steel)

> **Problem:** A **0.4 wt% C** steel is slowly cooled from 1000 °C to room temperature. Determine: (a) the phases present just above 727 °C, (b) the phase fractions just above 727 °C, (c) the phases present just below 727 °C, (d) the weight percent of proeutectoid ferrite and pearlite in the final microstructure at room temperature.

**Given:** $C_0 = 0.4 \text{ wt\% C}$

### Part (a) & (b): Just above 727 °C (e.g., at 730 °C)

At 730 °C, 0.4% C is in the **(α + γ)** two-phase field.

Reading the tie-line at 727 °C⁺:
- $C_{\alpha} = 0.022 \text{ wt\% C}$ (boundary of α solubility)
- $C_{\gamma} = 0.76 \text{ wt\% C}$ (boundary of γ solubility)

Applying the lever rule:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\alpha} \;&=\; \frac{C_{\gamma} - C_0}{C_{\gamma} - C_{\alpha}} \;=\; \frac{0.76 - 0.40}{0.76 - 0.022} \;=\; \frac{0.36}{0.738} \;\approx\; 0.488 \\[6pt]
W_{\gamma} \;&=\; \frac{C_0 - C_{\alpha}}{C_{\gamma} - C_{\alpha}} \;=\; \frac{0.40 - 0.022}{0.76 - 0.022} \;=\; \frac{0.378}{0.738} \;\approx\; 0.512
\end{aligned}
$$

**Just above 727 °C: 48.8% ferrite (α) + 51.2% austenite (γ)**

### Part (c) & (d): Just below 727 °C (e.g., at 725 °C)

The remaining austenite (0.76% C) **decomposes via the eutectoid reaction** into pearlite (α + Fe₃C mixture). The pre-existing α remains unchanged.

**At room temperature:**
- The α that existed at 727 °C⁺ = **proeutectoid ferrite** = 48.8% of the alloy
- The γ at 727 °C⁺ = 51.2% of the alloy **transforms entirely to pearlite**

**Phase fractions in final microstructure:**
- **Proeutectoid ferrite** = **48.8 wt%**
- **Pearlite (α + Fe₃C aggregate)** = **51.2 wt%**

> **Note on finer calculation:** If you are asked the amounts of *α* and *Fe₃C* within the pearlite, apply the lever rule again to pearlite treated as an "alloy" of composition 0.76% C, with phases α (0.022% C) and Fe₃C (6.70% C):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\alpha \text{ in pearlite}} \;&=\; \frac{6.70 - 0.76}{6.70 - 0.022} \;\approx\; 0.889 \\[4pt]
W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C} \text{ in pearlite}} \;&=\; \frac{0.76 - 0.022}{6.70 - 0.022} \;\approx\; 0.111
\end{aligned}
$$

So within the 51.2% pearlite:
- α (from pearlite) = 0.512 × 0.889 = **0.455 = 45.5%** of total
- Fe₃C (from pearlite) = 0.512 × 0.111 = **0.057 = 5.7%** of total
- Total α in microstructure = 48.8% + 45.5% = **94.3%**
- Total Fe₃C in microstructure = **5.7%**

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When asked for "amount of pearlite" in a hypoeutectoid steel, the shortcut is simply: **$W_{\text{pearlite}} = \frac{C_0 - 0.022}{0.76 - 0.022}$**. Similarly, the amount of proeutectoid ferrite = $\frac{0.76 - C_0}{0.76 - 0.022}$.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Ag–Pt System (Isomorphous, Lever Rule)

> **Problem:** A **50 wt% Pt – 50 wt% Ag** alloy is at **1500 °C**. Determine: (a) the phases present, (b) the compositions of each phase, and (c) the mass fraction of each phase.

**Given:** $C_0 = 50 \text{ wt\% Pt}$, $T = 1500 \;°C$

### Step 1: Read the Phase Diagram

At 1500 °C and 50% Pt, we are inside the **(L + α) two-phase lens**.

Reading tie-line endpoints:
- $C_L \approx 40 \text{ wt\% Pt}$
- $C_\alpha \approx 65 \text{ wt\% Pt}$

### Step 2: Apply the Lever Rule

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_L \;&=\; \frac{C_{\alpha} - C_0}{C_{\alpha} - C_L} \;=\; \frac{65 - 50}{65 - 40} \;=\; \frac{15}{25} \;=\; 0.60 \\[6pt]
W_{\alpha} \;&=\; \frac{C_0 - C_L}{C_{\alpha} - C_L} \;=\; \frac{50 - 40}{65 - 40} \;=\; \frac{10}{25} \;=\; 0.40
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** **60% liquid (40% Pt) + 40% α-solid (65% Pt)** by mass.

> **Sanity check:** $0.60 + 0.40 = 1.00$ ✓
> Mass balance on Pt: $0.60 \times 40 + 0.40 \times 65 = 24 + 26 = 50$ ✓ (matches $C_0$)

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Gibbs Phase Rule Application

> **Problem:** In a binary Cu–Zn alloy at atmospheric pressure, three phases (α, β, and liquid) coexist in equilibrium. What is the degrees of freedom $F$?

**Solution:** Using the condensed phase rule $F = C - P + 1$:

$$
F \;=\; 2 \;-\; 3 \;+\; 1 \;=\; 0
$$

**Interpretation:** This is an **invariant point** (e.g., a peritectic or eutectic). Both temperature and composition are fixed — we cannot change either without losing the three-phase equilibrium.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — At a Glance

| Alloy / System | Region | Phases | Composition Range | Tie-Line Formula |
|----------------|--------|--------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Pb–Sn, 30% Sn at 150 °C** | α + β | 2 | 5% ≤ C ≤ 98% Sn | $W_\alpha = (98-30)/(98-5)$ |
| **Fe–C, 0.4% C at 727 °C⁺** | α + γ | 2 | 0.022% ≤ C ≤ 0.76% | $W_\alpha = (0.76-0.4)/(0.76-0.022)$ |
| **Fe–C, 1.0% C at 727 °C⁺** | γ + Fe₃C | 2 | 0.76% ≤ C ≤ 6.70% | $W_\gamma = (6.70-1.0)/(6.70-0.76)$ |
| **Ag–Pt, 50% Pt at 1500 °C** | L + α | 2 | 40% ≤ C ≤ 65% Pt | $W_L = (65-50)/(65-40)$ |
| **Fe–C, at 1147 °C, 4.3% C** | L + γ + Fe₃C | 3 | Invariant | Eutectic |
| **Fe–C, at 727 °C, 0.76% C** | α + γ + Fe₃C | 3 | Invariant | Eutectoid |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Phase Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[BINARY PHASE DIAGRAM] --> B[Single-Phase Regions]
    A --> C[Two-Phase Regions]
    A --> D[Three-Phase Invariant Points]

    B --> B1["Liquid L region<br/>above liquidus"]
    B --> B2["Solid alpha region<br/>FCC or BCC solvent-rich"]
    B --> B3["Solid beta region<br/>secondary solid solution"]
    B --> B4["Intermetallic compound<br/>e.g. Fe3C cementite"]

    C --> C1["L plus alpha<br/>solidification range"]
    C --> C2["L plus beta<br/>solidification range"]
    C --> C3["alpha plus beta<br/>solid-state two-phase"]
    C --> C4["alpha plus gamma<br/>e.g. ferrite-austenite"]

    D --> D1["Eutectic point<br/>L to alpha plus beta"]
    D --> D2["Eutectoid point<br/>gamma to alpha plus Fe3C"]
    D --> D3["Peritectic point<br/>L plus delta to gamma"]

    C1 --> R1["Apply LEVER RULE<br/>to find phase fractions"]
    C2 --> R1
    C3 --> R1
    C4 --> R1
```

## 4.4 Decision Flow for Phase Identification (KTU Problem-Solving Aid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    Q2 -->|SINGLE-PHASE| SP["Answer: One phase only.<br/>Note phase identity and<br/>composition = alloy composition."]

    Q2 -->|TWO-PHASE| TP["Step 1: Draw tie-line<br/>Step 2: Read C_alpha and C_beta<br/>Step 3: Apply Lever Rule<br/>Step 4: Report W_alpha and W_beta"]

```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    A2 --> A3["Step 3: Identify phase<br/>change arrest points<br/>at each composition"]
    A3 --> A4["Step 4: Plot T vs<br/>composition; mark<br/>each arrest temperature"]
    A4 --> A5["Step 5: Connect points<br/>to draw liquidus,<br/>solidus, solvus lines"]
    A5 --> A6["Step 6: Identify and<br/>label invariant points<br/>eutectic, eutectoid, peritectic"]
    A6 --> A7["Step 7: Verify using<br/>Gibbs Phase Rule and<br/>thermodynamic data"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**State and explain the Gibbs Phase Rule. Why is the constant "2" sometimes replaced by "1" in binary phase diagrams?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Gibbs Phase Rule** relates the number of degrees of freedom ($F$), number of components ($C$), and number of coexisting phases ($P$) in a system at equilibrium:

$$F \;=\; C \;-\; P \;+\; 2$$

- $F$ = degrees of freedom (independent variables: T, P, composition)
- $C$ = minimum number of chemical species to describe the system
- $P$ = number of phases coexisting at equilibrium
- The "**2**" accounts for the two external intensive variables: **temperature (T)** and **pressure (P)**.

For **binary phase diagrams**, the diagrams are constructed at **constant atmospheric pressure**. With $P$ fixed, the rule simplifies to the **Condensed Phase Rule**:

$$F \;=\; C \;-\; P \;+\; 1$$

The constant "2" is replaced by "1" because one external variable (pressure) is held constant. The remaining variables we can vary are **temperature** and **composition** only.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between a eutectic and a eutectoid reaction with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Eutectic Reaction | Eutectoid Reaction |
|--------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Generic form | L → α + β | γ → α + β |
| Phases involved | **1 liquid** → 2 solids | **1 solid** → 2 solids |
| Example | In Pb–Sn: L (61.9% Sn) → α + β at 183 °C | In Fe–Fe₃C: γ (0.76% C) → α + Fe₃C at 727 °C |
| Industrial significance | Low-melting solders | Foundation of steel heat treatment |

The key distinction: **Eutectic involves a liquid phase; eutectoid involves only solids.** Both are invariant three-phase reactions where $F = 0$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates internal choice in Part B. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are the two alternatives.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** Draw the **iron–iron carbide (Fe–Fe₃C) equilibrium diagram** and label all the temperatures, compositions, phases, and invariant points. Describe the **eutectoid reaction** in detail.

**(b) [7 marks]** A **0.4 wt% C** hypoeutectoid steel is slowly cooled from 950 °C to room temperature. Calculate:
1. The amount of **proeutectoid ferrite** at room temperature.
2. The amount of **pearlite** at room temperature.
3. The amount of **total ferrite** (proeutectoid + eutectoid) at room temperature.
4. The amount of **cementite** at room temperature.

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Drawing of Fe–Fe₃C Diagram:**
The diagram should show:
- **X-axis:** 0 to 6.70 wt% C
- **Y-axis:** Temperature from 0 °C to ~1600 °C
- **Single-phase regions:** α (ferrite, BCC), γ (austenite, FCC), δ (delta ferrite, BCC), L (liquid), Fe₃C (cementite)
- **Two-phase regions:** α + γ, γ + Fe₃C, α + Fe₃C, L + γ, L + δ, δ + γ
- **Three horizontal lines** at 1493 °C (peritectic), 1147 °C (eutectic), 727 °C (eutectoid)
- **Critical points:** A, B, C, D, E, F, G, P, S, Q, N, H, J

**Eutectoid Reaction (at 727 °C, 0.76 wt% C):**

$$\gamma \;(0.76\% \, C) \;\;\xrightarrow{\text{cool below } 727 \, °C}\;\; \alpha \;(0.022\% \, C) \;+\; \text{Fe}_3\text{C} \;(6.70\% \, C)$$

- The **austenite** (γ) of eutectoid composition decomposes on slow cooling into a **lamellar aggregate of alternating ferrite and cementite plates**, called **PEARLITE**.
- Pearlite is a **two-phase microstructure** (not a single phase).
- $F = 2 - 3 + 1 = 0$ — invariant.
- The mechanical mixture exhibits an **average composition of 0.76% C**.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Given:** $C_0 = 0.4 \text{ wt\% C}$, hypoeutectoid steel.

**Step 1: Just above 727 °C, the alloy lies in the (α + γ) region.**

Reading tie-line endpoints:
- $C_{\alpha} = 0.022 \text{ wt\% C}$
- $C_{\gamma} = 0.76 \text{ wt\% C}$

**Step 2: Apply the Lever Rule at 727 °C⁺:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\alpha} \;&=\; \frac{C_{\gamma} - C_0}{C_{\gamma} - C_{\alpha}} \;=\; \frac{0.76 - 0.40}{0.76 - 0.022} \;=\; \frac{0.36}{0.738} \;\approx\; 0.488 \\[6pt]
W_{\gamma} \;&=\; \frac{C_0 - C_{\alpha}}{C_{\gamma} - C_{\alpha}} \;=\; \frac{0.40 - 0.022}{0.76 - 0.022} \;=\; \frac{0.378}{0.738} \;\approx\; 0.512
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: At 727 °C⁻, all austenite (51.2%) transforms to pearlite.**

**(1) Proeutectoid ferrite =** 0.488 = **48.8 wt%**

**(2) Pearlite =** 0.512 = **51.2 wt%**

**(3) Total ferrite = proeutectoid ferrite + α within pearlite:**

The α within pearlite = $W_{\text{pearlite}} \times W_{\alpha \, \text{in} \, \text{pearlite}}$

$$
W_{\alpha \, \text{in} \, \text{pearlite}} \;=\; \frac{6.70 - 0.76}{6.70 - 0.022} \;=\; \frac{5.94}{6.678} \;\approx\; 0.8895
$$

So $\alpha$ in pearlite = $0.512 \times 0.8895 = 0.4554 = 45.54\%$

**Total ferrite = 48.8% + 45.54% = 94.34 wt%**

**(4) Total cementite = Fe₃C within pearlite:**

$W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C} \, \text{in pearlite}} = \frac{0.76 - 0.022}{6.70 - 0.022} = \frac{0.738}{6.678} \approx 0.1105$

Fe₃C in pearlite = $0.512 \times 0.1105 = 0.0566 = 5.66\%$

**Total cementite = 5.66 wt%**

**Sanity check:** $94.34\% + 5.66\% = 100.00\%$ ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** What is an **isomorphous phase diagram**? Explain the features of the **Ag–Pt phase diagram**. Justify why Ag and Pt form a complete solid solution.

**(b) [7 marks]** A **50 wt% Pt – 50 wt% Ag** alloy is cooled from the liquid state.
1. At what temperature does the **first solid** appear?
2. At what temperature does the **last liquid** solidify?
3. What is the **composition of the last liquid** to solidify?
4. Determine the **amounts of liquid and solid** at 1500 °C.

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Isomorphous Phase Diagram Definition:**

An **isomorphous phase diagram** is one in which the two components exhibit **complete mutual solid solubility in the solid state** over the entire composition range (0 to 100%). The word *isomorphous* comes from Greek *iso* (same) + *morphe* (form), meaning the two components have the **same crystal structure**, allowing them to substitute for each other in a single solid solution phase.

**Features of the Ag–Pt Diagram:**

1. **No eutectic, no peritectic, no intermetallic compound.**
2. The diagram has only **three regions**:
   - Single-phase **liquid (L)** above the upper curve (liquidus)
   - Single-phase **solid solution α** below the lower curve (solidus)
   - Two-phase **(L + α)** lens-shaped region between them
3. The liquidus and solidus meet at the **pure-metal melting points**: pure Ag at **961 °C**, pure Pt at **1769 °C**.
4. The maximum gap between liquidus and solidus occurs at intermediate compositions (around 50–60% Pt) — this is the region of greatest solidification range.

**Why Ag and Pt Form a Complete Solid Solution (Hume-Rothery's 4 Conditions):**

| # | Condition | Ag | Pt | Satisfied? |
|---|-----------|----|----|-----------|
| 1 | Same crystal structure | FCC | FCC | ✓ |
| 2 | Atomic radius difference < 15% | 0.144 nm | 0.139 nm (~3.5% diff) | ✓ |
| 3 | Same valence | +1 | +2 (but similar metallic character) | ✓ (approx) |
| 4 | Similar electronegativity | 1.93 | 2.28 | ✓ (close) |

All four conditions are satisfied → **complete solid solubility (isomorphous system)**.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Given:** 50 wt% Pt – 50 wt% Ag, $C_0 = 50\% \text{ Pt}$

**Reading from the Ag–Pt diagram (approximate values):**

**(1) First solid forms** when the 50% Pt vertical line first intersects the **liquidus** from above:
$$T_{\text{liquidus}} \;\approx\; 1700 \;°C$$

The composition of this first solid (read at the solidus intersection of the same isothermal line) is:
$$C_{\alpha, \text{first}} \;\approx\; 60 \text{ wt\% Pt}$$

**(2) Last liquid solidifies** when the 50% Pt line crosses the **solidus** (going down):
$$T_{\text{solidus}} \;\approx\; 1550 \;°C$$

**(3) Composition of the last liquid** (read at the liquidus intersection of the 1550 °C isothermal line):
$$C_{L, \text{last}} \;\approx\; 40 \text{ wt\% Pt}$$

**(4) Amounts of L and α at 1500 °C** (within the (L + α) lens):

At 1500 °C, the 50% Pt tie-line endpoints are approximately:
- $C_L \approx 38 \text{ wt\% Pt}$
- $C_\alpha \approx 63 \text{ wt\% Pt}$

Applying the lever rule:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_L \;&=\; \frac{C_{\alpha} - C_0}{C_{\alpha} - C_L} \;=\; \frac{63 - 50}{63 - 38} \;=\; \frac{13}{25} \;=\; 0.52 \\[6pt]
W_{\alpha} \;&=\; \frac{C_0 - C_L}{C_{\alpha} - C_L} \;=\; \frac{50 - 38}{63 - 38} \;=\; \frac{12}{25} \;=\; 0.48
\end{aligned}
$$

**At 1500 °C: 52% liquid (38% Pt) + 48% α-solid (63% Pt)** by mass.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. **Confusing "first solid forms" temperature with "eutectic" temperature.** The first solid in an isomorphous system forms when the liquidus is crossed, NOT at a fixed invariant point.
> 2. **Misreading "composition of first solid."** It is read from the **solidus**, not the liquidus.
> 3. **Inverting the lever rule** — the fraction is proportional to the lever arm on the **opposite** side, not the same side.
> 4. **Using wt% in one formula and mol% in another.** Always check units.
> 5. **Forgetting the sanity check:** Always verify $W_\alpha + W_\beta = 1$ and the mass balance of the solute.

---

## 5.3 KTU 2024 Board Exam — Additional Practice Questions

### Quick-Fire Conceptual Questions (2–3 marks)

1. **[KTU 2022]** What is the **maximum solubility of carbon in austenite** and at what temperature? *(Ans: 2.14 wt% at 1147 °C)*
2. **[KTU 2023]** Name the **eutectoid product** in the Fe–Fe₃C system. *(Ans: Pearlite)*
3. **[KTU 2024]** Why is **60/40 Pb–Sn solder** so widely used? *(Ans: Near-eutectic composition → low melting point, sharp solidification)*
4. **[KTU 2024]** Define **solvus line** with reference to the Fe–Fe₃C diagram. *(Ans: Locus representing the limit of solid solubility — e.g., the α/(α+γ) boundary up to point P)*
5. **[KTU 2023]** State **Hume-Rothery's rules** for extensive solid solubility. *(Ans: 4 conditions listed in §2.5.2)*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Exam Checklist — Memorize This:**

### Phase Rule
- [ ] General: $F = C - P + 2$
- [ ] Condensed (binary at constant P): $F = C - P + 1$
- [ ] At invariant point: $F = 0$ (three phases coexist)
- [ ] Every horizontal line on a binary diagram = 3-phase invariant reaction

### Lever Rule
- [ ] $W_\alpha = (C_\beta - C_0) / (C_\beta - C_\alpha)$
- [ ] Tie-line is **horizontal** at the temperature of interest
- [ ] Fraction is proportional to **opposite** lever arm
- [ ] Always verify: $W_\alpha + W_\beta = 1$

### Pb–Sn System
- [ ] Eutectic at **183 °C, 61.9 wt% Sn**
- [ ] Eutectic reaction: L → α + β
- [ ] Max Sn in α = 19.2%; Max Pb in β = 2.5%
- [ ] 60/40 Sn–Pb = eutectic solder

### Ag–Pt System
- [ ] Isomorphous (complete solid solubility)
- [ ] Single α-phase field + (L + α) lens + pure liquid
- [ ] Hume-Rothery: same FCC structure, similar radius, similar valence, similar electronegativity

### Fe–Fe₃C System — THE MOST CRITICAL
- [ ] Eutectoid at **727 °C, 0.76 wt% C** → γ → α + Fe₃C (pearlite)
- [ ] Eutectic at **1147 °C, 4.30 wt% C** → L → γ + Fe₃C (ledeburite)
- [ ] Peritectic at **1493 °C, 0.16 wt% C** → L + δ → γ
- [ ] **Phases:** α (BCC, ferrite, 0.022% C max), γ (FCC, austenite, 2.14% C max), δ (BCC, 0.09% C), Fe₃C (cementite, 6.70% C, hard brittle)
- [ ] **Steel classifications:** Hypoeutectoid (< 0.76% C) = α + pearlite; Eutectoid (0.76% C) = pearlite only; Hypereutectoid (0.76–2.14% C) = Fe₃C + pearlite
- [ ] **Cast iron classifications:** Hypoeutectic (2.14–4.30% C), Eutectic (4.30% C), Hypereutectic (4.30–6.70% C)

### Construction of Phase Diagram
- [ ] Step 1: Prepare alloys of varying compositions
- [ ] Step 2: Record cooling curves (T vs time) for each alloy
- [ ] Step 3: Identify phase-change arrest points (slope changes in cooling curve)
- [ ] Step 4: Plot T vs composition, mark each arrest temperature
- [ ] Step 5: Connect points → draw liquidus, solidus, solvus
- [ ] Step 6: Identify and label invariant points
- [ ] Step 7: Verify with phase rule & thermodynamic data (CALPHAD, etc.)

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- [ ] Do NOT confuse **eutectic** (L → solids) with **eutectoid** (solid → solids).
- [ ] Do NOT confuse **peritectic** (L + solid → solid) with **eutectic** (L → solids).
- [ ] Do NOT use **mol%** if the problem gives **wt%** (or vice versa) without conversion.
- [ ] In the lever rule, the numerator and denominator of $W_\alpha$ are on **opposite sides** of $C_0$.
- [ ] At eutectoid composition, the alloy is **100% pearlite** (not 100% ferrite, even though ferrite dominates by volume).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recovery, Recrystallization and Grain Growth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Recovery, Recrystallization and Grain Growth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Happens to a Metal After Cold Working?

When a metal is **cold worked** (plastically deformed at a temperature below its recrystallization temperature), its mechanical and physical properties change drastically:

- **Strength** and **hardness** increase (strain hardening / work hardening).
- **Ductility** decreases.
- **Electrical resistivity** increases.
- A large amount of **internal (stored) energy** — roughly **1–10 % of the total plastic work** — is locked inside the crystal as a tangle of **dislocations** and **point defects** (vacancies, interstitials).

This stored energy is the *thermodynamic fuel* that drives the next three thermally activated processes we are about to study.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 3**
> Annealing is a heat-treatment process consisting of **three sequential but overlapping stages**:
> 1. **Recovery**
> 2. **Recrystallization**
> 3. **Grain Growth**
> Together they are sometimes called *"the three R's"* and represent the *thermodynamic return* of a cold-worked metal toward its equilibrium, low-energy state.

## 1.2 The Three R's — One-Line Academic Definitions

| Stage | Formal KTU Definition |
|---|---|
| **Recovery** | The low-temperature annealing stage in which physical properties (especially electrical resistivity) are largely restored to their pre-cold-work values *without any observable change* in the optical microstructure (grain size, shape, or orientation remain unchanged). |
| **Recrystallization** | The formation of a *new set of strain-free, equiaxed grains* that nucleate and grow at the expense of the deformed matrix, replacing the cold-worked structure. |
| **Grain Growth** | The continued migration of grain boundaries *after recrystallization is complete*, driven by the reduction of total grain-boundary area (and hence total interfacial energy). |

## 1.3 The Spring-and-Crumpled-Paper Analogy 🧻

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy (read this twice — it is worth 3 marks on its own)**
> Imagine you take a perfectly smooth sheet of paper and crumple it into a tight ball. The paper is now:
> - **Stronger** to tear in some directions (analogous to work hardening),
> - **Full of internal creases** that store energy (analogous to dislocations),
> - **Stiff but not ductile** (analogous to loss of ductility).
>
> Now you **gently warm the paper** in a warm room:
> - *Recovery* → the small creases *uncurl themselves*; the paper "feels" softer, but you still cannot see individual fibres moving. The macroscopic shape (the ball) is unchanged.
> - *Recrystallization* → the paper *uncrumples entirely* and a brand-new, flat, smooth sheet emerges in its place — old creases vanish, new boundaries appear.
> - *Grain Growth* → the new sheet has many small "regions" (grains). With *more* heat and *more* time, the small regions **swallow each other up** to form fewer, larger regions — exactly like soap bubbles fusing together to reduce their total surface area.

This single analogy will let you answer **almost every conceptual question** on this topic.

## 1.4 Why Study This? — Engineering Relevance

- **Controlling final grain size** directly controls yield strength (Hall–Petch), toughness, and formability.
- **Annealing schedules** in industry (batch annealing of steel sheets, continuous annealing lines in automotive plants) are designed *precisely* around these three stages.
- **Superplastic forming** of Ti and Al alloys is only possible *after* grain growth has produced a *very fine, stable* grain size — the *opposite* of what one normally wants.
- **Welding** produces a heat-affected zone (HAZ) that undergoes all three stages; predicting HAZ grain size is critical for joint toughness.

---

> **Key Constants & Metrics (memorise for Part A):**
> - Stored energy of cold work ≈ **0.5 – 10 cal/g** (≈ 2 – 40 J/g)
> - Grain-boundary energy γ ≈ **0.5 – 1.0 J/m²** for most metals
> - Activation energy for grain boundary diffusion ≈ **100 – 200 kJ/mol**
> - Typical recrystallization temperature $T_R \approx 0.4\,T_m$ (Kelvin) for pure metals
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stage 1 — Recovery (Low-Temperature Annealing)

### What is happening at the atomic level?

The dislocation structure does **not disappear**, but it **reorganises** into a lower-energy configuration:

- **Point defects** (vacancies, interstitials) migrate to **dislocation cores** or **free surfaces** and annihilate. This is why *electrical resistivity* drops sharply — resistivity is extremely sensitive to point-defect concentration.
- Dislocations of **opposite sign** on the same slip plane *annihilate* each other.
- Dislocations of opposite sign on *parallel* slip planes **rearrange** into low-angle tilt or twist boundaries — these are called **sub-grain boundaries** or **subgrains**.

### Observable effects

| Property | Direction of change during recovery |
|---|---|
| Electrical resistivity | ↓ drops significantly (point defects removed) |
| Yield strength / hardness | ↓ slight drop only (dislocations remain) |
| Residual stresses | ↓↓ large drop (macro-stresses relieved) |
| Microstructure (optical) | No visible change |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key point the examiner expects:**
> Recovery is essentially a *microstructural* event (defect-level) but *not* a *microstructural* event in the optical-microscope sense. The grain boundaries we see under a light microscope are unchanged.

---

## 2.2 Stage 2 — Recrystallization

### Driving force

The driving force is the **stored energy of cold work** $E_s$ (J/m³ or J/mol). When a small, strain-free nucleus of radius $r$ forms inside the strained matrix, the *net* free-energy change is

$$\Delta G = -\frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}\, E_{s} + 4\pi r^{2}\,\gamma_{b}$$

where $\gamma_b$ is the new grain-boundary energy. The critical radius and activation energy are obtained exactly as in solidification theory:

$$r^{\star} = \frac{2\gamma_{b}}{E_{s}}, \qquad \Delta G^{\star} = \frac{16\pi \gamma_{b}^{3}}{3 E_{s}^{2}}$$

> **Why this matters:** Because $E_s$ appears *in the denominator squared*, a *small increase* in cold work produces a *huge decrease* in the nucleation barrier — this is why heavily cold-worked metals recrystallize almost explosively at the right temperature.

### The Recrystallization Temperature $T_R$

The **recrystallization temperature** is defined (KTU convention) as:

> *The temperature at which a heavily cold-worked metal (~ 40 – 70 % reduction) completely recrystallizes in approximately 1 hour.*

The well-known empirical rule is:

$$T_{R} \approx 0.4 \, T_{m} \quad \text{(in Kelvin, for pure metals)}$$

For alloys, $T_R$ is *higher* (solute atoms pin boundaries and dislocations). For very low melting metals like tin and lead, $T_R$ is *below room temperature* — they recrystallize at ambient conditions, which is why you cannot work-harden them.

### The Five Variables That Control the Recrystallized Grain Size

| # | Variable | Effect on recrystallized grain size |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Degree of cold work** ↑ | Grain size ↓ (more nuclei) |
| 2 | **Annealing temperature** ↑ | Grain size ↑ (faster growth) |
| 3 | **Annealing time** ↑ | Grain size ↑ (more growth) |
| 4 | **Purity / alloying** ↑ | Grain size ↓ (solute drag) |
| 5 | **Initial grain size of parent** ↓ | Recrystallized grain size ↓ (more nucleation sites) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Favourite:**
> "Discuss the variables that affect the recrystallized grain size" — the table above, *with one short explanatory sentence for each row*, is a complete answer.

---

## 2.3 Stage 3 — Grain Growth

After recrystallization is complete, the system is now an assembly of strain-free grains. The driving force is no longer $E_s$ (which is now zero) but the **grain-boundary energy itself**.

### Driving force for a spherical grain of radius $r$

The pressure difference across a curved grain boundary of surface energy $\gamma$ is the **Laplace / capillary pressure**:

$$P = \frac{2\gamma}{r}$$

> The boundary migrates *toward its centre of curvature* (i.e., the smaller grain is consumed by the larger one). The result is *fewer, larger* grains with a *lower* total boundary area.

---

## 2.4 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning / Use | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta G^{\star} = \dfrac{16\pi \gamma_{b}^{3}}{3 E_{s}^{2}}$ | Critical nucleus for recrystallization | J |
| 2 | $T_{R} \approx 0.4\,T_{m}$ | Empirical recrystallization temperature (Kelvin) | K |
| 3 | $P = \dfrac{2\gamma}{r}$ | Capillary pressure driving grain-boundary migration | Pa (N/m²) |
| 4 | $\dfrac{dD}{dt} = \dfrac{K}{D}$ | Ideal grain-growth law (parabolic) | m/s |
| 5 | $D^{n} - D_{0}^{\,n} = K\,t\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$ | **Beck's equation** (general grain growth) | m, s, K |
| 6 | $\text{Rate} = A\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius kinetics (any thermally activated process) | s⁻¹ |
| 7 | $\sigma_{y} = \sigma_{0} + k_{y}\,d^{-1/2}$ | **Hall–Petch** — links grain size to yield strength | MPa, m⁻¹ᐟ² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip — Beck's Equation (5):**
> $n = 2$ for *ideal* (parabolic) grain growth; $n \ge 2$ for real materials where solute drag, second-phase particles, or texture slow boundary migration.

### Reading Beck's equation like a KTU topper

- Take $\ln$ of both sides:

$$\ln\!\left(D^{n} - D_{0}^{\,n}\right) = \ln K + \ln t - \frac{Q}{R\,T}$$

- For a fixed time $t$, plot $\ln(D^n - D_0^n)$ vs $1/T$ → slope $= -Q/R$ gives the **activation energy of grain boundary migration**.
- This is how examiners (and researchers) *measure* $Q$ experimentally.

---

## 2.5 Pinning of Grain Boundaries — The Zener Limit

Second-phase particles pin grain boundaries. The *limiting* (maximum) grain radius a particle of radius $r_p$ and volume fraction $f$ can support is given by the **Smith–Zener relation**:

$$D_{\max} = \frac{4\,r_{p}}{3\,f}$$

> **Real-world use:** This is why aluminium alloys with dispersed $\text{Al}_3\text{Sc}$ particles, or steels with $\text{NbC}$ or $\text{TiC}$ precipitates, retain a *fine* grain size even after long, high-temperature service.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section is **derivation-heavy by design**. Every algebraic step is shown; no step is skipped. These are *exactly* the working-out style the KTU Board examiner expects in the 14-mark answer.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Driving Force for Grain-Boundary Migration (Capillary Pressure)

**Statement:** Show that the net hydrostatic pressure pushing a curved grain boundary toward its centre of curvature equals $P = 2\gamma/r$.

### Step 1 — Set up the geometry

Consider a *spherical* grain of radius $r$ surrounded by a continuous matrix. The grain boundary is a thin spherical shell of surface energy $\gamma$ (J/m²).

### Step 2 — Virtual displacement

Suppose the boundary moves outward by an infinitesimal distance $\mathrm{d}r$. The new volume gained by the grain is

$$\mathrm{d}V = 4\pi r^{2}\,\mathrm{d}r$$

The new boundary area becomes

$$A = 4\pi (r + \mathrm{d}r)^{2} \approx 4\pi r^{2} + 8\pi r\,\mathrm{d}r$$

so the *change* in boundary area is

$$\mathrm{d}A = 8\pi r\,\mathrm{d}r$$

### Step 3 — Change in total surface energy

The total grain-boundary energy is $E_{b} = \gamma A$, so

$$\mathrm{d}E_{b} = \gamma\,\mathrm{d}A = \gamma \cdot 8\pi r\,\mathrm{d}r$$

### Step 4 — Energy balance with the work done by the driving pressure

The pressure $P$ acting on the boundary does work $P\,\mathrm{d}V$ as the boundary sweeps out:

$$P\,\mathrm{d}V = P \cdot 4\pi r^{2}\,\mathrm{d}r$$

For the system to *decrease* its energy, this work must equal the *decrease* in boundary energy:

$$P \cdot 4\pi r^{2}\,\mathrm{d}r = \gamma \cdot 8\pi r\,\mathrm{d}r$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $P$

Cancelling $4\pi r\,\mathrm{d}r$ from both sides:

$$\boxed{\,P = \frac{2\gamma}{r}\,}$$

### Step 6 — Physical interpretation

- For a *small* grain (small $r$), $P$ is *large* — the driving force for that grain to shrink is high.
- For a *large* grain, $P$ is *small* — large grains barely feel a driving force.
- This asymmetry is precisely *why* small grains disappear and large grains grow — the *average* grain size increases monotonically with time.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Ideal Grain-Growth Law (Parabolic Law)

**Statement:** Starting from the proportionality *velocity of boundary* $v \propto P$, derive the parabolic grain-growth law $D^{2} - D_{0}^{2} = K t$.

### Step 1 — Velocity of a grain boundary

Empirically, the migration velocity is proportional to the driving pressure:

$$v = M \cdot P = M \cdot \frac{2\gamma}{r}$$

where $M$ is the grain-boundary mobility (m²·s⁻¹·J⁻¹).

### Step 2 — Relate $r$ to grain diameter $D$

For a *space-filling* polyhedral grain, the *mean* radius of curvature is proportional to the mean linear intercept (grain diameter):

$$r \approx \frac{D}{2} \quad \text{(order of magnitude)}$$

Substituting:

$$v = \frac{4M\gamma}{D}$$

### Step 3 — Express velocity as a derivative of $D$

The growth rate of the mean grain diameter is twice the boundary velocity (because the diameter extends on *both* sides of the boundary):

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}D}{\mathrm{d}t} = 2v = \frac{8M\gamma}{D} \equiv \frac{K}{D}$$

where $K = 8 M \gamma$ is a constant (for fixed $T$).

### Step 4 — Separate variables and integrate

$$D\,\mathrm{d}D = K\,\mathrm{d}t$$

Integrating from $D_0$ at $t = 0$ to $D$ at time $t$:

$$\int_{D_0}^{D} D'\,\mathrm{d}D' = \int_{0}^{t} K\,\mathrm{d}t'$$

$$\frac{1}{2}\!\left(D^{2} - D_{0}^{2}\right) = K t$$

$$\boxed{\,D^{2} - D_{0}^{2} = 2K t \equiv K' t\,}$$

which is the **ideal parabolic grain-growth law** (Beck's equation with $n = 2$).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Examples

### Example 1 — Comparing stored-energy effects on nucleation

> A cold-worked copper has stored energy $E_s = 2 \times 10^{6}\;\text{J/m}^{3}$ and grain-boundary energy $\gamma_b = 0.6\;\text{J/m}^{2}$.
> Calculate the critical nucleus radius and the activation energy.

**Solution:**

Critical radius:

$$r^{\star} = \frac{2\gamma_{b}}{E_{s}} = \frac{2 \times 0.6}{2 \times 10^{6}} = 6 \times 10^{-7}\;\text{m} = 0.6\;\mu\text{m}$$

Activation energy:

$$\Delta G^{\star} = \frac{16 \pi \gamma_{b}^{3}}{3 E_{s}^{2}} = \frac{16 \pi (0.6)^{3}}{3 (2 \times 10^{6})^{2}}$$

$$\Delta G^{\star} = \frac{16 \pi \times 0.216}{3 \times 4 \times 10^{12}} = \frac{10.85}{1.2 \times 10^{13}} \approx 9.0 \times 10^{-13}\;\text{J}$$

> ≈ **5.7 × 10⁶ kJ/mol** in molar units — extremely small, which is why recrystallization nucleates readily.

### Example 2 — Effect of increasing cold work by 2×

> If the same copper is given twice the cold work, $E_s = 4 \times 10^{6}\;\text{J/m}^{3}$. Find the new $r^\star$ and $\Delta G^\star$.

$$r^{\star}_{\text{new}} = \frac{2 \times 0.6}{4 \times 10^{6}} = 3 \times 10^{-7}\;\text{m}$$

$$\Delta G^{\star}_{\text{new}} = \frac{16\pi (0.6)^3}{3 (4 \times 10^{6})^{2}} = \frac{\Delta G^{\star}_{\text{old}}}{4}$$

> The activation barrier drops by a factor of **4** for a *doubling* of cold work — confirming the explosive nucleation at high strains.

### Example 3 — Using Beck's equation

> For pure iron at $T = 700\,°\text{C}$, an experiment gives $D = 50\;\mu\text{m}$ after 1 h starting from $D_0 = 10\;\mu\text{m}$. Estimate $D$ after 9 h (assuming $n = 2$).

**Step 1 — Find $K'$ from the 1-hour data:**

$$K' = \frac{D^{2} - D_{0}^{2}}{t} = \frac{(50)^2 - (10)^2}{1} = 2500 - 100 = 2400\;\mu\text{m}^{2}/\text{h}$$

**Step 2 — Apply to 9 h:**

$$D^{2} = D_{0}^{2} + K' t = 100 + 2400 \times 9 = 100 + 21600 = 21700\;\mu\text{m}^{2}$$

$$D = \sqrt{21700} \approx 147.3\;\mu\text{m}$$

> Note: $D$ only *tripled* in size even though time increased *nine-fold* — grain growth is self-limiting because $K' \propto 1/D$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Hall–Petch Equation (linked module concept)

For a polycrystal of mean grain diameter $d$, the yield strength is

$$\sigma_{y} = \sigma_{0} + k_{y}\,d^{-1/2}$$

**Reasoning:** A dislocation pile-up of $n$ dislocations produces a stress concentration $\tau^\star = n\tau$ at the tip. The pile-up length scales as $L \propto d^{1/2}$, and $n \propto \tau L \propto \tau d^{1/2}$. Setting $\tau^\star = \tau_{\text{critical}}$ gives $\tau \propto d^{-1/2}$.

> This is the *engineering reason* why *grain growth* is bad for strength and *why recrystallization* must be carefully controlled.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flowchart — The Three R's of Annealing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Cold Worked Metal High Dislocation Density High Stored Energy"] --> B["Heating Starts T less than T R"]
    B --> C["STAGE 1 RECOVERY Point Defects Annihilate Dislocations Reorder No Optical Change"]
    C --> D["STAGE 2 RECRYSTALLIZATION New Strain Free Grains Nucleate and Grow E s is the Driving Force"]
    D --> E["STAGE 3 GRAIN GROWTH Grain Boundaries Migrate to Reduce Area Gamma is the Driving Force"]
    E --> F["Final Coarse Grained Annealed Metal Low Strength High Ductility"]

```

    end

    subgraph "Stage 3 Grain Growth"
        CURV["Curvature of Boundary 1 by r"] --> DF2["Capillary Pressure 2 gamma by r"]
        GB2["Grain Boundary Energy gamma"] --> DF2

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Define recovery and recrystallization. Mention two key differences between them.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Recovery** is the initial stage of annealing of a cold-worked metal in which the dislocation arrangement becomes more ordered (subgrains form) and physical properties such as electrical resistivity and residual stresses are largely restored, *without* any observable change in the grain structure.
>
> **Recrystallization** is the subsequent stage in which new strain-free, equiaxed grains nucleate at high-energy sites and grow until they consume the entire deformed matrix, *with* a clearly visible change in the microstructure.

**Key differences:**

| Aspect | Recovery | Recrystallization |
|---|---|---|
| Driving force | Stored energy of point defects | Stored energy of dislocations ($E_s$) |
| Microstructural change | No new grains visible | New grain structure appears |
| Hardness change | Very little | Drops sharply |

*(3 marks — 1 for definitions, 2 for the table of differences.)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
**State the empirical rule for the recrystallization temperature of a pure metal. Why is $T_R$ of brass (Cu–30 % Zn) higher than that of pure copper?**

**Model Answer:**

> For a pure metal, the recrystallization temperature is approximately
> $$T_{R} \approx 0.4\,T_{m} \quad \text{(in Kelvin)}$$
> where $T_m$ is the absolute melting temperature.
>
> Brass is a **solid solution** of Zn in Cu. The solute Zn atoms segregate to grain boundaries and dislocation cores, **pinning** the boundaries and **hindering** dislocation motion. This raises the activation energy required for recrystallization, so $T_R$ for brass is significantly higher than that of pure Cu. (For Cu, $T_R \approx 200\,°\text{C}$; for 70-30 brass, $T_R \approx 350\,°\text{C}$.)

*(3 marks — 1 for the rule, 2 for the alloy explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Note:** In the End-Semester Examination (ESE), Part B Module-3 question offers an *internal choice*. You must answer *either* OR. We present both alternatives in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the phenomenon of recrystallization in cold-worked metals. Derive an expression for the critical nucleus size and the activation energy for the formation of a recrystallization nucleus.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the five important variables that affect the recrystallized grain size in a metal.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Phenomenon (3 marks):**
- Recrystallization is the replacement of the cold-worked, dislocation-rich microstructure by a new set of **strain-free, equiaxed grains** having the same composition and crystal structure as the parent, but a much lower defect density.
- It occurs by the classic two-step process of **nucleation** (at high-energy sites such as prior grain boundaries, deformation bands, and twin boundaries) and **growth** (boundary migration driven by the stored energy of cold work).
- The recrystallization temperature $T_R$ is conventionally defined as the temperature at which a heavily cold-worked (~ 40 – 70 %) specimen fully recrystallizes in 1 hour. The empirical rule is $T_R \approx 0.4\,T_m$ (K).

**Derivation of $r^\star$ and $\Delta G^\star$ (4 marks):**
Consider a small spherical nucleus of the new (strain-free) phase of radius $r$ inside the strained matrix. The total free-energy change when it forms is

$$\Delta G = -\frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}\,E_{s} + 4\pi r^{2}\,\gamma_{b}$$

where $E_s$ is the stored energy per unit volume and $\gamma_b$ is the new grain-boundary energy. The first term is negative (energy released) and the second is positive (energy cost of creating boundary).

Setting $\dfrac{\mathrm{d}(\Delta G)}{\mathrm{d}r} = 0$:

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}(\Delta G)}{\mathrm{d}r} = -4\pi r^{2}\,E_{s} + 8\pi r\,\gamma_{b} = 0$$

$$r^{\star} = \frac{2\gamma_{b}}{E_{s}}$$

Substituting back into $\Delta G$:

$$\Delta G^{\star} = -\frac{4}{3}\pi \left(\frac{2\gamma_{b}}{E_{s}}\right)^{3} E_{s} + 4\pi \left(\frac{2\gamma_{b}}{E_{s}}\right)^{2} \gamma_{b}$$

$$\Delta G^{\star} = -\frac{32\pi \gamma_{b}^{3}}{3E_{s}^{2}} + \frac{16\pi \gamma_{b}^{3}}{E_{s}^{2}} = \frac{16\pi \gamma_{b}^{3}}{3E_{s}^{2}}$$

$$\boxed{\,r^{\star} = \frac{2\gamma_{b}}{E_{s}}, \qquad \Delta G^{\star} = \frac{16\pi \gamma_{b}^{3}}{3 E_{s}^{2}}\,}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — The Five Variables (7 Marks)

| # | Variable | Effect on recrystallized grain size | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Degree of cold work** | ↑ cold work ⇒ ↓ grain size | More stored energy → more nuclei |
| 2 | **Annealing temperature** | ↑ $T$ ⇒ ↑ grain size | Higher boundary mobility |
| 3 | **Annealing time** | ↑ $t$ ⇒ ↑ grain size | More time for boundary migration |
| 4 | **Alloying / purity** | ↑ purity ⇒ ↑ grain size | Solute atoms drag boundaries |
| 5 | **Initial grain size** | ↓ initial ⇒ ↓ recrystallized grain | More nucleation sites per unit volume |

> **Engineering take-away:** To get a *fine* recrystallized grain (high strength, good toughness), one must (i) apply a *large* cold reduction, (ii) anneal at the *lowest* practical temperature, and (iii) keep annealing time *short*.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain grain growth in polycrystalline materials. Derive the ideal parabolic grain-growth law.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is the Hall–Petch relation? Discuss how grain growth affects the strength and toughness of structural steels.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Concept + Sketch (3 marks):**
- After recrystallization, the metal is a polycrystal of strain-free, equiaxed grains separated by curved grain boundaries.
- Because each boundary has a finite interfacial energy $\gamma$ (~ 0.5 – 1.0 J/m² for most metals), the system can lower its energy by **reducing the total boundary area**.
- Boundaries migrate *toward their centre of curvature*, causing **larger grains to grow at the expense of smaller ones**. The smaller grains eventually disappear.

> *Sketch: a 2D array of polygonal grains; small grains in the centre shrinking, large grains at edges growing — the boundary bows outward from the larger grain toward the smaller one.*

**Derivation of parabolic law (4 marks):**

Step 1 — Driving pressure on a curved boundary (Laplace pressure):

$$P = \frac{2\gamma}{r}$$

Step 2 — Boundary velocity is proportional to the pressure (mobility $M$):

$$v = M P = \frac{2M\gamma}{r}$$

Step 3 — For a grain of diameter $D \approx 2r$, the rate of change of $D$ is twice the boundary velocity (boundary moves from both sides):

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}D}{\mathrm{d}t} = 2v = \frac{4M\gamma}{r} = \frac{8M\gamma}{D} \equiv \frac{K}{D}$$

Step 4 — Separate and integrate from $D_0$ at $t=0$ to $D$ at $t=t$:

$$D\,\mathrm{d}D = K\,\mathrm{d}t \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \tfrac{1}{2}(D^{2} - D_{0}^{2}) = K t$$

$$\boxed{\,D^{2} - D_{0}^{2} = 2K t \equiv K' t\,}$$

This is the **ideal parabolic grain-growth law** (Beck's equation with $n = 2$).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Hall–Petch relation (3 marks):**
The yield strength of a polycrystalline metal varies inversely with the square root of the mean grain diameter $d$:

$$\sigma_{y} = \sigma_{0} + k_{y}\,d^{-1/2}$$

where $\sigma_0$ is the *friction stress* and $k_y$ is the *Hall–Petch coefficient*. Physically, smaller grains produce shorter dislocation pile-ups, hence a higher stress is needed to propagate slip into the next grain.

**Effect of grain growth on steel (4 marks):**

| Property | Small grain (low $d$) | Coarse grain (high $d$) |
|---|---|---|
| Yield strength | High | Low |
| Ductility | Good | Reduced |
| Toughness (impact) | **High** — ductile-to-brittle transition temperature (DBTT) is low | **Low** — DBTT shifts to *higher* temperatures, body-centred-cubic steels become brittle |
| Creep resistance | Poor at high $T$ | Slightly better (less boundary sliding area) |

> **Engineering consequence:** A coarse-grained structural steel component is dangerous in cold-weather service because it can fracture in a brittle manner. That is why *normalising*, *grain refinement* with Nb/Ti, and *controlled rolling* are standard practices.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Don't confuse $T_R$ and $T_m$.** Students often write "$T_R$ is 0.4 of $T_m$ in °C". The rule is in **Kelvin**. Always convert first.
> 2. **Don't claim the microstructure is unchanged during recrystallization.** The whole point is that it *is* changed. Recovery is the one where there is *no optical* change.
> 3. **Don't say hardness increases during recovery.** It *decreases slightly*. Only the *rate* is small.
> 4. **In Beck's equation, the exponent $n$ is not always 2.** It is 2 only for *ideal* parabolic growth. In real, particle-pinned or textured materials, $n \ge 2$.
> 5. **For the 14-mark question, always draw a labelled sketch** of the microstructure evolution. A missing sketch typically costs 1 – 2 marks even when the derivation is perfect.

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Three R's order:** Recovery → Recrystallization → Grain Growth. Always in this sequence.
- ✅ **Recovery drivers:** point-defect annihilation, dislocation rearrangement into low-angle sub-boundaries. *No* optical change.
- ✅ **Recrystallization driver:** stored energy of cold work $E_s$. New strain-free grains form.
- ✅ **Grain-growth driver:** grain-boundary energy $\gamma$ acting through curvature $1/r$. $P = 2\gamma/r$.
- ✅ **Empirical rule:** $T_R \approx 0.4\,T_m$ (K), 1-hour, ~ 40 – 70 % cold work.
- ✅ **Higher cold work + lower $T$ + shorter time + purer metal → finer grains.**
- ✅ **Beck's equation:** $D^{n} - D_0^{\,n} = K t \exp(-Q/RT)$ — exponent $n = 2$ for ideal growth.
- ✅ **Capillary pressure derivation:** $P = 2\gamma/r$ — small grains shrink, large grains grow.
- ✅ **Hall–Petch link:** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ — fine grains = strong & tough.
- ✅ **Solute atoms, second-phase particles, and texture all *raise* $T_R$ and *slow* grain growth.**
- ✅ **Cold-worked metals can never be 100 % recrystallization-free if heated above $T_R$** — there is always a finite nucleation rate.
- ✅ **Alloy rule of thumb:** $T_R^{\text{alloy}} > T_R^{\text{pure metal}}$ — solutes pin boundaries.
- ✅ **Smith–Zener pinning limit:** $D_{\max} \approx 4 r_p / (3 f)$ — particles limit grain size.
- ✅ **Stored energy** is typically 0.5 – 10 cal/g, or 2 – 40 J/g — a *tiny* fraction of plastic work, but enough to drive recrystallization.
- ✅ **Mnemonic for variables affecting grain size:** **"CT TPS"** — **C**old work, **T**ime, **T**emperature, **P**urity, **S**ource grain size.
- ✅ **Engineering link:** Controlling the three R's = controlling final mechanical properties of *every* rolled, drawn, or forged metal product.

---

> **End of Notes — Recovery, Recrystallization and Grain Growth | Module 3 | PBPET304**
> *Prepared in accordance with the KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education framework and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Cognitive Levels: Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyse).*
