```mermaid
    end

    subgraph shiftNode["CCT NOSE SHIFT"]
        s1["Shift Right Longer Time"]
        s2["Shift Down Lower Temperature"]
        s3["Larger Nose Region"]
    end

    subgraph resultNode["ENGINEERING OUTCOME"]
        r1["Increased Hardenability"]
        r2["Easier Quenching Slower Media OK"]
        r3["Deeper Martensite Formation"]
    end

    f1 --> s1
    f2 --> s1
    f3 --> s1
    f4 --> s1
    f1 --> s2
    f2 --> s2
    f3 --> s2

    s1 --> r1
    s2 --> r1
    s1 --> r2
    s2 --> r3
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Heat Treatment Processes Mapped on CCT

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph htNode["HEAT TREATMENT PROCESSES"]
        h1["Full Annealing Furnace Cool Very Slow"]
        h2["Normalizing Air Cool Slow"]
        h3["Austempering Salt Bath at Bainite T"]
        h4["Quench and Temper Oil or Water plus Temper"]
    end

    subgraph coolNode["COOLING RATE REGION ON CCT"]
        c1["Far Left of Nose V less than 1 C per s"]
        c2["Slightly Left of Nose V 1 to 10 C per s"]
        c3["Bain Bay V 10 to 50 C per s"]
        c4["Right of Nose V greater than 50 C per s"]
    end

    subgraph outNode["FINAL PRODUCT"]
        o1["Soft Coarse Pearlite Machinable"]
        o2["Fine Pearlite Balanced Strength"]
        o3["Bainite Tough Ductile Hard"]
        o4["Tempered Martensite Strong Tough"]
    end

    h1 --> c1 --> o1
    h2 --> c2 --> o2
    h3 --> c3 --> o3
    h4 --> c4 --> o4
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Schematic: CCT Diagram Coordinate System (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph axisNode["COORDINATE SYSTEM"]
        yaxis["Y axis Temperature in C linear scale 0 to 1000"]
        xaxis["X axis Time in seconds logarithmic scale 1 to 100000"]
    end

    subgraph regionTop["UPPER REGION OF CCT"]
        top1["Austenitizing Line 845 C horizontal"]
        top2["A1 Eutectoid Line 723 C horizontal"]
    end

    subgraph regionMid["MIDDLE REGION OF CCT"]
        mid1["Pearlite Transformation Start Curve C shape upper"]
        mid2["Pearlite Transformation Finish Curve C shape lower"]
    end

    subgraph regionLow["LOWER REGION OF CCT"]
        low1["Bainite Region Bs to Bf 480 to 280 C"]
        low2["Martensite Start Ms 320 C horizontal"]
        low3["Martensite Finish Mf 180 C horizontal"]
    end

    axisNode --> regionTop
    regionTop --> regionMid
    regionMid --> regionLow
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** In the actual KTU exam, students are expected to **draw a labeled schematic CCT diagram** showing all the above regions, the nose, the bain bay, and at least 2-3 cooling curves. The diagrams above provide the **logical structure** of what should be drawn by hand.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q1. Define a CCT diagram. How is it different from a TTT diagram?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Continuous Cooling Transformation (CCT) diagram** is a plot of **temperature vs. logarithm of time** that shows the phase transformations occurring in a steel during **continuous cooling** from the austenitic region to room temperature.

**Key Differences from TTT Diagram:**

| **Aspect** | **TTT Diagram** | **CCT Diagram** |
|---|---|---|
| Cooling mode | Isothermal (sudden quench + hold) | Continuous, uninterrupted |
| Nose position | Original | Shifted to the **right and downward** |
| Industrial use | Laboratory only | **Directly used in industry** |
| Bainite region | Fully developed | Often absent or truncated at the nose |

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q2. What is the critical cooling rate? How is it determined from a CCT diagram?**

**Model Answer:**

The **critical cooling rate (V_c)** is the **minimum rate of cooling** required to **bypass the nose** of the CCT diagram entirely, thereby avoiding the pearlite/bainite transformation and producing **100% martensite** upon reaching the Ms temperature.

**Determination from CCT diagram:**

It is the slope of the **tangent line** drawn from the austenitizing temperature to the **nose** of the transformation curve:

$$V_c = \dfrac{T_{\gamma} - T_{nose}}{t_{nose}}$$

where $T_{\gamma}$ is the austenitizing temperature, $T_{nose}$ is the temperature at the nose, and $t_{nose}$ is the time at the nose.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Set A]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the construction and salient features of a CCT diagram with a neat labeled sketch. Discuss why the CCT curves are shifted to the right of the TTT curves. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A plain carbon steel (0.8% C) has the following CCT data: Austenitizing temperature = 850 °C, Nose temperature = 550 °C, Nose time (start) = 1 s, M_s = 220 °C, M_f = 50 °C. Determine:
   (i) The critical cooling rate. **(2 Marks)**
   (ii) The microstructure obtained when the steel is cooled at 100 °C/s. **(2 Marks)**
   (iii) The microstructure obtained when cooled at 1 °C/s. **(2 Marks)**
   (iv) The percentage of martensite formed at 100 °C surface temperature if the surface just reaches M_s. **(1 Mark)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Construction of a CCT Diagram:**

1. **Sample preparation:** Small specimens of the steel (e.g., 0.8% C eutectoid steel) are heated to the **austenitizing temperature** (~850 °C) and held until fully austenitized.
2. **Continuous cooling:** Each specimen is cooled at a **different, controlled rate** (e.g., 0.1, 1, 10, 100, 1000 °C/s) using various media (furnace, air, oil, water, brine).
3. **Dilatometric/Metallographic Analysis:** The temperature-time data is recorded, and the **transformation start and finish points** are identified using **dilatometry** (volume change during transformation) or **metallography** (microstructure examination).
4. **Plotting:** These points are plotted on a **temperature vs. log(time)** graph, and smooth curves are drawn through them.

**Salient Features of a CCT Diagram:**

- **Austenite region** at the top (above A₁ = 723 °C).
- **Transformation start curve** (upper C-curve).
- **Transformation finish curve** (lower C-curve).
- **Nose** at the most unstable temperature (~550 °C for eutectoid steel).
- **Martensite start (M_s)** and **M_f (finish)** as horizontal lines.
- **Bain bay** between the transformation finish curve and M_s line.
- **Cooling curves** overlaid as straight lines on the log-time axis.

**Why CCT Curves Shift Right of TTT Curves:**

During **isothermal transformation (TTT)**, the steel is held at a constant temperature, allowing ample time for nucleation and growth of new phases. In **continuous cooling (CCT)**, the steel spends **less time at each temperature** because it is constantly cooling. Therefore, the transformation is **delayed** (shifted to **longer times**) and occurs at **lower temperatures**. This is the fundamental reason why the **CCT nose lies below and to the right** of the TTT nose.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Critical Cooling Rate:**

$$V_c = \dfrac{T_{\gamma} - T_{nose}}{t_{nose}} = \dfrac{850 - 550}{1} = 300 \, °C/s$$

**(ii) Cooling rate 100 °C/s:**

Since $V = 100 \, °C/s < V_c = 300 \, °C/s$, the cooling curve **intersects the nose** of the CCT diagram. The transformation will begin at the nose and complete below it.

- The curve passes through both the pearlite start and finish curves.
- Final microstructure: **100% fine pearlite** (since the transformation is completed entirely before reaching M_s).

Wait, let me re-evaluate: If the cooling curve at 100 °C/s passes through the nose at $t = 1s$ and $T = 550°C$, and continues down, it will reach M_s = 220 °C. The time to reach M_s would be approximately $(850-220)/100 = 6.3$ s. Since the nose time is 1s, the curve will be **above the transformation start curve at 1s** (because 1s < the time it takes to reach transformation start at 550°C on a 100°C/s curve, which would be 3s). 

Let me reconsider: The **cooling curve at 100 °C/s** passes through the nose point. So it **tangentially touches the nose**, meaning it **just barely** avoids the pearlite transformation. Therefore, it produces **mostly martensite** with a small amount of fine pearlite.

**Corrected answer (ii):** At $V = 100 \, °C/s$, the cooling curve is very close to the critical rate (300 °C/s is the tangent). The actual transformation is partial, giving **martensite with some fine pearlite** (mixed structure).

**(iii) Cooling rate 1 °C/s:**

Since $V = 1 \, °C/s \ll V_c = 300 \, °C/s$, the cooling curve is **far to the right** of the nose at all temperatures. It **deeply intersects** the transformation start and finish curves at high temperatures (~700 °C). The transformation completes entirely in the **pearlite region** above M_s.

- Final microstructure: **100% coarse pearlite**.

**(iv) Percentage of Martensite at M_s:**

If the surface **just reaches M_s** = 220 °C, then $\Delta T = M_s - T = 220 - 220 = 0$.

$$f_M = 1 - e^{-0.011 \cdot 0} = 1 - e^0 = 1 - 1 = 0\%$$

> **0% Martensite** is formed at the moment the surface reaches M_s. Martensite formation **begins** at M_s but requires further cooling below M_s to form.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Set B (Internal Choice)]**

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the various microstructures formed during continuous cooling of a eutectoid steel at different cooling rates. How does the addition of alloying elements affect the CCT diagram? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A medium carbon steel (0.4% C) has the following data:
   - T_γ = 870 °C, Nose T = 600 °C, Nose time = 4 s, M_s = 380 °C, M_f = 200 °C.
   - The steel is quenched in oil and the surface temperature drops to 150 °C.
   - Calculate:
     (i) Critical cooling rate. **(2 Marks)**
     (ii) Type of microstructure formed (qualitative). **(2 Marks)**
     (iii) Percentage of martensite using Koistinen-Marburger equation. **(3 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Microstructures Formed at Different Cooling Rates (Eutectoid Steel):**

*(Students are expected to draw a CCT diagram with at least 4 cooling curves labeled)*

1. **Very slow cooling (Furnace, ~0.1 °C/s):** Cooling curve lies far to the right, completely in the pearlite region. → **100% Coarse Pearlite** (soft, ~10-15 HRC).

2. **Slow cooling (Air, ~1-5 °C/s):** Curve still in pearlite region but closer to the nose. → **100% Fine Pearlite** (medium hardness, ~25-30 HRC).

3. **Medium cooling (Oil, ~10-50 °C/s):** Curve just touches or partially enters the nose, with a portion dipping into the bain bay. → **Bainite + Fine Pearlite** mix (high strength, ~35-45 HRC).

4. **Fast cooling (Water, ~100-500 °C/s):** Curve bypasses the nose, reaching M_s directly. → **100% Martensite** (very hard, ~60-65 HRC, but brittle).

5. **Very fast cooling (Brine, > 500 °C/s):** Bypasses nose with maximum undercooling. → **100% Martensite** (with possible retained austenite).

**Effect of Alloying Elements on CCT Diagram:**

1. **Nose shift to the right:** Alloying elements like **Ni, Cr, Mo, Mn** delay the transformation, shifting the nose to **longer times** (lower critical cooling rate).
2. **Nose shift downward:** The nose temperature may **decrease** slightly.
3. **Increased hardenability:** The steel can be cooled in **oil instead of water** to achieve martensite.
4. **Bainite region expansion:** The bain bay widens, making bainitic transformations more accessible.
5. **M_s and M_f reduction:** Most alloying elements **lower** the martensite start temperature.

**Examples:**
- **Mn (1%):** Shifts nose ~10× to the right.
- **Cr (1%):** Shifts nose ~5-7× to the right.
- **Mo (0.5%):** Most potent — shifts nose ~20× to the right (used in Ni-Cr-Mo alloy steels).
- **Ni (3-5%):** Substantially lowers M_s (used in maraging steels).

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Critical Cooling Rate:**

$$V_c = \dfrac{T_{\gamma} - T_{nose}}{t_{nose}} = \dfrac{870 - 600}{4} = \dfrac{270}{4} = 67.5 \, °C/s$$

**(ii) Microstructure (qualitative):**

The oil quench rate (typically 30-100 °C/s for a medium carbon steel) is **comparable to or less than V_c = 67.5 °C/s**. Therefore:
- The cooling curve **partially intersects the nose** or **just bypasses it**.
- Some pearlite may form initially, followed by martensite below M_s.
- Final microstructure: **Martensite + some fine pearlite/bainite** (mixed structure, ~50% martensite approximate).

**(iii) Percentage of Martensite (Koistinen-Marburger):**

Given: $M_s = 380 \, °C$, $T = 150 \, °C$.

$$\Delta T = M_s - T = 380 - 150 = 230 \, °C$$

$$f_M = 1 - e^{-0.011 \cdot 230}$$

$$f_M = 1 - e^{-2.53}$$

$$f_M = 1 - 0.0796$$

$$f_M = 0.9204 = 92.04\%$$

> **Approximately 92% martensite** is formed at the surface, with ~8% retained austenite.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks (CCT Diagram Questions):**
>
> 1. **Confusing CCR formula direction:** Many students write $V_c = (M_s - T_{nose})/t_{nose}$. The correct numerator is $(T_{\gamma} - T_{nose})$, NOT $(M_s - T_{nose})$. **Loss: 2-3 marks.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting log-scale of time axis:** When drawing the CCT diagram, students often use a linear time axis. This is a fundamental error. The X-axis **must be logarithmic**. **Loss: 2 marks.**
>
> 3. **Misidentifying Bain Bay:** Students often label the entire region below M_s as "Martensite region" without distinguishing the **Bain Bay** (where bainite can form). **Loss: 1-2 marks.**
>
> 4. **Skipping the M_s and M_f lines:** These are mandatory horizontal lines in any KTU diagram. **Loss: 1-2 marks.**
>
> 5. **Wrong Koistinen-Marburger constant:** Some students use $-0.015$ or $-0.010$ instead of the correct $-0.011$. The KTU accepted value is **0.011**. **Loss: 1-2 marks.**
>
> 6. **Confusing TTT and CCT transformations:** A cooling curve analysis on a TTT diagram is a different question. The continuous cooling curve must always be drawn on the **CCT diagram**. **Loss: 3-4 marks.**
>
> 7. **Not labeling cooling curves with cooling rates:** Always annotate each cooling curve with its numerical rate (e.g., "10 °C/s — Oil Quench"). **Loss: 1 mark.**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 CCT Diagram:**
>
> - [ ] CCT = **Continuous Cooling Transformation**; plots **Temperature (linear) vs. log(Time)**.
> - [ ] **CCT curves are always below and to the right of TTT curves.**
> - [ ] **Critical Cooling Rate (V_c)** is the slope of the tangent from $T_{\gamma}$ to the **nose** of the CCT diagram.
> - [ ] Formula: $V_c = (T_{\gamma} - T_{nose}) / t_{nose}$ — units in **°C/s**.
> - [ ] Faster than V_c → **100% Martensite**; Slower → **Pearlite/Bainite** mix.
> - [ ] **M_s and M_f are HORIZONTAL lines** on the CCT diagram (independent of cooling rate).
> - [ ] **Bain Bay** is the region between the transformation finish curve and the M_s line.
> - [ ] **Koistinen-Marburger Equation:** $f_M = 1 - e^{-0.011 \cdot (M_s - T)}$
> - [ ] **Alloying elements** (Ni, Cr, Mo, Mn) shift the nose **to the right** → increase **hardenability**.
> - [ ] Adding **1% Cr** can reduce CCR by up to **90%**.
> - [ ] **Quenching media (slowest to fastest):** Furnace → Air → Oil → Water → Brine.
> - [ ] **Air cool** → Fine pearlite; **Oil quench** → Bainite; **Water quench** → Martensite.
> - [ ] **Andrew's equation** for M_s: $M_s = 539 - 423(\%C) - 30(\%Mn) - 17.7(\%Ni) - 12(\%Cr)$.
> - [ ] **Pearlite is soft and ductile; Bainite is tough; Martensite is hard and brittle.**
> - [ ] **Tempering** reduces brittleness of martensite by forming **tempered martensite (sorbite)**.
> - [ ] The **eutectoid temperature A₁ = 723 °C** is the upper limit for pearlitic transformation.
> - [ ] For **hypoeutectoid steel (< 0.77% C):** Proeutectoid **ferrite** forms first, then pearlite.
> - [ ] For **hypereutectoid steel (> 0.77% C):** Proeutectoid **cementite** forms first, then pearlite.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications - Annealing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Applications - Annealing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Annealing** in materials science and physical metallurgy is a heat treatment process that alters the physical and sometimes chemical properties of a material to increase its **ductility** and reduce its **hardness**, thereby making it more workable. It involves heating a material above its recrystallization temperature, maintaining a suitable temperature (soaking), and then cooling it slowly in a furnace to obtain a refined microstructure, equilibrium phase distribution, and relieved internal stresses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Annealing is a generic term denoting a treatment consisting of heating to and holding at a suitable temperature, followed by cooling at an appropriate rate, primarily used for softening metallic materials, refining grain size, improving machinability, and relieving internal stresses induced by prior processing (e.g., cold working, welding, casting).*

### 1.2 Types of Annealing Covered in the KTU Syllabus

The KTU 2024 scheme specifically categorizes annealing applications into the following technical sub-classes:

1. **Full Annealing**
2. **Process Annealing (also called Sub-critical or Recovery Annealing)**
3. **Stress Relief Annealing**
4. **Spheroidization Annealing**
5. **Diffusion Annealing (Homogenization Annealing)**
6. **Recrystallization Annealing**
7. **Isothermal Annealing**

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Tired Elastic Band" Experiment**
> Imagine an elastic band you have stretched thousands of times. It becomes hard, brittle, and snaps easily because the internal "fibres" (analogous to **dislocations** and **distorted grains** in a metal) are all tangled and stretched. If you warm that band gently in lukewarm water (analogous to **sub-critical process annealing**), the internal stresses relax and it regains some flexibility. If you heat it slightly more vigorously (analogous to **recrystallization annealing**), the fibres completely rearrange into a brand-new, stress-free configuration.
>
> **The metal behaves exactly the same way.** Cold working (rolling, drawing, hammering) tangles its crystalline structure. Annealing "untangles" it by giving atoms enough thermal energy to migrate into low-energy, equilibrium positions.

### 1.4 Critical Industrial Metrics

The three fundamental process variables that govern every annealing application are:

| Variable | Symbol | Standard Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Austenitizing / Soaking Temperature** | $T$ | $^\circ$C or K |
| **Holding (Soaking) Time** | $t$ | minutes (min) or hours (h) |
| **Cooling Rate** | $C_R$ | $^\circ$C/h (furnace cooling typical) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constant for Steels:** The lower critical temperature **A₁ = 727 °C** and the upper critical temperature **A₃ (hypo-eutectoid) / A_cm (hyper-eutectoid)** are the cornerstone boundaries for steel annealing. All temperatures in steel annealing are referenced to these lines on the **Iron-Carbon (Fe-Fe₃C) Equilibrium Phase Diagram**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Application 1 — Full Annealing

**Objective:** Achieve maximum softness, refine coarse grains, and improve ductility/machinability in hot-worked or cold-worked steels.

**Process Steps:**
1. **Heating:** Heat the steel **30 °C to 50 °C above the upper critical temperature** (A₃ for hypo-eutectoid steels; A₁ for eutectoid and just above A₁ for hyper-eutectoid steels).
2. **Soaking:** Hold at temperature for approximately **1 hour per 25 mm of section thickness** (rule of thumb).
3. **Cooling:** Furnace cool (slow cooling) to a temperature well below A₁, then air cool to room temperature.

**Microstructural Result:**
- Hypo-eutectoid steel → **Coarse Pearlite + Equiaxed Ferrite**.
- Eutectoid steel → **Coarse Lamellar Pearlite**.
- Hyper-eutectoid steel → **Spheroidized Cementite in Ferrite Matrix** (partial).

**Engineering Application:**
- Used for **machining pre-treatment of large castings, forgings, and weldments** in carbon and alloy steels.

## 2.2 Application 2 — Process (Sub-critical / Recovery) Annealing

**Objective:** Restore ductility lost due to cold working (strain hardening) without causing full recrystallization or grain growth.

**Process Steps:**
1. Heat to a temperature **below the lower critical temperature A₁** — typically **450 °C to 650 °C** for steels.
2. Hold for a pre-determined time.
3. Air cool to room temperature.

**Microstructural Result:**
- **Sub-grain formation, dislocation annihilation, residual stress relief**.
- No phase transformation occurs.

**Engineering Application:**
- Intermediate soft-state restoration between successive **cold-rolling passes** in sheet-metal production of low-carbon steel, copper, brass, and aluminum.

## 2.3 Application 3 — Stress Relief Annealing

**Objective:** Eliminate **residual internal stresses** introduced by non-uniform plastic deformation, welding, machining, or quenching, without significantly altering the microstructure or mechanical properties.

**Process Steps:**
1. Heat uniformly to a temperature **below A₁** (typically 550 °C–650 °C for steels; 200 °C–300 °C for non-ferrous alloys).
2. Hold for adequate soak time.
3. Cool slowly to avoid re-introducing thermal stresses.

**Engineering Application:**
- **Critical for welded pressure vessels, boiler tubes, aircraft structural components, and large fabricated assemblies** to prevent stress-corrosion cracking and dimensional distortion during service.

## 2.4 Application 4 — Spheroidization Annealing

**Objective:** Transform hard, brittle **lamellar cementite** in high-carbon steels (tool steels, bearing steels) into **globular/spheroidal cementite** dispersed in a soft ferritic matrix, producing the softest possible structure for superior machinability.

**Process Steps (One of three common methods):**
1. **Method A:** Heat just above A₁ (≈ 750 °C), hold for extended time (15–25 h), then slow furnace cool.
2. **Method B:** Alternate heating between **A₁ + 10 °C** and **A₁ − 20 °C** repeatedly (thermal cycling).
3. **Method C:** Heat directly to a temperature **just below A₁** (≈ 700 °C) and hold for prolonged time.

**Microstructural Result:**
- **Globular / spheroidal Fe₃C particles in continuous α-Ferrite matrix**.

**Engineering Application:**
- **Pre-machining heat treatment for high-carbon tool steels (e.g., AISI 1095, W1, O1), bearing steels (AISI 52100), and die steels** prior to final quenching and tempering.

## 2.5 Application 5 — Recrystallization Annealing

**Objective:** Produce a **new, strain-free equiaxed grain structure** to restore ductility and reduce hardness after severe cold work.

**Process Steps:**
1. Heat to a temperature **above the recrystallization temperature (T_R)** but **below the melting point**.
2. Hold for sufficient time for new grain nuclei to form and grow.
3. Cool at any convenient rate (air cool is standard).

**Key Empirical Rule (KTU High-Yield):**
$$T_R \approx 0.4 \, T_m \quad \text{(in Kelvin)}$$

where $T_m$ is the absolute melting point of the metal.

**Microstructural Result:**
- Complete replacement of the deformed, elongated grain structure with a **new equiaxed, stress-free grain structure**.

**Engineering Application:**
- **Deep-drawing sheet steels, electrical steel (silicon steel) laminations for transformer cores, and copper/brass wire manufacturing.**

## 2.6 Application 6 — Diffusion (Homogenization) Annealing

**Objective:** Eliminate **chemical segregation (coring)** in cast ingots by solid-state diffusion, producing a uniform composition throughout.

**Process Steps:**
1. Heat to a **very high temperature** — typically **1100 °C to 1300 °C** — close to the solidus line but below incipient melting.
2. Hold for several hours (commonly **10–20 hours**).
3. Furnace cool.

**Microstructural Result:**
- **Chemically homogeneous solid solution** with dendritic segregation eliminated.

**Engineering Application:**
- **Pre-treatment of high-alloy steel ingots, nickel-based superalloys (Inconel, Monel), and titanium alloy billets** prior to subsequent hot working.

## 2.7 Application 7 — Isothermal Annealing

**Objective:** Achieve a uniform, fine pearlitic structure with reduced annealing time compared to conventional full annealing.

**Process Steps:**
1. Heat above A₃ (or A₁) and soak.
2. Quench rapidly into a **salt bath held at a temperature just below A₁** (typically 600 °C–650 °C).
3. Hold in the salt bath until the austenite-to-pearlite transformation is complete.
4. Air cool to room temperature.

**Engineering Application:**
- **Mass-production heat treatment of alloy steels** where predictable, uniform hardness is required (e.g., automotive gears, shafts).

---

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **canonical reference** KTU examiners expect students to reproduce in ESE answers. Memorize all entries.

| # | Concept | Formula / Parameter | Typical Value | Application |
| :-: | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Lower critical temperature | $A_1$ (eutectoid) | **727 °C** | All steel heat treatments |
| 2 | Recrystallization temperature | $T_R \approx 0.4 \, T_m$ | — | Recrystallization annealing |
| 3 | Grain size (ASTM) | $n = 2^{N-1}$ grains/in² | $N = 1 \text{ to } 10$ | Grain size grading |
| 4 | Hall-Petch strengthening | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \, d^{-1/2}$ | — | Strength vs grain size |
| 5 | Soaking time (rule of thumb) | $t_{soak} \approx 1\,\text{h per } 25\,\text{mm}$ thickness | — | Furnace design |
| 6 | Diffusion distance | $x \approx \sqrt{Dt}$ | — | Homogenization |
| 7 | Fick's first law | $J = -D \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | — | Diffusion annealing |
| 8 | Arrhenius diffusivity | $D = D_0 \exp\left(-\dfrac{Q_d}{RT}\right)$ | — | Temperature dependence |
| 9 | Austenitizing (full anneal) | $T_{full} = A_3 + (30 \text{ to } 50)\,^\circ\text{C}$ | hypo-eutectoid | Full annealing |
| 10 | Sub-critical process anneal | $T_{process} < A_1$ | 450–650 °C | Process annealing |
| 11 | Stress relief (steel) | $T_{SR} \approx 550\text{–}650\,^\circ\text{C}$ | < A₁ | Welded structures |
| 12 | Spheroidization | $T \approx A_1 \pm 20\,^\circ\text{C}$ | ≈ 700–750 °C | Tool steels |
| 13 | Homogenization | $T \approx 1100\text{–}1300\,^\circ\text{C}$ | near solidus | Cast ingots |
| 14 | Isothermal anneal salt bath | $T_{bath} \approx 600\text{–}650\,^\circ\text{C}$ | < A₁ | Alloy steels |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Safeguard:** Absolute values and magnitudes in equations (e.g., $\vert x \vert$, $\mid d \mid$) MUST be written using the LaTeX `\vert` or `\mid` command in KTU exam answer sheets. Never write a bare vertical bar `|` inside a math expression as it conflicts with the markdown table delimiter.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Recrystallization Temperature (Bever's Empirical Relation)

**Question:** *Derive an expression relating the recrystallization temperature to the melting point of a pure metal.*

**Starting Premise:** Empirical observations by Bever, von Mises, and Schmid across FCC, BCC, and HCP metals show that the recrystallization temperature is a fixed fraction of the absolute melting point.

**Step 1 — Define melting point in Kelvin.**
$$T_m \text{ (K)} = T_m \text{ (°C)} + 273.15$$

**Step 2 — State the empirical proportionality.**
$$T_R \propto T_m$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the empirical constant $\alpha \approx 0.4$.**
$$T_R = \alpha \cdot T_m$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\alpha = 0.4$.**
$$T_R = 0.4 \cdot T_m$$

**Step 5 — Worked example for pure iron.**
$$T_m \text{ (Fe)} = 1538\,^\circ\text{C} = 1811 \text{ K}$$

**Step 6 — Compute recrystallization temperature.**
$$T_R = 0.4 \times 1811 \text{ K}$$

$$T_R = 724.4 \text{ K}$$

**Step 7 — Convert back to Celsius.**
$$T_R \text{ (°C)} = 724.4 - 273.15 = 451.3\,^\circ\text{C}$$

**Interpretation:** Pure iron's recrystallization temperature is approximately **451 °C**, consistent with experimental observations for high-purity iron.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Time Required for Homogenization (Diffusion Annealing)

**Question:** *Estimate the time required to reduce dendritic segregation across a dendrite arm spacing of $L = 100\,\mu m$ for carbon diffusion in austenite at $1200\,^\circ\text{C}$.*

**Step 1 — State the mean diffusion distance equation.**
$$x = \sqrt{Dt} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \frac{x^2}{D}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the diffusion distance.**
For full homogenization, the diffusion distance equals **half the dendrite arm spacing**:
$$x = \frac{L}{2} = \frac{100 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}}{2} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Look up carbon diffusivity in austenite at 1200 °C.**
For C in γ-Fe at 1200 °C (1473 K):
$$D_0 = 2.3 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}, \quad Q_d = 148 \times 10^3 \text{ J/mol}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Arrhenius equation.**
$$D = D_0 \exp\left(-\frac{Q_d}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Compute the exponent.**
$$-\frac{Q_d}{RT} = -\frac{148 \times 10^3}{8.314 \times 1473} = -\frac{148000}{12246.5}$$

$$-\frac{Q_d}{RT} = -12.085$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the exponential.**
$$\exp(-12.085) = 5.65 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 7 — Compute D.**
$$D = (2.3 \times 10^{-5}) \times (5.65 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$D \approx 1.30 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 8 — Compute the time.**
$$t = \frac{x^2}{D} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-5})^2}{1.30 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$t = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-9}}{1.30 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$t \approx 19.23 \text{ seconds}$$

**Interpretation:** At 1200 °C, carbon homogenization across a 100 μm dendrite spacing takes only about **20 seconds** — which is why diffusion annealing of carbon steels is rarely needed, but highly alloyed steels with sluggish substitutional diffusion (e.g., Ni, Cr) require **10–20 hours** at 1200 °C.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — All Annealing Types

| Annealing Type | Temperature | Cooling | Primary Purpose | Phase Change? | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Full Anneal** | A₃ + (30–50) °C | Furnace cool | Maximum softening, grain refinement | **Yes** (α → γ → α) | Castings, forgings |
| **Process Anneal** | 450–650 °C (sub-A₁) | Air cool | Restore ductility between cold-work passes | **No** | Sheet rolling mills |
| **Stress Relief** | 550–650 °C (sub-A₁) | Slow cool | Remove residual stresses | **No** | Welded pressure vessels |
| **Spheroidization** | A₁ ± 20 °C | Very slow FC | Globular cementite, max machinability | **Partial** | High-carbon tool steels |
| **Recrystallization** | Above T_R, below T_m | Air cool | New strain-free grains | **No** (in pure metals) | Cold-rolled sheets, wires |
| **Diffusion (Homogenization)** | 1100–1300 °C | Furnace cool | Eliminate chemical segregation | **No** (single phase) | Alloy ingots, superalloys |
| **Isothermal** | Salt bath ≈ 600–650 °C | Rapid quench to bath, then AC | Uniform fine pearlite | **Yes** (γ → pearlite at constant T) | Alloy steel automotive parts |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Annealing Process Block Flow

The following Mermaid block-level functional architecture flow illustrates the canonical 3-stage annealing process regardless of specific type.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start Cold or Hot Worked Steel] --> B1[Stage 1 Heating Stage]
    B1 --> C1[Ramp Up to Target Soaking Temperature T soak]
    C1 --> D1[Stage 2 Soaking Holding Stage]
    D1 --> E1[Hold for 1 h per 25 mm of Section Thickness]
    E1 --> F1[Stage 3 Controlled Cooling Stage]
    F1 --> G1{Cooling Medium Selection}
    G1 --> H1[Furnace Cool Very Slow]
    G1 --> I1[Air Cool Moderate]
    G1 --> J1[Salt Bath Isothermal Hold]
    H1 --> K1[Annealed Steel with Refined Microstructure and Reduced Hardness]
    I1 --> K1
    J1 --> K1
    K1 --> L1[Output Inspection Hardness and Microstructure]
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree — Selecting the Correct Annealing Application

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[What Is the Engineering Objective] --> Q1{Goal}
    Q1 --> R1[Maximum Softness and Grain Refinement] --> X1[Apply Full Annealing]
    Q1 --> R2[Restore Ductility Between Cold Work Passes] --> X2[Apply Process Annealing]
    Q1 --> R3[Eliminate Residual Welding or Machining Stresses] --> X3[Apply Stress Relief Annealing]
    Q1 --> R4[Maximize Machinability of High Carbon Steel] --> X4[Apply Spheroidization Annealing]
    Q1 --> R5[Eliminate Cast Ingot Chemical Segregation] --> X5[Apply Diffusion Homogenization Annealing]
    Q1 --> R6[Achieve Uniform Predictable Hardness in Alloy Steel] --> X6[Apply Isothermal Annealing]
    Q1 --> R7[Replace Cold Worked Grain Structure] --> X7[Apply Recrystallization Annealing]
```

## 4.3 Fe-Fe₃C Phase Diagram with Annealing Temperature Zones

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Z1[Below A1 Sub Critical Zone 450 to 727 C] --> Z1A[Process Anneal and Stress Relief Zone]
    Z2[Between A1 and A3 Inter Critical Zone 727 to 912 C for 0.8 percent C] --> Z2A[Partial Austenitization Zone]
    Z3[Above A3 Full Austenitization Zone] --> Z3A[Full Annealing and Homogenization Zone]
    Z4[Above Acm Hyper Eutectoid Zone] --> Z4A[Spheroidization for Hyper Eutectoid Steels]
```

## 4.4 Microstructural Evolution During Annealing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Stage A Deformed Grains from Cold Work] --> M2[Stage B Recovery Dislocation Annihilation]
    M2 --> M3[Stage C Recrystallization New Strain Free Nuclei]
    M3 --> M4[Stage D Grain Growth Equiaxed Equilibrium Grains]
    M4 --> M5[Final Annealed Microstructure with Reduced Hardness and Increased Ductility]
```

## 4.5 Spheroidization Schematic — Lamellar to Globular Transformation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Initial Microstructure Lamellar Pearlite from Eutectoid Steel] --> P2[Heat to A1 plus 10 to 20 C and Hold]
    P2 --> P3[Cementite Lamellae Break Down into Spherical Particles]
    P3 --> P4[Final Microstructure Globular Cementite in Continuous Ferrite Matrix]
    P4 --> P5[Result Maximum Softness and Superior Machinability]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Differentiate between Process Annealing and Full Annealing in steels.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Process Annealing | Full Annealing |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Temperature | Sub-critical, 450–650 °C (below A₁) | Above A₃ (hypo-eutectoid) + 30–50 °C |
| Cooling | Air cooling | Furnace cooling |
| Microstructural change | Recovery only; no phase transformation | Complete α → γ → α transformation |
| Purpose | Restore ductility between cold-work passes | Maximum softening, grain refinement |
| Application | Sheet rolling intermediate stage | Pre-machining of castings and forgings |

*(1 mark for temperature difference, 1 mark for microstructural change, 1 mark for application distinction.)*

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define Spheroidization Annealing. Why is it preferred for high-carbon tool steels prior to machining?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Spheroidization annealing is a heat treatment in which high-carbon steel (typically 0.6–1.4 % C) is heated to a temperature just above A₁ (≈ 750 °C), held for prolonged time (15–25 h), and then cooled very slowly to transform the brittle lamellar cementite of pearlite into **soft, globular cementite particles** dispersed in a continuous ferrite matrix *(1 mark)*. The resulting structure is the **softest possible state** for the steel, with hardness often reduced to 190–220 BHN *(1 mark)*. This dramatically improves machinability, reduces cutting-tool wear, and prevents chatter during turning, milling, and drilling operations on high-carbon tool steels such as AISI W1, O1, and D2 *(1 mark)*.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question B-A — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Question (14 Marks):** *(a)* Describe with the aid of a temperature–time diagram, the **full annealing heat treatment cycle for a hypo-eutectoid medium-carbon steel (0.4 % C)**. Explain the microstructural changes occurring at each stage. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Discuss the **industrial applications of full annealing, process annealing, and stress relief annealing**, highlighting the temperature ranges, cooling methods, and typical use cases. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Steel Composition and Critical Temperatures (1 mark):**
For 0.4 % C hypo-eutectoid steel, from the Fe-Fe₃C phase diagram:
$$A_1 = 727\,^\circ\text{C}, \quad A_3 \approx 790\,^\circ\text{C}$$

**2. Full Annealing Cycle (Temperature–Time Diagram) (3 marks):**

| Stage | Description | Temperature | Time |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Heating ramp (slow, uniform) | RT → 820 °C | ~1.5 h for 25 mm section |
| 2 | Soaking (holding) | 820 °C (A₃ + 30 °C) | 1 h per 25 mm thickness |
| 3 | Furnace cooling | 820 °C → 500 °C | 0.5–1 °C/min (slow) |
| 4 | Air cooling | 500 °C → RT | Natural convection |

**3. Microstructural Changes at Each Stage (3 marks):**
- **Below A₁ (during heating):** Pearlite + Ferrite structure, no transformation.
- **At A₁ (727 °C):** Pearlite begins transforming to austenite (γ-Fe).
- **Between A₁ and A₃:** Progressive transformation; remaining proeutectoid ferrite dissolves into γ.
- **Above A₃ (820 °C):** **100 % austenite** with uniform carbon distribution.
- **During furnace cooling — above A₃:** Austenite begins to decompose; proeutectoid ferrite nucleates at austenite grain boundaries.
- **At A₁ (727 °C):** Remaining austenite transforms to **coarse lamellar pearlite**.
- **Final microstructure:** **Equiaxed ferrite + coarse pearlite** with refined grain size.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

| Annealing Type | Temperature Range | Cooling | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Full Annealing** | Above A₃ + 30–50 °C (≈ 820 °C for 0.4 % C steel) | Furnace cool | Pre-machining of large castings, hot-forged components, weldments; refines coarse as-cast grain structure |
| **Process Annealing** | 450–650 °C (below A₁) | Air cool | Intermediate restoration of ductility between successive cold-rolling passes in sheet and strip production |
| **Stress Relief Annealing** | 550–650 °C (below A₁) | Slow cool or furnace cool | Welded pressure vessels, boiler tubes, ship hulls, aircraft structural assemblies — to prevent stress-corrosion cracking and dimensional distortion |

*(2 marks per row × 3 = 6 marks, 1 mark for differentiating conclusion/summary statement.)*

---

### Question B-B — Alternative Choice — 14 Marks

**Question (14 Marks):** *(a)* What is **Spheroidization Annealing**? List the three common methods used to achieve it. Explain why globular cementite is preferred over lamellar cementite for machining. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Describe **Diffusion (Homogenization) Annealing** with reference to a nickel-based superalloy ingot. Why is it necessary, and what are the limitations? *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Definition (2 marks):**
Spheroidization annealing is a sub-critical (or near-A₁) heat treatment applied to high-carbon steels (0.6–1.4 % C) and tool steels, in which the hard, brittle **lamellar cementite** (Fe₃C) of the pearlitic structure is transformed into **soft, globular (spheroidal) cementite particles** dispersed in a continuous ferrite matrix. The resulting structure exhibits the **lowest possible hardness and maximum ductility** for that alloy.

**2. Three Common Methods (3 marks):**
1. **Method A — Prolonged Holding Just Above A₁:** Heat to A₁ + 10–20 °C (≈ 750 °C), hold for 15–25 hours, then slow furnace cool.
2. **Method B — Thermal Cycling:** Alternate heating between A₁ + 10 °C and A₁ − 20 °C multiple times to progressively spheroidize the cementite lamellae.
3. **Method C — Sub-critical Holding:** Heat directly to A₁ − 20 °C (≈ 700 °C) and hold for very long time (e.g., 30–50 hours).

**3. Why Globular Cementite is Preferred for Machining (2 marks):**
- Globular cementite particles act as **discrete, non-continuous micro-chips** that break off cleanly during cutting, reducing tool wear.
- Continuous lamellar cementite in pearlite causes **continuous tool–workpiece contact**, increasing friction, heat, and tool degradation.
- Spheroidized structure provides **uniform plastic flow** during machining, producing better surface finish and dimensional accuracy.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**1. Definition and Necessity (3 marks):**
Diffusion annealing (homogenization annealing) is a high-temperature heat treatment (1100–1300 °C) applied to as-cast alloy ingots to eliminate **dendritic microsegregation (coring)** — the chemical inhomogeneity that arises during non-equilibrium solidification. For nickel-based superalloys (e.g., Inconel 718, Waspaloy) containing Ti, Al, Cr, and Mo, segregation of these alloying elements between dendrite cores and interdendritic regions severely impairs hot-workability and mechanical properties. Prolonged exposure to high temperature enables **solid-state substitutional diffusion** that equalizes composition.

**2. Process Parameters (2 marks):**
- Temperature: **1180–1230 °C** (below incipient melting/solidus).
- Time: **10–20 hours**.
- Atmosphere: Protective (argon or vacuum) to prevent oxidation.
- Cooling: Furnace cool to avoid thermal stress.

**3. Limitations (2 marks):**
- **Excessive grain growth** at high temperature reduces toughness and fatigue life.
- Risk of **incipient melting** if temperature approaches the solidus line.
- **Energy-intensive** and expensive; not economically viable for low-alloy steels.
- May form **undesirable coarse precipitate phases** (e.g., σ-phase, carbides) that must be subsequently dissolved by re-solutioning.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing process annealing with full annealing** — the temperature criterion (sub-A₁ vs above-A₃) is the single most-tested discriminator; getting it reversed costs a full 3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the cooling method** — full anneal = furnace cool; process anneal = air cool; stress relief = slow cool. Examiners specifically allocate 1 mark for the cooling method.
> 3. **Not referencing the Fe-Fe₃C diagram** — for any steel heat treatment, failure to mention A₁ = 727 °C and whether the treatment is above or below this line is treated as an incomplete answer.
> 4. **Confusing spheroidization with full annealing** — both may use temperatures near A₁, but the objectives, hold times, and microstructures are entirely different.
> 5. **Omitting numerical examples** — KTU expects at least one numerical/quantitative statement (e.g., "above A₃ + 30 °C" or "1 h per 25 mm soaking rule") in any 7-mark sub-question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pre-Exam Quick Recall — Memorize Before Entering the KTU Exam Hall:**

- ✅ **Annealing** = Heat + Hold + Controlled Cool. Three words define every variant.
- ✅ **A₁ = 727 °C** — the master critical temperature for all steel annealing.
- ✅ **Full Anneal:** Above A₃ (hypo) or A₁ (hyper), furnace cool → coarse pearlite + ferrite.
- ✅ **Process Anneal:** 450–650 °C, sub-A₁, air cool → no phase change, just recovery.
- ✅ **Stress Relief:** 550–650 °C, sub-A₁, slow cool → no phase change, residual stress removal.
- ✅ **Spheroidization:** A₁ ± 20 °C, long hold (15–25 h) → globular Fe₃C, softest state.
- ✅ **Recrystallization Anneal:** Above $T_R = 0.4 \, T_m$ (K), below T_m → new strain-free grains.
- ✅ **Diffusion/Homogenization Anneal:** 1100–1300 °C, 10–20 h → eliminates segregation.
- ✅ **Isothermal Anneal:** Salt bath at 600–650 °C (sub-A₁) → uniform fine pearlite.
- ✅ **Soaking Rule:** **1 h per 25 mm of section thickness** — universal rule.
- ✅ **Hall-Petch:** Strength increases as grain size decreases ($d^{-1/2}$ dependence).
- ✅ **Spheroidized structure is the softest → best machinability** for high-carbon steels.
- ✅ **Diffusion distance** $x = \sqrt{Dt}$ — essential for estimating homogenization time.
- ✅ **Arrhenius diffusivity** $D = D_0 \exp(-Q_d/RT)$ — higher T → exponentially higher D.
- ✅ **Welded components** → Stress Relief Anneal. **Castings** → Full Anneal. **Cold-worked sheet** → Process Anneal. **Tool steel blanks** → Spheroidization.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Normalizing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Normalizing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Normalizing** is a heat treatment process in which a ferrous alloy (steel/cast iron) is heated to a temperature **approximately 30°C – 50°C above its upper critical temperature** (Ac₃ for hypoeutectoid steels or Acₘ for hypereutectoid steels), held at this austenitizing temperature long enough to achieve a **homogeneous single-phase austenite (γ-FCC) structure**, and then **cooled in still air at room temperature** to produce a refined pearlitic microstructure.

In the iron–iron carbide (Fe–Fe₃C) phase diagram, the relevant reference temperatures for normalizing are:

- **Ac₁** = 727 °C (eutectoid temperature, lower critical)
- **Ac₃** = upper critical temperature for **hypoeutectoid** steels (carbon < 0.76 %)
- **Acₘ** = upper critical temperature for **hypereutectoid** steels (carbon > 0.76 %)
- **Ac₃ ≈ Acₘ** for **eutectoid** steel at 0.76 % C

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Think of normalizing like "resetting" the internal grain structure of a metal — similar to how a chef cools a hot dish in open air to firm it up evenly.
> - Imagine a piece of bread that has been baked unevenly, leaving large, rough air pockets. To "normalize" it, you reheat the bread to a soft, uniform state and let it cool naturally in still air. The resulting texture is finer, more uniform, and mechanically stronger.
> - In steel, the **coarse, non-uniform grains** formed after hot working, casting, or welding are transformed by heating into a uniform **austenite phase**, and the natural air cooling produces a **fine, evenly distributed pearlite + ferrite (or pearlite + cementite) microstructure** that is stronger and tougher than the original.

### 1.3 Salient Features (KTU Key-Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Highlights for KTU Board Exam:**
> 1. Heating range: **Ac₃ + 30–50 °C** (hypoeutectoid) or **Acₘ + 30–50 °C** (hypereutectoid).
> 2. Cooling medium: **Still air (furnace-cooled air, no fan/water)**.
> 3. Cooling rate: Faster than furnace annealing, slower than quenching.
> 4. Primary goal: **Refine grain size** and **homogenize microstructure**.
> 5. Resulting structure: **Fine pearlite + proeutectoid phase** (uniform distribution).
> 6. Outcomes: **Improved strength, hardness, toughness, and machinability** (vs. as-cast or coarse-grained condition).

### 1.4 Reference Temperatures for Common Steels (Bold Constants Table)

| Steel Grade | Carbon Content | Ac₃ / Acₘ (°C) | Normalizing Temperature (°C) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Low-carbon steel | 0.10 % | ~870 | **900 – 925** |
| Medium-carbon steel | 0.40 % | ~790 | **820 – 870** |
| Eutectoid steel | 0.76 % | ~727 | **760 – 800** |
| High-carbon steel | 1.00 % | ~850 | **870 – 900** |
| Tool steel (1.2 % C) | 1.20 % | ~900 | **900 – 950** |
| Cast iron (3.5 % C) | 3.50 % | — | **850 – 900** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bold takeaway:** Normalizing temperature is **always 30–50 °C above the upper critical temperature** to ensure complete austenitization without grain coarsening or decarburization.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Stages of Normalizing (Structured Steps)

The normalizing process is broken down into **three sequential stages**:

**Step 1 — Heating (Austenitization)**
- The steel is heated to a temperature **30–50 °C above Ac₃ (or Acₘ)**.
- The original ferrite + pearlite microstructure transforms completely into **austenite (γ)**.
- This is a **diffusion-controlled, reconstructive** phase transformation.
- Holding time: usually **1 hour per 25 mm of cross-section thickness** (or per inch of thickness) for complete homogenization.

**Step 2 — Soaking (Homogenization)**
- The metal is held at the normalizing temperature to ensure:
  - Complete dissolution of cementite into austenite.
  - Homogeneous distribution of carbon throughout the γ-phase.
  - Elimination of chemical segregation from prior casting/hot working.
- Typical soak time: **15 – 30 minutes per 25 mm** of section thickness.

**Step 3 — Air Cooling (Phase Transformation on Cooling)**
- The component is removed from the furnace and cooled in **still air** (no fans, no water).
- Cooling rate: typically **0.5 – 1.5 °C/s** depending on section size and air flow.
- The γ-austenite transforms to:
  - **Fine pearlite** + **proeutectoid ferrite** (hypoeutectoid steels)
  - **Fine pearlite** + **proeutectoid cementite** (hypereutectoid steels)
- The resulting grain size is **finer (ASTM 6–8)** than as-cast or annealed structures.

### 2.2 Metallurgical Objectives of Normalizing

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Normalize? The 'Why' Behind the Process:**
> 1. **Grain refinement** — eliminates coarse Widmanstätten structures from prior thermal/mechanical abuse.
> 2. **Homogenization** — removes chemical segregation inherited from solidification.
> 3. **Improved mechanical properties** — slight increase in tensile strength, hardness, and yield strength.
> 4. **Better machinability** — uniform hardness makes subsequent machining predictable.
> 5. **Preparation for further heat treatment** — provides a consistent starting microstructure for hardening (quenching), tempering, or case-hardening.
> 6. **Stress relief** — removes residual stresses from prior cold/hot working, forging, or welding.
> 7. **Improves toughness and impact resistance** — finer grains impede dislocation motion and crack propagation.

### 2.3 Comparison Matrix: Normalizing vs. Annealing vs. Quenching

| Property | Normalizing | Full Annealing | Quenching |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heating temperature | **Ac₃ + 30–50 °C** | **Ac₃ + 30–50 °C** | **Ac₃ + 30–50 °C** |
| Soaking time | Short | Long (1 h/inch) | Short |
| Cooling medium | **Still air** | **Furnace (very slow)** | **Water/oil/brine (rapid)** |
| Cooling rate | Moderate (~1 °C/s) | Very slow (~0.01 °C/s) | Very fast (>100 °C/s) |
| Microstructure | Fine pearlite + ferrite | Coarse pearlite + ferrite | Martensite (BCT) |
| Grain size | **Fine (ASTM 6–8)** | Coarse (ASTM 1–4) | Fine (refined) |
| Hardness (0.4 % C) | ~140–180 HB | ~120–140 HB | ~550–650 HB (as-quenched) |
| Strength | Medium-high | Lowest | Highest |
| Toughness | Good | Highest (soft state) | Low (brittle) |
| Ductility | Moderate | Highest | Lowest |
| Purpose | Refine grain, homogenize | Soften, relieve stress | Harden |
| Cost | Low | High (long furnace time) | Moderate |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for Board Examinations**

| # | Concept | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Lever rule – Proeutectoid ferrite fraction (hypoeutectoid)** | $W_{\alpha} = \dfrac{0.76 - C_{0}}{0.76 - 0.022}$ | Mass fraction of proeutectoid ferrite |
| 2 | **Lever rule – Pearlite fraction (hypoeutectoid)** | $W_{P} = \dfrac{C_{0} - 0.022}{0.76 - 0.022}$ | Mass fraction of eutectoid pearlite |
| 3 | **Lever rule – Proeutectoid cementite (hypereutectoid)** | $W_{Fe_{3}C} = \dfrac{C_{0} - 0.76}{6.70 - 0.76}$ | Mass fraction of proeutectoid cementite |
| 4 | **Rule of mixtures – Hardness of normalized steel** | $\text{HB}_{\text{steel}} = W_{\alpha} \cdot \text{HB}_{\alpha} + W_{P} \cdot \text{HB}_{P}$ | $\text{HB}_{\alpha} \approx 80$, $\text{HB}_{P} \approx 200$ |
| 5 | **Hall-Petch grain refinement relation** | $\sigma_{y} = \sigma_{0} + k_{y} \cdot d^{-1/2}$ | $d$ = average grain diameter (mm) |
| 6 | **Normalizing temperature (hypoeutectoid)** | $T_{N} = \text{Ac}_{3} + (30 \text{ to } 50)$ | Temperature in °C |
| 7 | **Normalizing temperature (hypereutectoid)** | $T_{N} = \text{Ac}_{m} + (30 \text{ to } 50)$ | Temperature in °C |
| 8 | **Soaking time estimate** | $t_{s} = 1 \text{ h per } 25 \text{ mm}$ thickness | Industrial rule of thumb |
| 9 | **Pearlite interlamellar spacing (Zener approximation)** | $\lambda = \dfrac{k \cdot (T_{E} - T)}{\Delta T}$ | $\lambda$ decreases as undercooling $\Delta T$ increases |
| 10 | **Hardness of normalized steel (empirical)** | $\text{HB} \approx 200 \cdot W_{P} + 80 \cdot W_{\alpha}$ | For plain-carbon steels |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX SAFETY:** In all equations above, all set/vertical-bar notation has been written as fractions or text (e.g., $(C_0 - 0.022)$) **instead of $\vert ... \vert$** to avoid breaking KTU markdown table rendering.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where is Normalizing Used in Industry?**
> - **Railway components** — rails, axles, wheels (need uniform strength and toughness).
> - **Forged shafts and gears** — pre-treatment before case-hardening or final machining.
> - **Welded fabrications** — stress relief and grain refinement of HAZ (heat-affected zone).
> - **Castings** — homogenization of dendritic segregation in cast iron and cast steel.
> - **Automotive components** — crankshafts, connecting rods, and camshafts.
> - **Pressure vessels and boilers** — uniform grain structure for predictable service life.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Problem 1 — Phase Fraction and Hardness of Normalized Hypoeutectoid Steel

**Problem Statement:**
A normalized plain-carbon steel contains **0.40 wt % C**. Compute:
- (a) The mass fraction of proeutectoid ferrite and pearlite at room temperature.
- (b) The approximate Brinell hardness of the normalized steel using the rule of mixtures.
- (c) The expected microstructure description.

**Given:**
- Carbon content of steel: $C_0 = 0.40\ \%$
- Eutectoid composition: $C_E = 0.76\ \%$
- Carbon in ferrite (α): $C_{\alpha} = 0.022\ \%$
- Hardness of pure ferrite: $\text{HB}_{\alpha} = 80$
- Hardness of fully pearlite: $\text{HB}_{P} = 200$

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Part (a) — Phase Fractions (Lever Rule Applied to the Fe–Fe₃C Diagram)**

The proeutectoid ferrite forms when austenite (0.40 % C) cools below Ac₃ down to 727 °C, expelling carbon into the remaining austenite until it reaches 0.76 % C, where it transforms to pearlite.

$$
W_{\alpha} = \frac{C_{E} - C_{0}}{C_{E} - C_{\alpha}} = \frac{0.76 - 0.40}{0.76 - 0.022}
$$

Performing the arithmetic step-by-step:

$$
W_{\alpha} = \frac{0.36}{0.738} = 0.4878
$$

Therefore, $W_{\alpha} \approx 0.488$ (i.e., **48.8 %** proeutectoid ferrite by mass).

For pearlite:

$$
W_{P} = \frac{C_{0} - C_{\alpha}}{C_{E} - C_{\alpha}} = \frac{0.40 - 0.022}{0.76 - 0.022}
$$

Performing the arithmetic:

$$
W_{P} = \frac{0.378}{0.738} = 0.5122
$$

Therefore, $W_{P} \approx 0.512$ (i.e., **51.2 %** pearlite by mass).

**Verification of mass balance:** $W_{\alpha} + W_{P} = 0.488 + 0.512 = 1.000$ ✓

**Part (b) — Brinell Hardness by Rule of Mixtures**

$$
\text{HB}_{\text{steel}} = W_{\alpha} \cdot \text{HB}_{\alpha} + W_{P} \cdot \text{HB}_{P}
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
\text{HB}_{\text{steel}} = (0.488)(80) + (0.512)(200)
$$

Computing each term step-by-step:

$$
(0.488)(80) = 39.04
$$

$$
(0.512)(200) = 102.4
$$

Adding the contributions:

$$
\text{HB}_{\text{steel}} = 39.04 + 102.4 = 141.44
$$

Therefore, $\text{HB}_{\text{steel}} \approx 141$ (i.e., **~141 BHN**).

**Part (c) — Microstructure Description**

- The normalized 0.4 % C steel has a microstructure of:
  - **~49 % fine proeutectoid ferrite** (white, soft, ductile matrix phase).
  - **~51 % fine pearlite** (dark, lamellar α + Fe₃C, harder and stronger).
- Due to air cooling (faster than furnace cooling), the pearlite is **fine-grained**, giving:
  - Higher strength than annealed state.
  - Slightly lower ductility than annealed state.
  - More uniform grain distribution than as-rolled or as-cast state.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Problem 2 — Normalizing Temperature Determination for Hypereutectoid Steel

**Problem Statement:**
Determine the normalizing temperature for a **1.0 wt % C** plain-carbon hypereutectoid steel. Also estimate the mass fraction of proeutectoid cementite.

**Given:**
- Carbon content: $C_0 = 1.0\ \%$
- Eutectoid composition: $C_E = 0.76\ \%$
- Cementite composition: $C_{Fe_{3}C} = 6.70\ \%$
- Approximate Acₘ for 1.0 % C: $T_{\text{Ac}_{m}} \approx 850\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Part (a) — Normalizing Temperature**

For hypereutectoid steels, we heat above Acₘ (not Ac₃):

$$
T_{N} = T_{\text{Ac}_{m}} + 30\ ^{\circ}\text{C (minimum offset)}
$$

$$
T_{N} = 850 + 30 = 880\ ^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

So, the normalizing temperature lies in the range:

$$
880\ ^{\circ}\text{C} \leq T_{N} \leq 900\ ^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

We choose $T_{N} = 880\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ (lower bound) to **avoid grain coarsening and decarburization** in this high-carbon steel.

**Part (b) — Proeutectoid Cementite Fraction**

The proeutectoid cementite forms when austenite (1.0 % C) cools just below Acₘ down to 727 °C. Cementite is rejected, and the remaining austenite reaches 0.76 % C and transforms to pearlite.

$$
W_{Fe_{3}C} = \frac{C_{0} - C_{E}}{C_{Fe_{3}C} - C_{E}} = \frac{1.0 - 0.76}{6.70 - 0.76}
$$

Performing the arithmetic step-by-step:

$$
W_{Fe_{3}C} = \frac{0.24}{5.94} = 0.0404
$$

Therefore, $W_{Fe_{3}C} \approx 0.040$ (i.e., **4.04 %** proeutectoid cementite).

The remaining **95.96 %** is pearlite.

**Verification:** $W_{Fe_{3}C} + W_{P} = 0.040 + 0.960 = 1.000$ ✓

---

### 3.3 Analytical Problem 3 — Hall-Petch Strengthening from Grain Refinement

**Problem Statement:**
A steel component is first annealed (coarse grains, $d_1 = 0.10$ mm) and then normalized (fine grains, $d_2 = 0.025$ mm). Estimate the **percentage increase in yield strength** due to grain refinement alone, given $\sigma_0 = 50$ MPa and $k_y = 0.65$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Part (a) — Yield Strength in Annealed Condition**

$$
\sigma_{y,1} = \sigma_{0} + k_{y} \cdot d_{1}^{-1/2}
$$

Computing $d_1^{-1/2}$:

$$
d_1 = 0.10 \text{ mm} = 0.10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}
$$

$$
d_{1}^{-1/2} = (1.0 \times 10^{-4})^{-1/2} = (10^{-4})^{-1/2} = 10^{2} = 100 \text{ m}^{-1/2}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\sigma_{y,1} = 50 + (0.65)(100) = 50 + 65 = 115 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Part (b) — Yield Strength in Normalized Condition**

$$
d_2 = 0.025 \text{ mm} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}
$$

$$
d_{2}^{-1/2} = (2.5 \times 10^{-5})^{-1/2} = (2.5)^{-1/2} \times 10^{2.5} = 0.6325 \times 316.23 = 200.0 \text{ m}^{-1/2}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\sigma_{y,2} = 50 + (0.65)(200) = 50 + 130 = 180 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Part (c) — Percentage Increase in Yield Strength**

$$
\% \text{ Increase} = \frac{\sigma_{y,2} - \sigma_{y,1}}{\sigma_{y,1}} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{ Increase} = \frac{180 - 115}{115} \times 100 = \frac{65}{115} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{ Increase} = 0.5652 \times 100 = 56.52\ \%
$$

**Conclusion:** Normalizing (grain refinement from ASTM ~3 to ASTM ~7) increases the yield strength by **~57 %** through the Hall-Petch mechanism alone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The Hall-Petch relation tells us that **halving the grain diameter increases yield strength by ~41 %** (theoretically). Normalizing achieves this through moderate air cooling, which produces fine pearlite colonies and fine ferrite grains — a cost-effective strengthening route compared to quenching (which is more energy-intensive and creates distortion risks).

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Normalizing Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Steel Component in As-Cast or As-Rolled State] --> stepB[Step 1: Heat to Ac3 or Acm + 30 to 50 deg C]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 2: Soak for 15 to 30 minutes per 25 mm thickness]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 3: Hold until complete austenitization: gamma FCC phase]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 4: Remove from furnace]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 5: Cool in still air at room temperature]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 6: Phase transformation gamma to fine pearlite plus proeutectoid phase]
    stepG --> stepH[Result: Fine grained uniform microstructure]
    stepH --> stepI[Improved: Strength, Hardness, Toughness, Machinability]
    stepI --> stepEnd[End: Component ready for service or further treatment]
```

### 4.2 Microstructural Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A1[As Cast Steel]
        a1[Coarse Dendritic Grains] --> a2[Chemical Segregation]
        a2 --> a3[Non uniform Properties]
    end
    subgraph A2[Normalized Steel]
        b1[Uniform Austenitization] --> b2[Fine Pearlite plus Ferrite]
        b2 --> b3[Refined ASTM 6 to 8 Grains]
    end
    subgraph A3[Annealed Steel]
        c1[Long Furnace Soak] --> c2[Coarse Pearlite plus Ferrite]
        c2 --> c3[Soft and Ductile State]
    end
    A1 -->|Heat Treatment Route: Normalizing| A2
    A1 -->|Heat Treatment Route: Annealing| A3
```

### 4.3 Fe-Fe3C Phase Diagram Mapping for Normalizing (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Hypo[Hypoeutectoid Steel Region]
        h1[Carbon less than 0.76 percent] --> h2[Normalize above Ac3 line]
        h2 --> h3[Microstructure: Fine Pearlite plus Proeutectoid Ferrite]
    end
    subgraph Eutec[Eutectoid Steel Region]
        e1[Carbon equals 0.76 percent] --> e2[Normalize above 727 deg C Ac3 equals Acm]
        e2 --> e3[Microstructure: 100 percent Fine Pearlite]
    end
    subgraph Hyper[Hypereutectoid Steel Region]
        hp1[Carbon greater than 0.76 percent] --> hp2[Normalize above Acm line]
        hp2 --> hp3[Microstructure: Fine Pearlite plus Proeutectoid Cementite]
    end
```

### 4.4 Effect of Cooling Rate on Resulting Microstructure (TTT Context)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startT[Steel at Austenitizing Temperature: gamma phase] --> cool1[Very Slow Cooling: Furnace]
    startT --> cool2[Moderate Cooling: Still Air Normalizing]
    startT --> cool3[Rapid Cooling: Quench]
    cool1 --> r1[Coarse Pearlite: Soft, Ductile]
    cool2 --> r2[Fine Pearlite plus Proeutectoid: Strong, Tough]
    cool3 --> r3[Martensite BCT: Very Hard, Brittle]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Interpretation:** Each block represents a *process window*. The arrows indicate the *transformation route*. Normalizing occupies the **middle ground** — faster than annealing (so grains are finer) but slower than quenching (so the structure is still pearlitic, not martensitic). This unique position makes it a versatile, low-cost heat treatment.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
> **Define normalizing. Mention any two objectives of normalizing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Normalizing is a heat treatment process in which steel is heated to a temperature **30 °C – 50 °C above its upper critical temperature** (Ac₃ for hypoeutectoid steels, Acₘ for hypereutectoid steels), held at that temperature for complete austenitization, and then **cooled in still air** at room temperature.

**Two Objectives (1.5 marks):**
1. To **refine the grain size** and eliminate coarse grains formed during prior hot working, casting, or welding.
2. To **homogenize the microstructure** by removing chemical segregation and producing uniform mechanical properties.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
> **Distinguish between normalizing and full annealing (any three points).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S. No. | Normalizing | Full Annealing |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cooled in **still air** (faster cooling) | Cooled **inside the furnace** (very slow cooling) |
| 2 | Produces **fine pearlite** and refined grains | Produces **coarse pearlite** and coarse grains |
| 3 | Higher **hardness and strength** | Lower hardness, higher **ductility and softness** |
| 4 | Shorter cycle time, **lower cost** | Long furnace cycle, higher cost |
| 5 | Used to **refine grains** and improve strength | Used to **soften** for maximum machinability |

(Any three points × 1 mark each = 3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the normalizing process in detail. Describe the various stages with a neat temperature-vs-time diagram and discuss the metallurgical changes that occur during each stage.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A normalized plain-carbon steel contains **0.55 wt % C**. Calculate the mass fraction of proeutectoid ferrite and pearlite in the microstructure. Also estimate the Brinell hardness of the steel. Given: $\text{HB}_{\alpha} = 80$, $\text{HB}_{P} = 200$, $C_{\alpha} = 0.022\ \%$.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Process Stages (7 marks):**

**Stage 1 — Heating (2 marks):** The steel is heated slowly (to avoid thermal shock) to a temperature **30–50 °C above Ac₃** (for hypoeutectoid). At this temperature, the ferrite + pearlite structure transforms completely to **austenite (γ)** via a diffusion-controlled reconstructive transformation. The reaction is:

$$
\alpha + \text{Fe}_{3}\text{C} \;\longrightarrow\; \gamma \quad (\text{at } T > \text{Ac}_{3})
$$

**Stage 2 — Soaking (2 marks):** The steel is held at the normalizing temperature for **~15–30 minutes per 25 mm of section thickness**. This ensures:
- Complete dissolution of cementite lamellae into austenite.
- Homogenization of carbon throughout the γ-phase.
- Removal of dendritic segregation.

**Stage 3 — Air Cooling (2 marks):** The component is removed from the furnace and cooled in still air. The austenite transforms to **fine pearlite + proeutectoid ferrite**. The cooling rate (~1 °C/s) is faster than furnace annealing but slower than quenching, producing **fine lamellar spacing** in pearlite and refined prior-austenite grain boundaries.

**Temperature-Time Diagram (1 mark):**

```
T (°C)
   │   ┌──── Ac₃ + 30 °C ─────┐
   │   │  Soaking (γ region)  │
   │   │                       │
Ac₃├ - │                       │ 
   │   │                       │
   │   │                       │
727├ - ├───────────────────────┤────── Ac₁ (eutectoid)
   │   │                       │ \
   │   │                       │  \  Air cooling
   │   │                       │   \ (still air)
   │   │                       │    \
RT │   └───────────────────────┴───── \────── 
   └────────────────────────────────────► Time
        Heating  Soak     Cooling
```

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Proeutectoid ferrite fraction:**

$$
W_{\alpha} = \frac{C_{E} - C_{0}}{C_{E} - C_{\alpha}} = \frac{0.76 - 0.55}{0.76 - 0.022} = \frac{0.21}{0.738} = 0.2846
$$

So $W_{\alpha} \approx 0.285$ or **28.5 %**.

**Step 2 — Pearlite fraction:**

$$
W_{P} = \frac{C_{0} - C_{\alpha}}{C_{E} - C_{\alpha}} = \frac{0.55 - 0.022}{0.76 - 0.022} = \frac{0.528}{0.738} = 0.7154
$$

So $W_{P} \approx 0.715$ or **71.5 %**.

**Step 3 — Brinell hardness (Rule of mixtures):**

$$
\text{HB}_{\text{steel}} = (0.285)(80) + (0.715)(200)
$$

$$
= 22.8 + 143.0 = 165.8
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{HB}_{\text{steel}} \approx 166 \text{ BHN}}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the metallurgical objectives and engineering applications of normalizing. Compare the microstructure and properties of normalized steel with those of annealed and quenched steels.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A steel component has an average grain diameter of **0.08 mm** in the as-rolled condition. After normalizing, the grain diameter is reduced to **0.02 mm**. Using the Hall-Petch relation, estimate the percentage increase in yield strength. Given: $\sigma_0 = 40$ MPa, $k_y = 0.70$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Objectives and Applications (7 marks):**

**Metallurgical Objectives (3.5 marks):**
1. **Grain refinement** — eliminates coarse grains and Widmanstätten plates.
2. **Homogenization** — removes chemical segregation from solidification.
3. **Stress relief** — reduces residual stresses from prior processing.
4. **Improved mechanical properties** — higher strength and hardness than as-rolled.
5. **Preparation for further treatment** — provides uniform starting structure for hardening or case-carburizing.

**Engineering Applications (1.5 marks):**
- Railway axles, rails, and wheels.
- Forged crankshafts, connecting rods, gears, and shafts.
- Welded structural components (offshore platforms, bridges).
- Castings of steel and ductile iron.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Property | Normalized | Annealed | Quenched |
|---|---|---|---|
| Microstructure | Fine pearlite + ferrite | Coarse pearlite + ferrite | Martensite (BCT) |
| Grain size | ASTM 6–8 (fine) | ASTM 1–4 (coarse) | Fine (refined) |
| Hardness (0.4 % C) | ~140–180 HB | ~120–140 HB | ~550–650 HB |
| Toughness | Good | Highest | Lowest |
| Ductility | Moderate | Highest | Lowest |

**Part (b) — Hall-Petch Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Yield strength in as-rolled condition (1.5 marks):**

$$
d_1 = 0.08 \text{ mm} = 8.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}
$$

$$
d_{1}^{-1/2} = (8.0 \times 10^{-5})^{-1/2} = (8.0)^{-1/2} \times 10^{2.5} = 0.3536 \times 316.23 = 111.80 \text{ m}^{-1/2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{y,1} = 40 + (0.70)(111.80) = 40 + 78.26 = 118.26 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Yield strength in normalized condition (1.5 marks):**

$$
d_2 = 0.02 \text{ mm} = 2.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}
$$

$$
d_{2}^{-1/2} = (2.0 \times 10^{-5})^{-1/2} = (2.0)^{-1/2} \times 10^{2.5} = 0.7071 \times 316.23 = 223.61 \text{ m}^{-1/2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{y,2} = 40 + (0.70)(223.61) = 40 + 156.53 = 196.53 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Percentage increase (2 marks):**

$$
\% \text{ Increase} = \frac{\sigma_{y,2} - \sigma_{y,1}}{\sigma_{y,1}} \times 100
$$

$$
= \frac{196.53 - 118.26}{118.26} \times 100 = \frac{78.27}{118.26} \times 100 = 66.18\ \%
$$

**Conclusion:** The yield strength increases by approximately **66 %** after normalizing.

**Physical Interpretation (2 marks):** The grain size has been reduced by a factor of 4 (from 0.08 to 0.02 mm). According to Hall-Petch, $d^{-1/2}$ doubles, so the grain-boundary strengthening term doubles, leading to substantial yield strength improvement. This is the principal strengthening mechanism of normalizing.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing Ac₃ with Ac₁** — Students often write "normalizing is done above Ac₁ (727 °C)." This is **wrong** for hypoeutectoid steels. Normalizing requires heating **above Ac₃** to ensure *complete* austenitization, not partial. Ac₁ is the temperature where austenite *starts* to form — insufficient for normalizing. **[Lose 1–2 marks]**
> 2. **Writing "cooling in air" without specifying "still air"** — Normalizing specifically uses **still (calm) air**, not forced air. Forced air cooling shifts the process toward a different cooling regime, closer to a mild quench. **[Lose 0.5–1 mark]**
> 3. **Mixing up normalizing with annealing or quenching in the process diagram** — The T-T diagram is critical. Normalizing has a **rapid descent** (air cooling) while annealing has a flat, slow-cooling tail. Drawing the wrong shape leads to mark deduction. **[Lose 1–2 marks]**
> 4. **Forgetting the 30–50 °C offset above Ac₃/Acₘ** — Students often just say "above Ac₃" without the +30–50 °C offset. This offset is essential to ensure complete austenite formation and is a frequently tested KTU point. **[Lose 0.5 mark]**
> 5. **Using $C_{\alpha} = 0$ instead of $C_{\alpha} = 0.022\ \%$** in lever-rule calculations — This is a classic numerical error. Ferrite at room temperature contains up to 0.022 % C in solid solution, which must be used in the lever rule. **[Lose 1 mark per occurrence]**
> 6. **Forgetting to verify mass balance** ($W_{\alpha} + W_{P} = 1$) — Examiners reward consistency checks. A simple verification shows correctness. **[May lose 0.5 mark]**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Normalizing (Module 3)**

- [x] **Definition:** Heating **30–50 °C above Ac₃ (or Acₘ)** + soaking + **air cooling (still)**.
- [x] **Primary purpose:** **Grain refinement** + **homogenization** of microstructure.
- [x] **Heating range:** $T_{N} = \text{Ac}_{3} + 30{-}50\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ (hypoeutectoid) or $\text{Ac}_{m} + 30{-}50\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ (hypereutectoid).
- [x] **Cooling medium:** **Still air** (not water, not oil, not furnace).
- [x] **Cooling rate:** ~0.5 – 1.5 °C/s (moderate, between annealing and quenching).
- [x] **Resulting microstructure:**
  - Hypoeutectoid → **Fine pearlite + proeutectoid ferrite**.
  - Eutectoid → **100 % fine pearlite**.
  - Hypereutectoid → **Fine pearlite + proeutectoid cementite**.
- [x] **Hardness (0.4 % C normalized):** ~140–180 HB.
- [x] **Hardness rule of mixtures:** $\text{HB} = W_{\alpha} \cdot \text{HB}_{\alpha} + W_{P} \cdot \text{HB}_{P}$.
- [x] **Lever rule formula:** $W_{\alpha} = (0.76 - C_0)/(0.76 - 0.022)$.
- [x] **Hall-Petch strengthening:** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$ — grain refinement increases strength.
- [x] **Comparison keywords:** Normalizing is **cheaper than annealing**, **stronger than annealing**, **softer and tougher than quenching**, **faster than full annealing**, **slower than quenching**.
- [x] **Industrial applications:** Railway axles, gears, shafts, welded structures, castings, crankshafts, pressure vessels.
- [x] **Soaking time:** 1 h per 25 mm of section thickness (industrial rule).
- [x] **Key distinction from annealing:** Normalizing uses **air cooling** (faster) → finer grains, higher strength.
- [x] **Key distinction from quenching:** Normalizing uses **air** (slower) → pearlite, not martensite.
- [x] **KTU favorite questions:** Define normalizing (3 marks), compare with annealing (3 or 7 marks), lever rule + hardness (7 or 14 marks), Hall-Petch calculation (7 marks).
- [x] **Always remember:** $C_{\alpha} = 0.022\ \%$ (NOT zero) in all numerical problems.
- [x] **Normalizing is a cost-effective, low-distortion alternative** to quenching for moderate strength improvement.
- [x] **Final microstructure descriptor word for exam:** "**uniform, fine-grained, equiaxed pearlitic structure**."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hardening
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING
> - **Topic:** Hardening
> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hardening** is defined as a controlled metallurgical process that deliberately increases the resistance of a metallic material to plastic deformation, indentation, scratching, and wear by impeding the motion of dislocations through microstructural engineering. It is quantified by the metric **Hardness (H)**, typically measured in units of **Vickers Hardness Number (HV)**, **Rockwell Hardness (HRC / HRB)**, or **Brinell Hardness Number (BHN)**.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBPET304 — Material Science and Engineering)**, hardening is treated as a **strengthening mechanism** — a category of processes that enhance the **Yield Strength (σy)**, **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS / σu)**, and **Hardness (H)** of a material by systematically obstructing dislocation glide.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically groups *Hardening* as one of the **strengthening mechanisms** of metals, alongside **Annealing**, **Recovery**, **Recrystallization**, and **Grain Growth**. The examiner expects you to distinguish *strengthening* (raising σy) from *toughening* (raising fracture energy).

## 1.2 The Five Engineering Mechanisms of Hardening

Hardening is **not a single process** — it is an umbrella term covering **five distinct metallurgical mechanisms**, each operating at a different length-scale and exploiting a different obstacle to dislocation motion:

| S.No | Hardening Mechanism | Length-Scale of Obstacle | KTU High-Yield Tag |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| **1** | **Strain Hardening (Work / Cold Hardening)** | ~ 10⁻⁸ m (forest dislocations) | Most frequently asked |
| **2** | **Solid Solution Hardening** | ~ 10⁻¹⁰ m (solute atoms) | Frequently asked |
| **3** | **Grain Boundary Hardening (Hall-Petch)** | ~ 10⁻⁶ – 10⁻³ m (grains) | Universally tested |
| **4** | **Precipitation Hardening (Age Hardening)** | ~ 10⁻⁹ – 10⁻⁸ m (second-phase particles) | Most numerical / derivations |
| **5** | **Dispersion Hardening** | ~ 10⁻⁸ – 10⁻⁶ m (oxide / carbide particles) | Concept-favourite |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Iron Bar That Became a Spring"

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine a long, orderly queue of people (atoms) walking through a narrow corridor (a metal crystal).** Now imagine the corridor has a few hurdles — some people are larger (solute atoms), some are standing still blocking the way (precipitates), and some have built small walls (grain boundaries). To get past each hurdle, the queue must slow, climb, or detour. **The more hurdles you place in the corridor, the more force the front of the queue must apply to keep moving.** That extra force is exactly what we measure as **increased hardness / yield strength** in a metal. The "queue" is the dislocation line — *hardening = making dislocation motion difficult.*

## 1.4 Physical Metric Anchors (Examiner-Ready Constants)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **1 GPa (Gigapascal)** = **10⁹ N/m²** = standard SI unit for yield strength and hardness.
> - **Burgers vector (b)** for FCC metals ≈ **0.25 nm** (≈ 2.5 × 10⁻¹⁰ m) — a recurring constant in dislocation-strengthening formulas.
> - **Shear Modulus (G)** for mild steel ≈ **80 GPa**; for aluminum ≈ **26 GPa**; for copper ≈ **42 GPa**.
> - The **Theoretical Shear Strength** of a perfect crystal ≈ **G / 2π ≈ 0.16 G**, but real engineering metals yield at **10⁻³ – 10⁻⁴ G** due to mobile dislocations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Mechanism 1 — Strain Hardening (Work Hardening / Cold Working)

> [!NOTE]
> **Strain hardening** is the phenomenon in which a metal becomes stronger and harder as it is plastically deformed *below its recrystallization temperature*. The applied stress generates new dislocations whose mutual interaction (intersection, jog formation, forest dislocations) raises the critical resolved shear stress (CRSS) required for further slip.

**Operative Logic — Step-by-Step:**

1. Apply plastic strain ε to the metal (e.g., rolling, drawing, forging at room temperature).
