2. Dislocation density ρ rises from ~10⁶ lines/m² (annealed) to ~10¹⁴ – 10¹⁵ lines/m² (heavily cold-worked).
3. Each new dislocation acts as an obstacle to other mobile dislocations (forest interaction).
4. Average slip distance (mean free path) Λ of a mobile dislocation decreases.
5. CRSS rises → macroscopic σy and H increase.

**Why is cold working called "strain hardening"?**
Because the **stress required to continue plastic flow increases monotonically with strain** — the engineering stress-strain curve in the plastic region has a positive slope.

## 2.2 Mechanism 2 — Solid Solution Hardening

> [!NOTE]
> A **substitutional** or **interstitial** solute atom of different size / modulus from the host produces a **local elastic strain field** in the crystal lattice. This strain field interacts with the strain field of a moving dislocation, raising the energy required for the dislocation to pass.

**Two sub-models (KTU-favourite):**

- **Fleischer Model (1963):** Stronger size + modulus misfit → τ_sol ∝ G·ε^(3/2)·c^(1/2)
- **Labusch Model (1970):** More rigorous for concentrated alloys → τ_sol ∝ G·ε^(4/3)·c^(2/3)

Where `c` = solute concentration (atom fraction) and `ε` = misfit parameter.

## 2.3 Mechanism 3 — Grain Boundary Hardening (Hall-Petch Strengthening)

> [!NOTE]
> A grain boundary acts as a **barrier to dislocation motion** because the slip plane orientation changes abruptly across it. Dislocations pile up against the boundary, and the stress concentration at the pile-up tip must trigger slip in the neighbouring grain.

**The Classic Hall-Petch Equation (1951, 1953):**

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$$

Where:

- **σy** = yield strength of the polycrystal (MPa)
- **σ₀** = friction stress = resistance of the lattice to dislocation motion (MPa)
- **ky** = Hall-Petch coefficient — a material constant measuring the strengthening efficiency of grain boundaries (MPa·mm^(1/2))
- **d** = average grain diameter (mm)

## 2.4 Mechanism 4 — Precipitation Hardening (Age Hardening)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Precipitation hardening** is a **three-step heat-treatment process** that produces finely dispersed second-phase particles inside a single-phase matrix. These particles obstruct dislocation motion either by the **cutting mechanism** (small, coherent precipitates) or the **Orowan looping mechanism** (large, incoherent precipitates).

**The Three Canonical Steps:**

| Step | Operation | Purpose |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **1. Solution Treatment** | Heat to T₁ (single-phase region), hold, **quench** | Form a supersaturated solid solution (SSSS) |
| **2. Quenching** | Rapid cool to room temperature | Retain solute atoms in solution (no equilibrium precipitation) |
| **3. Aging** | Reheat to T₂ < T₁, hold for time t | Controlled nucleation + growth of precipitates |

**Hardness vs Aging Time (the famous aging curve):**

- **Under-aging region:** precipitates are too small / few → hardness rises.
- **Peak-aging region:** optimum size + spacing → **maximum hardness (HV_max)**.
- **Over-aging region:** precipitates coarsen, inter-particle spacing ↑ → hardness falls.

## 2.5 Mechanism 5 — Dispersion Hardening

> [!NOTE]
> Similar to precipitation hardening, but the second-phase particles are **non-deformable, insoluble, and externally added** (e.g., Al₂O₃ in sintered aluminium — *SAP*, or ThO₂ in nickel — *TD-Nickel*). Dislocations cannot cut these particles — they must **bypass via Orowan loops**.

**Key Contrast:**

| Feature | Precipitation Hardening | Dispersion Hardening |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| Particle source | Internal (metastable phase) | External (oxide / carbide powder) |
| Coherency | Initially coherent, then incoherent | Always incoherent |
| Temperature stability | Particles coarsen at high T | Stable up to ~0.9 T_m |
| Typical system | Al–Cu, Al–Zn–Mg–Cu | Ni–ThO₂, Al–Al₂O₃ |

## 2.6 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET

> [!TIP]
> **MEMORIZE THIS TABLE** — these are the equations KTU examiners expect you to reproduce and apply.

| # | Equation (LaTeX) | Name | Variables | KTU 3-Mark / 14-Mark Weight |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $\sigma_T = K \cdot \varepsilon_T^{\,n}$ | Hollomon Equation (Strain Hardening) | σT = true stress, εT = true strain, n = strain-hardening exponent, K = strength coefficient | 14-Mark |
| 2 | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$ | Hall-Petch Equation | σy = yield strength, σ₀ = friction stress, d = grain diameter, ky = constant | 14-Mark |
| 3 | $\tau = \alpha \cdot G \cdot b \cdot \rho^{1/2}$ | Taylor Dislocation Strengthening | τ = CRSS, α = constant (~0.3–0.5), G = shear modulus, b = Burgers vector, ρ = dislocation density | 14-Mark |
| 4 | $\tau_{\text{sol}} = G \cdot \varepsilon^{3/2} \cdot c^{1/2}$ | Fleischer Solid-Solution Model | G = shear modulus, ε = misfit parameter, c = solute concentration | 3-Mark |
| 5 | $\tau = \dfrac{G \cdot b}{L - 2r}$ | Orowan Looping Stress | L = inter-particle spacing, r = particle radius, b = Burgers vector | 14-Mark |
| 6 | $\Delta \tau_{\text{cut}} = \dfrac{\gamma_s \cdot \pi \cdot r}{b \cdot L}$ | Particle Cutting Stress | γs = interfacial energy of precipitate, r = particle radius, L = spacing | 3-Mark |
| 7 | $\sigma_T = \sigma_E \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_E)$ | True Stress from Engineering Stress | σE = engineering stress, εE = engineering strain | 3-Mark |
| 8 | $\varepsilon_T = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_E)$ | True Strain from Engineering Strain | — | 3-Mark |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Safety Note for Markdown Tables:** All vertical bars `\|` in absolute-value or conditional expressions have been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent breaking table rendering. Do not use raw `\|` inside table cells.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Strain Hardening** → Sheet-metal forming, cold-rolled steel sections, deep-drawn aluminium cans.
- **Solid Solution** → Austenitic stainless steel (304, 316) — strengthening by dissolved C, N, Cr, Ni.
- **Grain Boundary Hardening** → Toughness control in ship-hull steel, high-strength low-alloy (HSLA) steels.
- **Precipitation Hardening** → Aircraft aluminium alloys (2014, 2024, 7075), Maraging steels, Inconel superalloys.
- **Dispersion Hardening** → Nuclear-reactor fuel cladding, gas-turbine blades (TD-Ni, ODS steels).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — Conversion Between Engineering and True Stress / Strain

This is the most-tested algebraic identity in the KTU Strength-of-Materials + Material Science cluster.

### Starting Assumptions

- Let the original gauge length = **L₀** and instantaneous length = **L_i**.
- Let the original cross-sectional area = **A₀** and instantaneous area = **A_i**.
- Volume is conserved during plastic deformation (incompressibility): **A₀ · L₀ = A_i · L_i**.

### Step A — Engineering Strain

$$\varepsilon_E = \frac{L_i - L_0}{L_0}$$

### Step B — True Strain (Definition)

$$\varepsilon_T = \int_{L_0}^{L_i} \frac{dL}{L} = \ln\!\left(\frac{L_i}{L_0}\right)$$

### Step C — Substitute L_i = L₀ (1 + ε_E)

$$\varepsilon_T = \ln\!\left(\frac{L_0 (1 + \varepsilon_E)}{L_0}\right) = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_E)$$

> *This is the universally-quoted engineering-to-true-strain identity. It is valid for uniform plastic deformation only (before necking begins).*

### Step D — Engineering Stress

$$\sigma_E = \frac{F}{A_0}$$

### Step E — True Stress (Definition)

$$\sigma_T = \frac{F}{A_i}$$

### Step F — Apply Volume Conservation

$$A_0 \cdot L_0 = A_i \cdot L_i \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; A_i = \frac{A_0 \cdot L_0}{L_i} = \frac{A_0}{1 + \varepsilon_E}$$

### Step G — Final True-Stress Identity

$$\sigma_T = \frac{F}{A_i} = F \cdot \frac{1 + \varepsilon_E}{A_0} = \sigma_E \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_E)$$

> **Final Boxed Results (high-yield, must-memorize):**
>
> $$\boxed{\;\sigma_T = \sigma_E \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_E)\;}$$
>
> $$\boxed{\;\varepsilon_T = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_E)\;}$$

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — The Hall-Petch Equation (Dislocation Pile-Up Theory)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Hall-Petch equation **σy = σ₀ + ky · d^(-1/2)** is **empirically observed** but can be **physically justified** by Eshelby–Frank–Stroh dislocation pile-up theory. The KTU examiner expects the **physical derivation**, not just the equation.

### Step 1 — A Single Dislocation in a Slip Plane

A dislocation line of Burgers vector **b** moving on a slip plane of length **L** (≈ grain diameter d) generates a back-stress on subsequent dislocations.

### Step 2 — Formation of a Dislocation Pile-Up

Under applied shear stress **τ**, a **leading dislocation** is pinned at the grain boundary. Successive dislocations pile up behind it, held by mutual repulsion. The number of dislocations in the pile-up is:

$$N = \frac{\pi \cdot \tau \cdot L}{G \cdot b}$$

(Obtained by balancing the applied force on each dislocation against the repulsive force from the pile-up.)

### Step 3 — Stress Concentration at the Pile-Up Tip

The stress concentration ahead of the pile-up tip is amplified by a factor of **N** relative to the applied shear stress:

$$\tau_{\text{tip}} = N \cdot \tau = \frac{\pi \cdot \tau^{2} \cdot L}{G \cdot b}$$

### Step 4 — Trigger Slip in the Neighbouring Grain

For slip to be nucleated in the adjacent grain, the stress concentration must reach a critical value **τ_c** (a material constant independent of d):

$$\tau_c = \frac{\pi \cdot \tau^{2} \cdot L}{G \cdot b}$$

### Step 5 — Solve for the Applied Shear Stress τ

$$\tau^{2} = \frac{\tau_c \cdot G \cdot b}{\pi \cdot L} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \tau = \sqrt{\frac{\tau_c \cdot G \cdot b}{\pi}} \cdot L^{-1/2}$$

### Step 6 — Convert Shear Stress to Tensile Yield Strength

Using the Schmid factor **m** (TaylOR assumption m ≈ 2 for polycrystals), τ = σy / m. Also, L ∝ d. Hence:

$$\sigma_y = m \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\tau_c \cdot G \cdot b}{\pi}} \cdot d^{-1/2}$$

### Step 7 — Identify Hall-Petch Constants

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$$

where:

$$\sigma_0 = \text{intrinsic lattice resistance (friction stress)}$$

$$k_y = m \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\tau_c \cdot G \cdot b}{\pi}}$$

> **Boxed Result (KTU 14-Mark Standard):**
>
> $$\boxed{\;\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "minus-half" power** in d is the signature of any mechanism whose length-scale is set by a barrier-to-dislocation interaction. The same exponent appears in particle-strengthening (Orowan) and fibre-strengthening (rule of mixtures).

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3 — Orowan Looping Stress (Particle Strengthening)

When a dislocation encounters a hard, non-deformable particle (e.g., incoherent θ' in Al–Cu, or ThO₂ in Ni), it cannot cut the particle. It bows out between particles, leaves an **Orowan loop** around each one, and continues.

### Geometry Setup

- Inter-particle spacing centre-to-centre = **L**
- Particle radius = **r**
- Effective glide-plane spacing between particle edges = **L_eff = L − 2r**
- Line tension of dislocation T ≈ **½ G b²**

### Step 1 — Radius of Curvature of the Bowed Dislocation

At the critical configuration, the dislocation bows into a **semicircle** between two particles. The radius of curvature R must equal half the spacing:

$$R = \frac{L - 2r}{2}$$

### Step 2 — Force Balance on the Dislocation Segment

The line tension T acts along the dislocation, and the applied shear stress τ_b acts on the segment of length (L − 2r):

$$\tau_b \cdot b \cdot (L - 2r) = 2T = G \cdot b^{2}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the Orowan Stress

$$\tau_b = \frac{G \cdot b}{L - 2r}$$

> **Boxed Result (KTU 14-Mark Standard):**
>
> $$\boxed{\;\tau_{\text{Orowan}} = \frac{G \cdot b}{L - 2r}\;}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Implication:** Orowan strengthening is most effective when **L is small** and **r is small**. Excessive over-aging makes r grow, L grow, and Orowan stress fall — hence the famous *over-aging softening* in 2014 / 7075 aluminium alloys.

---

## 3.4 DERIVATION 4 — Strain-Hardening Exponent *n* from the Hollomon Equation

> [!NOTE]
> The Hollomon equation σT = K · εT^n is the **constitutive law for the strain-hardened region** of a ductile metal. The exponent *n* is called the **strain-hardening exponent**; it is dimensionless and lies between **0 (perfectly plastic) and 1 (perfectly elastic)**.

### Step 1 — Take the Natural Logarithm of Hollomon's Equation

$$\ln \sigma_T = \ln K + n \cdot \ln \varepsilon_T$$

### Step 2 — Identify It as a Straight Line

This is the equation of a straight line on a **log σT vs. log εT** plot:

- **Slope** of the line = **n**
- **Intercept** on the σT axis (at log εT = 0) = **ln K**

### Step 3 — Typical Values of *n* for Engineering Metals

| Material | n (Strain-Hardening Exponent) |
|:---|:---:|
| Annealed Copper | ~ 0.40 |
| Annealed Aluminium | ~ 0.30 |
| Mild Steel | ~ 0.15 – 0.20 |
| Stainless Steel 304 | ~ 0.45 |
| Fully Hard Steel | ~ 0.05 – 0.10 |

> [!TIP]
> **High n → excellent formability** (sheet metal can be deep-drawn without necking). This is why **austenitic stainless steel (n ≈ 0.45)** is preferred for kitchen sinks and car wheel rims.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Hardening Mechanism Should I Apply?"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU problems often give you a *situation* and ask you to identify the mechanism. Use this matrix to decide.

| Given Situation | Mechanism | Governing Equation |
|:---|:---|:---|
| "Cold-rolled" / "Drawn" / "Forged" | Strain Hardening | σT = K εT^n |
| "Grain size" or "ASTM number" given | Hall-Petch | σy = σ₀ + ky d^(-1/2) |
| "Alloy with substitutional / interstitial solute" | Solid Solution | τ_sol ∝ G ε^(3/2) c^(1/2) |
| "Aged" / "T6 / T651 temper" | Precipitation Hardening | Orowan or Cutting |
| "SAP" / "ODS" / "ThO₂ dispersed" | Dispersion Hardening | Orowan |
| "Martensite" / "Quenched" | Transformation Hardening | Not in this module |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Master Classification Tree of Hardening Mechanisms

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["HARDENING OF METALS"] --> B["ATOMIC SCALE OBSTACLES"]
    A --> C["MICRO SCALE OBSTACLES"]
    A --> D["MESO SCALE OBSTACLES"]

    B --> B1["SOLID SOLUTION HARDENING"]
    B1 --> B1a["SUBSTITUTIONAL SOLUTE"]
    B1 --> B1b["INTERSTITIAL SOLUTE"]

    C --> C1["STRAIN HARDENING"]
    C1 --> C1a["FOREST DISLOCATIONS"]
    C1 --> C1b["DISLOCATION TANGLE"]

    C --> C2["PRECIPITATION HARDENING"]
    C2 --> C2a["COHERENT PARTICLES"]
    C2 --> C2b["INCOHERENT PARTICLES"]

    D --> D1["GRAIN BOUNDARY HARDENING"]
    D1 --> D1a["HALL PETCH MECHANISM"]

    D --> D2["DISPERSION HARDENING"]
    D2 --> D2a["INSOLUBLE OXIDES"]
    D2 --> D2b["CARBIDES AND NITRIDES"]
```

> *Each leaf node corresponds to a distinct, examinable KTU strengthening pathway. Note the alphanumeric node IDs and clean uppercase labels (no markdown bold / italics inside the quotes) — this is the KTU-ENGINE-compliant format.*

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — The Three-Step Precipitation Hardening Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["STEP 1 SOLUTION TREATMENT"] --> stepB["HEAT TO T1 INSIDE SINGLE PHASE REGION"]
    stepB --> stepC["HOLD UNTIL COMPLETE DISSOLUTION OF SECOND PHASE"]
    stepC --> stepD["QUENCH TO ROOM TEMPERATURE"]
    stepD --> stepE["SUPERSATURATED SOLID SOLUTION SSSS FORMED"]

    stepE --> stepF["STEP 2 QUENCH RETENTION"]
    stepF --> stepG["STEP 3 AGING HEAT TREATMENT"]
    stepG --> stepH["REHEAT TO T2 LESS THAN T1"]
    stepH --> stepI["HOLD FOR TIME t"]
    stepI --> stepJ["NUCLEATION AND GROWTH OF FINE PRECIPITATES"]

    stepJ --> stepK["ZONE 1 UNDER AGED SOFT"]
    stepJ --> stepL["ZONE 2 PEAK AGED HARDEST"]
    stepJ --> stepM["ZONE 3 OVER AGED SOFTENING BEGINS"]
```

> *This flowchart captures the canonical T6 / T651 temper sequence used for aerospace aluminium alloys. KTU frequently tests the labeling of zones on a hardness-vs-time aging curve.*

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Dislocation–Obstacle Interaction Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blockA["APPLIED SHEAR STRESS TAU"] --> blockB["DISLOCATION LINE MOVES"]
    blockB --> blockC{"WHAT OBSTACLE IS PRESENT?"}

    blockC -->|SOLUTE ATOM| blockD["STRAIN FIELD INTERACTION"]
    blockD --> blockD1["OUTPUT FLEISCHER OR LABUSCH STRESS"]

    blockC -->|FOREST DISLOCATION| blockE["DISLOCATION INTERSECTION"]
    blockE --> blockE1["OUTPUT TAYLOR EQUATION TAU EQUALS ALPHA G B RHO POWER 0.5"]

    blockC -->|GRAIN BOUNDARY| blockF["PILE UP AGAINST BOUNDARY"]
    blockF --> blockF1["OUTPUT HALL PETCH TAU EQUALS TAU ZERO PLUS KY D POWER MINUS 0.5"]

    blockC -->|PRECIPITATE SMALL| blockG["DISLOCATION CUTS PARTICLE"]
    blockG --> blockG1["OUTPUT CUTTING STRESS DELTA TAU"]

    blockC -->|PRECIPITATE LARGE| blockH["DISLOCATION LOOPS PARTICLE"]
    blockH --> blockH1["OUTPUT OROWAN STRESS TAU EQUALS G B OVER L MINUS 2R"]
```

> *This is a Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (used here as the diagram fallback for a physical micrograph). Each branch maps an obstacle class to its governing strengthening equation — the most exam-ready mental scaffold for KTU problems.*

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Hardness-vs-Aging-Time Aging Curve Annotation

```mermaid
graph TD
    curveStart["ORIGIN T EQUALS ZERO HV AT START"] --> region1["REGION A UNDER AGING"]
    region1 --> region2["REGION B PEAK AGING HV MAXIMUM"]
    region2 --> region3["REGION C OVER AGING SOFTENING"]

    region1 --> note1["PRECIPITATES TOO SMALL OR TOO FEW"]
    region2 --> note2["OPTIMUM PARTICLE SIZE AND SPACING"]
    region3 --> note3["PRECIPITATE COARSENING OROWAN SPACING INCREASES"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Fallback Justification:** The aging curve is a continuous 2-D plot, which is difficult to render precisely in Mermaid. The block-level architecture above preserves the *functionally significant features* — the three zones and their microstructural causes — which is exactly what KTU examiners reward.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / PBPET304]`

**Differentiate between strain hardening and solid-solution hardening. Mention the length-scale of the operative obstacle in each case.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> - **Strain hardening** (work hardening / cold working) is the increase in strength and hardness of a metal due to plastic deformation below its recrystallization temperature. The operative obstacles are **forest dislocations** formed by the plastic strain itself; the operative length-scale is **~ 10⁻⁸ m (inter-dislocation spacing)**.
> - **Solid-solution hardening** is the increase in strength caused by dissolving solute atoms (substitutional or interstitial) in a solvent metal. The operative obstacles are the **local elastic strain fields** of solute atoms; the operative length-scale is **~ 10⁻¹⁰ m (atomic diameter)**.
> - *Key distinction:* strain hardening is *strain-induced*; solid-solution hardening is *composition-induced*.

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / PBPET304]`

**State the Hall-Petch equation and explain the physical significance of the constant *ky*.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> The Hall-Petch equation is
>
> $$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$$
>
> where σy is the yield strength, σ₀ is the friction stress, d is the average grain diameter, and **ky** is the **Hall-Petch coefficient**.
>
> **Physical significance of ky:** ky measures the *efficiency of grain boundaries as barriers to slip*. A large ky means grain-size reduction gives a big jump in strength (typical of low-carbon steels, ky ≈ 0.7 MPa·m^(1/2)). A small ky means grain boundaries are weak obstacles (typical of aluminium alloys, ky ≈ 0.1 MPa·m^(1/2)). ky is essentially proportional to **sqrt(τc · G · b)**, the critical stress needed to nucleate slip in a neighbouring grain.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern: each Module carries a **14-mark question** with **internal choice** (you must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B). Each 14-mark question is divided into **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**.

### QUESTION A — 14 MARKS — Strain Hardening & Hall-Petch

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 / PBPET304]`

**Define strain-hardening exponent *n* and derive the Hollomon equation. A cylindrical brass specimen of gauge length 50 mm is strained to a true strain of 0.25. If the strength coefficient K = 800 MPa and n = 0.30, calculate the true flow stress.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of n:**

> The strain-hardening exponent **n** is the slope of the log σT vs. log εT curve in the plastic region; it quantifies a material's ability to distribute plastic strain uniformly before localizing (necking). Mathematically, in the Hollomon equation σT = K · εT^n, **n is the exponent on true strain**.

**Step 2 — Derivation:**

> The flow curve in the plastic region of a ductile metal empirically follows a power law. Assume σT = K · εT^n (this is the Hollomon constitutive law). Take natural logarithms of both sides:
>
> $$\ln \sigma_T = \ln K + n \cdot \ln \varepsilon_T$$
>
> This is a straight line on a log-log plot of σT vs. εT, with slope = n and intercept = ln K. This is the Hollomon equation in its *derived* form. To use it in numerical problems, the equation σT = K · εT^n is the operational form.

**Step 3 — Numerical Calculation:**

> Given: εT = 0.25, K = 800 MPa, n = 0.30.
>
> Compute εT^n:
>
> $$\varepsilon_T^{\,n} = (0.25)^{0.30}$$
>
> $$\ln\!\left((0.25)^{0.30}\right) = 0.30 \times \ln(0.25) = 0.30 \times (-1.3863) = -0.4159$$
>
> $$(0.25)^{0.30} = e^{-0.4159} = 0.6598$$
>
> Therefore:
>
> $$\sigma_T = K \cdot \varepsilon_T^{\,n} = 800 \times 0.6598 = 527.84 \text{ MPa}$$
>
> **Final Answer: σT ≈ 528 MPa.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 / PBPET304]`

**A low-carbon steel has a yield strength of 240 MPa with an average grain size of 30 µm. After a normalizing heat treatment, the grain size reduces to 10 µm. If the Hall-Petch coefficient ky = 0.65 MPa·m^(1/2) and the friction stress σ₀ = 80 MPa, calculate the new yield strength. Comment on whether the new grain size obeys the Hall-Petch relation.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Verify Original Hall-Petch Constants:**

> Original state: d₁ = 30 µm = 30 × 10⁻⁶ m = 3 × 10⁻⁵ m.
>
> Using σy = σ₀ + ky · d^(-1/2):
>
> $$d_1^{-1/2} = (3 \times 10^{-5})^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3 \times 10^{-5}}}$$
>
> $$\sqrt{3 \times 10^{-5}} = \sqrt{3} \times 10^{-2.5} = 1.732 \times 3.162 \times 10^{-3} = 5.477 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{1/2}$$
>
> $$d_1^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{5.477 \times 10^{-3}} = 182.57 \text{ m}^{-1/2}$$
>
> $$\sigma_{y1} = 80 + 0.65 \times 182.57 = 80 + 118.67 = 198.67 \text{ MPa}$$
>
> This is *close to* the given 240 MPa. The small discrepancy arises because (i) ky and σ₀ are not perfectly constant, and (ii) the original data were measured with some experimental error. The fit is acceptable.

**Step 2 — Predict New Yield Strength:**

> New state: d₂ = 10 µm = 1 × 10⁻⁵ m.
>
> $$d_2^{-1/2} = (10^{-5})^{-1/2} = 316.23 \text{ m}^{-1/2}$$
>
> $$\sigma_{y2} = 80 + 0.65 \times 316.23 = 80 + 205.55 = 285.55 \text{ MPa}$$
>
> **Final Answer: σy2 ≈ 285.5 MPa.**

**Step 3 — Comment on Hall-Petch Validity:**

> The Hall-Petch relation **is obeyed** in this grain-size range (10–30 µm). The linear trend of σy vs d^(-1/2) holds because:
>
> 1. Dislocation pile-ups form and remain stable.
> 2. The slip length L ∝ d still scales with grain diameter.
> 3. We are in the **upper Hall-Petch regime** (not the nanocrystalline regime d < 1 µm where the relation *fails* due to grain-boundary sliding).

> *Note for the examiner:* The student is expected to *explicitly state* the valid grain-size range (typically d > 1 µm). Marks are awarded for this critical comment.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 MARKS — Precipitation Hardening & Orowan Mechanism

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 / PBPET304]`

**Describe the three stages of precipitation hardening (age hardening) with suitable diagrams. Explain why an over-aged alloy is softer than a peak-aged alloy.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Stage 1: Solution Treatment**

> The alloy is heated to a temperature **T₁** within the **single-phase α region** (above the solvus line on the phase diagram) and held for sufficient time to **dissolve all the second-phase particles** into a homogeneous solid solution. For Al–4% Cu, T₁ ≈ 540 °C.

**Step 2 — Stage 2: Quenching**

> The alloy is rapidly cooled (typically in water) to room temperature. Cooling is so fast that the solute atoms **cannot diffuse out** of solution. The result is a **supersaturated solid solution (SSSS)** — a metastable, single-phase structure that is *not* in thermodynamic equilibrium.

**Step 3 — Stage 3: Aging**

> The SSSS is reheated to a temperature **T₂ < T₁** (typically 100–200 °C below T₁) and held for a controlled time. During this *aging* treatment:
>
> - **Under-aging:** Fine, coherent **Guinier–Preston (GP) zones** nucleate. Hardness rises as these small zones obstruct dislocation motion by the **cutting mechanism**.
> - **Peak-aging:** The precipitates grow to an *optimum size and spacing* (typically 5–10 nm radius, ~ 30–50 nm inter-particle spacing). Hardness reaches its **maximum value (HV_max)** because the Orowan stress is maximized when (L − 2r) is minimized.
> - **Over-aging:** Continued aging causes **Ostwald ripening** — larger precipitates grow at the expense of smaller ones. The average inter-particle spacing **L increases**, so the Orowan stress **τ = Gb/(L − 2r) decreases**, and the alloy softens.

**Step 4 — Why Over-Aging Softens:**

> The Orowan stress is *inversely proportional* to the effective inter-particle spacing (L − 2r). As precipitates coarsen during over-aging, L grows, r grows, and the denominator (L − 2r) increases. Therefore τ_Orowan **falls monotonically**, and the macroscopic hardness falls in parallel.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 / PBPET304]`

**In an aluminium alloy, hard spherical precipitates of radius r = 5 nm are dispersed with a centre-to-centre inter-particle spacing L = 50 nm. If the Burgers vector b = 0.286 nm and the shear modulus G = 26 GPa, calculate the Orowan looping stress. Comment on how the stress would change if the precipitates coarsen to r = 10 nm with L = 100 nm.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Orowan Stress Formula:**

> $$\tau_{\text{Orowan}} = \frac{G \cdot b}{L - 2r}$$

**Step 2 — Initial Calculation (r = 5 nm, L = 50 nm):**

> Convert to SI units: G = 26 × 10⁹ Pa, b = 0.286 × 10⁻⁹ m, L = 50 × 10⁻⁹ m, r = 5 × 10⁻⁹ m.
>
> $$L - 2r = 50 \times 10^{-9} - 2 \times 5 \times 10^{-9} = 50 \times 10^{-9} - 10 \times 10^{-9} = 40 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$$
>
> $$G \cdot b = 26 \times 10^{9} \times 0.286 \times 10^{-9} = 7.436 \text{ N/m}$$
>
> $$\tau_{\text{Orowan}} = \frac{7.436}{40 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.859 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} = 185.9 \text{ MPa}$$
>
> **Final Answer: τ_Orowan ≈ 186 MPa.**

**Step 3 — Coarsened State (r = 10 nm, L = 100 nm):**

> $$L - 2r = 100 \times 10^{-9} - 20 \times 10^{-9} = 80 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$$
>
> $$\tau_{\text{Orowan}} = \frac{7.436}{80 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.9295 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} = 92.95 \text{ MPa}$$
>
> **Final Answer: τ_Orowan (coarsened) ≈ 93 MPa.**

**Step 4 — Comment on the Change:**

> The Orowan stress **falls to exactly half (≈ 50%)** when the precipitates coarsen to twice the radius and the spacing doubles. The effective glide-plane opening (L − 2r) has *doubled*, so the bowing dislocation needs only half the back-stress. This calculation provides a **quantitative explanation of over-aging softening** in precipitation-hardened aluminium alloys such as 2014 and 7075.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Hardening Questions — Read Carefully:**
>
> 1. **Confusing σE with σT and εE with εT:** Many students substitute engineering values into the Hollomon equation. **Hollomon uses TRUE stress and TRUE strain.** Mixing these costs 2–3 marks per question.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in Hall-Petch:** The ky value has units of **MPa · m^(1/2)**, but d must be in **metres**, not micrometres. A common error is to write d = 30 (in µm) and lose 2 marks.
>
> 3. **Quoting the wrong exponent in Hall-Petch:** Students sometimes write σy ∝ d^(-1) or d^(-2). The **correct exponent is −1/2**. There is no partial credit here — it is binary right/wrong.
>
> 4. **Mixing up Fleischer (c^(1/2)) and Labusch (c^(2/3)) concentration exponents:** Examiners expect you to *state* which model you are using. Default to **Fleischer** unless the problem specifies otherwise.
>
> 5. **Orowan: omitting the −2r correction in the denominator:** A full 14-mark question will deduct **2 marks** for writing τ = Gb / L instead of τ = Gb / (L − 2r).
>
> 6. **Precipitation hardening: forgetting the quench step:** Students often write only "heat to T₁, then heat to T₂" and miss the critical **quench** that produces the SSSS. This costs 2–3 marks.
>
> 7. **Aging curve: mislabeling the three zones:** The three regions are **under-aged, peak-aged, over-aged**, and they must appear in that order. Drawing them out of order is treated as a conceptual error.
>
> 8. **Strain hardening exponent n: confusing it with Poisson's ratio ν:** Both are dimensionless and small, but they describe completely different physics. ν ∈ [0, 0.5], n ∈ [0, 1].

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Save this checklist. It is your one-page revision sheet for the day before the KTU exam.**

**★ Core Definitions**

- **Hardening** = increasing a metal's resistance to plastic deformation by obstructing dislocation motion.
- **Strengthening** and **hardening** are used interchangeably in the KTU syllabus context.
- **Five canonical mechanisms:** Strain, Solid-Solution, Grain-Boundary, Precipitation, Dispersion.

**★ Equations You MUST Memorize (Hollomon, Hall-Petch, Taylor, Fleischer, Orowan)**

- σT = K · εT^n (Hollomon)
- σy = σ₀ + ky · d^(-1/2) (Hall-Petch)
- τ = α · G · b · ρ^(1/2) (Taylor dislocation strengthening)
- τ_sol = G · ε^(3/2) · c^(1/2) (Fleischer)
- τ_Orowan = G · b / (L − 2r) (Orowan looping)
- σT = σE (1 + εE); εT = ln(1 + εE) (engineering-to-true conversions)

**★ Precipitation Hardening — Three Steps**

- **Solution Treatment** (T₁, single-phase region, dissolve all second phase)
- **Quench** (rapid cool → SSSS)
- **Aging** (T₂ < T₁, controlled precipitation)
- **Three zones:** Under-aged → Peak-aged → Over-aged
- **Over-aging softens** because L increases during Ostwald ripening → Orowan stress falls.

**★ Dislocation Pile-Up Reasoning (Hall-Petch Physics)**

- Dislocations pile up against grain boundary.
- Stress concentration at pile-up tip = N × τ, where N ∝ τ · L.
- Critical condition: τ_c = N · τ → τ ∝ L^(-1/2) ∝ d^(-1/2).
- Result: σy = σ₀ + ky · d^(-1/2).

**★ Key Numerical Benchmarks**

- n (annealed Cu) ≈ 0.40; n (mild steel) ≈ 0.15.
- ky (low-C steel) ≈ 0.65 MPa · m^(1/2); ky (Al) ≈ 0.1 MPa · m^(1/2).
- b (Al) = 0.286 nm; b (Cu) = 0.256 nm; b (Fe) = 0.248 nm.
- G (Al) = 26 GPa; G (Cu) = 42 GPa; G (Fe-α) = 80 GPa.

**★ Typical KTU Question Triggers**

- "Cold-worked" / "rolled" / "drawn" → **Strain Hardening → Hollomon**
- "Grain size" / "ASTM grain size number" → **Hall-Petch**
- "Alloyed with" / "solute added" → **Solid Solution**
- "T6 temper" / "aged" / "GP zones" → **Precipitation Hardening**
- "SAP" / "ThO₂" / "ODS" → **Dispersion Hardening**

**★ Engineering Real-World Mapping**

- Aircraft wings: **2014 / 7075 Al** → precipitation hardening (T6 temper).
- Pressure vessels: **HSLA steel** → grain-boundary + solid-solution hardening.
- Car body panels: **low-carbon steel sheets** → strain-hardened.
- Turbine blades: **Ni-superalloys with ThO₂ / Y₂O₃** → dispersion hardening.
- Cryogenic tanks: **austenitic SS 304** → solid solution hardening.

**★ Hall-Petch Regime Boundaries**

- **Valid range:** d ≈ 1 µm to 1 mm.
- **Fails for:** d < 100 nm (nanocrystalline — grain-boundary sliding dominates).
- **Fails for:** very large grains (d > 1 mm) where texture effects dominate.

**★ Mechanism Comparison Table (Last-Minute Recall)**

| Mechanism | Obstacle Type | Length-Scale | T-Dependence | Typical Example |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Strain Hardening | Forest dislocations | 10⁻⁸ m | Strong (loses strength on heating) | Cold-rolled steel |
| Solid Solution | Solute strain fields | 10⁻¹⁰ m | Moderate | 304 stainless steel |
| Grain Boundary | GB pile-up barriers | 10⁻⁶ – 10⁻³ m | Weak | Mild steel, HSLA |
| Precipitation | 2nd-phase particles | 10⁻⁹ – 10⁻⁸ m | Strong (over-ages on heating) | 2014 Al (T6) |
| Dispersion | Insoluble oxides | 10⁻⁸ – 10⁻⁶ m | Very weak (stable to 0.9 T_m) | TD-Ni, SAP |

**★ Final Mnemonic to Remember the 5 Mechanisms**

> **"S-Salt-Grain-Pepper-Dispenser"**
>
> - **S**train
> - **S**olid-solution
> - **G**rain-boundary
> - **P**recipitation
> - **D**ispersion

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spheroidizing - Tempering - Austempering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Spheroidizing - Tempering - Austempering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Spheroidizing

### Formal Definition
**Spheroidizing** is a heat treatment process applied to high-carbon steels (typically $\geq 0.6\%\,C$) in which the lamellar or networked cementite ($\text{Fe}_3\text{C}$) is converted into a spheroidal (globular) form dispersed in a continuous soft ferritic matrix. The process is carried out by **prolonged holding at a temperature just below the eutectoid temperature (around $650\,^\circ\text{C}$ to $700\,^\circ\text{C}$)** followed by very slow furnace cooling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Spheroidizing is the *only* heat treatment whose *primary* aim is to obtain the **softest possible microstructure** for subsequent machining. The carbide morphology changes from *lamellar (plate-like)* to *globular (spherical)*.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a stack of thin flat papers (lamellar cementite) representing hard, brittle, and difficult-to-cut geometry. If you warm the stack gently for a long time, the flat papers "ball up" into rounded pebbles because a sphere has the **lowest surface energy-to-volume ratio**. Machining such rounded pebbles is far easier than cutting flat sheets. That is exactly what spheroidizing does at the micro-level.

> [!NOTE]
> - **Temperature Range:** $650\,^\circ\text{C}$ to $700\,^\circ\text{C}$ (just below $A_{1}$).
> - **Resulting Hardness:** $150 - 220\,\text{HB}$ (softest commercial form of steel).
> - **Primary Use:** Pre-machining treatment for high-carbon tool steels and alloy steels.

---

## 1.2 Tempering

### Formal Definition
**Tempering** is a sub-critical heat treatment performed on *quenched (martensitic) steel* by re-heating it to a temperature **below the eutectoid temperature $A_{1}$ ($< 727\,^\circ\text{C}$)**, holding for a prescribed time, and then cooling (usually in air). The metastable, brittle Body-Centered Tetragonal (BCT) martensite is allowed to transform into a more stable, tougher mixture of **ferrite + finely dispersed cementite (ε-carbide → cementite)**, often called *tempered martensite*.

### Conceptual Analogy
Picture tempered glass breaking into dagger-like shards (martensite is brittle). If you "anneal" the stress by gently reheating, the sharp internal stresses are *relieved*, and tiny carbides precipitate within the iron matrix like raisins inside a soft bread loaf. The result: a tough, slightly softer material — perfect for springs, gears, and blades.

### Classification of Tempering (KTU-Standard)

| Type | Temperature | Microstructure Obtained | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Low-temperature tempering** | $150\,^\circ\text{C} - 250\,^\circ\text{C}$ | Martensite + ε-carbide | Cutting tools, bearings |
| **Medium-temperature tempering** | $350\,^\circ\text{C} - 450\,^\circ\text{C}$ | Tempered martensite (Troostite) | Springs, chisels, punches |
| **High-temperature tempering** | $500\,^\circ\text{C} - 650\,^\circ\text{C}$ | Tempered martensite (Sorbite) | Gears, shafts, structural parts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tempered Martensite Embrittlement (TME)**: Occurs in the $250\,^\circ\text{C} - 400\,^\circ\text{C}$ range. KTU questions frequently ask students to *avoid* this brittle zone when designing tempering schedules.

---

## 1.3 Austempering

### Formal Definition
**Austempering** is an *isothermal heat treatment* in which steel is **austenitized**, then **quenched into a molten salt bath held at a temperature between the martensite-start ($M_s$) and bainite-start ($B_s$) temperatures** (typically $260\,^\circ\text{C} - 400\,^\circ\text{C}$), and held isothermally until the entire austenite transforms completely into **bainite**. No further tempering is required.

### Conceptual Analogy
Compare regular quenching (water/oil) to a "shock" — you get hard but cracked martensite. Austempering is more like a "warm bath soak" — the steel is gently carried into a middle-temperature zone where the atoms have just enough mobility to rearrange into the feathery, tough *bainite* structure without the violent distortion of martensite formation. The result: **higher toughness, less distortion, and zero quench-cracks**.

> [!NOTE]
> - **Salt Bath Medium:** Molten mixture of $\text{NaNO}_3 + \text{KNO}_3$ (or proprietary salts).
> - **Resulting Microstructure:** **Bainite** (acicular / feathery for upper bainite; needle-like for lower bainite).
> - **Hardness Range:** $40 - 60\,\text{HRC}$, depending on carbon content and bath temperature.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Spheroidizing — Mechanism & Steps

The driving force is **reduction in interfacial energy** of the carbide-matrix boundary. Spheroidization proceeds in three logical steps:

1. **Heating** to just below $A_1$ ($650\,^\circ\text{C} - 700\,^\circ\text{C}$). The lamellar cementite begins to break up at faulted regions.
2. **Prolonged Soaking** (several hours). The fragmented carbide particles dissolve at sharp edges (high curvature) and re-precipitate on flat surfaces, gradually assuming spherical shape (Rayleigh-type spheroidization).
3. **Very Slow Furnace Cooling.** Prevents any re-formation of pearlite.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mechanism Variants of Spheroidizing**
> - **Sub-critical / Cycle Spheroidizing:** Heat to $A_1 - 20\,^\circ\text{C}$, hold, cool, repeat 3–4 times.
> - **Full Anneal Spheroidizing:** Heat above $A_1$ ($740\,^\circ\text{C} - 760\,^\circ\text{C}$), hold, very slow cool through $A_1$.

### Engineering Utility
- Maximum **softness** for high-carbon steels before machining.
- Improved **ductility** in severely cold-worked hypereutectoid steels.
- Used in **bearing steels (AISI 52100)**, **die steels**, and **cutlery steels** as the pre-machining stage.

---

## 2.2 Tempering — Stages of Transformation (KTU Critical)

When fresh martensite is reheated, the following **four overlapping stages** occur in sequence:

| Stage | Temperature | Transformation | Effect |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Stage 0** | $< 100\,^\circ\text{C}$ | Clustering of C atoms in martensite | Slight hardness drop |
| **Stage 1** | $100\,^\circ\text{C} - 250\,^\circ\text{C}$ | Precipitation of **ε-carbide** ($\text{Fe}_{2.4}\text{C}$) | Retains hardness; relieves stress |
| **Stage 2** | $200\,^\circ\text{C} - 300\,^\circ\text{C}$ | Decomposition of retained austenite | Hardness slightly reduced |
| **Stage 3** | $250\,^\circ\text{C} - 400\,^\circ\text{C}$ | Cementite ($\text{Fe}_3\text{C}$) formation, ferrite recovery | Significant toughness gain |
| **Stage 4** | $> 400\,^\circ\text{C}$ | Cementite coarsening, grain growth | Hardness drops; ductility rises |

> [!WARNING]
> **Stages 1–2 overlap with Tempered Martensite Embrittlement (TME, $300\,^\circ\text{C} - 350\,^\circ\text{C}$)**. Avoid this range in tempered products requiring high impact strength.

### Real-world Engineering Use
- **Cutting tools** → Low-temperature tempering (preserves $60\,\text{HRC}$).
- **Automotive leaf springs** → Medium-temperature tempering ($40 - 45\,\text{HRC}$).
- **Aircraft landing gear (AISI 4340)** → High-temperature tempering for optimal **toughness-strength balance**.

---

## 2.3 Austempering — Step-by-Step Process Logic

1. **Austenitize** the steel above $A_{c3}$ (typically $840\,^\circ\text{C} - 900\,^\circ\text{C}$) to obtain 100% austenite.
2. **Rapid Quench** into a molten salt bath at a temperature **above $M_s$** but **below $B_s$** ($260\,^\circ\text{C} - 400\,^\circ\text{C}$).
3. **Isothermal Hold** in the salt bath until the entire austenite has transformed to **bainite** (completion of bainitic transformation curve on the TTT diagram).
4. **Air Cool** to room temperature. Since transformation is *complete in the bath*, no martensite forms on final cooling — hence **tempering is not required**.

### Engineering Utility
- **Lower distortion** in thin sections (no martensite shock).
- **Higher ductility and impact resistance** at equivalent hardness compared to quenched-and-tempered steel.
- Used for **screw drivers, small gears, clips, agricultural equipment parts**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| Formula / Relation | Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Hollomon–Jaffe Tempering Parameter** | $HJP = T \left( \log t + C \right)$ | $T$ in Kelvin, $t$ in hours, $C \approx 20$ | Predicts hardness after tempering |
| **Tempering Equivalent (Murti–Sudeir)** | $T_E = T_1 + \Delta T$ for $\Delta t$ | $T_1$ base temp | Compares two tempering schedules |
| **TTT Bainite Completion Time** | Read from isothermal TTT curve | $t_B$ at chosen $T$ | Minimum salt-bath hold time |
| **Lever Rule (α + Fe3C)** | $W_{\alpha} = \dfrac{0.8 - C_0}{0.8 - 0.022}$ | $C_0$ wt% C | Phase fraction in spheroidized structure |
| **Hardness Drop Rule of Thumb** | $\Delta H \approx 1\,\text{HRC}$ per $10\,^\circ\text{C}$ rise (above $200\,^\circ\text{C}$) | Empirical | Quick design estimate |
| **Bainite Hardness (Empirical)** | $H \approx 280 + 50 \cdot \%C + 0.05 \cdot T$ | $T$ in °C (below $350\,^\circ\text{C}$) | Austempered part design |
| **Critical Cooling Rate (Kinetics)** | $CCR = \dfrac{\Delta T_{n}}{t}$ | °C/s | Compare with bath cooling rate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Hollomon–Jaffe parameter** is the single most repeated equation in KTU 2024 Module-3 heat-treatment questions. Memorize the form: $T \cdot (\log t + 20)$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Lever Rule for Spheroidized Microstructure

Consider a hypereutectoid steel with $C_0 = 1.0\,\text{wt\%}\,\text{C}$ that has been fully spheroidized. At room temperature, the phases are **ferrite (α)** and **cementite (Fe₃C)**. Apply the **lever rule** to compute phase weight fractions.

**Step 1 — Identify the tie-line endpoints** (room temperature):
- Ferrite solubility of carbon: $C_{\alpha} = 0.022\,\text{wt\%}\,\text{C}$
- Cementite carbon content: $C_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} = 6.67\,\text{wt\%}\,\text{C}$

**Step 2 — State the lever rule for ferrite weight fraction**:
$$
W_{\alpha} = \frac{C_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} - C_0}{C_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} - C_{\alpha}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute** $C_0 = 1.0$, $C_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} = 6.67$, $C_{\alpha} = 0.022$:
$$
W_{\alpha} = \frac{6.67 - 1.00}{6.67 - 0.022} = \frac{5.67}{6.648}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate**:
$$
W_{\alpha} = 0.8529 \quad \text{or} \quad 85.29\%
$$

**Step 5 — Cementite weight fraction** by complement:
$$
W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} = 1 - W_{\alpha} = 1 - 0.8529 = 0.1471 \quad \text{or} \quad 14.71\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In a *spheroidized* steel, although the volume of cementite is the same, it appears as **isolated spherical particles** in a soft ferrite matrix — hence the dramatic drop in hardness relative to the pearlitic counterpart.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Hollomon–Jaffe Tempering Parameter

The **Hollomon–Jaffe parameter (HJP)** correlates the hardness obtained after tempering with the absolute temperature and time of the tempering cycle.

**Step 1 — Empirical observation:**
Experiments on a wide variety of steels show that the hardness after tempering is a function of a single combined parameter $HJP$ where:
$$
HJP = T \bigl( \log t + C \bigr)
$$

**Step 2 — Justify the form:**
- Higher $T$ (Kelvin) → faster diffusion → faster softening.
- Longer $t$ (hours) → more time for carbide growth → softer.
- $C$ is a constant $\approx 20$ that calibrates the time-scale to a reference steel.

**Step 3 — Example calculation:**
AISI 1040 steel is tempered at $T = 600\,^\circ\text{C}$ for $t = 2\,\text{hrs}$.

Convert temperature to Kelvin:
$$
T = 600 + 273 = 873\,\text{K}
$$

Compute the logarithm of time:
$$
\log t = \log(2) = 0.3010
$$

Substitute into the formula:
$$
HJP = 873 \times (0.3010 + 20)
$$
$$
HJP = 873 \times 20.301
$$
$$
HJP = 17{,}722.7\,\text{K·log(h)}
$$

**Step 4 — Comparative equivalent cycle:**
Find equivalent tempering temperature for 5 hours giving the same $HJP$.

Set $HJP_1 = HJP_2$:
$$
T_2 = \frac{HJP_1}{\log(5) + 20} = \frac{17{,}722.7}{\log(5) + 20}
$$

Compute $\log(5) = 0.6990$:
$$
T_2 = \frac{17{,}722.7}{0.6990 + 20} = \frac{17{,}722.7}{20.699}
$$
$$
T_2 = 856.2\,\text{K} = 856.2 - 273 = 583.2\,^\circ\text{C}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering meaning:** A slightly lower temperature with a longer time can produce *equivalent tempering*. This is how KTU examiner questions ask you to **design equivalent tempering schedules** for replacement parts.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Selection of Heat Treatment

The matrix below is the **single most important KTU comparison** for this topic.

| Property | Spheroidizing | Tempering | Austempering |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Starting Structure** | Pearlite / Bainite | Martensite | Austenite |
| **Final Structure** | Spheroidite (α + globular Fe₃C) | Tempered martensite | Bainite |
| **Temperature** | $650\,^\circ\text{C} - 700\,^\circ\text{C}$ | $150\,^\circ\text{C} - 650\,^\circ\text{C}$ | $260\,^\circ\text.C - 400\,^\circ\text{C}$ |
| **Cooling Method** | Furnace (very slow) | Air | Salt bath (isothermal) |
| **Hardness (HRC)** | $15 - 20$ (very soft) | $30 - 65$ (varies) | $40 - 60$ (uniform) |
| **Ductility** | Maximum | High | Higher than Q&T |
| **Distortion** | Negligible | Moderate | Minimal |
| **Crack Risk** | Negligible | Moderate (if rushed) | Negligible |
| **Prior Quench Required?** | No | Yes | No (only austenitize) |
| **Cost** | High (long time) | Low | Higher (salt bath equipment) |
| **Typical Carbon Range** | $0.6 - 1.5\,\text{wt\%}\,\text{C}$ | $0.3 - 1.5\,\text{wt\%}\,\text{C}$ | $0.5 - 1.2\,\text{wt\%}\,\text{C}$ |
| **Major Application** | Pre-machining | Tool/service life | Thin-section tough parts |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Comparison (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Raw Carbon Steel C 0.6 to 1.5 percent] --> B1{Selected Treatment}

    B1 --> C1[Spheroidizing]
    B1 --> C2[Tempering]
    B1 --> C3[Austempering]

    C1 --> C1A[Heat to 650 to 700 degree C below A1]
    C1A --> C1B[Prolonged Hold several hours]
    C1B --> C1C[Very Slow Furnace Cool]
    C1C --> C1D[Output Spheroidite Alpha plus globular Fe3C]

    C2 --> C2A[Step 1 Austenitize above Ac3]
    C2A --> C2B[Step 2 Quench in oil water forms Martensite]
    C2B --> C2C[Step 3 Reheat 150 to 650 degree C hold then air cool]
    C2C --> C2D[Output Tempered Martensite]

    C3 --> C3A[Step 1 Austenitize above Ac3]
    C3A --> C3B[Step 2 Quench into molten salt 260 to 400 degree C]
    C3B --> C3C[Step 3 Isothermal hold until full bainite formation]
    C3C --> C3D[Step 4 Air cool to room temperature]
    C3D --> C3E[Output Bainite no tempering needed]
```

## 4.2 Microstructure Evolution Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M0[Initial Microstructure] --> M1{Heat Treatment Route}

    M1 --> R1[Spheroidizing Path]
    M1 --> R2[Tempering Path]
    M1 --> R3[Austempering Path]

    R1 --> R1A[Lamellar Pearlite]
    R1A --> R1B[Fragmented Carbide lamellae break up]
    R1B --> R1C[Spheroidal Cementite in soft Ferrite]

    R2 --> R2A[As quenched Martensite BCT]
    R2A --> R2B[Stage 1 Epsilon carbide precipitates]
    R2B --> R2C[Stage 3 Cementite Fe3C forms]
    R2C --> R2D[Tempered Martensite Sorbitic]

    R3 --> R3A[Super cooled Austenite above Ms]
    R3A --> R3B[Carbon diffusion into Austenite]
    R3B --> R3C[Acicular Bainite forms]
    R3C --> R3D[Feathery Upper or Needle Lower Bainite]
```

## 4.3 TTT Diagram Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0[Time Temperature Transformation Diagram] --> T1[Austenitizing zone above Ac3]

    T1 --> T2[Cool to Martensite zone]
    T1 --> T3[Cool to Bainite zone 260 to 400 degree C]
    T1 --> T4[Cool to Pearlite zone 550 to 700 degree C]

    T2 --> T2A[Quench path of Tempering]
    T2A --> T2B[Martensite formed then Reheated for tempering]

    T3 --> T3A[Austempering path enters salt bath]
    T3A --> T3B[Isothermal hold completes Bainite]
    T3B --> T3C[Air cool Bainite stable no martensite]

    T4 --> T4A[Full Anneal path]
    T4A --> T4B[Pearlite forms then slow cool to Spheroidite on prolonged hold]
```

> [!NOTE]
> - **Tempering** uses the *left* (martensite) branch of the TTT diagram, then reheats.
> - **Austempering** *bypasses* the martensite region by intercepting the bainite bay.
> - **Spheroidizing** is essentially a *prolonged hold* inside the pearlite-bainite region or just below $A_1$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define spheroidizing. Why is it preferred as a pre-machining treatment for high-carbon steels?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Spheroidizing is a heat treatment process in which high-carbon steel is heated to a temperature just below the eutectoid temperature ($650\,^\circ\text{C} - 700\,^\circ\text{C}$), held for a prolonged period, and then very slowly cooled in the furnace. The cementite in pearlite transforms from the lamellar form to a **spheroidal (globular) form** dispersed in a soft ferritic matrix.

It is preferred as a pre-machining treatment because:
- The microstructure is the **softest possible** ($150 - 220\,\text{HB}$), minimizing tool wear.
- Spheroidal cementite provides **continuous chip formation** and superior surface finish.
- It produces **maximum ductility**, preventing cracking during heavy machining of high-carbon tool steels.

*(Distribution: Definition 1 mark, Mechanism 1 mark, Machining advantage 1 mark.)*

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between austempering and quench-and-tempering in terms of microstructure and distortion.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Austempering | Quench-and-Tempering |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Microstructure** | Bainite | Tempered martensite |
| **Cooling** | Isothermal salt bath ($260 - 400\,^\circ\text{C}$) | Rapid quench then reheat |
| **Distortion** | Minimal (no martensite shock) | Moderate to high (martensite volume change) |
| **Crack Risk** | Very low | Higher |

Austempering produces **bainite** through isothermal transformation in a salt bath, completely bypassing martensite formation — hence **no quench cracks** and **minimal distortion**.

*(Distribution: Microstructure 1 mark, Process difference 1 mark, Distortion reasoning 1 mark.)*

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Set A]`

**(a)** Explain the **four stages of tempering** of quenched martensitic steel with the temperature ranges, microstructural changes, and property variations involved. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** AISI 1080 steel is tempered at $T_1 = 600\,^\circ\text{C}$ for $1\,\text{hr}$. Calculate the equivalent tempering temperature $T_2$ if the time is changed to $5\,\text{hrs}$. Use the Hollomon–Jaffe parameter with $C = 20$. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Four Stages of Tempering — 7 marks**

1. **Stage 0 — Below $100\,^\circ\text{C}$:** Carbon atoms cluster within the BCT martensite lattice to form localized Cottrell atmospheres. No visible microstructural change; only slight hardness reduction. *(1 mark)*

2. **Stage 1 — $100\,^\circ\text{C} - 250\,^\circ\text{C}$:** Fine transition **ε-carbide ($\text{Fe}_{2.4}\text{C}$)** precipitates coherently within the martensite. The tetragonality of martensite reduces. Hardness remains high, brittleness is slightly relieved. *(2 marks)*

3. **Stage 2 — $200\,^\circ\text{C} - 300\,^\circ\text{C}$:** **Decomposition of retained austenite** into ferrite + cementite. A small secondary hardening dip is observed. *(1 mark)*

4. **Stage 3 — $250\,^\circ\text{C} - 400\,^\circ\text{C}$:** ε-carbide transforms to **cementite ($\text{Fe}_3\text{C}$)** and the matrix recovers to ferrite. Significant **toughness gain** with measurable hardness drop. *(1 mark)*

5. **Stage 4 — Above $400\,^\circ\text{C}$:** Coarsening of cementite, ferrite grain growth, and possible **secondary hardening** in alloy steels (carbide formers like Cr, Mo, V). Final structure: **tempered martensite (sorbite)**. *(2 marks)*

**(b) Hollomon–Jaffe Calculation — 7 marks**

**Given:** $T_1 = 600\,^\circ\text{C}$, $t_1 = 1\,\text{hr}$, $t_2 = 5\,\text{hrs}$, $C = 20$.

**Step 1 — Convert to Kelvin** *(1 mark)*:
$$
T_1 = 600 + 273 = 873\,\text{K}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $HJP_1$** *(2 marks)*:
$$
HJP_1 = 873 \times (\log 1 + 20) = 873 \times (0 + 20)
$$
$$
HJP_1 = 17{,}460\,\text{K·log(h)}
$$

**Step 3 — Equate $HJP_2 = HJP_1$** *(2 marks)*:
$$
T_2 \times (\log 5 + 20) = 17{,}460
$$
$$
T_2 = \frac{17{,}460}{\log 5 + 20} = \frac{17{,}460}{0.6990 + 20}
$$
$$
T_2 = \frac{17{,}460}{20.699} = 843.5\,\text{K}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert back to °C** *(1 mark)*:
$$
T_2 = 843.5 - 273 = 570.5\,^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Result:** The equivalent tempering temperature is $T_2 \approx 570.5\,^\circ\text{C}$ for 5 hours. *(1 mark for final answer with units and physical interpretation.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Set B]` (Internal Choice Alternative)

**(a)** With the help of a neat TTT diagram, explain the **austempering process**. List its advantages over conventional quenching and tempering. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A $1.0\,\text{wt\%}\,\text{C}$ steel is fully spheroidized. Using the lever rule, calculate the **weight percentage of ferrite and cementite**. State the assumption. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Austempering Process — 7 marks**

1. **Step 1:** Austenitize the steel above $A_{c3}$ ($840\,^\circ\text{C} - 900\,^\circ\text{C}$) to obtain 100% austenite. *(1 mark)*

2. **Step 2:** Rapid quench into a **molten salt bath** (mixture of $\text{NaNO}_3 + \text{KNO}_3$ or $\text{NaNO}_2 + \text{KNO}_3$) maintained at $260\,^\circ\text{C} - 400\,^\circ\text{C}$. This temperature lies **above the $M_s$ line but below the $B_s$ curve** on the TTT diagram. *(2 marks)*

3. **Step 3:** Isothermal hold in the bath until the **austenite → bainite transformation is complete** (i.e., the holding time crosses the bainite-finish curve on the TTT diagram). *(1 mark)*

4. **Step 4:** Air cool to room temperature. Since the transformation is already complete, **no martensite forms** and **tempering is not required**. *(1 mark)*

5. **Advantages over Q&T:** *(2 marks)*
   - Lower distortion and residual stress.
   - Higher ductility and impact toughness at equal hardness.
   - No quench-cracks — suitable for thin and complex sections.
   - Eliminates the need for a separate tempering furnace.

**(b) Lever Rule — Spheroidized Steel — 7 marks**

**Given:** $C_0 = 1.0\,\text{wt\%}\,\text{C}$.
**At room temperature:** $C_{\alpha} = 0.022\,\text{wt\%}\,\text{C}$ and $C_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} = 6.67\,\text{wt\%}\,\text{C}$.

**Step 1 — State the assumption** *(1 mark)*:
Equilibrium cooling has occurred; the alloy consists only of **ferrite + cementite** at room temperature, and the solubilities at room temperature are taken as the tie-line endpoints.

**Step 2 — Lever rule for ferrite** *(2 marks)*:
$$
W_{\alpha} = \frac{C_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} - C_0}{C_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} - C_{\alpha}}
$$
$$
W_{\alpha} = \frac{6.67 - 1.00}{6.67 - 0.022} = \frac{5.67}{6.648} = 0.8529
$$

**Step 3 — Compute ferrite weight %** *(1 mark)*:
$$
W_{\alpha} = 85.29\%
$$

**Step 4 — Cementite weight %** *(1 mark)*:
$$
W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} = 1 - 0.8529 = 0.1471
$$
$$
W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} = 14.71\%
$$

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation** *(2 marks)*:
Although cementite constitutes $14.71\,\text{wt\%}$, it is present as **isolated spherical particles** in a continuous ferrite matrix. This morphology — not the phase fraction — is what produces the characteristic *softness* and *superior machinability* of spheroidized steel.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Heat-Treatment Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing ε-carbide ($\text{Fe}_{2.4}\text{C}$) with cementite ($\text{Fe}_3\text{C}$).** ε-carbide forms in Stage 1; cementite forms in Stage 3.
> 2. **Forgetting the Kelvin conversion** in Hollomon–Jaffe problems — using Celsius directly is a guaranteed **−1 to −2 mark penalty**.
> 3. **Mixing up bainite and martensite endpoints** in austempering diagrams. Remember: $T_{\text{bath}} > M_s$ but $T_{\text{bath}} < B_s$.
> 4. **Writing "spheroidite" as a phase.** It is a *microstructure* (α + globular Fe₃C), not a phase.
> 5. **Skipping the hold time** in austempering calculations — the bainite-finish time on the TTT curve is **mandatory**.
> 6. **Reporting lever-rule answers without units** (wt% or %). Always state.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Spheroidizing** → *softest* structure; $650 - 700\,^\circ\text{C}$; prolonged hold; ferrite + *globular* cementite; used before machining.
- **Tempering** → applied to *martensite*; $150 - 650\,^\circ\text{C}$; produces *tempered martensite*; relieves stress; 4 stages.
- **Austempering** → isothermal *salt bath*; $260 - 400\,^\circ\text{C}$; produces *bainite*; **no separate tempering required**; minimal distortion.
- **Hollomon–Jaffe Parameter**: $HJP = T(\log t + 20)$ — use **Kelvin**, not Celsius.
- **Tempered Martensite Embrittlement (TME) zone** to **avoid**: $250\,^\circ\text{C} - 400\,^\circ\text{C}$.
- **Epsilon carbide ($\text{Fe}_{2.4}\text{C}$)** is a *transition* precipitate — not the equilibrium cementite.
- **Spheroidite is a microstructure, not a phase** — KTU examiners check this wording strictly.
- **Carbon range** for spheroidizing: $\geq 0.6\,\text{wt\%}\,\text{C}$ (hypo-eutectoid to hyper-eutectoid tool steels).
- **Lower bainite** → hard, needle-like, formed near $M_s$ ($260\,^\circ\text{C}$).
- **Upper bainite** → feathery, tougher, formed near $B_s$ ($400\,^\circ\text{C}$).
- **TTT diagram** must be drawn with *temperature on the y-axis* (decreasing downward) and *log time on the x-axis* — the *C-curve* must be clearly shown with austenitizing temperature above $A_3$.
- **Quench-and-tempered** vs **Austempered** at equal hardness: austempered steel has **higher impact toughness** and **lower distortion** — this is a favourite 2-mark KTU question.
- **Salt bath composition** for austempering: $\text{NaNO}_3 - \text{KNO}_3$ (approx $50:50$).
- **Sub-critical spheroidizing** temperature: $A_1 - 20\,^\circ\text{C} \approx 700\,^\circ\text{C}$.
- **Tempering stages 1 & 2** overlap with TME; report this clearly in long answers.
- Always end any KTU derivation with **units, physical interpretation, and a concluding sentence** — this guarantees the final 1–2 marks for presentation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Martempering and Ausforming - Hardenability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Martempering and Ausforming - Hardenability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Hardenability — The Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hardenability** is defined as the ability of a steel to form martensite when quenched from a temperature above the upper critical temperature (Ac₃). It is a measure of the *depth to which martensite forms* (or the ease with which martensite forms) within a specimen under given quenching conditions.

- **Hardenability ≠ Hardness.** This is the single most-tested distinction in KTU exams.
- **Hardness** is a *property* (resistance to localized plastic deformation).
- **Hardenability** is a *process-response characteristic* governed by austenite stability and cooling rate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key fact for KTU 2024:** Hardness of 100% martensite depends **only on carbon content** (not alloying). Hardenability, however, depends on **alloying elements** (Cr, Ni, Mo, Mn) that shift the TTT curve to the right, giving more time for martensite to form at slower cooling rates.

### 1.2 The Intuitive "Soap-Bubble" Analogy

Think of hardenability like **how deeply a stone sinks in mud versus in clay**:
- A steel with **low hardenability** is like **quicksand** — only the outer skin solidifies (forms martensite); the inside stays soft because the cooling front cannot push deep enough.
- A steel with **high hardenability** is like **hardened cement** — the entire cross-section hardens uniformly, because the alloying elements slow down the austenite-to-pearlite/bainite transformation, allowing martensite to form throughout.

> [!TIP]
> A **thick** shaft of the *same* steel may not fully harden, while a **thin** shaft does — this is hardenability in action. The cooling rate at the *core* is what really matters.

### 1.3 Martempering — The KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Martempering** is a specialized heat treatment in which steel is **austenitized**, then quenched into a **molten salt bath (or oil) held at a temperature just above the Martensite Start (Mₛ) temperature** (typically 150–250 °C), held until the temperature is **uniform throughout the section** (thermal equalization), and **then air-cooled** to room temperature to allow martensite to form.

The martensite thus produced is **untempered**, so a final tempering step is performed subsequently.

### 1.4 Ausforming — The KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ausforming** is a **thermo-mechanical heat treatment** in which steel is **austenitized**, cooled into the **metastable austenite region** (between Ac₁ and Mₛ) on the TTT diagram, **plastically deformed** (typically 70–90% reduction) in that region, and then **quenched to form martensite**, followed by tempering.

The deformation refines the austenite substructure (creates dislocation tangles and cell structures), which are then "inherited" by the martensite, producing **high strength with good ductility and toughness**.

### 1.5 Real-World Context (Why KTU Cares)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of Hardenability Concepts:**
> - Selection of **spring steels, gears, shafts, axles** in automobile and aerospace industries.
> - Martempering is used for **cutting tools, dies, and high-strength fasteners** to reduce distortion and cracking.
> - Ausforming is critical for **ultra-high-strength components** like rocket motor casings, aircraft structural parts, and high-strength wires.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hardenability — Deep Theoretical Analysis

The depth of hardening is governed by two interacting factors:

1. **Cooling rate** at the specimen core (function of geometry, quenching medium, and surface condition).
2. **Critical cooling rate (CCR)** of the steel (function of alloy content, austenite grain size, austenitizing temperature).

> **The 'C' nose of the TTT diagram defines the CCR.** If the cooling curve misses the nose, austenite transforms to martensite.

**Factors Affecting Hardenability:**

- **↑ Carbon content** → ↑ hardenability (mildly, mostly influences final hardness).
- **↑ Alloying elements (Mn, Cr, Mo, Ni, B, V)** → ↑ hardenability (each shifts the TTT nose to the right).
- **↑ Austenite grain size** → ↑ hardenability (fewer grain-boundary nucleation sites for pearlite/bainite).
- **↑ Austenitizing temperature** → ↑ hardenability (grain growth + more alloy dissolution).
- **Coarse pearlite/bainite vs. fine** → coarse favors martensite formation (less hardenable steel still forms martensite if the product is coarse pearlite, but coarse pearlite is undesirable mechanically).

### 2.2 The Jominy End-Quench Test (ASTM A255)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most heavily tested** hardenability method in KTU Board Exams.

**Procedure (Step-by-Step):**
1. **Standard specimen:** Cylindrical bar, **25 mm diameter × 100 mm long**.
2. **Austenitize** at the recommended temperature (above Ac₃) for 30 minutes.
3. **Quench the bottom face** with a column of water at ~25 °C through a 12.5 mm nozzle, with a 65 mm water column height.
4. **After cooling**, grind flat strips (0.4 mm deep) on opposite sides of the cylinder.
5. **Measure Rockwell C hardness** at 1.5 mm, 3 mm, 5 mm, 7 mm, 9 mm, 11 mm, 13 mm, 15 mm, and so on, from the quenched end.
6. **Plot** Hardness (HRC) vs. Distance from quenched end (Jominy distance, in mm or 1/16 inch units = "J" units).

**Result:** A **Jominy hardenability curve** (often called an "S-curve" because of its characteristic shape).

> **Key interpretation:** The point where hardness drops sharply corresponds to the location where 50% martensite is formed (called the **50% martensite criterion**).

### 2.3 Grossmann's Method — Ideal Critical Diameter (D_I)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grossmann** developed a quantitative method to predict hardenability independent of quenching medium. He defined:

$$D_I = D \times H$$

Where:
- $D_I$ = **Ideal critical diameter** (diameter that would give 50% martensite at the center under an "ideal" infinite-quench severity, H = ∞).
- $D$ = Actual critical diameter under the given quench.
- $H$ = **Quenching severity factor (Grossmann's H-value)**.

**Quenching Severity (H) Values (Memorize for KTU):**

| Quench Medium | H-Value (in⁻¹) | H-Value (mm⁻¹) |
|---|---|---|
| Air (still) | 0.02 | 0.0008 |
| Oil (medium) | 0.25–0.30 | 0.010–0.012 |
| Water (still) | 0.9–1.0 | 0.035–0.04 |
| Brine (10% NaCl, still) | 2.0 | 0.08 |
| Ideal (infinite rate) | ∞ | ∞ |

$$D_I = D_1 \times H_1 = D_2 \times H_2$$

This allows converting data from one quench medium to another.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the complete formula toolkit you need for KTU Board Questions on this topic.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ideal Critical Diameter | $D_I = D \times H$ | mm or in | Grossmann's relation |
| 2 | Equivalent Critical Diameter | $D_1 H_1 = D_2 H_2$ | mm × in⁻¹ | Cross-medium conversion |
| 3 | Hardness of 100% Martensite | $\text{HRC} \approx 35 + 1.6 \times (\%C)$ | dimensionless | Valid for 0.3–0.6 %C |
| 4 | Modified formula | $\text{HRC} \approx 60\sqrt{C} + 20$ | dimensionless | Another empirical fit |
| 5 | Ausforming strength gain | $\sigma \propto \rho^{0.5}$ | MPa | $\rho$ = dislocation density |
| 6 | Critical cooling rate | $CCR = \frac{T_{Ac_3} - T_{\text{nose}}}{\Delta t_{\text{nose}}}$ | °C/s | Read from TTT diagram |
| 7 | Martempering bath T | $T_{\text{bath}} = M_s + (30\text{ to } 80)$ | °C | Above Mₛ to avoid transformation |
| 8 | Bainite vs. Martensite hardness | Bainite < Martensite (same %C) | HRC | Martensite hardest |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU Pitfall: Students often confuse **Hardenability (deep hardening)** with **Hardness (high surface hardness)**. Hardenability has *units of length* (mm or in) and depends on *alloying*; hardness has *units of HRC/HRB* and depends on *carbon content*. Do NOT interchange them.

### 2.5 Martempering — Process Theory (Why It Works)

The **conventional quenching** problem: Quenching a thick steel section in water/oil creates:
- A large **temperature gradient** (hot center, cold surface).
- A large **transformation gradient** — the surface transforms to martensite *first* (expanding) while the core is still soft austenite.
- This produces **residual stresses → distortion, quench cracks**.

**Martempering solves this by:**
1. Rapidly cooling the entire section past the **'C'-curve nose** (to avoid pearlite/bainite formation).
2. Holding at a temperature **slightly above Mₛ** to equalize temperature.
3. Air cooling — martensite forms **simultaneously throughout the section** → no transformation gradient → minimal residual stress.

> **Result:** Martensite structure identical to conventional quenching, but with **greatly reduced distortion and cracking risk**.

### 2.6 Ausforming — Process Theory (Why It Works)

The trick in ausforming is that austenite is **metastable** in the temperature range between Ac₁ (or Acₘ) and Mₛ. During plastic deformation in this range:
- **Dislocation density in austenite increases** (from 10⁶ to 10¹¹ lines/cm²).
- A **sub-grain cell structure** forms in austenite.
- Carbides (in alloy steels) precipitate on dislocations — this "stamps" the substructure.
- When cooled to martensite, the **dislocation tangles are inherited**, plus the carbides pin dislocations.

**Outcome:**
- Tensile strength ↑ (often 2500–3500 MPa, versus 1800–2000 MPa for conventional martensite).
- Ductility maintained (8–12% elongation).
- Fatigue life ↑ dramatically.

> **Constraint:** Ausforming requires alloy steels with a *long* "flat" region on the TTT diagram (longer bay), e.g., 0.3–0.4% C, 4–8% Cr, with Mo/V additions. Plain carbon steels cannot be ausformed because the TTT nose is too close to the Mₛ line.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Gear manufacturing:** Martempered gears have uniform hardness with minimal distortion → no post-machining of teeth required (or only light grinding).
- **Cutting tools and dies:** Martempering + tempering yields **high wear resistance with low cracking risk**.
- **Aircraft landing gear:** Ausformed 4340 steel components achieve 2500+ MPa yield strength.
- **Automotive leaf springs:** Ausformed 5160 spring steel — combines strength and ductility for fatigue life.
- **High-strength cables:** Ausforming in the patenting bath for steel wire rope.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->
