### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Why is Manganese added to almost all commercial steels? Mention two major effects. (3 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Answer:
1. **Desulphurisation:** Mn combines with harmful **Sulphur** to form **MnS** (instead of brittle **FeS** that causes hot shortness at grain boundaries).
2. **Hardenability Boost:** Mn is a strong **austenite stabiliser** and dramatically increases the **hardenability** of steel by delaying pearlite/bainite transformation.
3. **Deoxidation:** Mn acts as a **deoxidiser** during steelmaking, removing dissolved oxygen.
4. **Strengthening:** Solid solution strengthening of ferrite, improving tensile strength.

---

## 📕 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### 🔹 **Question A — Carburizing, Nitriding & Alloying Elements (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 — Model Paper Q4]**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **Pack Carburizing process** in detail. State the chemical reactions, temperature range, holding time, and typical case depth obtained.

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** Pack carburizing is a **solid-state thermochemical diffusion process** where carbon is transferred to the surface of low-carbon steel from a **solid carbonaceous medium** packed around the components inside a sealed carburizing box.

**Equipment:** A cast-iron or heat-resistant steel box (retort) with an air-tight lid sealed with fireclay.

**Pack Composition (Typical):**

| Component | Percentage | Function |
|-----------|:---:|---------|
| **Wood charcoal** (granular 3-6 mm) | 85 – 90 % | Main source of CO |
| **BaCO₃ (Barium carbonate)** | 10 – 15 % | Energizer (catalyst) |
| **CaCO₃ / Na₂CO₃** | 0 – 5 % | Secondary energizer |

**Procedure:**
1. **Pre-cleaning** of components (degrease, sand blast)
2. **Pack the box:** Charcoal at bottom → component → charcoal → repeat
3. **Seal lid** with fireclay
4. **Heat to 880 – 950 °C** in furnace
5. **Hold for 4 – 12 hours** depending on required case depth
6. **Cool in box** to room temperature

**Chemical Reactions:**

$$C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$$

$$CO_2 + C \rightarrow 2\,CO$$

$$2\,CO \rightarrow CO_2 + [C]_{diffused\ into\ Fe}$$

$$BaCO_3 \rightarrow BaO + CO_2$$

$$CO_2 + C \rightarrow 2\,CO \quad (\text{regenerated})$$

The energizer **BaCO₃** decomposes to **BaO + CO₂** and the **CO₂** reacts with charcoal to produce additional **CO** — increasing the carbon potential of the atmosphere.

**Temperature:** 880 – 950 °C (above A₃)

**Case depth:** 0.5 – 4 mm (time-dependent, follows $x \approx \sqrt{Dt}$)

**Post-process:** Components are re-heated to **820 – 850 °C** and **oil-quenched** to obtain martensitic case.

**Advantages:** Low cost, simple equipment, suitable for large/massive components, no skilled operator needed.

**Disadvantages:** Slow (long cycle), uneven heating, high energy cost, limited carbon control.

**Applications:** Pinions, gears, axles, shafts of heavy machinery, mining equipment.

---

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Discuss the effect of **Chromium, Nickel, and Molybdenum** as alloying elements in steel.

**Model Answer:**

**1. CHROMIUM (Cr) — 0.5 % to 30 %:**
- **Strongest carbide former** → forms Cr₂₃C₆, Cr₇C₃ (hardness > 1500 HV)
- **Ferrite stabilizer** → shrinks γ-loop in Fe-C diagram
- **Improves hardenability** (≈ + 3.3 hardenability points per 1 % Cr)
- **Corrosion resistance** through Cr₂O₃ passive film (stainless steels need **> 10.5 % Cr**)
- **Improves wear resistance and hot strength**
- **Application:** Bearing steels (1 % Cr), tool steels (4 – 12 % Cr), stainless steels (12 – 30 % Cr)

**2. NICKEL (Ni) — 1 % to 35 %:**
- **Strong austenite stabilizer** → expands γ-loop
- **Solid solution strengthener** of ferrite
- **Improves low-temperature toughness** (cryogenic grade: **9 % Ni** for LNG tanks)
- **Does NOT form carbides** — stays in solid solution
- **Reduces coefficient of thermal expansion** (Invar alloy: 36 % Ni)
- **Improves corrosion resistance** in combination with Cr
- **Application:** Maraging steels (18 % Ni), austenitic SS (8 – 12 % Ni), armour plate

**3. MOLYBDENUM (Mo) — 0.2 % to 5 %:**
- **Very strong carbide former** → Mo₂C (extremely stable up to 600 °C)
- **Most potent hardenability booster** — even 0.2 – 0.3 % Mo doubles hardenability
- **Reduces temper brittleness** (replaces Cr in this role)
- **Improves creep strength** (resistance to slow deformation at high T)
- **Secondary hardening** in tool steels
- **Application:** Pressure vessels, Cr-Mo boiler steels (T22, T11), HSS, nitriding steels

| Element | Carbide? | Stabiliser | Best for |
|---------|----------|------------|----------|
| **Cr** | ✓ Strong | Ferrite | Hardness, wear, corrosion |
| **Ni** | ✗ | Austenite | Toughness, low T, ductility |
| **Mo** | ✓ Strong | Ferrite | Hardenability, creep, tempering |

---

### 🔹 **Question B — Induction Hardening & Effect of Alloying (14 Marks)** *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Model Paper Q5]**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **Induction Hardening process** with a neat diagram. State the principle, parameters, and applications.

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:**
Induction hardening is a **selective surface hardening** technique based on the principle of **electromagnetic induction** and the **skin effect**. When a high-frequency alternating current (AC) is passed through a coil, an **alternating magnetic field** is generated. This field induces **eddy currents** in the conductive steel component placed inside the coil. Due to the **skin effect**, these eddy currents are concentrated near the surface, rapidly heating it to above the **A₃ (austenitization) temperature**. The cold core acts as a **heat sink**, and a **water/polymer quench** follows immediately, producing a **martensitic surface layer**.

**Setup (as per Mermaid Section 4.4):** A water-cooled copper induction coil surrounds the component; power supply (10 kW – 1 MW) at frequencies **1 kHz – 1 MHz**; quenchant sprayed through the coil or from a separate ring.

**Key Process Parameters:**

| Parameter | Typical Range | Effect |
|-----------|---------------|--------|
| **Frequency** | 1 kHz – 1 MHz | Lower = deeper case |
| **Power density** | 1 – 5 kW/cm² | Higher = faster heating |
| **Heating time** | 1 – 10 s | Very brief |
| **Quench delay** | < 1 s | Critical to avoid pearlite |
| **Quenchant** | Water, polymer, oil | Depends on steel |

**Skin Depth Formula:**

$$\delta = 503\,\sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\mu_r\,f}}\quad (\delta\ \text{in mm})$$

where $\rho$ = resistivity, $\mu_r$ = relative permeability, $f$ = frequency in Hz.

**Steel Requirement:** Medium carbon (**0.4 – 0.5 % C**) with Mn, Cr, Mo additions for **adequate hardenability** (otherwise the surface won't fully transform to martensite on quenching).

**Process Steps:**
1. **Pre-machined** component (final dimensions — no post-machining)
2. Place in **induction coil**
3. **Heat surface** to 850 – 900 °C in seconds
4. **Spray water** quench → martensite forms
5. **Optional tempering** at 150 – 200 °C (rare; usually self-tempered)

**Advantages:**
- Extremely fast cycle (seconds)
- Selective heating (only required zones)
- No distortion (cold core)
- Energy efficient
- Easily automated

**Disadvantages:**
- Equipment cost is high
- Restricted to medium-carbon steels
- Coil design is critical
- Limited to surface, no through-hardening

**Applications:** Gear teeth, crankshafts, camshafts, axle journals, machine tool ways, rocker arms, splines, valve stems.

---

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** What is **Hardenability**? Explain how the alloying elements Mo, Cr, Mn, and B influence it.

**Model Answer:**

**Hardenability Definition:**
> [!NOTE]
> **Hardenability** is the ability of a steel to form **martensite** to a given depth when quenched from above the austenitization temperature. It is **NOT the same as hardness** — a steel may have high surface hardness but low hardenability (e.g., plain carbon steel gets hard only on the surface, not the core). Hardenability is measured by the **Jominy End-Quench Test** (ASTM A255) and quantified by the **Grossmann Number ($D_I$)** or **Jominy distance (J)**.

**Mechanism:** Alloying elements **delay the pearlitic and bainitic transformation** during cooling, allowing the steel to form martensite at slower cooling rates. The **TTT/CCT diagram shifts to the right** with increasing alloy content → longer incubation time.

**Effect of Alloying Elements on Hardenability (with multipliers):**

**1. Molybdenum (Mo) — Most potent effect:**
- Even 0.2 – 0.3 % Mo significantly shifts the **TTT nose to the right**
- Forms stable **Mo₂C** that resists dissolution
- Multiplier: ~**3 – 4 ×** for 0.5 % Mo

**2. Chromium (Cr):**
- Strong carbide former → **chromium carbides** must dissolve first
- Shifts **bainite region** to longer times
- Multiplier: ~**2.5 – 3 ×** for 1 % Cr

**3. Manganese (Mn):**
- Austenite stabiliser → depresses **bainite start temperature**
- Soluble in austenite (no carbide barrier)
- Multiplier: ~**2 – 2.5 ×** for 1 % Mn

**4. Boron (B) — Most cost-effective:**
- Just **0.001 – 0.003 %** (3 – 30 ppm)
- Acts by **segregating to austenite grain boundaries** and **delaying ferrite nucleation**
- **Effect = equivalent to 1 % Cr or 1.5 % Ni**
- **Must be protected by Ti or Al** to prevent BN formation

**Rank Order of Hardenability Effect (per wt %):**
$$\text{B (10×)} > \text{Mo} > \text{Cr} > \text{Mn} > \text{Ni} > \text{Si} > \text{C}$$

**Grossmann Multiplier Table (Common Values):**

| Element Added | Per 0.1 % | Per 1 % |
|---------------|:---------:|:-------:|
| C | 1.05 | — |
| Mn | 1.04 | 1.5 |
| Cr | 1.02 | 3.3 |
| Ni | 1.01 | 1.5 |
| Mo | 1.05 | 2.5 |
| B (30 ppm) | 1.5 | — |

**Practical Example:** AISI 4140 (0.4 % C, 1.0 % Cr, 0.85 % Mn, 0.2 % Mo) has a **Jominy distance of 12 mm** for HRC 50, while a plain C40 (0.4 % C) has only **3 mm** — proving the huge hardenability boost of alloys.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "Hardenability" with "Hardness"** — A common KTU trap. Hardenability = depth of hardening; Hardness = absolute value. **5–8 marks** can be lost in a 14-mark answer if this is mixed up.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting chemical reactions in Carburizing/Nitriding** — Examiners specifically ask for the chemical equations. Memorize:
>    - Carburizing: $2\,CO \rightarrow CO_2 + [C]$
>    - Nitriding: $2\,NH_3 \rightarrow 2\,[N] + 3\,H_2$
> 
> 3. **Wrong temperature ranges** — Mixing up nitriding (500 °C) with carburizing (900 °C) is a frequently-observed error. The 14-mark answer can lose **3 marks** for this alone.
> 
> 4. **Listing alloying elements without explaining mechanism** — Examiner wants **how** each element works (carbide formation, austenite stabilization, etc.), not just a bullet list.
> 
> 5. **Missing the "quench not required" point for nitriding** — Often asked as a 2-mark question.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting Fick's 2nd law application** — Many students miss the diffusion part in carburizing problems; the **error function solution** must be shown.
> 
> 7. **In induction hardening, forgetting to state that core remains tough** — A core answer point.
> 
> 8. **Mixing up martensite with pearlite** in case depth definitions.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — Must Memorise for KTU 2024 ESE**

### 🔑 Key Surface Hardening Facts

- ⭐ **Pack carburizing temperature** = **880 – 950 °C**; energizer = **BaCO₃**
- ⭐ **Nitriding temperature** = **500 – 550 °C**; **NO quench needed**
- ⭐ **Cyanide (NaCN) baths are toxic** — modern industry uses **gas carbonitriding**
- ⭐ **Induction hardening case depth** $d = 503\sqrt{\rho/(\mu_r f)}$ — higher frequency = shallower case
- ⭐ **Flame hardening** uses **oxy-acetylene** at ~3100 °C
- ⭐ **Effective case depth** = depth at **HRC ≥ 50**
- ⭐ **Jominy End-Quench Test** (ASTM A255) measures hardenability
- ⭐ **Grossmann number $D_I$** quantifies hardenability

### 🔑 Key Alloying Element Facts

- ⭐ **Mn** → kills S, boosts hardenability, in EVERY steel
- ⭐ **Ni** → austenite, no carbide, low-T toughness (9 % Ni for LNG)
- ⭐ **Cr** → > 10.5 % = stainless, strongest carbide former (for corrosion)
- ⭐ **Mo** → most potent creep resistance, prevents temper embrittlement
- ⭐ **V** → grain refiner, hardest carbide (VC ~ 2500 HV)
- ⭐ **W** → hot hardness / red hardness (HSS T1 = 18 % W)
- ⭐ **Co** → hot hardness in HSS (T15, M42)
- ⭐ **Si** → spring (EN45) and electrical (3 % Si transformer) steels
- ⭐ **B** → 30 ppm gives massive hardenability boost — cheapest

### 🔑 High-Yield Numerical Formulae

- ⭐ Fick's 2nd Law solution: $\dfrac{C_s - C_x}{C_s - C_0} = \mathrm{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$
- ⭐ Case depth in induction: $d = 503\,\sqrt{\rho/(\mu_r f)}$
- ⭐ MnS in steel (kills hot shortness)
- ⭐ **Eutectoid** of Fe-C: 0.8 % C at 727 °C
- ⭐ **Max C in γ-Fe** = 2.14 % at 1147 °C

### 🔑 Quick Recall — Process vs Case Depth

| Process | Case Depth (mm) | Hardness | Quench? |
|---------|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Pack Carburizing | 1 – 4 | HRC 60-65 | Yes |
| Gas Carburizing | 0.5 – 3 | HRC 60-65 | Yes |
| Nitriding | 0.1 – 0.7 | 700-1100 HV | No |
| Cyaniding | 0.1 – 0.8 | HRC 60-65 | Yes |
| Induction | 0.5 – 4 | HRC 60-65 | Yes |
| Flame | 1 – 6 | HRC 55-63 | Yes |
| Laser | 0.1 – 1 | HRC 60-65 | Self |

### 🔑 Exam Strategy Tips (KTU 2024)

- 🎯 In 14-mark answers, use **headings + tables** for readability — examiner-friendly
- 🎯 Always **draw neat labelled diagrams** in carburizing/nitriding questions
- 🎯 For alloying elements, state **% range + 2 effects + 1 application** = full marks
- 🎯 For 3-mark questions, use **comparison tables** to cover more points concisely
- 🎯 **End your answer with a real-world example** (e.g., "AISI 8620 for automotive gears") — this is the KTU touch that gives edge marks

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strengthening Mechanisms - Characteristics of Fatigue Failure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Strengthening Mechanisms - Characteristics of Fatigue Failure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Strengthening Mechanisms — Formal Definition

In materials science and engineering, **strengthening mechanisms** refer to the metallurgical, thermal, and mechanical strategies employed to increase a material's resistance to plastic deformation, thereby enhancing its yield strength, tensile strength, and hardness. Under the KTU 2024 Scheme, these are studied as the four primary mechanisms: **grain-boundary strengthening**, **solid-solution strengthening**, **strain (work) hardening**, and **precipitation (age) hardening**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> The student must be able to identify which strengthening mechanism dominates under a given microstructural condition, and quantify the contribution using the appropriate mathematical relation (e.g., Hall–Petch for grain size, Orowan looping for precipitates).

## 1.2 Characteristics of Fatigue Failure — Formal Definition

**Fatigue failure** is the progressive, localized structural damage caused by cyclic loading at stress levels considerably lower than the static tensile or yield strength of the material. The defining feature is that failure occurs at stresses *below* the yield point after a sufficient number of stress cycles. The KTU 2024 scheme categorizes its characteristics through the **S–N curve (Wöhler curve)**, the concept of **endurance limit**, the three-stage fatigue life (crack initiation, stable crack growth, catastrophic fracture), and the microscopic features of the fracture surface (beach marks, striations).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology (from KTU 2024 Module Outcomes):**
> - *Cycle*: One complete sequence of stress variation.
> - *Mean Stress (σₘ)*: The arithmetic mean of maximum and minimum stress in a cycle.
> - *Stress Amplitude (σₐ)*: Half the difference between maximum and minimum stress.
> - *Endurance Limit (σₑ)*: The stress level below which a material can endure an effectively infinite number of cycles (typically > 10⁷ for steels).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

### 1.3.1 Analogy for Strengthening Mechanisms
Imagine a pack of cards lying loosely on a table. You can easily push your finger through the pack because the cards slip past one another (analogous to dislocations moving freely in a coarse-grained metal). Now imagine gluing the cards together — pushing through becomes much harder. The glue represents obstacles that pin dislocations. **Every strengthening mechanism is, at its core, just a different strategy of "gluing" the dislocations in place** so they cannot glide easily under an applied shear stress.

### 1.3.2 Analogy for Fatigue Failure
Think of a paperclip. If you bend it once gently, nothing happens. If you bend it back and forth 20–30 times, it snaps. The stress you applied each time was tiny compared to the force required to tear the clip in half in one go. **Fatigue is "death by a thousand small bends"** — the cyclic accumulation of sub-yield damage until the structure can no longer hold together.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Examiners frequently award full marks only when students correctly differentiate between *static* failure (single overload) and *fatigue* failure (cyclic sub-yield failure), and explicitly mention that **fatigue is the most common cause of mechanical failure in service (≈ 80–90 % of all mechanical failures).**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Classical Strengthening Mechanisms

### 2.1.1 Grain-Boundary Strengthening (Hall–Petch Mechanism)
Grain boundaries act as barriers to dislocation motion because the slip plane orientation changes abruptly across the boundary. A dislocation pile-up generates a back-stress that must be overcome for slip to continue in the next grain.

- **Microstructural Origin:** Atoms at the boundary are disordered; the slip plane is discontinuous.
- **Why it works:** Smaller grains ⇒ more boundaries per unit length ⇒ more barriers.
- **Engineering Utility:** Used to strengthen structural steels, aluminum alloys, and ceramics by thermomechanical processing (controlled rolling, normalizing).

### 2.1.2 Solid-Solution Strengthening
Solute atoms dissolved in the parent lattice create localized stress fields that hinder dislocation motion.

- **Substitutional solutes** (e.g., Zn in Cu) ⇒ size-mismatch strain fields.
- **Interstitial solutes** (e.g., C in Fe) ⇒ much stronger effect because of severe lattice distortion.
- **Why it works:** The dislocation must either cut through (solute drag) or bypass the stress field, raising the critical resolved shear stress (CRSS).

### 2.1.3 Strain Hardening (Work Hardening / Cold Working)
Plastic deformation increases dislocation density dramatically. Dislocations interact, entangle, and pin each other.

- **Dislocation density (ρ)** rises from ~10⁶ cm⁻² (annealed) to ~10¹⁰–10¹² cm⁻² (heavily cold-worked).
- **Why it works:** More dislocations ⇒ more mutual obstruction ⇒ higher flow stress.
- **Engineering Utility:** Cold-rolled sheets, drawn wires, and cold-headed bolts all rely on this mechanism.

### 2.1.4 Precipitation (Age) Hardening
Fine, hard, coherent precipitates (e.g., Al₂Cu in Al–Cu alloys, Ni₃Al in Ni-superalloys) impede dislocation motion.

- **Small/coherent precipitates** ⇒ dislocations *cut through* them.
- **Larger/incoherent precipitates** ⇒ dislocations *bypass* via the **Orowan looping** mechanism.
- **Why it works:** Both cutting and looping require extra energy/work, increasing yield strength.

## 2.2 Characteristics of Fatigue Failure — Analytical Framework

### 2.2.1 The S–N Curve (Wöhler Curve)
A plot of cyclic stress amplitude (S) versus number of cycles to failure (N, on log scale).

- **For ferrous alloys (steel, Fe):** Distinct *knee* at ~10⁶–10⁷ cycles → **endurance limit (σₑ)**.
- **For non-ferrous alloys (Al, Cu, Ti):** No true knee; fatigue strength reported at 10⁷ or 10⁸ cycles → **fatigue strength (σ_f at N cycles)**.

### 2.2.2 Three Stages of Fatigue Life
1. **Stage I — Crack Initiation (≈ 90 % of life in high-cycle fatigue):** Persistent slip bands (PSBs) form at the surface where slip is easiest; micro-voids coalesce into a sub-surface embryonic crack of order 1–10 µm.
2. **Stage II — Stable Crack Propagation:** Crack advances perpendicular to the maximum tensile stress, leaving characteristic **striations** on the fracture surface; one striation ≈ one load cycle.
3. **Stage III — Final Fracture:** Crack reaches a critical size (K_IC); rapid unstable ductile or brittle fracture occurs; surface becomes rough and shows **beach marks** (macroscopic) and **dimples** (microscopic).

### 2.2.3 Mean-Stress Correction Theories
When mean stress is non-zero, fatigue strength is reduced. Three classical relationships give the allowable alternating stress σ_a for a given σ_m:

| Theory | Equation | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Soderberg** (most conservative) | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_y} = 1$ | Ductile design safety |
| **Goodman** (linear, common) | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = 1$ | Brittle materials / general machine design |
| **Gerber** (parabolic) | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \left(\dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^{2} = 1$ | Ductile materials, tensile mean stress |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** Goodman diagrams are the industry standard for shaft design (e.g., ASME and Shigley's *Mechanical Engineering Design* uses Goodman as the default). The Gerber parabola is the closest to experimental data for ductile metals in tension, but it allows higher σ_a — risky for safety-critical parts.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \, d^{-1/2}$ | $\sigma_0$ = friction stress (MPa), $k_y$ = Hall–Petch coefficient (MPa·µm^½), $d$ = average grain diameter (µm) | Hall–Petch grain-boundary strengthening |
| 2 | $\Delta \sigma_{ss} = k_s \, c^{n}$ | $c$ = solute concentration (at %), $k_s, n$ = constants | Solid-solution strengthening |
| 3 | $\tau = \tau_0 + \alpha \, G \, b \, \sqrt{\rho}$ | $\tau_0$ = CRSS, $G$ = shear modulus, $b$ = Burgers vector, $\rho$ = dislocation density (lines/m²), $\alpha$ ≈ 0.3–0.5 | Strain hardening (Taylor equation) |
| 4 | $\Delta \tau_{Orowan} = \dfrac{G \, b}{L - 2r}$ | $L$ = inter-particle spacing, $r$ = precipitate radius | Orowan looping (precipitation hardening, large particles) |
| 5 | $\sigma_a = \dfrac{S_{ut} - S_e}{2} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)$ | — | Goodman line (rearranged) |
| 6 | $\sigma_a^{Gerber} = S_e \, \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^{2}\right]$ | — | Gerber parabola |
| 7 | $\dfrac{da}{dN} = C \left(\Delta K\right)^{m}$ | $a$ = crack length, $N$ = cycles, $\Delta K$ = stress-intensity range, $C, m$ = Paris-law constants | Stage II crack growth (Paris Law) |
| 8 | $\Delta K = Y \, \Delta\sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | $Y$ = geometry factor, $\Delta\sigma = \sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}$ | Crack-tip stress intensity range |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** Memorize equations 1, 3, 5, and 7 — these four appear in almost every Module-3 Part-B question. Equation 7 (Paris Law) is *the* formula for fatigue crack propagation problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Hall–Petch Relation

### 3.1.1 Concept
A dislocation pile-up of $n$ dislocations forms against a grain boundary. The leading dislocation experiences a back-stress that scales with the number of dislocations in the pile-up and the length of the slip band (proportional to grain diameter $d$).

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

Let the shear stress acting on a slip plane be $\tau$, and the applied shear stress required to move a free dislocation be $\tau_0$. The number of dislocations $n$ that can pile up against a boundary in a grain of diameter $d$ is found by force balance.

The shear stress $\tau$ acts on each dislocation. The leading dislocation is held by the grain boundary which exerts a reaction force $n \tau b$. Equating this to the local stress concentration at the head of the pile-up:

$$
\tau \cdot d \;\propto\; n \cdot \tau_0 \cdot b
$$

This implies that $n$ is proportional to the grain diameter $d$. A larger grain admits a longer pile-up, meaning more dislocations, meaning a higher stress concentration at the tip.

The stress at the tip of the pile-up that activates slip in the next grain is:

$$
\tau_{tip} = n \cdot \tau_0 \;\propto\; d
$$

The macroscopic yield stress is the applied stress at which this tip-stress reaches a critical value $\tau_c$ required to nucleate slip in the adjacent grain. Therefore:

$$
\tau_y \;\propto\; d^{1/2}
$$

The applied *normal* yield stress is related to shear stress via the Taylor factor $m \approx 2$ for polycrystals:

$$
\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \, d^{-1/2}
$$

where $\sigma_0$ is the friction stress (lattice resistance + solid-solution contribution) and $k_y$ is the Hall–Petch coefficient (units: MPa·µm^½).

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example
A mild steel has $\sigma_0 = 70 \text{ MPa}$ and $k_y = 0.74 \text{ MPa·m}^{0.5}$ (note $k_y$ with grain size in m).

For a grain size $d = 25 \text{ µm} = 25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$:

$$
d^{-1/2} = (25 \times 10^{-6})^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 200 \text{ m}^{-1/2}
$$

$$
\sigma_y = 70 + (0.74)(200) = 70 + 148 = 218 \text{ MPa}
$$

For a finer grain size $d = 5 \text{ µm}$:

$$
d^{-1/2} = (5 \times 10^{-6})^{-1/2} \approx 447.2 \text{ m}^{-1/2}
$$

$$
\sigma_y = 70 + (0.74)(447.2) \approx 70 + 330.9 = 400.9 \text{ MPa}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Reducing the grain size from 25 µm to 5 µm nearly **doubles** the yield strength — explaining why modern HSLA (high-strength low-alloy) steels are produced with extremely fine grain sizes.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Strain-Hardening (Taylor) Relation

The flow stress $\tau$ of a metal increases with dislocation density $\rho$ because each dislocation interacts with the elastic stress field of all others. The shear stress required for a dislocation to glide past a "forest" of dislocations is empirically given by:

$$
\tau = \tau_0 + \alpha \, G \, b \, \sqrt{\rho}
$$

### 3.2.1 Why the Square Root?
The elastic interaction energy between two dislocations scales as $1/r$ where $r$ is the separation. In a 2D random array of $N$ dislocations in area $A$, the average separation is $\rho^{-1/2}$. The flow stress to push a moving dislocation past this average spacing is proportional to the resolved stress at that distance, which is proportional to $\rho^{1/2}$. Hence the square root.

### 3.2.2 Worked Numerical Example
Pure copper (annealed): $\rho_0 = 10^{12} \text{ m}^{-2}$, $G = 48 \text{ GPa}$, $b = 0.256 \text{ nm} = 2.56 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}$, $\alpha = 0.4$, $\tau_0 = 0.5 \text{ MPa}$.

$$
\alpha \, G \, b = 0.4 \times (48 \times 10^9) \times (2.56 \times 10^{-10}) = 4.915 \text{ MPa·m}
$$

$$
\sqrt{\rho_0} = \sqrt{10^{12}} = 10^6 \text{ m}^{-1}
$$

$$
\tau = 0.5 + (4.915)(10^6 \text{ m}^{-1})(\text{wait, units check})
$$

Let me redo with correct units. $\alpha G b$ has units of Pa·m (stress × length). $\sqrt{\rho}$ has units m^{-1}. So $\alpha G b \sqrt{\rho}$ has units Pa. ✓

$$
\alpha G b \sqrt{\rho} = 4.915 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} \times \dots
$$

(Unit check: $\alpha G b$ = 4.915 Pa·m; $\sqrt{\rho}$ = $10^6$ m^{-1}; product = $4.915 \times 10^6$ Pa = 4.915 MPa)

$$
\tau = 0.5 + 4.915 = 5.415 \text{ MPa}
$$

After cold-rolling to $\rho = 10^{16} \text{ m}^{-2}$:

$$
\sqrt{\rho} = 10^8 \text{ m}^{-1}
$$

$$
\tau = 0.5 + (4.915)(10^8) \text{ Pa} = 0.5 + 491.5 \text{ MPa} \approx 492 \text{ MPa}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** A 10 000-fold rise in dislocation density (from $10^{12}$ to $10^{16}$ m^{-2}) produces nearly a **90-fold rise** in flow stress — the basis of cold working.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Goodman Relation (Rearranged Form)

The Goodman line assumes a straight-line relationship between fatigue strength and mean stress. Two reference points are used:

- When $\sigma_m = 0$: $\sigma_a = S_e$ (endurance limit).
- When $\sigma_a = 0$: $\sigma_m = S_{ut}$ (ultimate tensile strength at the static-failure limit).

The straight line connecting these two points in $\sigma_a$–$\sigma_m$ space is:

$$
\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = 1
$$

Solving explicitly for the allowable alternating stress:

$$
\sigma_a = S_e \left(1 - \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)
$$

### 3.3.1 Worked Numerical Example
AISI 1040 steel has $S_e = 280 \text{ MPa}$, $S_{ut} = 565 \text{ MPa}$. A rotating shaft is subjected to a bending stress that fluctuates between $\sigma_{min} = -50 \text{ MPa}$ and $\sigma_{max} = 350 \text{ MPa}$.

$$
\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2} = \frac{350 + (-50)}{2} = 150 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2} = \frac{350 - (-50)}{2} = 200 \text{ MPa}
$$

Allowable $\sigma_a$ from Goodman:

$$
\sigma_{a, \text{allow}} = 280 \left(1 - \frac{150}{565}\right) = 280 \times (1 - 0.2655) = 280 \times 0.7345 = 205.7 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Comparison:** The actual $\sigma_a = 200 \text{ MPa} < 205.7 \text{ MPa}$ — the shaft is **safe**. If actual exceeded allowable, a larger shaft diameter would be required.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic for Identifying the Mechanism

| Observation in the Material | Dominant Mechanism | Tell-Tale Sign |
|---|---|---|
| Yield strength increases linearly with $d^{-1/2}$ | **Grain-boundary** | Plot $\sigma_y$ vs $d^{-1/2}$ gives a straight line |
| Hardness rises with solute content; lattice parameter shifts | **Solid-solution** | XRD peak shift; substitutional atoms visible in EDS |
| Hardness rises during plastic deformation; no phase change | **Strain hardening** | TEM shows entangled dislocation networks |
| Hardness peaks at a specific aging time/temperature | **Precipitation hardening** | TEM shows coherent precipitates; DSC peak on aging |
| Fatigue failure with no gross plastic deformation | **Fatigue** | Beach marks, striations, multiple crack origins at surface |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Fatigue Failure

Below is a high-level architecture mapping the interactions among the stages of fatigue, the S–N curve, and the contributing factors. This is rendered in Mermaid to comply with the diagram-safeguard policy.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cyclic Stress Applied sigma a sigma m] --> B[Stage I: Crack Initiation]
    B --> B1[Persistent Slip Bands form at surface]
    B1 --> B2[Micro-voids coalesce at inclusions or grain boundaries]
    B2 --> C[Embryonic crack of size 1 to 10 micrometres]
    C --> D[Stage II: Stable Crack Propagation]
    D --> D1[Paris Law: da per dN equals C times delta K to the m]
    D1 --> D2[Striations appear on fracture surface one per cycle]
    D2 --> E[Stage III: Final Fracture]
    E --> E1[Crack reaches critical size a sub c]
    E1 --> E2[Stress intensity K equals K sub IC]
    E2 --> F[Catastrophic Rupture]

    A --> G[S-N Wohler Curve Plotted]
    G --> G1[Ferrous alloys show endurance limit sigma sub e]
    G --> G2[Non-ferrous alloys show no knee]
    G --> G3[High cycle fatigue N greater than 10 to the 5]
    G --> G4[Low cycle fatigue N less than 10 to the 4]

    A --> H[Mean Stress Effect]
    H --> H1[Soderberg Conservative line]
    H --> H2[Goodman Linear line]
    H --> H3[Gerber Parabolic line]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Strengthening Mechanism Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Material Selection Required] --> Q{Working Temperature}
    Q -->|Below 0.4 T sub m| R[Strengthening via dislocation mechanisms]
    Q -->|Above 0.4 T sub m| S[Strengthening via grain boundary and precipitates only]

    R --> T{Type of load}
    T -->|Static| U[Strain hardening by cold working]
    T -->|Cyclic fatigue| V[Fine grain plus precipitation hardening]
    T -->|Impact| W[Fine grain plus solid solution]

    S --> X[Dispersion strengthening with stable oxides]
    S --> Y[Coarse grain to limit creep]

    U --> Z1[Increase dislocation density rho]
    V --> Z2[Hall Petch plus Orowan looping]
    W --> Z3[Interstitial solutes like C and N]
    X --> Z4[ODS alloys e.g. MA 754]
    Y --> Z5[Directional solidification single crystals]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Map — Fracture Surface Features

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage I Initiation Site] --> S2[Stage II Striations Beach Marks]
    S2 --> S3[Stage III Final Fracture Zone Dimples or Cleavage]
    S1 --> M1[Microscopic: persistent slip bands]
    S2 --> M2[Microscopic: fatigue striations one per cycle]
    S2 --> MAC1[Macroscopic: beach marks visible to naked eye]
    S3 --> M3[Microscopic: dimples for ductile, cleavage for brittle]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram for KTU exams:** When asked to "describe the features of a fatigue fracture surface," use this three-stage map. Examiners specifically look for the phrase *one striation per load cycle* — a phrase worth 1 mark on its own.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between strengthening by grain size reduction and strengthening by cold working. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Grain-Size Reduction | Cold Working |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Hall–Petch: grain boundaries block dislocations | Taylor: dislocation–dislocation interaction |
| Microstructural change | Decreases average grain diameter $d$ | Increases dislocation density $\rho$ |
| Mathematical form | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ | $\tau = \tau_0 + \alpha G b \sqrt{\rho}$ |
| Process | Normalising, controlled rolling | Cold rolling, drawing, extrusion |
| Example | Fine-grained HSLA structural steel | Cold-drawn steel wire for suspension bridges |

> **Mark split:** 1 mark for correct definition, 1 mark for difference, 1 mark for example.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is meant by endurance limit? Why do non-ferrous metals not exhibit a true endurance limit?**

**Model Answer:**
The **endurance limit (σₑ)** is the maximum cyclic stress amplitude that a material can withstand for an effectively infinite number of cycles (conventionally N ≥ 10⁶) without failure. It is identified on the S–N curve as the *knee* beyond which the curve becomes horizontal.

**Why non-ferrous metals lack a true endurance limit:** In non-ferrous metals such as aluminium, copper, and titanium, no strain-ageing (Cottrell atmosphere pinning) occurs because the dislocations are not strongly locked by interstitial atmospheres. Hence the S–N curve continues to slope gently downward indefinitely. Designers therefore use the **fatigue strength at a specified life (e.g., 10⁸ cycles)** as the design criterion.

> **Mark split:** 2 marks for definition + curve description, 1 mark for non-ferrous explanation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**(a)** Explain the Hall–Petch mechanism of strengthening. Derive the relation $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ and state the units of each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A brass component must operate at alternating stress σ_a = 110 MPa with mean stress σ_m = 60 MPa. The material properties are $S_{ut} = 320 \text{ MPa}$ and $S_e = 150 \text{ MPa}$. Using the Goodman relation, check whether the design is safe. If not, suggest two corrective measures. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

1. **Concept (1 mark):** Grain boundaries impede dislocation motion. Smaller grain size means more boundaries per unit length.
2. **Dislocation pile-up picture (2 marks):** Under an applied shear stress $\tau$, dislocations pile up against a grain boundary. The number of dislocations in the pile-up is $n \propto d$. The stress concentration at the tip of the pile-up activates slip in the next grain when the tip-stress reaches a critical value.
3. **Derivation (3 marks):**
   - $n \propto d$ from the force balance at the pile-up.
   - Tip stress $\tau_{tip} = n \tau_0 \propto d^{1/2}$.
   - Yield condition requires this tip stress to equal the critical nucleation stress.
   - Converting to normal stress via the Taylor factor $m$ and rearranging:

   $$
   \sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \, d^{-1/2}
   $$

4. **Units (1 mark):** $\sigma_0$ in MPa, $k_y$ in MPa·µm^½, $d$ in µm.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

1. **Compute allowable σ_a from Goodman (3 marks):**

   $$
   \sigma_{a,\text{allow}} = S_e \left(1 - \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right) = 150 \left(1 - \frac{60}{320}\right)
   $$

   $$
   \sigma_{a,\text{allow}} = 150 \times (1 - 0.1875) = 150 \times 0.8125 = 121.875 \text{ MPa}
   $$

2. **Compare (2 marks):** Actual $\sigma_a = 110 \text{ MPa} < \sigma_{a,\text{allow}} = 121.875 \text{ MPa}$. The design is **safe** by the Goodman criterion.

3. **Two corrective measures (2 marks):**
   - Reduce the mean stress by introducing a compressive residual stress (e.g., shot peening or autofrettage).
   - Increase the endurance limit by surface polishing, case-hardening, or nitriding the brass.
   - Or simply increase the cross-section to lower both σ_a and σ_m.

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the three stages of fatigue failure. List the macroscopic and microscopic features of the fatigue fracture surface. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A structural component is subjected to a fully reversed stress cycle. The S–N curve follows the Basquin relation $\sigma_a = \sigma_f \, (2N)^{b}$, where $\sigma_f = 1000 \text{ MPa}$ and $b = -0.10$. Find the fatigue life if the applied stress amplitude is 250 MPa. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

1. **Sketch (2 marks):** Show the three regions: (1) initiation site at the surface, (2) smooth region with striations, (3) rough final-fracture zone.
2. **Stage descriptions (3 marks):**
   - **Stage I (Crack Initiation):** Persistent slip bands at the free surface; micro-crack of order grain size.
   - **Stage II (Stable Crack Growth):** Crack propagates normal to maximum principal stress; striations form (one per cycle); Paris Law region.
   - **Stage III (Final Fracture):** When K reaches K_IC, fast fracture; may be ductile (dimples) or brittle (cleavage).
3. **Fracture-surface features (2 marks):**
   - *Macroscopic:* beach marks (clam-shell pattern), ratchet marks (multiple origins), smooth bright region, rough final region.
   - *Microscopic:* striations (one per cycle), dimples (final ductile rupture), cleavage facets (final brittle rupture).

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

1. **Apply Basquin's relation (3 marks):**

   $$
   \sigma_a = \sigma_f (2N)^{b}
   $$

   $$
   250 = 1000 (2N)^{-0.10}
   $$

2. **Rearrange (2 marks):**

   $$
   (2N)^{-0.10} = 0.25
   $$

   $$
   2N = (0.25)^{1/(-0.10)} = (0.25)^{-10}
   $$

3. **Compute (2 marks):**

   $$
   2N = (0.25)^{-10} = 4^{10} = 1{,}048{,}576
   $$

   $$
   N = \frac{1{,}048{,}576}{2} = 524{,}288 \text{ cycles}
   $$

   $$
   N \approx 5.24 \times 10^5 \text{ cycles}
   $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse endurance limit with fatigue strength.** Endurance limit applies only to ferrous alloys; non-ferrous alloys have *fatigue strength at N cycles*. Many students lose 1 mark by using the terms interchangeably.
> 2. **Units of $k_y$ in Hall–Petch must be matched with units of $d$.** If $d$ is in µm, then $k_y$ is in MPa·µm^½; if $d$ is in m, $k_y$ is in MPa·m^½. Mixing units is the most common arithmetic error in this topic.
> 3. **In the Basquin problem, students often forget the factor of 2 inside the parenthesis** ($\sigma_a = \sigma_f' (2N)^b$, not $N^b$). Lose 1 mark if you write $\sigma_a = \sigma_f N^b$.
> 4. **Do not state that fatigue cracks always initiate at the surface.** Surface initiation is typical in high-cycle fatigue, but *sub-surface* initiation is common in rolling-contact fatigue (bearings) and inclusion-induced fatigue. Examiners appreciate this nuance.
> 5. **In Goodman problems, always state the criterion explicitly**: "actual σ_a < allowable σ_a ⇒ safe; else unsafe." Vague conclusions lose the final mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Four strengthening mechanisms:** Grain-boundary (Hall–Petch), Solid-solution, Strain hardening (Taylor), Precipitation (Orowan).
- ✅ **Hall–Petch equation:** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ — finer grains ⇒ higher strength.
- ✅ **Strain hardening equation:** $\tau = \tau_0 + \alpha G b \sqrt{\rho}$ — more dislocations ⇒ higher strength.
- ✅ **Fatigue is sub-yield cyclic failure; ≈ 80–90 % of all mechanical failures in service are fatigue failures.**
- ✅ **Three stages of fatigue:** Initiation (persistent slip bands, surface), Stable propagation (striations), Final fracture (dimples or cleavage).
- ✅ **One striation ≈ one load cycle** (this phrase guarantees at least 1 mark).
- ✅ **S–N curve:** Ferrous alloys → distinct endurance limit σₑ; non-ferrous → no true knee, use fatigue strength at 10⁸ cycles.
- ✅ **Mean-stress corrections:** Soderberg (conservative), Goodman (linear, industry default), Gerber (parabolic, ductile).
- ✅ **Paris Law:** $da/dN = C (\Delta K)^m$ governs Stage II crack growth.
- ✅ **Endurance limit modifiers** (Marin factors): surface factor, size factor, loading factor, temperature factor, reliability factor, miscellaneous factor — multiply to get $S_e$ from $S_e'$ (rotating-beam baseline).
- ✅ **Surface finish matters most** for fatigue: rough surface → lower endurance limit. Polishing, shot peening, nitriding all improve fatigue life.
- ✅ **One-liner for exams:** "Fatigue is *localized*, *progressive*, and occurs at *stress below* the yield strength under *cyclic* loading."

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Strengthening Mechanisms & Characteristics of Fatigue Failure**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fatigue Tests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fatigue Tests

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Fatigue?

**Fatigue** is the progressive, localized structural damage and failure of a material subjected to dynamic/cyclic loading conditions (stresses and strains fluctuating with time) at stress levels considerably lower than the tensile or yield strength of the material under static loading.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expected):**
> *Fatigue is the phenomenon of failure of materials under fluctuating (cyclic) stresses at stress levels well below their static fracture strength. Fatigue tests determine the endurance limit (for ferrous alloys) or fatigue strength at a specified number of cycles (for non-ferrous alloys).*

## 1.2 What is a Fatigue Test?

A **fatigue test** is a destructive materials testing procedure used to determine the fatigue life of a material specimen by subjecting it to a controlled, repetitive load (tensile, compressive, bending, or torsional) until failure occurs or until a pre-defined number of cycles is reached.

The test is typically performed on a **fatigue testing machine** (also called a fatigue testing rig or rotating-beam machine) that applies cyclic loading at a constant amplitude.

## 1.3 Why is Fatigue Testing Important?

- Over **90% of all in-service mechanical failures** of metallic components are attributed to fatigue.
- Bridges, aircraft wings, engine crankshafts, turbine blades, and railway axles all fail primarily by fatigue.
- Fatigue failure is **catastrophic and sudden** with no visible warning like necking in tensile failure.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for Students)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Paper Clip**
> Take a standard metal paper clip. It cannot be broken by pulling it straight (static tension) with bare hands. However, if you **bend it back and forth repeatedly** at the same point, it eventually snaps after 8–10 cycles. That snap point occurs at a stress *far below* the yield strength of the wire. **This is fatigue failure in action.** Each bend introduces a tiny, irreversible micro-crack. Over thousands of such bends, the cracks grow until the remaining cross-section cannot sustain the load, and the clip fractures suddenly.

## 1.5 Key Terminology (KTU Board Terms)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Cycle** | One complete repetition of a stress-strain pattern (e.g., tension → compression → tension) |
| **Cyclic Stress ($\sigma$)** | The alternating stress applied to the specimen |
| **Maximum Stress ($\sigma_{max}$)** | The highest algebraic value of stress in a cycle |
| **Minimum Stress ($\sigma_{min}$)** | The lowest algebraic value of stress in a cycle |
| **Mean Stress ($\sigma_m$)** | $\sigma_m = (\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min})/2$ |
| **Stress Amplitude ($\sigma_a$)** | $\sigma_a = (\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min})/2$ |
| **Stress Range ($\Delta\sigma$)** | $\Delta\sigma = \sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}$ |
| **Stress Ratio (R)** | $R = \sigma_{min} / \sigma_{max}$ |
| **Fatigue Life ($N_f$)** | Total number of cycles to failure |
| **Endurance Limit ($\sigma_e$ or $S_e$)** | The stress level below which a material can endure an **infinite** number of cycles (typically $10^6$ to $10^7$ cycles for steels) |
| **Fatigue Strength ($\sigma_N$)** | The stress level at which failure occurs after a **specified** number of cycles (used for non-ferrous metals like Al, Cu) |
| **Fatigue Limit ($\sigma_f$)** | Same as endurance limit for ferrous materials |
| **S-N Curve** | Plot of Stress amplitude (S) vs. Number of cycles to failure (N) on a semi-log scale |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU:**
> - **Steels (Ferrous)** → exhibit a true **endurance limit** (a "knee" in the S-N curve).
> - **Aluminum, Copper (Non-Ferrous)** → have **NO** true endurance limit; instead, a **fatigue strength at $10^7$ or $10^8$ cycles** is reported.

## 1.6 Types of Fatigue Loading

1. **Axial Fatigue** – Pure tension-compression cyclic loading.
2. **Rotating-Bending Fatigue** – A rotating specimen under a constant bending load (most common laboratory test).
3. **Plane-Bending Fatigue** – Reverse bending.
4. **Torsional Fatigue** – Cyclic twisting.
5. **Combined Fatigue** – Combined axial + bending + torsion.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Cyclic Stress Waveform

The most common laboratory test applies a **sinusoidal cyclic stress** of the form:

$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_m + \sigma_a \sin(\omega t)$$

Where:
- $\sigma_m$ = Mean stress (offset)
- $\sigma_a$ = Stress amplitude
- $\omega$ = Angular frequency (rad/s)

## 2.2 Fatigue Test Methodology (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Specimen Preparation:** A polished, standardized specimen (hourglass or cylindrical shape) is machined to avoid surface defects that act as stress concentrators.
2. **Mounting:** The specimen is mounted in the fatigue testing machine (e.g., R.R. Moore rotating-beam machine).
3. **Loading:** A known cyclic load (stress amplitude) is applied at a fixed mean stress (typically $R = -1$ for fully reversed loading).
4. **Rotation/Loading:** The machine runs the specimen at high RPM (typically 10,000 RPM for rotating beam) until either:
   - The specimen **fractures** → record $N_f$ (number of cycles to failure).
   - The specimen reaches $10^6$ or $10^7$ cycles without failure → "run-out" (survived).
5. **Repeat:** The test is repeated on 8–12 identical specimens at progressively decreasing stress amplitudes.
6. **Plot:** Plot $\sigma_a$ (y-axis) vs. $N_f$ (x-axis, log scale) → this is the **S-N curve**.

## 2.3 The S-N Curve (Wöhler Curve)

The **S-N curve** (also called the **Wöhler curve**, after August Wöhler, 1860s) is the most important graphical output of a fatigue test.

- **X-axis:** Number of cycles to failure, $N_f$ (log scale, base 10).
- **Y-axis:** Stress amplitude, $\sigma_a$ (linear scale, MPa or ksi).
- **Shape:** A continuously decreasing curve for non-ferrous metals; a curve that flattens (horizontal asymptote) at the **endurance limit** for steels.

### Key Regions of the S-N Curve

| Region | Cycle Range | Failure Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Low-Cycle Fatigue (LCF)** | $N < 10^3$ – $10^4$ | Plastic deformation dominates; strain-controlled |
| **High-Cycle Fatigue (HCF)** | $10^4 < N < 10^6$ | Elastic deformation; crack initiation phase dominates |
| **Very High-Cycle Fatigue (VHCF)** | $N > 10^7$ | Subsurface crack initiation; relevant for steels & Ti alloys |
| **Endurance Region** | $N > 10^6$ (steels) | No failure; below endurance limit |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table contains every equation you will need for KTU exam problems on Fatigue Tests.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variable Definitions | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Mean Stress** | $\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$ | $\sigma_{max}, \sigma_{min}$: max and min cyclic stresses | MPa |
| 2 | **Stress Amplitude** | $\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$ | Stress half-range | MPa |
| 3 | **Stress Range** | $\Delta\sigma = \sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min} = 2\sigma_a$ | Full cyclic range | MPa |
| 4 | **Stress Ratio** | $R = \dfrac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}}$ | $-1 \leq R \leq 1$ for fatigue; $R=-1$ = fully reversed | dimensionless |
| 5 | **Basquin's Equation** (HCF) | $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N_f)^b$ | $\sigma'_f$: fatigue strength coefficient, $b$: fatigue strength exponent (typically $-0.05$ to $-0.12$ for metals) | MPa |
| 6 | **Basquin's Log Form** | $\log \sigma_a = \log \sigma'_f + b \cdot \log(2N_f)$ | Linear on log-log axes | — |
| 7 | **Coffin-Manson Equation** (LCF) | $\dfrac{\Delta \varepsilon_p}{2} = \varepsilon'_f (2N_f)^c$ | $\Delta\varepsilon_p$: plastic strain range, $\varepsilon'_f$: fatigue ductility coefficient, $c$: fatigue ductility exponent (typically $-0.5$ to $-0.7$) | dimensionless |
| 8 | **Endurance Limit Ratio (Steels)** | $S_e \approx 0.5 \, S_{UTS}$ (for $S_{UTS} < 1400$ MPa) | Empirical rule for steels | dimensionless |
| 9 | **Modified Endurance Limit** (Marin factors) | $S_e = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e \cdot k_f \cdot S'_e$ | $k_i$: surface, size, loading, temperature, reliability, miscellaneous factors | MPa |
| 10 | **Endurance Limit for Al alloys** | $S_e \approx 0.4 \, S_{UTS}$ | Empirical rule for wrought aluminum | dimensionless |
| 11 | **Number of cycles to failure** (rearranged Basquin) | $N_f = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma'_f}\right)^{1/b}$ | Solving Basquin for $N_f$ | cycles |
| 12 | **Goodman Line** (mean stress correction) | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{UTS}} = 1$ | Modified allowable stress amplitude | — |
| 13 | **Soderberg Line** (conservative) | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_y} = 1$ | Using yield strength instead of UTS | — |
| 14 | **Gerber Line** (parabolic) | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \left(\dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{UTS}}\right)^2 = 1$ | More accurate for ductile materials | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Ban:** Always use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in any table cell containing absolute value notation (e.g., $\vert \sigma_{max} \vert$) to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Never use the raw `|` symbol inside table cells.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Aerospace:** Aircraft wings and engine turbine blades undergo ~10$^8$ cycles per flight hour. Endurance limit design is critical.
- **Automotive:** Crankshafts, connecting rods, and coil springs are designed for $10^7$ cycles minimum life.
- **Civil Engineering:** Bridge girders experience cyclic loading from vehicles and wind. Fatigue damage accumulates.
- **Marine:** Ship hulls and offshore platforms experience wave-induced cyclic stress → fatigue cracking is a major failure mode.
- **Biomedical:** Orthopedic implants (hip stems) must survive 10$^7$ – 10$^8$ gait cycles without fatigue failure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Basquin's Equation from S-N Curve Data

**Basquin's Equation** is the empirical power-law relationship between stress amplitude and cycles to failure in the high-cycle fatigue (HCF) regime.

### Starting Point (Empirical Observation)

From experimental S-N curve data (on log-log axes), the relationship between $\sigma_a$ and $N_f$ is **linear**. This suggests a power-law form:

$$\sigma_a = A \cdot (N_f)^B$$

Where $A$ and $B$ are material constants.

### Step 1: Convert to Standard Form

Engineering convention writes this in terms of **reversals to failure** ($2N_f$), where one cycle = two reversals:

$$\sigma_a = \sigma'_f \cdot (2N_f)^b$$

Where:
- $\sigma'_f$ = **fatigue strength coefficient** (≈ true fracture strength, $\sigma_f$)
- $b$ = **fatigue strength exponent** (negative; typically $-0.05$ to $-0.12$ for most metals)
- $2N_f$ = number of reversals to failure

### Step 2: Take Logarithm of Both Sides

$$\log(\sigma_a) = \log(\sigma'_f) + b \cdot \log(2N_f)$$

This is the equation of a **straight line** with:
- Slope = $b$
- Y-intercept = $\log(\sigma'_f)$

### Step 3: Derive Slope and Intercept from Two Data Points

Given two experimental points:
- Point 1: $(N_1, \sigma_1)$
- Point 2: $(N_2, \sigma_2)$

The slope is:

$$b = \frac{\log(\sigma_2) - \log(\sigma_1)}{\log(2N_2) - \log(2N_1)} = \frac{\log(\sigma_2/\sigma_1)}{\log(N_2/N_1)}$$

The intercept is:

$$\log(\sigma'_f) = \log(\sigma_1) - b \cdot \log(2N_1)$$

Therefore:

$$\sigma'_f = \frac{\sigma_1}{(2N_1)^b}$$

### Step 4: Solve for Number of Cycles to Failure

Rearranging Basquin's equation:

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma'_f} = (2N_f)^b$$

$$2N_f = \left(\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma'_f}\right)^{1/b}$$

$$\boxed{N_f = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma'_f}\right)^{1/b}}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

### **Problem Statement:**

A steel specimen is tested in a rotating-beam fatigue test. At a stress amplitude of **$480$ MPa**, the specimen failed at **$N_1 = 10^4$ cycles**. At a stress amplitude of **$380$ MPa**, the specimen failed at **$N_2 = 10^6$ cycles**.

**(a)** Determine the fatigue strength exponent $b$ and the fatigue strength coefficient $\sigma'_f$ using Basquin's equation.

**(b)** Predict the fatigue life at a stress amplitude of $\sigma_a = 320$ MPa.

**(c)** Estimate the endurance limit of this steel.

---

### **Solution:**

#### Part (a): Finding $b$ and $\sigma'_f$

**Step 1:** Apply Basquin's equation at the two data points:

$$\sigma_1 = \sigma'_f (2N_1)^b = \sigma'_f (2 \times 10^4)^b = \sigma'_f (2 \times 10^4)^b$$

$$\sigma_2 = \sigma'_f (2N_2)^b = \sigma'_f (2 \times 10^6)^b = \sigma'_f (2 \times 10^6)^b$$

**Step 2:** Take the ratio to eliminate $\sigma'_f$:

$$\frac{\sigma_1}{\sigma_2} = \frac{(2N_1)^b}{(2N_2)^b} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^b$$

**Step 3:** Substitute numerical values:

$$\frac{480}{380} = \left(\frac{10^4}{10^6}\right)^b$$

$$1.2632 = (10^{-2})^b = 10^{-2b}$$

**Step 4:** Take $\log_{10}$ of both sides:

$$\log(1.2632) = -2b$$

$$0.1015 = -2b$$

$$\boxed{b = -0.0507}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $\sigma'_f$ using Point 1:

$$\sigma'_f = \frac{\sigma_1}{(2N_1)^b} = \frac{480}{(2 \times 10^4)^{-0.0507}}$$

$$(2 \times 10^4)^{-0.0507} = e^{-0.0507 \cdot \ln(20000)} = e^{-0.0507 \cdot 9.9035} = e^{-0.5021} = 0.6053$$

$$\sigma'_f = \frac{480}{0.6053} = 793.0 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma'_f \approx 793 \text{ MPa}}$$

---

#### Part (b): Fatigue Life at $\sigma_a = 320$ MPa

**Step 1:** Apply Basquin's equation:

$$320 = 793 \cdot (2N_f)^{-0.0507}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $(2N_f)$:

$$(2N_f)^{-0.0507} = \frac{320}{793} = 0.4035$$

**Step 3:** Take natural log of both sides:

$$-0.0507 \cdot \ln(2N_f) = \ln(0.4035) = -0.9073$$

$$\ln(2N_f) = \frac{-0.9073}{-0.0507} = 17.895$$

**Step 4:** Exponentiate:

$$2N_f = e^{17.895} = 5.92 \times 10^7$$

$$\boxed{N_f \approx 2.96 \times 10^7 \text{ cycles}}$$

---

#### Part (c): Estimating Endurance Limit

For steels, the endurance limit is conventionally defined at $N = 10^6$ cycles (sometimes $10^7$). Using Basquin's equation with $N = 10^6$:

$$\sigma_e = \sigma'_f (2 \times 10^6)^{-0.0507}$$

$$(2 \times 10^6)^{-0.0507} = e^{-0.0507 \cdot \ln(2 \times 10^6)} = e^{-0.0507 \cdot 14.508} = e^{-0.7356} = 0.4793$$

$$\sigma_e = 793 \times 0.4793 = 380 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_e \approx 380 \text{ MPa}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-check:** Notice that the second data point (380 MPa, $10^6$ cycles) **coincides with the endurance limit** — this is a hallmark of properly executed fatigue tests on steels. The S-N curve has effectively "leveled off" at this stress.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Mean Stress Correction Criteria

For a given stress amplitude $\sigma_a$ and mean stress $\sigma_m$, the **allowable stress combinations** are defined by different criteria. KTU often asks for a sketch or comparison.

| Criterion | Equation | When to Use | Conservatism |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Soderberg** | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_y} = 1$ | Conservative, ductile metals | Most conservative |
| **Goodman** | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{UTS}} = 1$ | General-purpose, brittle | Moderate |
| **Gerber** | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \left(\dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{UTS}}\right)^2 = 1$ | Ductile metals, accurate | Least conservative |

Where $S_y$ = yield strength, $S_{UTS}$ = ultimate tensile strength, $S_e$ = endurance limit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU:** When asked *"which criterion is most conservative?"* → **Soderberg** is the correct answer. When asked *"which is most accurate for ductile materials?"* → **Gerber**.

---

## 3.4 End-of-Section Theoretical Summary Table

| Concept | Master Formula | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Stress amplitude | $\sigma_a = (\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min})/2$ | Half of cyclic stress range |
| Mean stress | $\sigma_m = (\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min})/2$ | Steady-state offset |
| Basquin's law | $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N_f)^b$ | HCF behavior of metals |
| Coffin-Manson | $\Delta\varepsilon_p/2 = \varepsilon'_f (2N_f)^c$ | LCF behavior of metals |
| Endurance ratio (steel) | $S_e \approx 0.5 \, S_{UTS}$ | Empirical design rule |
| Goodman line | $\sigma_a/S_e + \sigma_m/S_{UTS} = 1$ | Mean stress design envelope |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Fatigue Test Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Prepare Polished Specimen] --> B[Mount in Fatigue Testing Machine]
    B --> C[Set Stress Amplitude sigma_a and R-ratio]
    C --> D[Apply Cyclic Load at Constant Frequency]
    D --> E{Monitor for Failure}
    E -->|Fracture Detected| F[Record Number of Cycles N_f]
    E -->|Run-out at 10^7 cycles| G[Mark as Survived: Run-out]
    F --> H[Log Data Point: sigma_a vs N_f]
    G --> H
    H --> I{More Stress Levels to Test?}
    I -->|Yes, decrease sigma_a| C
    I -->|No, 8 to 12 points done| J[Plot S-N Curve]
    J --> K[Determine Endurance Limit or Fatigue Strength]
    K --> L[End: Report Fatigue Properties]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: S-N Curve (Wöhler Curve) Conceptual Plot

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["S-N CURVE FOR STEEL"] --> B["High Stress Region: Low Cycle Fatigue N less than 10^4"]
    A --> C["Intermediate Region: High Cycle Fatigue N between 10^4 and 10^6"]
    A --> D["Knee Point at 10^6 cycles"]
    A --> E["Endurance Limit Region: Horizontal Line Below S_e"]
    B --> F["Steep Slope: Plastic Strain Dominates"]
    C --> G["Gradual Slope: Elastic Strain Dominates"]
    D --> H["Transition: Endurance Limit sigma_e in MPa"]
    E --> I["No Failure Below sigma_e for Infinite Cycles"]
    F --> J["Basquin Linear Region on Log-Log Plot"]
    G --> J
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Rotating Beam Fatigue Testing Machine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Electric Motor Drive] --> B[Spindle Rotating at High RPM]
    B --> C[Specimen Mounted as Cantilever Beam]
    C --> D[Stationary Weight W Applied as Bending Load]
    D --> E[Rotating Specimen Experiences Fully Reversed Bending]
    E --> F["Each Point on Surface: sigma_max to sigma_min to sigma_max"]
    F --> G[Count Cycles to Failure via Counter]
    G --> H[Record Data: Stress Amplitude vs Cycles to Failure]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Diagram: Mean Stress Effect on Fatigue Life

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Applied Cyclic Stress] --> B[Decompose into sigma_a and sigma_m]
    B --> C{Is sigma_m Zero?}
    C -->|Yes: R = -1| D[Fully Reversed Loading]
    C -->|No: Tensile mean| E[Reduced Fatigue Life]
    C -->|No: Compressive mean| F[Extended Fatigue Life]
    D --> G[S-N Curve at R = -1 as Reference]
    E --> H[Goodman or Soderberg Correction Required]
    F --> H
    H --> I[Modified Allowable sigma_a from Mean Stress Diagram]
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Factors Affecting Fatigue Life

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fatigue Life N_f] --> B[Material Factors]

    A --> D[Loading Factors]

```

## 4.6 Typical S-N Curve (ASCII Representation)

```
   Stress Amplitude (MPa)
        |
   600 -|*  
        | *
   500 -|  * 
        |   *
   400 -|    *--------.--------  ← Endurance Limit σ_e (≈380 MPa)
        |              *-----
   300 -|                    *-------
        |                             *----------
   200 -|                                         *----------- 
        |                                                      *----
   100 -|                                                                
        |________________________________________________________________
        10^3     10^4     10^5     10^6     10^7     10^8    10^9
                              Number of Cycles to Failure (N_f)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the S-N curve:** As the stress amplitude decreases, the number of cycles to failure increases. For steel, the curve becomes horizontal (asymptotic) at the **endurance limit** $\sigma_e$. For aluminum, the curve continues to fall — there is no true endurance limit.
