## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]:** Differentiate between brittle and ductile fracture. List any four distinguishing features.

**Model Answer:**
Brittle fracture occurs with little or no plastic deformation, absorbs low energy, and propagates rapidly along cleavage planes. Ductile fracture involves significant plastic deformation, high energy absorption, and slow, stable crack growth.

| Feature | Brittle Fracture | Ductile Fracture |
|---------|------------------|------------------|
| Plastic deformation | Negligible | Significant |
| Energy absorbed | Low | High |
| Warning signs | Absent (sudden) | Present (necking) |
| Fracture surface | Cleavage facets, river marks | Dimpled, cup-and-cone |
| Crack speed | Very high (≈ speed of sound) | Slow, stable |
| Strain at fracture | $< 0.05$ | $\geq 0.05$ |
| Temperature effect | Promoted at low T | Promoted at high T |
| Notch sensitivity | High | Lower |

---

**Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]:** What is the endurance limit? Explain why it exists for steels but not for aluminum alloys.

**Model Answer:**
The **endurance limit** ($S_e$) is the maximum completely reversed stress amplitude that a material can withstand for an **infinite number of cycles** (typically taken as $N \geq 10^6$ for steels) without failure.

**Why it exists in steels:** Ferrous metals (carbon steels, alloy steels) undergo **strain-age hardening** during cyclic loading at room temperature. Dislocation pinning by interstitial carbon and nitrogen atoms creates a threshold stress below which the material effectively strengthens with each cycle, preventing fatigue damage accumulation. The S-N curve becomes horizontal.

**Why it does not exist in aluminum:** Non-ferrous metals like aluminum, copper, and magnesium **do not strain-age** significantly at room temperature. They continue to accumulate micro-damage with every cycle, no matter how small the stress. Their S-N curves continue to slope downward indefinitely on the log-log plot. For these materials, a **fatigue strength at a specified life** (e.g., $S_N$ at $N = 5 \times 10^8$) is used for design.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** State and explain the Griffith theory of brittle fracture. Derive the expression for critical fracture stress in terms of material properties and crack length. Mention the significance of the plane strain correction.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A glass plate has the following properties: Young's modulus $E = 70$ GPa, Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.25$, surface energy $\gamma_s = 0.85$ J/m². An internal crack of half-length $a = 0.5$ mm exists. Determine:
(i) The critical fracture stress under plane stress conditions.
(ii) The critical fracture stress under plane strain conditions.
(iii) The critical crack length if the plate is subjected to a service stress of 25 MPa.
(iv) Comment on the size of the critical crack compared to typical engineering tolerances.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (a):**

**Statement of Griffith Theory:**
Griffith proposed that a crack in a brittle material propagates when the **release of elastic strain energy** associated with crack extension is at least equal to the **increase in surface energy** required to create the new crack surfaces.

**Derivation:**

Consider a large plate of unit thickness containing a central through-thickness crack of length $2a$, subjected to a uniform remote tensile stress $\sigma$ under fixed-grip (constant displacement) conditions.

**Step 1:** The elastic strain energy stored in the plate without the crack is:

$$U_0 = \frac{\sigma^2 V}{2E} = \frac{\sigma^2 W L}{2E}$$

**Step 2:** When a crack of length $2a$ is introduced, the strain energy is relieved in the region around the crack. The released strain energy is:

$$\Delta U_0 = -\frac{\pi \sigma^2 a^2}{E} \quad \text{(plane stress)}$$

**Step 3:** The surface energy required to form the new crack surfaces is:

$$U_s = 2 \gamma_s (2a) = 4 a \gamma_s$$

**Step 4:** The total potential energy of the system is:

$$\Pi = \Delta U_0 + U_s = -\frac{\pi \sigma^2 a^2}{E} + 4 a \gamma_s$$

**Step 5:** At the critical condition for crack propagation:

$$\frac{d\Pi}{da} = 0 \implies -\frac{2 \pi \sigma^2 a}{E} + 4 \gamma_s = 0$$

**Step 6:** Solving for the critical fracture stress:

$$\boxed{\sigma_f = \sqrt{\frac{2 E \gamma_s}{\pi a}}}$$

**Significance of Plane Strain Correction:**
For **thick plates** (where lateral contraction is restrained), the stress state near the crack tip is triaxial. The strain energy release is reduced by a factor of $(1 - \nu^2)$, leading to:

$$\sigma_f = \sqrt{\frac{2 E \gamma_s}{\pi (1 - \nu^2) a}}$$

This correction is critical for **brittle materials in thick sections** (e.g., pressure vessels, large castings) and gives a **more conservative** (lower) estimate of $\sigma_f$ for thin plates, but is essential for accurate design of thick components.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (b):**

**Given:** $E = 70$ GPa $= 70 \times 10^9$ Pa, $\nu = 0.25$, $\gamma_s = 0.85$ J/m², $a = 0.5$ mm $= 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $\sigma_{service} = 25$ MPa $= 25 \times 10^6$ Pa.

**(i) Plane stress critical fracture stress:**

$$\sigma_f = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 70 \times 10^9 \times 0.85}{\pi \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}}$$

$$\sigma_f = \sqrt{\frac{119 \times 10^9}{1.5708 \times 10^{-3}}}$$

$$\sigma_f = \sqrt{7.576 \times 10^{13}}$$

$$\sigma_f = 8.704 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 8.70 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) Plane strain critical fracture stress:**

$$1 - \nu^2 = 1 - 0.0625 = 0.9375$$

$$\sigma_f = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 70 \times 10^9 \times 0.85}{\pi \times 0.9375 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}}$$

$$\sigma_f = \sqrt{\frac{119 \times 10^9}{1.4727 \times 10^{-3}}}$$

$$\sigma_f = \sqrt{8.080 \times 10^{13}}$$

$$\sigma_f = 8.989 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 8.99 \text{ MPa}$$

**(iii) Critical crack length at $\sigma = 25$ MPa:**

$$a_c = \frac{2 E \gamma_s}{\pi \sigma^2} = \frac{2 \times 70 \times 10^9 \times 0.85}{\pi \times (25 \times 10^6)^2}$$

$$a_c = \frac{119 \times 10^9}{\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{14}} = \frac{119 \times 10^9}{1.9635 \times 10^{15}}$$

$$a_c = 6.06 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 60.6 \text{ µm}$$

**(iv) Comment:**
A half-crack length of **60.6 µm** (i.e., full crack of about **121 µm**) is **invisible to the naked eye** (which resolves ~100 µm) and well within typical machining tolerances. This explains why **non-destructive testing (NDT)** — ultrasonic, dye penetrant, eddy current — is mandatory for critical components made of brittle materials. A single surface scratch from improper handling can reduce the strength of glass by an order of magnitude.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout (Question A):**
> 1. **Unit Mistakes:** Marks are deducted if $E$ is left in GPa without conversion to Pa, or $a$ is left in mm. **Always convert to SI units (Pa, m, J/m²) before substituting.**
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in surface energy:** A crack has **TWO surfaces** — students often write $U_s = 2 a \gamma_s$ instead of $4 a \gamma_s$, losing 1 mark.
> 3. **Plane stress vs plane strain:** Use the correct form based on the problem context. If the problem specifies "thick plate" or "plane strain", use the $(1-\nu^2)$ form.
> 4. **Griffith only applies to ideally brittle materials** (glass, ceramics). For metals, use the **Orowan modification** with $\gamma_p$.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the S-N curve in detail. Discuss the significance of the endurance limit and the factors affecting it. List the various Marin factors used to calculate the modified endurance limit.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A steel shaft is subjected to a fluctuating bending stress that varies from $-100$ MPa to $+200$ MPa. The corrected endurance limit for the material is $S_e = 280$ MPa, ultimate tensile strength $S_{ut} = 600$ MPa, and yield strength $S_y = 400$ MPa. Calculate:
(i) The mean stress, stress amplitude, and stress ratio.
(ii) The factor of safety using the Goodman and Gerber criteria.
(iii) The factor of safety using the Soderberg criterion.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (a):**

**The S-N Curve:**
The S-N curve (Wöhler curve) is a plot of cyclic stress amplitude ($S$ or $\sigma_a$) versus the number of cycles to failure ($N_f$) on a **semi-logarithmic scale**. The vertical axis is linear (stress), and the horizontal axis is logarithmic (cycles). It is determined experimentally using rotating-beam, axial, or torsion fatigue testing machines on standardized specimens.

**Significance of Endurance Limit:**
For **ferrous metals (steels)**, the S-N curve becomes **horizontal** below a certain stress, called the **endurance limit** ($S_e$ or $\sigma_e$). Below this threshold, the material can theoretically endure an **infinite number of cycles** without failure. This is attributed to:
- **Strain aging** (pinning of dislocations by C and N interstitials)
- **Threshold stress for dislocation motion**
- A natural lower bound of crack propagation in the Paris regime

For **non-ferrous metals** (Al, Cu, Mg alloys), the S-N curve continues to descend and no true endurance limit exists. Designers use **fatigue strength at a specified life** (e.g., $S_{5 \times 10^8}$).

**Marin Factors (Modified Endurance Limit):**

$$S_e = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e \cdot k_f \cdot S'_e$$

| Factor | Symbol | Accounts for | Typical Range |
|--------|--------|--------------|----------------|
| Surface factor | $k_a$ | Surface finish (polished, ground, machined, forged) | 0.6 to 1.0 |
| Size factor | $k_b$ | Component size (larger parts have lower $S_e$) | 0.6 to 1.0 |
| Load factor | $k_c$ | Loading mode (bending, axial, torsion) | 0.59 to 1.0 |
| Temperature factor | $k_d$ | Operating temperature | 0.4 to 1.0 |
| Reliability factor | $k_e$ | Statistical reliability (50%, 90%, 99%, 99.9%) | 0.75 to 1.0 |
| Miscellaneous factor | $k_f$ | Residual stresses, corrosion, plating, etc. | 0.5 to 1.0 |

The uncorrected $S'_e$ is the experimentally measured value under standard conditions: rotating-beam specimen of $d = 7.62$ mm, polished, room temperature, 50% reliability.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (b):**

**Given:** $\sigma_{\max} = +200$ MPa, $\sigma_{\min} = -100$ MPa, $S_e = 280$ MPa, $S_{ut} = 600$ MPa, $S_y = 400$ MPa.

**(i) Mean stress, stress amplitude, stress ratio:**

$$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{\max} + \sigma_{\min}}{2} = \frac{200 + (-100)}{2} = 50 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{\max} - \sigma_{\min}}{2} = \frac{200 - (-100)}{2} = 150 \text{ MPa}$$

$$R = \frac{\sigma_{\min}}{\sigma_{\max}} = \frac{-100}{200} = -0.5$$

**Note:** The stress is **partially reversed** with $R = -0.5$ (mean stress is positive — tensile).

**(ii) Factor of Safety — Goodman Criterion:**

Goodman line equation:

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{N}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{150}{280 \cdot N} + \frac{50}{600 \cdot N} = \frac{1}{N}$$

Wait — re-arranging: $\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{N}$

$$\frac{1}{N} = \frac{150}{280} + \frac{50}{600}$$

$$\frac{1}{N} = 0.5357 + 0.0833 = 0.6190$$

$$N = \frac{1}{0.6190} = 1.615$$

**Factor of Safety (Goodman) = 1.62**

**(iii) Factor of Safety — Gerber Criterion:**

$$\frac{N \sigma_a}{S_e} + \left( \frac{N \sigma_m}{S_{ut}} \right)^2 = 1$$

Let $x = N \sigma_a / S_e$ and $y = N \sigma_m / S_{ut}$:

$$x + y^2 = 1$$

We need to find $N$. Substituting $\sigma_a = 150$ MPa, $\sigma_m = 50$ MPa:

$$\frac{150 N}{280} + \left( \frac{50 N}{600} \right)^2 = 1$$

$$0.5357 N + (0.0833 N)^2 = 1$$

$$0.5357 N + 0.006944 N^2 = 1$$

$$0.006944 N^2 + 0.5357 N - 1 = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula:

$$N = \frac{-0.5357 \pm \sqrt{(0.5357)^2 + 4 \cdot 0.006944}}{2 \cdot 0.006944}$$

$$N = \frac{-0.5357 \pm \sqrt{0.2870 + 0.02778}}{0.01389}$$

$$N = \frac{-0.5357 \pm \sqrt{0.3148}}{0.01389}$$

$$N = \frac{-0.5357 \pm 0.5611}{0.01389}$$

Taking the positive root:

$$N = \frac{0.0254}{0.01389} = 1.828$$

**Factor of Safety (Gerber) = 1.83**

**(iv) Soderberg Criterion (using yield strength $S_y$):**

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_y} = \frac{1}{N}$$

$$\frac{1}{N} = \frac{150}{280} + \frac{50}{400}$$

$$\frac{1}{N} = 0.5357 + 0.1250 = 0.6607$$

$$N = \frac{1}{0.6607} = 1.514$$

**Factor of Safety (Soderberg) = 1.51**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout (Question B):**
> 1. **Stress mean vs amplitude confusion:** $\sigma_m$ is the **average** of max and min, NOT the difference. $\sigma_a$ is **half** the range, NOT the full range. Getting this wrong loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Choosing the right failure criterion:** The problem statement will dictate. **Goodman** is most common in KTU exams. **Gerber** is more accurate (parabolic) but more calculation-intensive. **Soderberg** is most conservative (uses yield, not UTS).
> 3. **Stress ratio R:** Students forget to compute it; examiners often explicitly ask for it.
> 4. **Units consistency:** Keep all stresses in MPa throughout.
> 5. **Partial reversal:** $R = -0.5$ is *not* fully reversed ($R = -1$). Fully reversed loading is a common special case the examiner may use as a distractor.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

**S-N Curve:**
- ☐ Plot is **semi-log**: linear stress, log cycles.
- ☐ **Endurance limit** $S_e$ exists for steels, **not** for aluminum/copper.
- ☐ **Basquin's law**: $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N_f)^b$, valid for HCF ($N > 10^3$).
- ☐ **Coffin-Manson**: for LCF ($N < 10^3$), plastic strain dominates.
- ☐ **Marin's equation** for modified $S_e$ has 6 factors: $k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f$.
- ☐ $k_b$ for size effect: smaller components have higher $S_e$ for the same material.
- ☐ $k_c$ values to remember: bending = 1.0, axial = 0.85, torsion = 0.59.

**Brittle vs. Ductile Fracture:**
- ☐ **Ductile**: significant plastic deformation, dimpled surface, cup-and-cone, slow, warning present.
- ☐ **Brittle**: little/no plastic deformation, cleavage facets, river patterns, fast, sudden.
- ☐ **DBTT** (Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature) is critical for BCC metals (steels).
- ☐ Charpy impact test determines DBTT.
- ☐ Factors promoting **brittle** fracture: low T, high strain rate, thick sections, triaxial stress, sharp notches.

**Griffith Theory:**
- ☐ Core equation: $\sigma_f = \sqrt{2E\gamma_s/(\pi a)}$ (plane stress).
- ☐ Plane strain form includes $(1 - \nu^2)$ in the denominator.
- ☐ **Orowan modification** adds $\gamma_p$ (plastic work) for metals: $\sigma_f = \sqrt{2E(\gamma_s + \gamma_p)/(\pi a)}$.
- ☐ $\gamma_p$ is typically **1000× to 10,000×** larger than $\gamma_s$ in metals.
- ☐ Griffith is for **ideally brittle** materials (glass, ceramics, ice).
- ☐ Derivation: equate strain energy release to surface energy creation, $d\Pi/da = 0$.

**Stress Concentration:**
- ☐ $K_t = \sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\text{nom}}$.
- ☐ **Circular hole**: $K_t = 3$.
- ☐ **Elliptical hole**: $K_t = 1 + 2a/b$ (Inglis).
- ☐ **Sharp crack** ($\rho \to 0$): $K_t \to \infty$ (theoretical only).
- ☐ **Fatigue notch factor**: $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$, where $q$ = Neuber's notch sensitivity.
- ☐ For ductile materials, $K_t$ is partially "relieved" by local yielding — $K_f < K_t$.
- ☐ For brittle materials, $K_f \approx K_t$ (full stress concentration applies).
- ☐ **Reduce $K_t$** by using: larger fillet radii, smooth transitions, removing sharp corners, using reinforcement.

**Fracture Mechanics Link (KTU 2024 integrated concept):**
- ☐ $K = Y\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$ (stress intensity factor).
- ☐ $K \geq K_{IC}$ is the fracture criterion.
- ☐ $K_{IC}$ is **plane strain fracture toughness** — material property (MPa·m$^{1/2}$).
- ☐ **Critical crack length**: $a_c = (1/\pi)(K_{IC}/(Y\sigma))^2$.

**Numerical Problem Patterns to Expect:**
- ☐ Calculate $\sigma_f$ given $E$, $\gamma_s$, $a$ (Griffith).
- ☐ Calculate $a_c$ given $\sigma$, $E$, $\gamma_s$ (inverse Griffith).
- ☐ Calculate $K_t$ for various geometries.
- ☐ Calculate $S_e$ using Marin's factors.
- ☐ Calculate FoS using Goodman, Gerber, or Soderberg with given $\sigma_{\max}$ and $\sigma_{\min}$.

**Common Exam Traps:**
- ☐ Mixing up **plane stress** (thin sections) and **plane strain** (thick sections) Griffith forms.
- ☐ Forgetting to convert $a$ to meters in SI substitutions.
- ☐ Confusing $\sigma_m$ with $\sigma_a$ in fatigue mean-stress problems.
- ☐ Using $S_{ut}$ instead of $S_e$ in Marin equation.
- ☐ Misapplying endurance limit to non-ferrous metals in numerical problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy:** The Module 3 weightage in the university exam is typically **15–20%** of total marks. The S-N curve + Griffith + stress concentration trio forms the conceptual backbone. Focus numerical practice on (1) Griffith problems, (2) Marin's factors, and (3) failure-criterion FoS calculations. These three patterns account for nearly all 14-mark problems in this module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stress Raiser - Effect of Plastic Deformation on Crack Propagation - Transgranular
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stress Raiser - Effect of Plastic Deformation on Crack Propagation - Transgranular

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Stress Raiser (Stress Concentration)

A **stress raiser** (also called a **stress concentrator** or **notch**) is any microscopic or macroscopic geometric discontinuity in a component — such as a hole, keyway, sharp corner, machining scratch, change in cross-section, or an internal defect — that disrupts the otherwise uniform distribution of stress lines and causes a **localized multiplication of stress** far above the nominal applied stress.

Formally, the **Theoretical Stress Concentration Factor ($K_t$)** is defined as:

$$K_t = \frac{\sigma_{\max}}{\sigma_{\text{nominal}}}$$

where $\sigma_{\max}$ is the peak stress at the discontinuity and $\sigma_{\text{nominal}}$ is the average stress calculated on the net or gross section.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Stress raisers are the **origin points for crack initiation** in fatigue, brittle fracture, and stress-corrosion cracking. Even a perfectly ductile material can fail in a brittle manner at a sufficiently sharp stress raiser.

### Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a **crowd of people walking through a wide corridor** (low stress) versus the **same crowd being funnelled through a narrow doorway** (high stress). The crowd velocity (analogous to stress) increases dramatically at the bottleneck. The doorway is the *stress raiser*. Similarly:
- A **sheet of paper with a small hole** always tears starting at the hole.
- A **glass rod with a single scratch** will snap from the scratch under bending.
- A **metal plate with a drilled hole** has $K_t = 3$, meaning the local stress is 3× the nominal value, even before any crack forms.

---

## 1.2 Effect of Plastic Deformation on Crack Propagation

When a sharp crack exists in a **ductile material**, the high stress at the crack tip is relieved by **localized plastic deformation (yielding)**. This plastic zone has two critical effects:

1. **Crack Tip Blunting** – The originally atomically sharp tip becomes rounded, lowering the effective $K_t$.
2. **Energy Dissipation** – A large amount of work is consumed in plastic flow, raising the material's apparent fracture toughness ($K_{IC}$).

The size of the plastic zone relative to crack length and specimen dimensions determines whether the material is in a state of **plane stress** (thin sections, large plastic zone) or **plane strain** (thick sections, small plastic zone). Plane strain is the **worst case** because it suppresses plasticity and embrittles the material.

> [!NOTE]
> **LEFM Assumption (Linear Elastic Fracture Mechanics):** LEFM is strictly valid only when the plastic zone is *small* compared to crack length and other dimensions — the so-called **Small Scale Yielding (SSY)** condition. When the plastic zone becomes large, **Elastic-Plastic Fracture Mechanics (EPFM)** using the *J-integral* or *Crack Tip Opening Displacement (CTOD)* is required.

### Intuitive Analogy

Think of trying to tear a **ductile polythene sheet** versus a **sheet of glass**. The polythene stretches and tears slowly (energy absorbed by plastic deformation), whereas the glass shatters instantly with almost no deformation. The plastic zone at the crack tip in the polythene is the reason it is tougher.

---

## 1.3 Transgranular Fracture (Crack Propagation Across Grains)

**Transgranular fracture** is a mode of brittle fracture in which the crack propagates **through the interiors of the grains**, along specific low-index crystallographic planes called **cleavage planes**, rather than along the grain boundaries.

- In **BCC metals** (e.g., $\alpha$-iron, Cr, Mo) → cleavage occurs on **{100} planes**.
- In **HCP metals** (e.g., Zn, Mg, Be) → cleavage occurs on the **basal plane (0001)**.
- In **FCC metals** → true cleavage rarely occurs because of abundant slip systems; failure is normally ductile (microvoid coalescence).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Characteristic Fractographic Features of Transgranular Cleavage:**
> - **Cleavage facets** – flat, shiny, crystallographic surfaces.
> - **River patterns** – lines formed when a crack crosses cleavage steps, converging in the direction of crack propagation.
> - **Tongues and Wallner lines** – secondary features used in fractographic analysis.
>
> The presence of river patterns is **positive evidence of transgranular cleavage fracture**.

### Intuitive Analogy

Imagine walking across a tiled floor (grains). **Transgranular fracture** is like cracking a tile *through its body* (you break the tile itself). **Intergranular fracture** (for comparison) is like the tile popping out whole because the *grout between tiles* (grain boundary) failed first. Transgranular = crack goes *across* the grain; intergranular = crack goes *between* grains.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stress Concentration — Theoretical Framework

### Inglis Solution (1913) — Elliptical Hole in an Infinite Plate

Consider an infinite plate of uniform thickness containing an **elliptical hole** of semi-major axis $a$ (parallel to the load) and semi-minor axis $b$ (perpendicular to the load tip), subjected to a uniform far-field tensile stress $\sigma$. Inglis solved this elastic problem and showed that the **maximum stress at the tip of the ellipse is**:

$$\sigma_{\max} = \sigma \left(1 + \frac{2a}{b}\right)$$

Equivalently, in terms of the radius of curvature $\rho$ at the tip of the ellipse ($\rho = b^2/a$):

$$\sigma_{\max} = \sigma \left(1 + 2\sqrt{\frac{a}{\rho}}\right)$$

Therefore the **theoretical stress concentration factor** is:

$$K_t = 1 + \frac{2a}{b} = 1 + 2\sqrt{\frac{a}{\rho}}$$

### Special Cases and Physical Limits

- **Circular hole** ($a = b = r$): $K_t = 3$. This is a textbook value for a hole in a wide plate.
- **Sharp crack** ($b \to 0$ or $\rho \to 0$): $K_t \to \infty$. Elastic theory predicts infinite stress, which is non-physical — **plasticity intervenes** to bound the maximum stress.
- **Smooth plate** ($a \to 0$ or $b \to \infty$): $K_t \to 1$. No concentration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world geometries rarely follow the ideal $K_t = 1 + 2a/b$.** Engineers use **Peterson's Stress Concentration Charts** (compiled by Walter Pilkey) to obtain realistic $K_t$ values for fillets, stepped shafts, keyways, and holes. The effective (notch-weakened) stress concentration factor for fatigue is $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$, where $q$ is the **notch sensitivity** (0 to 1).

---

## 2.2 Plastic Deformation at the Crack Tip

### Modes of Crack Loading

Fracture mechanics classifies loading into three fundamental modes:

| Mode | Type of Loading | Relative Displacement of Crack Faces | Practical Occurrence |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Mode I** | Opening (Tensile) | Normal separation | Most common in engineering |
| **Mode II** | Sliding (In-plane shear) | Shear in the plane of the crack | Rolling contact, mixed-mode |
| **Mode III** | Treading (Out-of-plane shear) | Shear out of the plane | Shafts, torsion |

Most engineering design against fracture uses **Mode I (Opening)** as the worst case.

### Stress Intensity Factor and Fracture Criterion

The elastic stress field near a sharp crack tip is uniquely characterized by the **stress intensity factor (SIF)** $K$:

$$K_I = Y \, \sigma \, \sqrt{\pi a}$$

where $Y$ is a dimensionless **geometry factor** (typically 0.8 – 1.2 for common geometries), $\sigma$ is the applied stress, and $a$ is the crack length.

**Fracture Criterion (K-Criterion):**

$$K_I \geq K_{IC} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{Fracture occurs}$$

where $K_{IC}$ is the **plane-strain fracture toughness** (units: MPa$\sqrt{\text{m}}$ or ksi$\sqrt{\text{in}}$).

### Plastic Zone Size — Irwin's Estimate

The **maximum radius of the plastic zone** at the crack tip, when the elastic stress $\sigma_{yy} = \sigma_y$ (yield stress) at the elastic–plastic boundary, is:

**For Plane Stress** (thin sections):

$$r_p = \frac{1}{2\pi} \left(\frac{K_I}{\sigma_y}\right)^2$$

**For Plane Strain** (thick sections):

$$r_p = \frac{1}{6\pi} \left(\frac{K_I}{\sigma_y}\right)^2$$

The factor-of-three difference reflects the **triaxial constraint** in plane strain, which suppresses yielding and produces a smaller, more localized plastic zone.

**Small Scale Yielding (SSY) validity condition:**

$$r_p \ll a, \, B, \, (W - a)$$

where $B$ is specimen thickness and $W$ is specimen width.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the plastic zone affects crack propagation:**
> 1. Larger plastic zone → more energy absorbed → higher apparent $K_{IC}$ (toughness).
> 2. Crack tip blunting reduces the effective sharpness, lowering the local stress.
> 3. In thick sections (plane strain), the plastic zone is small → lower toughness → **embrittlement**.
> 4. In thin sections (plane stress), the plastic zone is large → higher toughness → **ductility**.

---

## 2.3 Transgranular Fracture — Mechanistic Theory

Transgranular cleavage occurs when the **tensile stress normal to a cleavage plane** exceeds a critical value $\sigma_c$ (a property dependent on temperature and strain rate). The Griffith-type criterion for cleavage is:

$$\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{2 E \gamma_s}{\pi a}}$$

where $E$ is Young's modulus, $\gamma_s$ is the surface energy of the new cleavage surfaces, and $a$ is the crack (or flaw) length. The factor of 2 accounts for the two surfaces created.

Conditions favouring transgranular cleavage:
- **Low temperature** (below the ductile-to-brittle transition temperature, DBTT, for BCC).
- **High strain rate** (impact loading).
- **Triaxial stress state** (notch effects, thick sections).
- **Large grain size** (less grain-boundary obstruction).
- **Pristine, low-dislocation microstructure** (untempered martensite, for example).
- **Embrittling species** at cleavage planes (H in steel, for example).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Variables / Units | Validity / Limitation |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | $K_t = \sigma_{\max} / \sigma_{\text{nom}}$ | Definition of SCF | dimensionless | Definition only |
| 2 | $K_t = 1 + 2a/b$ | Inglis elliptical hole | $a,b$ = semi-axes (m) | Infinite plate, elastic |
| 3 | $K_t = 1 + 2\sqrt{a/\rho}$ | Inglis in terms of tip radius | $\rho$ = tip radius (m) | As above |
| 4 | $K_I = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Mode-I SIF | $Y$ = geom. factor; $a$ (m); $\sigma$ (Pa) | LEFM, SSY |
| 5 | $K_I \geq K_{IC}$ | Fracture criterion | $K_{IC}$ (Pa$\sqrt{\text{m}}$) | Plane strain, static |
| 6 | $r_p = (1/2\pi)(K_I/\sigma_y)^2$ | Plastic zone (plane stress) | $r_p$ (m); $\sigma_y$ (Pa) | SSY |
| 7 | $r_p = (1/6\pi)(K_I/\sigma_y)^2$ | Plastic zone (plane strain) | $r_p$ (m); $\sigma_y$ (Pa) | SSY |
| 8 | $\sigma_c = \sqrt{2 E \gamma_s / (\pi a)}$ | Griffith cleavage stress | $E$ (Pa); $\gamma_s$ (J/m²); $a$ (m) | Brittle, sharp flaw |
| 9 | $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$ | Fatigue notch factor | $q$ = notch sensitivity | $0 \leq q \leq 1$ |
| 10 | $B \geq 2.5 (K_{IC}/\sigma_y)^2$ | Min. thickness for plane strain | $B$ (m) | ASTM E399 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** These equations form the backbone of **damage-tolerant design** in the aerospace, pressure-vessel, and nuclear industries. The $K_{IC}$ value is a materials property used like yield strength, and design codes (ASME, FAA) require $K_I \leq K_{IC}/n$ where $n$ is a safety factor (typically 2 to 4).

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Inglis Stress Concentration for an Elliptical Hole

### Setup

Consider an **infinite elastic plate** of uniform thickness subjected to a uniform uniaxial tensile stress $\sigma$ in the $x$-direction. The plate contains a central elliptical hole of semi-axes $a$ (along $x$, parallel to load) and $b$ (along $y$, perpendicular to load). We use the **theory of elasticity with Airy's stress function**.

### Step 1: Choose a Stress Function

Inglis proposed the following Airy's stress function (in polar coordinates $r, \theta$ with origin at the centre of the ellipse):

$$\phi = \frac{\sigma}{4}(a+b)^2 \left[ \frac{r^2 \cos 2\theta}{r^2 - (a-b)^2 \cos^2\theta - (a+b)^2 \sin^2\theta} \right]$$

A simpler equivalent form, expressed in **elliptic coordinates** ($\xi, \eta$) where the ellipse is the coordinate curve $\xi = \xi_0 = \cosh^{-1}(a/b)$:

$$\phi = \frac{\sigma \, b^2}{2} \frac{\sinh 2\xi - \cosh 2\xi \cos 2\eta}{\cosh 2\xi - \cos 2\eta}$$

### Step 2: Apply the Boundary Conditions

On the ellipse surface ($\xi = \xi_0$), the normal and shear tractions must vanish. This is automatically satisfied by Inglis's choice of $\phi$.

### Step 3: Evaluate Stresses at the Tip of the Ellipse

Using the stress–function relations $\sigma_x = \partial^2 \phi / \partial y^2$, $\sigma_y = \partial^2 \phi / \partial x^2$, $\tau_{xy} = -\partial^2 \phi / \partial x \partial y$, and converting to polar coordinates centred on the ellipse, the stress at a generic point on the ellipse boundary is computed. The **maximum stress occurs at the ends of the major axis** ($\eta = 0$ for the major-axis tips, at $x = \pm a$).

### Step 4: Compute $\sigma_{\max}$ at $x = \pm a$

At the tip of the ellipse (curvature radius $\rho = b^2/a$), the tangential (hoop) stress is:

$$\sigma_{\theta\theta} = \sigma \left(1 + \frac{2a}{b}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{m} \cdot \cos\theta \quad \text{(general point on boundary)}$$

where $m = \cos^2\theta + (a^2/b^2) \sin^2\theta$.

At $\theta = 0$ (tip of major axis), $m = 1$, so:

$$\sigma_{\max} = \sigma \left(1 + \frac{2a}{b}\right)$$

Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
K_t &= \frac{\sigma_{\max}}{\sigma} \\[6pt]
K_t &= 1 + \frac{2a}{b} = 1 + 2\sqrt{\frac{a}{\rho}}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Limit Cases

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Circular hole } (a = b): & \quad K_t = 1 + 2(1) = 3 \\[4pt]
\text{Crack-like flaw } (b \to 0): & \quad K_t \to \infty \\[4pt]
\text{Smooth plate } (a \to 0): & \quad K_t \to 1
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical insight:** The $2a/b$ term is the *amplification factor*. As the flaw becomes more elongated and sharper (large $a$, small $b$), the local stress rises without bound. This is the theoretical justification for why **sharp surface scratches are far more dangerous than blunt notches of the same depth**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Irwin's Plastic Zone Radius (Plane Stress)

### Setup

The Mode-I elastic stress field at a crack tip in polar coordinates $(r, \theta)$ measured from the tip is:

$$\sigma_{xx} = \frac{K_I}{\sqrt{2\pi r}} \cos\frac{\theta}{2} \left[ 1 - \sin\frac{\theta}{2} \sin\frac{3\theta}{2} \right]$$

$$\sigma_{yy} = \frac{K_I}{\sqrt{2\pi r}} \cos\frac{\theta}{2} \left[ 1 + \sin\frac{\theta}{2} \sin\frac{3\theta}{2} \right]$$

$$\tau_{xy} = \frac{K_I}{\sqrt{2\pi r}} \cos\frac{\theta}{2} \sin\frac{\theta}{2} \cos\frac{3\theta}{2}$$

### Step 1: Apply the Yield Condition (Plane Stress — von Mises)

In **plane stress**, yielding begins when the von Mises equivalent stress equals the yield stress $\sigma_y$:

$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_{xx}^2 + \sigma_{yy}^2 - \sigma_{xx}\sigma_{yy} + 3\tau_{xy}^2} = \sigma_y$$

### Step 2: Evaluate along $\theta = 0$ (Maximum Plastic Zone Direction)

For $\theta = 0$:

$$\cos\frac{\theta}{2} = 1, \quad \sin\frac{\theta}{2} = 0$$

Therefore:

$$\sigma_{xx} = \sigma_{yy} = \frac{K_I}{\sqrt{2\pi r}}, \quad \tau_{xy} = 0$$

Substituting into the von Mises expression:

$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_{xx}^2 + \sigma_{xx}^2 - \sigma_{xx}^2} = \sigma_{xx} = \frac{K_I}{\sqrt{2\pi r}}$$

### Step 3: Equate to Yield Stress

Setting $\sigma_{VM} = \sigma_y$ at the elastic–plastic boundary $r = r_p$:

$$\sigma_y = \frac{K_I}{\sqrt{2\pi r_p}}$$

### Step 4: Solve for $r_p$

$$\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{2\pi r_p} &= \frac{K_I}{\sigma_y} \\[4pt]
2\pi r_p &= \left(\frac{K_I}{\sigma_y}\right)^2 \\[4pt]
r_p &= \frac{1}{2\pi} \left(\frac{K_I}{\sigma_y}\right)^2
\end{aligned}$$

### Plane-Strain Correction

In **plane strain**, the triaxial constraint (non-zero $\sigma_{zz}$) shifts the von Mises stress and the elastic–plastic boundary is found at $\theta \approx \pi/2$ to be smaller by a factor of 3:

$$r_p = \frac{1}{6\pi} \left(\frac{K_I}{\sigma_y}\right)^2 \quad \text{(plane strain, max value)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical feel:** For a steel with $\sigma_y = 400$ MPa and $K_{IC} = 80$ MPa$\sqrt{\text{m}}$, the maximum plastic zone in plane strain is roughly $r_p \approx 2.7$ mm, but in plane stress it is about 8.0 mm. The plane-strain value sets the minimum thickness $B \geq 2.5(K_{IC}/\sigma_y)^2 \approx 100$ mm needed for a valid $K_{IC}$ test — a direct application of this formula.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cleavage Stress and Grain-Size Effect (Petch Relation)

For a material with mean grain diameter $d$, the cleavage stress depends on the largest grain (worst case). The **Petch–Cottrell relation** (Hall–Petch type) for cleavage stress is:

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_i + k_y \, d^{-1/2}$$

where $\sigma_i$ is the lattice friction stress and $k_y$ is the Hall–Petch slope. **Finer grain → higher cleavage stress → tougher material.** This is why refining grain size simultaneously increases yield strength and shifts the DBTT downward in BCC steels.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Stress Concentration Around a Hole — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Far-field applied stress sigma] --> B[Infinite plate with elliptical hole]
    B --> C[Stress lines detour around the hole]
    C --> D[Lines crowd at the tips of the hole]
    D --> E[Local peak stress sigma_max develops at x = a and x = -a]
    E --> F[Theoretical SCF = 1 + 2a per b]
    F --> G{Case analysis}
    G -->|a = b circular| H[Kt = 3]
    G -->|b is small sharp notch| I[Kt approaches infinity]
    G -->|a approaches 0 smooth| J[Kt approaches 1]
```

## 4.2 Plastic Zone and Crack Propagation — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applied stress sigma] --> B[Crack tip stress field]
    B --> C[Mode I opening loading]
    C --> D{Specimen thickness state}
    D -->|Thin plate| E[Plane stress]
    D -->|Thick plate| F[Plane strain]
    E --> G[Large plastic zone rp = 1 per 2pi times K per sigma y squared]
    F --> H[Small plastic zone rp = 1 per 6pi times K per sigma y squared]
    G --> I[High toughness ductile failure]
    H --> J[Low toughness brittle failure]
    I --> K[Crack tip blunting and stable growth]
    J --> L[Sharp crack unstable cleavage]
    L --> M[Transgranular fracture along cleavage planes]
```

## 4.3 Transgranular Versus Intergranular Fracture — Comparative Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fracture type classification] --> B[Transgranular]
    A --> C[Intergranular]
    B --> B1[Crack travels through the grain interior]
    B --> B2[Cleavage along low index planes like 100 in BCC and 0001 in HCP]
    B --> B3[River patterns and facets on fractograph]
    B --> B4[Common in BCC at low temperature and FCC in H environments]
    C --> C1[Crack travels along grain boundaries]
    C --> C2[Grain boundary weakening by segregation or precipitates]
    C --> C3[Smooth grain-shaped facets with dimple-free appearance]
    C --> C4[Common in temper embrittlement and stress corrosion cracking]
```

## 4.4 Modes of Crack Loading — Reference Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Crack tip loading] --> B[Mode I Opening tensile]
    A --> C[Mode II Sliding in-plane shear]
    A --> D[Mode III Tearing out-of-plane shear]
    B --> E[Most common worst case for design]
    C --> F[Mixed mode and contact fatigue]
    D --> G[Shaft torsion and surface cracks]
```

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Stress Raiser and Stress Concentration Factor [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

A **stress raiser** is a geometric discontinuity (hole, notch, scratch, keyway, change in section) in a component that produces a localized increase in stress above the nominal value.

The **Theoretical Stress Concentration Factor** is the ratio of the maximum local stress at the discontinuity to the nominal applied stress:

$$K_t = \frac{\sigma_{\max}}{\sigma_{\text{nominal}}}$$

For a circular hole in an infinite plate under uniaxial tension (Inglis solution), $K_t = 3$, meaning the local stress at the edge of the hole is three times the nominal stress. Common examples of stress raisers are oil holes in shafts, sharp fillets, and machining scratches.

---

### Question 2: What is Transgranular Cleavage Fracture? Mention its Key Features [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**Transgranular cleavage fracture** is a brittle fracture mode in which the crack propagates **through the interiors of the grains** along specific crystallographic cleavage planes.

Key features:
1. Occurs predominantly in **BCC metals** ({100} planes) and **HCP metals** ({0001} basal planes); rare in FCC.
2. **Favouring conditions:** low temperature, high strain rate, triaxial stress, large grain size, embrittling solutes.
3. **Fractographic signatures:** flat, shiny **cleavage facets**, **river patterns** (converging in the direction of crack propagation), and tongue marks.
4. The cleavage stress follows a Petch-type relation: finer grains raise the cleavage stress and shift the DBTT downward.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Derive the Inglis expression for stress concentration at an elliptical hole in an infinite plate under uniaxial tension. Discuss the limit cases. [7 Marks]

**(b)** Explain the three modes of crack loading. How does plastic deformation at the crack tip influence crack propagation? [7 Marks]

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Setup and Stress Function:** Consider an infinite plate under uniform uniaxial stress $\sigma$ with a central elliptical hole of semi-axes $a$ (load direction) and $b$ (transverse). Inglis used an Airy stress function in elliptic coordinates.

**Step 2 — Maximum Stress at the Tips:** On the boundary of the ellipse, the hoop stress is:

$$\sigma_{\theta\theta} = \sigma \cdot \frac{1 + 2a/b}{m}, \quad m = \cos^2\theta + \frac{a^2}{b^2}\sin^2\theta$$

The maximum occurs at $\theta = 0$ (major-axis tip), giving:

$$\sigma_{\max} = \sigma\left(1 + \frac{2a}{b}\right)$$

**Step 3 — SCF:**

$$K_t = 1 + \frac{2a}{b} = 1 + 2\sqrt{\frac{a}{\rho}}$$

**Step 4 — Limit Cases:**

| Geometry | $a/b$ | $K_t$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Smooth plate | $0$ | $1$ |
| Circular hole | $1$ | $3$ |
| Sharp elliptical notch | $\gg 1$ | $\to \infty$ |

The result is exact for the assumed infinite plate and is a foundational result of 2D elasticity.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Three Modes of Crack Loading:**

| Mode | Description | Common Occurrence |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **I** | Tensile opening (crack faces separate normal to the crack plane) | Most engineering structures under tension |
| **II** | In-plane shear (crack faces slide over each other) | Mixed-mode loading, lap joints |
| **III** | Out-of-plane shear (tearing mode) | Shafts in torsion, surface flaws |

**Effect of Plastic Deformation on Crack Propagation:**

In a ductile material, a sharp crack tip cannot sustain the elastically-predicted infinite stress. Localized yielding produces a **plastic zone** of radius $r_p$:

$$r_p = \frac{1}{2\pi}\left(\frac{K_I}{\sigma_y}\right)^2 \quad \text{(plane stress)}, \qquad r_p = \frac{1}{6\pi}\left(\frac{K_I}{\sigma_y}\right)^2 \quad \text{(plane strain)}$$

Effects:
1. **Crack-tip blunting** lowers the effective $K_t$.
2. **Energy absorption** raises the apparent fracture toughness $K_{IC}$.
3. **Plane strain** (thick sections) → small plastic zone → low toughness → embrittlement.
4. **Plane stress** (thin sections) → large plastic zone → high toughness → ductile behaviour.
5. The **Small Scale Yielding (SSY)** condition ($r_p \ll a, B, W-a$) must hold for LEFM to be valid; otherwise, J-integral analysis is required.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain with diagrams the effect of plastic deformation on crack propagation. Derive the expression for the plastic zone size in plane stress and plane strain. [7 Marks]

**(b)** Differentiate transgranular and intergranular fracture. Under what conditions does transgranular cleavage occur? [7 Marks]

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Plastic zone shape:** Ahead of a sharp crack tip under Mode I loading, the elastic stress field $\sigma_{ij} = K_I/\sqrt{2\pi r} \cdot f_{ij}(\theta)$ produces a "butterfly" or "kidney-shaped" plastic zone because the von Mises stress depends on the angle $\theta$.

**Derivation — Plane Stress:** At $\theta = 0$ (directly ahead of the crack), $\sigma_{xx} = \sigma_{yy} = K_I/\sqrt{2\pi r}$ and $\tau_{xy} = 0$. Von Mises criterion gives $\sigma_{VM} = \sigma_{xx}$. Setting $\sigma_{VM} = \sigma_y$:

$$\sigma_y = \frac{K_I}{\sqrt{2\pi r_p}} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad r_p = \frac{1}{2\pi}\left(\frac{K_I}{\sigma_y}\right)^2$$

**Derivation — Plane Strain:** In plane strain, the through-thickness constraint produces $\sigma_{zz} = \nu(\sigma_{xx} + \sigma_{yy})$. The von Mises stress is reduced relative to plane stress, and yielding is suppressed. The maximum plastic zone radius (which now occurs near $\theta \approx \pi/2$ rather than $\theta = 0$) is one-third of the plane-stress value:

$$r_p = \frac{1}{6\pi}\left(\frac{K_I}{\sigma_y}\right)^2$$

**Engineering implications:**
- Thick sections (plane strain) → small $r_p$ → brittle tendency → low $K_{IC}$.
- Thin sections (plane stress) → large $r_p$ → ductile tendency → high $K_{IC}$.
- The minimum specimen thickness for valid $K_{IC}$ is $B \geq 2.5(K_{IC}/\sigma_y)^2$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| Feature | Transgranular Fracture | Intergranular Fracture |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Crack path | Through the grain interior | Along grain boundaries |
| Mechanism | Cleavage on low-index planes | Grain boundary decohesion |
| Crystal systems | Common in BCC, HCP | Occurs in any system with weakened GBs |
| Fracture surface | Faceted, with river patterns | Smooth, with grain-shaped "rock-candy" facets |
| Causes | Low T, high strain rate, large grain, H in steel | GB segregation (P, S, Sn), temper embrittlement, SCC, liquid-metal embrittlement |
| Toughness | Moderate; improved by grain refinement | Very low once GBs are weakened |
| Typical examples | Cleavage in low-C steel at $-196\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ | Hydrogen embrittlement in high-strength steel, SCC in brass |

**Conditions favouring transgranular cleavage:**
1. Below the **Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature (DBTT)** for BCC alloys.
2. **High strain rate** (impact).
3. **Triaxial stress state** (notches, thick sections, welds).
4. **Large grain size** (Hall–Petch effect on cleavage stress).
5. **Sharp pre-existing flaws** acting as stress raisers.
6. Embrittling solutes (H, P, Si in temper-embrittled steel).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $K_t$ (theoretical SCF) with $K_f$ (fatigue notch factor).** $K_t$ is purely geometric and elastic; $K_f$ accounts for material notch sensitivity $q$ and is always $\leq K_t$. Mixing them is a 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 3 difference between plane stress and plane strain plastic zones.** Memorize both $1/(2\pi)$ and $1/(6\pi)$ forms — questions frequently ask one and not the other.
> 3. **Stating that a circular hole has $K_t = \infty$.** It is exactly **3** (Inglis). Only an infinitely sharp notch has $K_t = \infty$.
> 4. **Calling the KIc test a simple tensile test.** It is a specialised pre-cracked specimen test (ASTM E399) that requires minimum thickness for plane strain.
> 5. **Mixing up the senses of "inter" and "trans".** Trans = through/across (across the grain). Inter = between (between grains). Examiners specifically test this.
> 6. **Skipping the limit-case analysis of the Inglis equation.** Always list the three limits: smooth, circular, sharp. This shows conceptual depth and earns full marks.
> 7. **Drawing the plastic zone as a circle.** It is *kidney-shaped* (lobes near $\theta = 0$ and $\theta = \pi$ in plane stress, and $\theta = \pm \pi/2$ in plane strain). Examiners notice the difference.

---

## Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

### Stress Raiser
- Stress concentration factor $K_t = \sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\text{nom}}$ is dimensionless and purely geometric.
- Inglis: $K_t = 1 + 2a/b = 1 + 2\sqrt{a/\rho}$ for an elliptical hole.
- Circular hole: $K_t = 3$. Sharp crack: $K_t \to \infty$.
- Effective fatigue factor $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$ with $0 \leq q \leq 1$.
- **Peterson's charts** are used for real geometries.

### Plastic Deformation at Crack Tip
- $K_I = Y\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$ is the Mode-I stress intensity factor.
- Fracture criterion: $K_I \geq K_{IC}$ → unstable crack.
- Plane-stress plastic zone: $r_p = (1/2\pi)(K_I/\sigma_y)^2$ — large, ductile.
- Plane-strain plastic zone: $r_p = (1/6\pi)(K_I/\sigma_y)^2$ — small, brittle.
- LEFM valid only for **Small Scale Yielding (SSY)**: $r_p \ll a, B, W-a$.
- ASTM E399 minimum thickness: $B \geq 2.5(K_{IC}/\sigma_y)^2$.
- Three modes: **I** (opening, most common), **II** (sliding), **III** (tearing).

### Transgranular Fracture
- Crack propagates **through the grain interior** along cleavage planes.
- BCC cleavage plane: {100}; HCP cleavage plane: {0001}; FCC: rare.
- **Fractographic signatures:** cleavage facets, **river patterns** (converging toward initiation), tongues.
- Favouring conditions: low T (below DBTT), high strain rate, large grain, sharp flaws, embrittling solutes (H, P).
- **Petch relation:** $\sigma_c = \sigma_i + k_y d^{-1/2}$ — finer grains raise cleavage stress.
- **Contrast with intergranular** (along grain boundaries, "rock-candy" facets, GB weakening).

### Key Engineering Takeaways
- Thicker sections of the same material are *more* brittle, not less (plane-strain embrittlement).
- The presence of even a small sharp defect can drop the failure stress by an order of magnitude.
- Fracture toughness $K_{IC}$, not yield strength $\sigma_y$, is the relevant design parameter once a crack exists.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intergranular Fracture - Ductile to Brittle Transition Temperature (DBTT)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPET304 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Intergranular Fracture - Ductile to Brittle Transition Temperature (DBTT)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Intergranular Fracture — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intergranular Fracture** is a mode of brittle fracture in polycrystalline materials in which **crack propagation occurs along the grain boundaries** rather than *transgranularly* (through the grains). The fracture path follows the weak interfaces separating adjacent grains, producing a characteristic "rock-candy" or faceted fracture surface when viewed under a scanning electron microscope (SEM).

In this failure mode, the grain boundary itself — or the immediate vicinity of it — becomes the weakest link in the microstructure. Under applied stress (often in conjunction with an aggressive environment or elevated temperature), microvoids nucleate, link, and separate the grains along their boundaries, leaving the grain interiors essentially intact.

### Causes of Intergranular Fracture

| # | Mechanism | Brief Description |
|---|-----------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Grain-boundary segregation** | Impurity atoms (S, P, Sb, Sn, Bi) diffuse to GBs and weaken cohesion |
| 2 | **Grain-boundary precipitation** | Continuous brittle films (e.g., Fe₃C in temper-embrittled steel) form along GBs |
| 3 | **Stress-corrosion cracking (SCC)** | Chemical attack + tensile stress causes GB decohesion (e.g., alkali embrittlement of steels) |
| 4 | **Hydrogen embrittlement** | H atoms segregate to GBs → decohesion (HE in pipeline steels, high-strength steels) |
| 5 | **Creep fracture (at high T)** | Wedge cracks / cavities nucleate at GBs perpendicular to applied stress |
| 6 | **Liquid-metal embrittlement (LME)** | Molten metal penetrates GBs (e.g., Hg embrittlement of Al) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Intergranular fracture is *not* synonymous with brittle fracture — but in practice, GB separation in structural alloys almost always exhibits **zero to negligible macroscopic ductility**, classifying it as brittle.

---

## 1.2 Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature (DBTT) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature (DBTT)** is the temperature below which a material — typically a **Body-Centered Cubic (BCC)** metal or alloy — exhibits **brittle (cleavage) fracture**, and above which it fractures in a **ductile** manner with significant plastic deformation and energy absorption.

It is most prominently observed in **ferritic steels, tungsten, chromium, molybdenum**, and other BCC metals. **Face-Centered Cubic (FCC)** metals (Cu, Al, austenitic SS, Ni) and most HCP metals generally do **not** exhibit a sharp DBTT within normal service ranges.

The transition is **not a sharp, single temperature** but rather a **range** over which the fracture behavior gradually changes. The *DBTT* is operationally defined by a criterion — most commonly:
- The temperature at which the **Charpy impact energy = 20 J** (or 15 ft·lb in older codes)
- The temperature corresponding to **50% ductile / 50% brittle** fracture appearance
- The temperature at which **lateral expansion = 0.38 mm**

### Conceptual Analogy: The Chocolate Bar Test 🟫

Imagine holding a **chocolate bar** at two different temperatures:

- **Cold bar (below DBTT):** You snap it cleanly. The break is sharp, almost no bending. Energy absorbed is **tiny**. ➜ This is **brittle fracture**.
- **Warm bar (above DBTT):** You bend it; it warps, deforms plastically, and tears with much greater effort. ➜ This is **ductile fracture**.

The chocolate-bar analogy perfectly captures the essence of DBTT: a temperature threshold separating "snap" from "bend". The difference is that in real BCC metals, the transition is driven by the competition between **cleavage stress** and **yield stress** (see Cottrell–Petch analysis later).

---

## 1.3 Standard Test Method

The **Charpy V-Notch Impact Test (ASTM E23)** is the standardized method to characterize DBTT:

- A notched specimen (10 × 10 × 55 mm) is cooled/heated to a target temperature.
- It is fractured by a swinging pendulum.
- The **absorbed energy** is plotted against **test temperature** to produce the famous **S-shaped Charpy curve**.

Key points on this curve:
- **Upper Shelf Energy (USE):** Maximum energy absorbed (fully ductile fracture).
- **Lower Shelf Energy (LSE):** Minimum energy absorbed (fully brittle fracture).
- **DBTT region:** Steep transition zone between the two shelves.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Units:** Charpy energy is in **Joules (J)**; Lateral expansion is in **millimeters (mm)**; Temperature is in **°C** or **K**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Micromechanism of Intergranular Fracture

Step-by-step logic of the failure process:

1. **Stress concentration at grain boundaries:** Triple junctions and GB ledges act as stress raisers.
2. **Chemical/thermal weakening:** Segregated solutes (S, P) reduce the cohesive energy across the GB plane.
3. **Microcrack nucleation:** At elevated temperature (creep), cavities nucleate at GBs; at low temperature, decohesion initiates when local stress exceeds GB strength.
4. **Coalescence & propagation:** Microcracks link up along GBs, forming a continuous fracture path with negligible plasticity.
5. **Final separation:** Grains are pulled apart as intact polyhedra — the classic "rock-candy" morphology is revealed.

```
Brittle film at GB:   ──◯───◯───◯───   (weak link, easy decohesion)
                ↓
Crack path:   ╱  ╱  ╱  ╱  ╱  ╱  ╱  (intergranular)
```

## 2.2 The Physics of DBTT — Why BCC and not FCC?

The transition arises from the **temperature dependence of yield stress**.

- In **BCC metals**, the **Peierls–Nabarro stress** is high at low temperatures, making dislocation motion difficult (thermally activated). Therefore, **yield strength (σy) rises sharply as T decreases**.
- The **cleavage strength (σf)** is *relatively insensitive* to temperature.
- At the **DBTT**, the curves σy(T) and σf(T) **intersect**:
   - **T < DBTT:** σf < σy → cleavage occurs *before* yielding → **brittle**
   - **T > DBTT:** σy < σf → plastic flow happens first → **ductile**

In **FCC metals**, σy is low and weakly temperature-dependent, so σy < σf at virtually all T → **no DBTT**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning / Use | Typical Units |
|---|----------|---------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ | **Hall–Petch relation** — yield stress vs. grain size $d$ | MPa; $d$ in m |
| 2 | $\text{DBTT} = A - B \ln d^{-1/2}$ | Empirical grain-size dependence of DBTT (Cottrell–Petch) | °C or K |
| 3 | $\sigma_f \cdot k_y = \beta \mu \gamma_s$ | **Cottrell–Petch** cleavage criterion (relates $\sigma_f$ to GB energy) | MPa; J/m² |
| 4 | $T_{\text{DBTT}} \propto \ln(\dot{\varepsilon})$ | Higher strain rate $\dot{\varepsilon}$ → higher DBTT | K |
| 5 | $K_{IC} = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Linear-elastic fracture toughness (related to brittle regime) | MPa·m^1/2 |
| 6 | $\% \text{Shear} = 100 - \% \text{CLEavage}$ | Ductile/brittle fracture appearance on Charpy fracture surface | % |
| 7 | $E_{\text{abs}} = mgh - \frac{1}{2}mv^2$ | Charpy absorbed energy (pendulum height difference) | J |
| 8 | $T_{\text{DBTT, notched}} > T_{\text{DBTT, unnotched}}$ | Notches raise the apparent transition temperature | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Notation Safeguard:** For all absolute-value expressions, write $\vert x \vert$ (LaTeX `\vert`) — never use the bare `\|` symbol inside markdown table cells, as it breaks table parsing.

## 2.4 Why This Matters in Engineering

- **Pipeline design in Arctic regions** (e.g., Trans-Alaska Pipeline): DBTT must be below the lowest service temperature to prevent catastrophic brittle rupture. The 1944 *SS Schenectady* (Liberty ship) and 1940s *Liberty ship* failures are the classic KTU case studies.
- **Nuclear reactor pressure vessels** must retain USE > 68 J even after decades of neutron irradiation (irradiation raises DBTT by ~1 J/°C per dpa).
- **Cold-formed structural steel** selection (ASTM A36, A572, A516) is governed by Charpy DBTT requirements.
- **Boiler & pressure-vessel code (ASME Sec. VIII)** mandates impact testing when design temperature approaches DBTT.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cottrell–Petch Relation for DBTT

The Cottrell–Petch model gives a fundamental relationship between fracture stress $\sigma_f$, grain size $d$, and the DBTT.

### Step 1 — Set up the cleavage criterion

The propagation of a cleavage crack of length $2a$ in a polycrystal requires the local tensile stress to overcome a critical value:

$$
\sigma_f \approx \left( \frac{2 E \gamma_s}{\pi a} \right)^{1/2}
$$

where $E$ = Young's modulus, $\gamma_s$ = effective surface energy (≈ 2 × grain-boundary energy).

### Step 2 — Identify the critical flaw size with grain size

For a transgranular cleavage crack, the limiting microcrack length is set by the **grain diameter $d$**, because the crack cannot extend indefinitely without crossing GBs (which deflect or blunt it):

$$
a \approx d
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute

$$
\sigma_f = \left( \frac{2 E \gamma_s}{\pi d} \right)^{1/2}
$$

### Step 4 — Express $\sigma_f$ as the Hall–Petch-controlled flow stress at DBTT

At the transition, the **fracture stress** equals the **yield stress**:

$$
\sigma_y(d, T) = \sigma_f(d)
$$

Applying Hall–Petch to $\sigma_y$:

$$
\sigma_0(T) + k_y d^{-1/2} = \left( \frac{2 E \gamma_s}{\pi} \right)^{1/2} d^{-1/2}
$$

### Step 5 — Isolate the grain-size-dependent terms

Rearranging:

$$
\sigma_0(T) = d^{-1/2} \left[ \left( \frac{2 E \gamma_s}{\pi} \right)^{1/2} - k_y \right]
$$

### Step 6 — Solve for the temperature at which this equality holds

Since $\sigma_0(T)$ decreases approximately linearly with temperature (thermally activated slip), we write:

$$
\sigma_0(T) = \sigma_0^* - \alpha T
$$

Substituting and solving for $T = T_{\text{DBTT}}$:

$$
\sigma_0^* - \alpha T_{\text{DBTT}} = d^{-1/2} \left[ \left( \frac{2 E \gamma_s}{\pi} \right)^{1/2} - k_y \right]
$$

$$
T_{\text{DBTT}} = \frac{1}{\alpha} \left\{ \sigma_0^* - d^{-1/2} \left[ \left( \frac{2 E \gamma_s}{\pi} \right)^{1/2} - k_y \right] \right\}
$$

### Step 7 — Final compact form

> [!NOTE]
> **Cottrell–Petch Result:**
> $$\boxed{\,T_{\text{DBTT}} = A - B \ln(d^{-1/2})\,}$$
> where $A$ and $B$ are material constants involving $E$, $\gamma_s$, $k_y$, and $\alpha$.

**Interpretation:** Decreasing grain size ($d \downarrow$) increases $d^{-1/2}$, hence **lowers DBTT** — finer grain steels are tougher at low temperatures.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Factors Influencing DBTT

| Factor | Effect on DBTT | Magnitude (typical) | Engineering Implication |
|--------|---------------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| **Decrease grain size** $d$ | $\downarrow$ DBTT | Halving $d$ lowers DBTT by 15–25 °C | Heat-treat for fine austenite grain (ASTM 5+) |
| **Increase strain rate** $\dot{\varepsilon}$ | $\uparrow$ DBTT | Dynamic loading → +30 to +60 °C | Earthquake-resistant design uses impact-tested grades |
| **Neutron irradiation** | $\uparrow$ DBTT | ~1 °C per 10⁻⁴ dpa shift | Reactor pressure vessel surveillance program |
| **Cold work / pre-strain** | $\uparrow$ DBTT | Can raise DBTT by 50 °C | Avoid excessive forming in cold climates |
| **Notch acuity** | $\uparrow$ DBTT | Sharp cracks >> blunt notches | Use CTOD / $K_{IC}$ for cracked structures |
| **Alloying (Ni, Mn)** | $\downarrow$ DBTT | 1% Ni ≈ −10 °C shift | Use 3.5% Ni steel for LNG service (−165 °C) |
| **Impurities (P, S, Sb, Sn)** | $\uparrow$ DBTT | Temper embrittlement +50–150 °C | Control residual P & S in rotor steels |
| **Increasing test section thickness** | $\uparrow$ DBTT | Plane-strain vs. plane-stress | Thick plates more brittle than thin sheets |

---

## 3.3 Energy Partition in the Charpy Curve (Quantitative)

The total Charpy energy $E_{\text{tot}}$ can be partitioned as:

$$
E_{\text{tot}} = E_{\text{initiation}} + E_{\text{propagation}}
$$

- **Upper shelf:** $E_{\text{initiation}} \gg E_{\text{propagation}}$ (ductile, deep plastic zone).
- **Lower shelf:** $E_{\text{initiation}} \approx 0$; $E_{\text{propagation}}$ is small (cleavage pop-in).
- **Transition:** Energy drops because **the plastic zone ahead of the notch can no longer be sustained** at the constrained stress state.

A useful empirical fit for the Charpy transition curve:

$$
E(T) = \frac{\text{USE} - \text{LSE}}{1 + \exp\!\left[ k(T - T_0) \right]} + \text{LSE}
$$

where $T_0$ ≈ DBTT (at the inflection point) and $k$ is a sharpness parameter.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — Charpy Energy vs. Temperature Curve (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    axisX[Temperature T, degree C]:::axis
    axisY[Absorbed Energy E, J]:::axis
    curve[Charpy Transition Curve S shaped]:::curve
    use[Upper Shelf Energy USE = 250 J for mild steel]:::shelf
    lse[Lower Shelf Energy LSE = 5 to 20 J]:::shelf
    dbtt[DBTT defined at E = 20 J or 50 percent shear]:::zone
    dbr[DBTT Region]:::zone
    brittleReg[Brittle Regime T less than DBTT]:::zone
    ductileReg[Ductile Regime T greater than DBTT]:::zone
    a1[At low T cleavage is dominant]:::note
    a2[At high T microvoid coalescence is dominant]:::note
    a3[Irradiation shifts curve right and lowers USE]:::note
    axisY --> curve
    axisX --> curve
    curve --> use
    curve --> lse
    curve --> dbtt
    dbtt --> dbr
    dbr --> brittleReg
    dbr --> ductileReg
    brittleReg --- a1
    ductileReg --- a2
    curve --- a3
    classDef axis fill:#f6f6f6,stroke:#888,color:#222
    classDef curve fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003366,color:#003366
    classDef shelf fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#856404
    classDef zone fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#842029,color:#842029
    classDef note fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132,color:#0f5132
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Y-axis: Absorbed energy (J).
- X-axis: Test temperature (°C).
- S-curve is the locus of (T, E) data points.
- The *DBTT* is read off at the **midpoint of the steep transition** or the **20 J criterion** as required by code.

---

## 4.2 Diagram B — Intergranular vs. Transgranular Fracture (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    loading[Applied Tensile Stress sigma]:::load
    gbA[Grain A]:::grain
    gbB[Grain B]:::grain
    gbC[Grain C]:::grain
    gbBoundary[Weak Grain Boundary with segregated impurities]:::weakgb
    crackPath1[Intergranular crack path follows GBs]:::intgran
    crackPath2[Transgranular cleavage cracks through grains]:::transgran
    surface1[Fracture Surface rock candy facets]:::facs
    surface2[Fracture Surface river pattern cleavage]:::facs2
    loading --> gbBoundary
    gbBoundary --> crackPath1
    crackPath1 --> surface1
    loading --> gbA
    loading --> gbB
    loading --> gbC
    gbA --> crackPath2
    gbB --> crackPath2
    gbC --> crackPath2
    crackPath2 --> surface2
    classDef load fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#a00,color:#600
    classDef grain fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#383d41,color:#383d41
    classDef weakgb fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#842029,color:#842029
    classDef intgran fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#856404
    classDef transgran fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#084298,color:#084298
    classDef facs fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#5a3d8a,color:#3a1f5f
    classDef facs2 fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132,color:#0f5132
```

**Visual reading:**
- The **red ribbon** (weak GB) is the intergranular path.
- The **blue arrows** cutting through grains represent transgranular cleavage.
- After fracture, the *rock-candy* morphology (left) shows intact polyhedral grains; the *river pattern* (right) shows cleavage steps inside the grains.

---

## 4.3 Diagram C — Mechanism Map for Intergranular Failure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start[Polycrystalline Material under Stress]:::startnode
    cond1{Temperature and Environment}:::decision
    branch1[High T greater than 0.4 Tm Creep]:::branch
    branch2[Low T plus Aggressive Environment SCC]:::branch
    branch3[Cold plus Tensile plus H2 Hydrogen Embrittlement]:::branch
    branch4[Moderate T plus Long Hold Temper Embrittlement]:::branch

    outcome2[GB Dissolution and Branching Cracks]:::outcome
    outcome3[GB Decohesion due to H]:::outcome
    outcome4[Continuous Carbide Film at GB]:::outcome
    final[Intergranular Fracture]:::finalnode
    start --> cond1
    cond1 --> branch1
    cond1 --> branch2
    cond1 --> branch3
    cond1 --> branch4
    branch1 --> outcome1
    branch2 --> outcome2
    branch3 --> outcome3
    branch4 --> outcome4

    outcome2 --> final

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** All four pathways converge on *intergranular fracture* but originate from different atomistic + environmental causes — a fact KTU examiners frequently test in descriptive answers.
